column9: C is incorrect because holding the breath before and during swallowing describes the supraglottic swallow, a technique used to protect the airway by closing the vocal folds, not prolonging laryngeal elevation
column10: D is incorrect because swallowing with increased effort describes the effortful swallow, a maneuver that improves pharyngeal clearance by increasing tongue base retraction and pharyngeal constriction, not enhancing UES opening
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 68-year-old patient with a history of stroke and recurrent aspiration pneumonia is being taught a swallowing maneuver to protect the airway during meals. Which of the following techniques involves holding the breath before swallowing, maintaining breath-hold during the swallow, and coughing immediately afterward to clear residue?
column7: The supraglottic swallow involves holding the breath before swallowing to close the vocal folds, maintaining breath-hold during the swallow to protect the airway, and coughing afterward to clear residue. This technique is used to prevent aspiration in patients with delayed airway closure
column8: Mendelsohn maneuver is incorrect because the Mendelsohn maneuver involves voluntarily prolonging laryngeal elevation to enhance upper esophageal sphincter opening, not airway protection
column9: Effortful swallow is incorrect because the effortful swallow involves swallowing with increased effort to improve pharyngeal clearance, not airway closure
column10: Head tilt maneuver is  incorrect because the head tilt maneuver involves tilting the head toward the stronger side to direct the bolus unilaterally, not airway protection
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 70-year-old male presents with oropharyngeal dysphagia after suffering a left hemispheric stroke. A modified barium swallow study reveals unilateral pharyngeal weakness. Based on the neuroanatomy of the corticobulbar tract, which of the following findings is most likely?
column3: Right-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head turn to the left
column4: Left-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head turn to the right
column5: Right-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head tilt to the left
column6: Left-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head tilt to the right
column7: Right-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head turn to the left is correct as the corticobulbar tract descends from the cerebral cortex and decussates before innervating the contralateral nucleus ambiguus, which controls pharyngeal muscles. Therefore, a left hemispheric stroke results in right-sided pharyngeal weakness. A head turn to the weak side (right) closes off the impaired pharynx, directing the bolus toward the stronger, left side to improve clearance and reduce aspiration risk
column8: Left-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head turn to the right is incorrect because a left hemispheric stroke affects the contralateral (right) pharynx, not the ipsilateral (left) side
column9: Right-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head tilt to the left is incorrect because head tilt compensates more for unilateral oral weakness, not pharyngeal deficits, making it ineffective for this patient
column10: Left-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head tilt to the right is incorrect because head tilt does not manage pharyngeal weakness, and a left hemispheric stroke would not cause left-sided pharyngeal dysfunction
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 70-year-old male presents with oropharyngeal dysphagia after suffering a right hemispheric stroke. A modified barium swallow study reveals unilateral pharyngeal weakness. Based on the neuroanatomy of the corticobulbar tract, which of the following findings is most likely?
column3: Left-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head turn to the right
column4: Left-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head turn to the left
column5: Right-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head tilt to the left
column6: Right-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head tilt to the right
column7: Left-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head turn to the right is correct as the corticobulbar tract decussates before innervating the contralateral nucleus ambiguus, which controls pharyngeal muscles. A right hemispheric stroke leads to left-sided pharyngeal weakness. A head turn to the weak side (left) closes off the impaired pharynx, redirecting the bolus toward the stronger, right side, improving bolus clearance and reducing aspiration risk
column8: Left-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head turn to the left is incorrect because turning toward the weak side does not redirect the bolus effectively to the stronger, functional pharyngeal musculature
column9: Right-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head tilt to the left is incorrect because head tilt is used to manage oral weakness, not pharyngeal dysfunction
column10: Right-sided pharyngeal weakness requiring a head tilt to the right is incorrect because the corticobulbar tract decussates, meaning a right hemispheric stroke would cause left pharyngeal weakness, not right
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 70-year-old male presents with oropharyngeal dysphagia and difficulty manipulating food within the oral cavity after suffering a right hemispheric stroke. On examination, his tongue deviates to the left when protruded, suggesting unilateral tongue weakness. Based on these findings, which of the following compensatory strategies is most appropriate?
column7: Head tilt to the left is correct as the corticobulbar tract decussates, meaning a right hemispheric stroke causes left-sided tongue weakness due to impaired function of the left hypoglossal nerve. The tongue deviates toward the weak side (left) upon protrusion. A head tilt to the stronger side (right) helps gravity direct the bolus away from the weak side, improving bolus control
column8: Head turn to the right is incorrect because head turn is primarily used to compensate for pharyngeal weakness, not oral-stage deficits like tongue dysfunction
column9: Head tilt to the right is incorrect because tilting toward the weak side (left) would not effectively direct the bolus to the stronger side for improved control
column10: Head turn to the left is incorrect because turning the head does not assist in overcoming unilateral tongue weakness; head tilt is the preferred strategy for oral-stage deficits
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 70-year-old male presents with oropharyngeal dysphagia and difficulty manipulating food within the oral cavity after suffering a left hemispheric stroke. On examination, his tongue deviates to the right when protruded, suggesting unilateral tongue weakness. Based on these findings, which of the following compensatory strategies is most appropriate?
column7: Head tilt to the right is correct as the corticobulbar tract decussates, meaning a left hemispheric stroke causes right-sided tongue weakness due to impaired function of the right hypoglossal nerve. The tongue deviates toward the weak side (right) upon protrusion. A head tilt to the stronger side (left) helps gravity direct the bolus away from the weak side, improving bolus control
column8: Head turn to the left is incorrect because head turn is primarily used to compensate for pharyngeal weakness, not oral-stage deficits like tongue dysfunction
column9: Head tilt to the left is incorrect because tilting toward the weak side (right) would not effectively direct the bolus to the stronger side for improved control
column10: Head turn to the right is incorrect because turning the head does not assist in overcoming unilateral tongue weakness; head tilt is the preferred strategy for oral-stage deficits
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 68-year-old female with a history of Parkinson’s disease presents with oropharyngeal dysphagia, reporting frequent choking on thin liquids. A modified barium swallow study reveals delayed initiation of the swallow and premature spillage of liquids into the pharynx, increasing the risk of aspiration. Which compensatory swallowing maneuver is most appropriate to improve her swallowing safety?
column4: Head turn to the weak side
column5: Head tilt to the strong side
column7: The chin tuck maneuver (also known as the "neck flexion posture") is correct as it helps by narrowing the airway entrance, reducing premature spillage into the pharynx, and improving control of thin liquids before swallowing. It is particularly beneficial in patients with delayed swallow initiation, such as those with Parkinson’s disease
column8: Head turn to the weak side is incorrect because head turn is used to direct the bolus toward the stronger pharyngeal side in cases of unilateral pharyngeal weakness, not delayed swallow initiation
column9: Head tilt to the strong side is incorrect because head tilt is primarily used for oral-stage deficits, such as unilateral tongue weakness, rather than preventing premature pharyngeal spillage
column10: Pharyngeal widening technique is incorrect because no such maneuver exists; in fact, widening the pharynx would increase aspiration risk rather than reduce it
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 55-year-old male with a history of laryngeal cancer treated with radiation presents with oropharyngeal dysphagia and frequent aspiration despite intact pharyngeal strength. A modified barium swallow study reveals reduced airway closure and penetration of food into the laryngeal vestibule, increasing his risk of aspiration. Which swallowing technique is most appropriate to improve airway protection?
column5: Head turn to the weak side
column7: The super-supraglottic swallow maneuver incorrect as it is designed to enhance airway closure and reduce aspiration risk by involving a forceful breath-hold (Valsalva) before swallowing, followed by an immediate cough after the swallow. This technique improves laryngeal vestibule closure, particularly in patients with impaired supraglottic function, such as those with a history of laryngeal radiation or surgery
column8: Chin tuck maneuver is incorrect because while it helps narrow the airway and delay spillage, it does not actively recruit supraglottic structures to enhance airway closure in patients with reduced laryngeal protection
column9: Head turn to the weak side is incorrect because this maneuver is used for unilateral pharyngeal weakness, not for patients with impaired supraglottic airway closure
column10: Effortful swallow technique is incorrect because it primarily increases bolus propulsion through the pharynx but does not directly improve airway closure or prevent aspiration into the laryngeal vestibule
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 45-year-old male with chronic low back pain secondary to lumbar degenerative disc disease presents to your clinic for rehabilitation treatment options. He reports worsening pain with movement and prolonged sitting. You decide to incorporate therapeutic modalities into his treatment plan. Which of the following is the most appropriate modality to improve his pain and function?
column7: Transcutaneous electrical nerve stimulation (TENS)’ is correct because it is a non-invasive modality that provides pain relief by stimulating afferent nerves and modulating pain perception via the gate control theory and endogenous opioid release. TENS has demonstrated efficacy in reducing pain and improving function in patients with chronic low back pain
column8: Ultrasound therapy’ is incorrect because while it provides deep heat and can promote tissue healing, there is limited evidence supporting its effectiveness in chronic low back pain management
column9: Cryotherapy’ is incorrect because it is primarily used for acute injuries or post-exercise recovery by reducing inflammation and local metabolic activity, rather than for chronic pain syndromes
column10: Shortwave diathermy’ is incorrect because although it delivers deep heat and may improve tissue extensibility, its use in chronic low back pain is not well-supported, and it carries risks such as burns in patients with sensory deficits

column2: A 25-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe pain and swelling in his left lower leg after a motor vehicle accident. On examination, he has pain out of proportion to the injury, tense compartments, and pain with passive dorsiflexion of the toes. The most definitive method to confirm the diagnosis of acute compartment syndrome is:
column7: Manometry is correct because it is the most definitive diagnostic test for acute compartment syndrome, confirming the diagnosis when pressures exceed 30 mmHg or when the differential pressure (diastolic BP minus intracompartmental pressure) is ≤30 mmHg. A common mnemonic is the "6 P's" (Pain, Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia), but pain out of proportion is the most sensitive early finding
column8: Clinical examination alone is incorrect because while pain out of proportion, tense compartments, and pain with passive stretch are suggestive, the diagnosis must be confirmed with direct pressure measurement, particularly in unconscious or obtunded patients
column9: Doppler ultrasound is incorrect because it is primarily used for vascular assessment and may miss compartment syndrome, as pulses are often present until late in the disease process
column10: MRI is incorrect because although it can show muscle edema and soft tissue changes, it is not a practical or timely diagnostic tool for acute compartment syndrome, where rapid diagnosis and fasciotomy are critical

column2: A 22-year-old long-distance runner presents with progressively worsening bilateral lower leg pain during exercise that resolves with rest. He reports no pain at rest but experiences tightness and aching in the anterior shins after running a few miles. His examination is normal at rest, with no signs of acute swelling, erythema, or neurological deficits. What is the most definitive test to confirm the diagnosis?
column7: Post-exercise manometry is correct because it is the gold standard for diagnosing chronic exertional compartment syndrome (CECS), with diagnostic criteria including pre-exercise pressures ≥15 mmHg, 1-minute post-exercise pressures ≥30 mmHg, or 5-minute post-exercise pressures ≥20 mmHg. Pain that worsens with activity and resolves with rest is characteristic
column8: MRI is incorrect because while it may show muscle edema in CECS, it is not definitive and is more useful for ruling out stress fractures or other soft tissue injuries
column9: Doppler ultrasound is incorrect because it is primarily used to assess vascular causes of exertional leg pain, such as popliteal artery entrapment syndrome, which presents with different symptoms
column10: Electromyography (EMG) is incorrect because it is useful for diagnosing nerve entrapments, such as peroneal neuropathy, but does not assess compartment pressures or confirm CECS

column2: A 56-year-old male is admitted to the ICU with sepsis and multi-organ failure. After prolonged mechanical ventilation, he develops diffuse weakness and difficulty weaning from the ventilator. An electromyography (EMG) and nerve conduction study (NCS) is performed. Which of the following findings is most consistent with critical illness polyneuropathy?
column3: Low-amplitude compound muscle action potentials (CMAPs) and sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) with normal conduction velocities
column4: Prolonged distal motor latencies and reduced conduction velocities with conduction block
column5: Normal sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) with low-amplitude compound muscle action potentials (CMAPs)
column6: Fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves with normal motor and sensory nerve conduction studies
column7: Low-amplitude compound muscle action potentials (CMAPs) and sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) with normal conduction velocities is correct because critical illness polyneuropathy (CIP) is a diffuse axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy affecting both motor and sensory nerves, leading to reduced amplitudes of both CMAPs and SNAPs while preserving conduction velocities. This is due to primary axonal degeneration rather than demyelination
column8: Prolonged distal motor latencies and reduced conduction velocities with conduction block is incorrect because these findings are characteristic of demyelinating neuropathies such as Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP) rather than the axonal loss seen in CIP
column9: Normal sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) with low-amplitude compound muscle action potentials (CMAPs) is incorrect because this pattern suggests a motor neuron disorder like ALS rather than a diffuse sensorimotor axonal polyneuropathy
column10: Fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves with normal motor and sensory nerve conduction studies is incorrect because these findings suggest an isolated myopathic process or a purely motor neuron disease rather than the axonal sensorimotor neuropathy seen in CIP

column2: A 65-year-old male with metastatic lung cancer presents with severe, constant bone pain localized to his right femur. The pain is significantly limiting his mobility and is not well controlled with NSAIDs. What is the most appropriate initial treatment for his pain?
column3: Long-acting opioid with a short-acting opioid for breakthrough pain
column7: Long-acting opioid with a short-acting opioid for breakthrough pain is correct because opioids are the cornerstone of cancer pain management, particularly for moderate to severe pain. A long-acting opioid provides baseline pain control, while a short-acting opioid addresses breakthrough pain. The WHO analgesic ladder supports the use of strong opioids for severe pain
column8: Bisphosphonates or denosumab is incorrect because while these agents help reduce skeletal-related events and provide long-term pain relief in bone metastases, they do not offer immediate analgesia and are not first-line for acute pain control
column9: Acetaminophen (Tylenol) is incorrect because while it may be used for mild cancer pain, it is insufficient as monotherapy for severe bone pain and does not address the pain intensity described in the case
column10: Gabapentin is incorrect because it is primarily used for neuropathic pain and does not effectively treat nociceptive pain from bone metastases

column2: A 50-year-old office worker presents with numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, middle, and radial half of the ring finger, along with hand weakness and difficulty gripping objects. A nerve conduction study (NCS) with a short segment incremental study (inching) across the wrist reveals a significant drop in amplitude without notable temporal dispersion. These findings are most consistent with:
column7: Conduction block is correct because the patient's symptoms of median nerve compression at the wrist (carpal tunnel syndrome), along with the NCS findings of a significant drop in amplitude without temporal dispersion, indicate focal demyelination at the wrist, characteristic of conduction block
column8: Axonotmesis is incorrect because this involves axon loss, which would result in reduced amplitudes both proximally and distally rather than a focal conduction block at the wrist
column9: Myopathic recruitment is incorrect because it refers to early recruitment of low-amplitude, short-duration motor unit action potentials (MUAPs) on electromyography (EMG), which is seen in muscle disorders rather than nerve entrapments
column10: Neuromuscular transmission defect is incorrect because conditions like myasthenia gravis cause a decremental response on repetitive nerve stimulation, not a focal conduction block seen in inching studies for entrapment neuropathies

column2: A 72-year-old male with Parkinson’s disease presents to physical therapy due to frequent freezing of gait episodes, particularly when initiating movement and turning. Which of the following is the most effective strategy to improve his gait and reduce freezing episodes?
column3: Utilizing rhythmic auditory and visual cueing
column4: Encouraging the patient to take shorter steps to prevent tripping
column5: Training the patient to rely on proprioception rather than external cues
column6: Emphasizing rapid, spontaneous movements to override freezing episodes
column7: Utilizing rhythmic auditory and visual cueing is correct because external cues, such as a metronome or visual floor markers, help bypass the defective basal ganglia circuitry, facilitating movement initiation and improving step regulation
column8: Encouraging the patient to take shorter steps to prevent tripping is incorrect because reducing step length can exacerbate freezing episodes and further impair gait fluidity
column9: Training the patient to rely on proprioception rather than external cues is incorrect because Parkinson’s disease affects automatic motor control, making external sensory cues essential to improve movement initiation
column10: Emphasizing rapid, spontaneous movements to override freezing episodes is incorrect because quick, unplanned movements can increase fall risk and do not effectively counteract the motor block seen in freezing of gait
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A patient presents with sudden-onset weakness affecting the face, arm, and leg on one side of the body, without sensory deficits, visual field abnormalities, or cortical signs such as aphasia or neglect. Which type of stroke is most likely responsible for this presentation?
column3: Lacunar infarct of the posterior limb of the internal capsule
column4: Watershed infarct between the anterior and middle cerebral arteries
column5: Large-vessel infarct of the middle cerebral artery
column6: Brainstem stroke affecting the medial medulla
column7: Lacunar infarct of the posterior limb of the internal capsule is correct because it affects the corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts, leading to contralateral hemiparesis of the face, arm, and leg without sensory loss, cortical signs, or visual deficits. This is a pure motor stroke
column8: Watershed infarct between the anterior and middle cerebral arteries is incorrect because it typically causes proximal limb weakness (man-in-the-barrel syndrome) rather than affecting the entire face, arm, and leg
column9: Large-vessel infarct of the middle cerebral artery is incorrect because it usually presents with additional cortical signs, such as aphasia (dominant hemisphere), neglect (non-dominant hemisphere), or hemianopia
column10: Brainstem stroke affecting the medial medulla is incorrect because it causes hemiparesis along with ipsilateral tongue weakness (medial medullary syndrome), which is not seen in this case
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A patient presents with sudden-onset numbness and decreased sensation in the face, arm, and leg on one side of the body, without weakness, visual field deficits, or cortical signs. MRI reveals a small subcortical infarct. Which type of stroke is most likely responsible for this presentation?
column3: Lacunar infarct of the ventroposterolateral (VPL) nucleus of the thalamus
column4: Lacunar infarct of the posterior limb of the internal capsule
column5: Large-vessel infarct of the middle cerebral artery
column6: Brainstem stroke affecting the medial medulla
column7: Lacunar infarct of the ventroposterolateral (VPL) nucleus of the thalamus is correct because it disrupts sensory pathways from the contralateral body, leading to pure sensory stroke with no motor deficits or cortical signs. The ventroposterolateral (VPL) nucleus of the thalamus is the most common site for a pure sensory stroke, but it can also occur in the ventroposteromedial (VPM) nucleus of the thalamus, affecting facial sensation, or in the thalamocortical projections (posterior limb of the internal capsule, corona radiata) if sensory pathways are selectively involved. Additionally, infarcts in the parietal subcortical white matter can cause a pure sensory deficit without cortical signs if restricted to sensory pathways
column8: Lacunar infarct of the posterior limb of the internal capsule is incorrect because it primarily affects motor fibers, leading to pure motor stroke rather than an isolated sensory deficit
column9: Large-vessel infarct of the middle cerebral artery is incorrect because it typically causes mixed motor and sensory deficits along with cortical signs such as aphasia, neglect, or hemianopia
column10: Brainstem stroke affecting the medial medulla is incorrect because it causes hemiparesis along with ipsilateral tongue weakness (medial medullary syndrome) rather than an isolated sensory loss
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 48-year-old male presents with progressive weakness that began in his distal lower extremities and has ascended to involve his upper limbs over the past five days. He denies sensory loss but reports difficulty walking due to weakness and mild shortness of breath. On examination, he has flaccid quadriparesis, absent deep tendon reflexes, and bilateral facial weakness. Cerebrospinal fluid analysis reveals an elevated protein concentration with a normal white blood cell count. Which of the following nerve conduction study findings would most strongly support the diagnosis?
column3: Prolonged F-wave latencies and conduction block with temporal dispersion
column4: Decremental response to repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz
column5: Reduced somatosensory evoked potential amplitudes with delayed central conduction time
column6: Increased insertional activity with fibrillation potentials on electromyography
column7: Prolonged F-wave latencies and conduction block with temporal dispersion is correct because Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy characterized by slowed conduction velocity, conduction block, and prolonged F-wave latencies due to demyelination of proximal nerve segments. The presence of conduction block with temporal dispersion reflects impaired saltatory conduction, a hallmark of demyelinating neuropathies like acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP), the most common variant of GBS
column8: Decremental response to repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz is incorrect because this is a characteristic finding of myasthenia gravis, a disorder of neuromuscular transmission that causes fatigable weakness rather than the progressive, symmetric, ascending weakness with areflexia seen in GBS
column9: Reduced somatosensory evoked potential amplitudes with delayed central conduction time is incorrect because these findings suggest central nervous system demyelination, as seen in multiple sclerosis or subacute combined degeneration, whereas GBS is a peripheral nervous system disorder
column10: Increased insertional activity with fibrillation potentials on electromyography is incorrect because these findings indicate active denervation, which is characteristic of motor neuron diseases like amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) rather than the demyelinating polyneuropathy seen in GBS

column2: A 72-year-old patient with Parkinson’s disease presents with significant gait instability and frequent freezing episodes, particularly in narrow spaces and while turning. Which of the following assistive devices would be the most beneficial in improving mobility and reducing fall risk?
column7: U-Step walker is correct because it is specifically designed for individuals with Parkinson’s disease, providing a stable base, resistance to prevent festination, and laser cueing to help overcome freezing episodes.
column8: Standard walker is incorrect because it lacks the stability and cueing features necessary to assist with freezing of gait and may contribute to forward festination due to its need for lifting during ambulation
column9: Four-wheeled rollator is incorrect because although it provides continuous support, it lacks the weighted resistance and cueing mechanisms of the U-Step walker, increasing the risk of festination and falls in Parkinson’s patients
column10: Hemi-walker is incorrect because it is designed for unilateral weakness, such as post-stroke hemiparesis, and does not provide adequate support for Parkinson’s-related gait instability or freezing episodes
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 38-year-old male with a C6 spinal cord injury presents with worsening renal function, recurrent urinary tract infections, and new onset of fever and flank pain. Urodynamic studies reveal detrusor overactivity with high voiding pressures and detrusor sphincter dyssynergia (DSD). Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of his renal deterioration?
column7: Vesicoureteral reflux is correct because detrusor sphincter dyssynergia (DSD) leads to high intravesical pressures, which can force urine retrogradely into the ureters and kidneys, resulting in acute or chronic pyelonephritis and progressive renal failure
column8: Atonic bladder is incorrect because it results in low-pressure urinary retention and overflow incontinence, not the high-pressure voiding that leads to vesicoureteral reflux and renal deterioration
column9: Urethral stricture is incorrect because while it can cause urinary retention and increased bladder pressures, it is not the primary mechanism behind reflux-associated renal failure in spinal cord injury patients
column10: Bladder calculi is incorrect because although bladder stones are common in neurogenic bladder, they primarily cause local irritation and infection rather than progressive renal failure due to reflux

column2: A 66-year-old man with a history of hypertension and diabetes presents with sudden-onset dysarthria and clumsiness of his right hand. Neurological examination reveals impaired fine motor control in the right hand but no significant weakness or sensory deficits. MRI reveals a lacunar infarct in the basis pontis. Which of the following is the most likely clinical syndrome?
column7: Dysarthria-clumsy hand syndrome is correct because a lacunar infarct in the basis pontis or the genu of the internal capsule affects corticobulbar and corticospinal fibers, leading to dysarthria and fine motor impairment of the hand without significant weakness
column8: Pure sensory stroke is incorrect because it results from an infarct in the thalamus (VPL nucleus) and causes isolated sensory deficits rather than motor dysfunction or dysarthria
column9: Pure motor stroke is incorrect because it involves the posterior limb of the internal capsule, causing significant hemiparesis rather than isolated clumsiness and dysarthria
column10: Ataxic hemiparesis is incorrect because it results from infarcts in the pons or corona radiata, leading to weakness with incoordination rather than dysarthria and isolated fine motor impairment
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 50-year-old man presents with progressive weakness that began in his lower extremities and has now involved his arms over the past several days. He reports a recent upper respiratory illness two weeks ago. On examination, he has symmetric weakness, absent deep tendon reflexes, and mild paresthesias in his hands and feet. His autonomic function is impaired, as evidenced by tachycardia and orthostatic hypotension. CSF analysis shows a markedly elevated protein concentration with a normal white blood cell count. Nerve conduction studies demonstrate prolonged F-wave latencies and reduced conduction velocities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP) is correct because it is the most common variant of Guillain-Barré syndrome, presenting with rapidly progressive, ascending weakness, areflexia, and autonomic dysfunction following an infection. The hallmark CSF finding of albuminocytologic dissociation (high protein with normal WBC count) and nerve conduction studies showing demyelination confirm the diagnosis
column8: Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) is incorrect because it is a chronic disorder with a progressive or relapsing course lasting more than 8 weeks, rather than an acute onset with rapid progression
column9: Poliomyelitis is incorrect because it presents with asymmetric weakness, fever, and meningeal signs due to poliovirus-induced destruction of anterior horn motor neurons, rather than a symmetric ascending paralysis with autonomic dysfunction
column10: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is incorrect because it causes proximal muscle weakness that improves with use, decreased reflexes that may return with repetitive stimulation, and is often associated with small cell lung cancer, rather than an acute, rapidly progressive ascending weakness
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 7-year-old boy with Duchenne muscular dystrophy presents for evaluation of gait abnormalities. Which of the following gait characteristics is most commonly observed in this condition?
column7: Waddling gait is correct because Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) causes progressive proximal muscle weakness, particularly in the hip girdle, leading to difficulty stabilizing the pelvis during walking. This results in a characteristic waddling gait, where the child exhibits excessive lateral trunk sway and hip drop to compensate for weak gluteal muscles
column8: Steppage gait is incorrect because it is associated with foot drop due to distal motor neuropathy, such as in peroneal nerve injury or Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease, rather than the proximal muscle weakness seen in DMD
column9: Trendelenburg gait is incorrect because while children with DMD may exhibit a positive Trendelenburg sign due to gluteus medius weakness, Trendelenburg gait alone does not fully describe the exaggerated lateral trunk movements characteristic of the waddling gait in DMD
column10: Ataxic gait is incorrect because it is typically seen in cerebellar dysfunction, presenting with unsteady, wide-based, and irregular stepping patterns, which are not characteristic of DMD
column12: A waddling gait, also known as a myopathic gait, is marked by exaggerated side-to-side trunk movement and increased lumbar lordosis due to proximal muscle weakness, particularly in the hip girdle. This gait pattern gives a distinctive “penguin-like” appearance, as the person leans backward to compensate for weak hip extensors while shifting their trunk excessively from side to side. The pelvis drops on the side opposite to the weight-bearing leg, a characteristic feature of bilateral Trendelenburg gait, which occurs due to gluteus medius weakness. Steps tend to be slow and wide-based as the individual struggles with stability, and movement initiation, such as rising from the floor or climbing stairs, becomes progressively difficult. Children with Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) often demonstrate Gowers’ sign, where they push off their thighs with their hands to stand up, highlighting their significant proximal muscle weakness. This gait abnormality worsens over time as muscle degeneration progresses in conditions such as DMD, limb-girdle muscular dystrophy, and other congenital myopathies

column2: A 35-year-old man sustains an uncomplicated T6 spinal cord injury (SCI) following a motor vehicle accident. He is started on thromboprophylaxis while hospitalized. According to current guidelines, how long should he receive deep vein thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis following his injury?
column7: 8 weeks is correct because patients with an uncomplicated spinal cord injury (SCI) are at high risk for venous thromboembolism (VTE) due to prolonged immobility and loss of muscle pump function. Current guidelines recommend at least 8 weeks of DVT prophylaxis with low-molecular-weight heparin (LMWH) or unfractionated heparin (UFH) in uncomplicated SCI cases to significantly reduce the risk of deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE)
column8: 12 weeks is incorrect because extended prophylaxis beyond 8 weeks is generally reserved for complicated SCI cases, such as those with additional risk factors (e.g., previous VTE, malignancy, or prolonged bed rest)
column9: 6 weeks is incorrect because while 6 weeks may be appropriate for some other immobilized patients, SCI patients have a prolonged hypercoagulable state, warranting at least 8 weeks of prophylaxis
column10: 4 weeks is incorrect because the risk of DVT remains elevated well beyond this timeframe, and shorter prophylaxis increases the likelihood of VTE recurrence or progression
column12: Patients with spinal cord injury (SCI) require 12 weeks of DVT prophylaxis if they have additional high-risk factors, including heart failure, prior VTE, active malignancy, severe lower extremity fractures or trauma, or prolonged immobility beyond the acute phase, as these conditions further increase the risk of venous thromboembolism

column2: A 35-year-old man with a history of heart failure sustains an uncomplicated T6 spinal cord injury (SCI) following a motor vehicle accident. He is started on thromboprophylaxis while hospitalized. According to current guidelines, how long should he receive deep vein thrombosis (DVT) prophylaxis following his injury?
column7: 12 weeks is correct because patients with spinal cord injury (SCI) and an additional high-risk condition, such as heart failure, have a prolonged hypercoagulable state and an increased risk of VTE recurrence. Current guidelines recommend extending prophylaxis to 12 weeks in SCI patients with complicating factors to further reduce the likelihood of thromboembolic events
column8: 8 weeks is incorrect because while 8 weeks is appropriate for uncomplicated SCI cases, additional risk factors like heart failure necessitate longer prophylaxis
column9: 6 weeks is incorrect because studies show that VTE risk persists beyond this duration, particularly in patients with additional cardiovascular comorbidities
column10: 4 weeks is incorrect because SCI patients remain at high risk for DVT/PE well beyond one month, and shorter prophylaxis is inadequate for preventing late VTE events
column12: Patients with spinal cord injury (SCI) require 12 weeks of DVT prophylaxis if they have additional high-risk factors, including heart failure, prior VTE, active malignancy, severe lower extremity fractures or trauma, or prolonged immobility beyond the acute phase, as these conditions further increase the risk of venous thromboembolism

column2: Which of the following types of amputations is associated with the highest energy expenditure during ambulation?
column7: Bilateral transfemoral amputation (vascular etiology) is correct because it results in the highest energy expenditure due to the loss of both knee joints, which are essential for efficient ambulation. Additionally, vascular amputees often have poorer cardiovascular reserve, diminished muscle function, and multiple comorbidities, all of which further increase the metabolic demands of walking
column8: Unilateral transfemoral amputation (traumatic etiology) is incorrect because while transfemoral amputations significantly increase energy expenditure, traumatic amputees generally have better residual limb function, stronger musculature, and greater cardiovascular capacity, leading to a more efficient gait than vascular amputees
column9: Bilateral transfemoral amputation (traumatic etiology) is incorrect because although bilateral transfemoral amputations require a high level of energy for ambulation, traumatic amputees typically maintain better overall strength and conditioning, which reduces the energy expenditure compared to vascular etiologies
column10: Bilateral transtibial amputation (vascular etiology) is incorrect because even though vascular amputees have higher energy demands than traumatic amputees, transtibial amputations preserve the knee joints, which allows for a more efficient gait pattern compared to transfemoral amputations

column2: A patient with a full-thickness burn to the lower extremity undergoes split-thickness skin grafting to a region that does not cross a joint and receives an immediate postoperative edema control dressing. When is the earliest safe time to initiate ambulation?
column7: Postoperative day 1 is correct because early ambulation with an appropriate edema control dressing promotes circulation, reduces venous stasis, and helps maintain graft adherence without compromising healing. Since the graft does not cross a joint, there is minimal risk of shearing forces disrupting the graft, making early mobility both safe and beneficial
column8: Postoperative day 5 is incorrect because delaying ambulation beyond the first day can lead to unnecessary stiffness, increased swelling, and a higher risk of functional decline
column9: Postoperative day 10 is incorrect because waiting this long contradicts modern rehabilitation protocols, which prioritize early controlled mobility to enhance recovery
column10: Postoperative day 14 is incorrect because immobilization for two weeks is excessive and can result in complications such as joint stiffness, muscle atrophy, and delayed functional independence
column12: Lagziel et al. (2021) found that early ambulation after skin grafting in burn patients did not increase graft loss compared to delayed mobilization, supporting the safety and benefits of initiating movement as early as postoperative day one

column2: A 40-year-old patient sustains deep partial-thickness burns involving the entire anterior torso, the entire right arm, and the anterior surface of both legs. What is the estimated total body surface area (TBSA) affected?
column7: 45% is correct because the anterior torso (18%), entire right arm (9%), and the anterior and posterior surfaces of both legs (18%) together total this percentage of TBSA involvement
column8: 27% is incorrect because it miscalculates the contribution of the torso and both legs, leading to a lower TBSA estimate
column9: 36% is incorrect because it underestimates the burn extent by failing to account for the posterior leg surfaces
column10: 18% is incorrect because it only accounts for a small portion of the affected areas and does not include the full anterior torso, right arm, and both legs
column12: The Rule of Nines is a method used to estimate total body surface area (TBSA) affected by burns by assigning standardized percentage values: 9% for the head, 9% per arm (4.5% anterior, 4.5% posterior), 18% per leg (9% anterior, 9% posterior), 18% for the anterior torso, 18% for the posterior torso, and 1% for the perineum

column2: Which of the following is the most appropriate treatment for a patient with myofascial pain syndrome and trigger points?
column7: Dry needling is correct because dry needling involves inserting a needle into the trigger point to release tension and relieve pain without the use of steroids. This technique is commonly used in the treatment of myofascial pain syndrome, where trigger points are the primary source of pain. Dry needling targets the trigger point directly to induce a local twitch response, leading to muscle relaxation
column8: Local anesthetic injection with corticosteroids is incorrect because while local anesthetics can be used for trigger point injections, corticosteroids are not necessary and may not be effective in treating myofascial pain syndrome. Steroids are more appropriate for inflammatory conditions, not for muscle-related trigger points
column9: Oral corticosteroids is incorrect because oral corticosteroids are typically used for inflammatory conditions and are not indicated for the management of myofascial pain syndrome, which is caused by muscle tension rather than inflammation
column10: Intramuscular botulinum toxin injections is incorrect because while botulinum toxin can be helpful in treating certain conditions like spasticity, it is generally not the first-line treatment for myofascial pain syndrome or trigger points, where dry needling or manual therapy is preferred and botulinum toxin is only sometimes used in refractory cases
column12: Myofascial pain syndrome is defined by the presence of hyperirritable trigger points within muscles that cause localized or referred pain, and proven treatments include dry needling, manual therapy, massage, physical therapy (stretching and strengthening exercises), nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), and, in some cases, botulinum toxin injections

column2: What is the mechanism by which chronic NSAID use leads to gastrointestinal bleeding?
column3: Inhibition of cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1), which reduces protective prostaglandin production
column4: Increased gastric acid secretion due to COX-2 inhibition
column5: Disruption of vitamin K absorption, impairing coagulation
column6: Overstimulation of mucus production in the stomach lining
column7: Inhibition of cyclooxygenase-1 (COX-1), which reduces protective prostaglandin production is correct because NSAIDs block COX-1, decreasing the production of prostaglandins that normally protect the stomach lining and help maintain blood flow, which increases the risk of ulcers and bleeding
column8: Increased gastric acid secretion due to COX-2 inhibition is incorrect because COX-2 inhibitors have a minimal effect on gastric acid secretion, and it is COX-1 inhibition that primarily causes this effect
column9: Disruption of vitamin K absorption, impairing coagulation is incorrect because NSAIDs do not affect vitamin K absorption, and their bleeding effects are due to gastric mucosal damage rather than changes in coagulation pathways
column10: Overstimulation of mucus production in the stomach lining is incorrect because NSAIDs reduce the production of gastric mucus and bicarbonate, which normally protect the stomach lining, contributing to increased risk of bleeding rather than overstimulation

column2: Which of the following NSAIDs is associated with the least gastrointestinal side effects?
column7: Celecoxib is correct because Celecoxib is a selective COX-2 inhibitor, which primarily targets the inflammatory pathway and spares COX-1, thus reducing the risk of gastrointestinal side effects such as bleeding and ulcers commonly associated with non-selective NSAIDs
column8: Ibuprofen is incorrect because while ibuprofen is considered a milder NSAID compared to others, it is non-selective, meaning it inhibits both COX-1 and COX-2, leading to a higher risk of gastrointestinal side effects
column9: Naproxen is incorrect because although naproxen has a longer half-life and is often used for chronic pain, it is also non-selective, which increases the likelihood of gastrointestinal issues similar to ibuprofen
column10: Indomethacin is incorrect because indomethacin is a potent non-selective NSAID, which is known to cause more severe gastrointestinal side effects, including ulcers and bleeding, compared to other NSAIDs

column2: Which of the following NSAIDs is least likely to increase cardiovascular risk?
column7: Naproxen is correct because naproxen is considered to have a lower cardiovascular risk profile compared to other NSAIDs. It is a non-selective COX inhibitor, but studies have suggested that it may have a neutral effect on cardiovascular events, particularly at lower doses
column8: Celecoxib is incorrect because although celecoxib is a COX-2 selective inhibitor, it is associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular events, particularly at higher doses or with long-term use, due to its effect on the COX-2 pathway involved in cardiovascular homeostasis
column9: Ibuprofen is incorrect because while ibuprofen is widely used, it is also a non-selective COX inhibitor, and studies have shown that high doses of ibuprofen are associated with an increased risk of cardiovascular events, similar to other non-selective NSAIDs
column10: Indomethacin is incorrect because indomethacin is a potent non-selective COX inhibitor and is known to have a higher cardiovascular risk compared to other NSAIDs, especially with long-term use, due to its strong inhibition of both COX-1 and COX-2

column2: Which of the following medications is FDA-approved for the treatment of diabetic neuropathy?
column7: Pregabalin is correct because pregabalin (Lyrica) is FDA-approved for the treatment of diabetic peripheral neuropathy and is one of the first-line medications due to its effectiveness in reducing nerve pain associated with diabetes
column8: Gabapentin is incorrect because although gabapentin is commonly used off-label for diabetic neuropathy, it is not FDA-approved specifically for this indication, unlike pregabalin
column9: NSAIDs (Nonsteroidal Anti-inflammatory Drugs) are not the answer because they are primarily used for treating pain related to inflammation, such as in conditions like arthritis, but they are not effective for neuropathic pain
column10: Topiramate is incorrect because topiramate is not FDA-approved for diabetic neuropathy; it is used primarily for seizures and migraine prevention
column12: To summarize, the FDA-approved medications for diabetic neuropathy include pregabalin, duloxetine, capsaicin 8% patch (Qutenza), and amitriptyline, although the latter is less commonly used due to its side effects.

column2: Which of the following local anesthetics has the longest duration of action?
column7: Bupivacaine is correct because it is a long-acting local anesthetic, with a duration of action ranging from 4 to 12 hours, depending on the specific formulation and dose. This makes it ideal for procedures that require prolonged analgesia, such as epidurals or nerve blocks
column8: Lidocaine is incorrect because it is an intermediate-duration local anesthetic with a typical duration of action of 1-2 hours. It is often used for shorter procedures
column9: Chloroprocaine is incorrect because it has the shortest duration of action among common local anesthetics, lasting only about 30 to 60 minutes. It is typically used for short outpatient procedures
column10: Mepivacaine is incorrect because it is also an intermediate-duration anesthetic, with a typical duration of 2-3 hours, making it more suitable for brief procedures compared to bupivacaine
column12: The longest-acting "caine" local anesthetic is bupivacaine. It has a duration of action ranging from 4 to 12 hours, depending on the dose and formulation, which makes it suitable for longer procedures or continuous infusion in nerve blocks and epidural anesthesia. The shortest-acting "caine" local anesthetic is chloroprocaine. It has a very brief duration of action, typically lasting about 30 to 60 minutes, which makes it ideal for short, outpatient procedures

column2: What type of orthotic is most commonly used in patients with an acute ACL tear to provide stability and prevent further injury during the healing phase?
column7: 'Hinged knee brace' is correct because it provides both medial-lateral and anterior-posterior stability, preventing excessive motion in the knee joint, which is critical in the acute phase following an ACL injury.
column8: 'Soft knee sleeve' is incorrect because it does not provide adequate stability or support to prevent abnormal knee movement in the acute phase of an ACL injury.
column9: 'Patella stabilizing brace' is incorrect because it is designed to manage patellar tracking issues and does not address the specific stability needs of the knee joint after an ACL tear.
column10: 'Unloader knee brace' is incorrect because it is primarily used to offload pressure from the joint in patients with arthritis and is not suitable for providing stability in an acute ACL injury.

column2: Determine the neurologic level of injury in a patient with a spinal cord injury based on the International Standards for Neurological Classification of Spinal Cord Injury (ISNCSCI) examination. The patient has intact sensation and 3/5 motor strength at C6, with absent sensation and 0/5 motor strength at C7. What is the neurologic level of injury?
column7: C6 is correct because the neurologic level of injury is defined as the lowest segment with intact sensory and motor function (≥3/5 strength) provided that the segment above is intact (5/5 strength).
column8: C5 is incorrect because, while it has intact motor function, it does not represent the lowest level with intact sensory and motor function.
column9: C7 is incorrect because motor function at C7 is absent, meaning it cannot be the lowest intact segment.
column10: T1 is incorrect because it is below the level where motor and sensory function is lost, making it inapplicable as the neurologic level of injury.

column2: What is the primary statistical limitation of using erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) as a diagnostic test for inflammatory conditions?
column7: High sensitivity but low specificity is correct because ESR is highly sensitive for detecting inflammation but lacks specificity, as it can be elevated in numerous conditions such as infections, malignancies, and autoimmune diseases.
column8: Low sensitivity and low specificity is incorrect because ESR has high sensitivity, meaning it is often elevated in inflammatory conditions, though it lacks specificity.
column9: High specificity but low sensitivity is incorrect because ESR has low specificity, leading to false positives in various conditions, while its high sensitivity makes it useful for detecting inflammation.
column10: Equal sensitivity and specificity is incorrect because ESR has a high sensitivity but a low specificity, making it more effective for ruling out inflammation rather than confirming a specific diagnosis.

column2: What is the primary mechanism of action of baclofen in reducing spasticity?
column5: Interference with ryanodine receptor-mediated calcium release
column7: Baclofen is correct because it is a GABA-B receptor agonist that acts at the spinal level to inhibit excitatory neurotransmitter release, leading to decreased muscle spasticity.
column8: GABA-A receptor agonism is incorrect because this is the mechanism of action of diazepam, which enhances inhibitory neurotransmission through a different GABA receptor subtype.
column9: Interference with ryanodine receptor-mediated calcium release is incorrect because this is the mechanism of action of dantrolene, which reduces muscle contraction by inhibiting calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum.
column10: Alpha-2 adrenergic receptor agonism is incorrect because this is the mechanism of action of tizanidine, which reduces spasticity by enhancing presynaptic inhibition in the spinal cord.

column2: What is the primary mechanism of action of dantrolene?
column3: Inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptor blockade
column4: Activates GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord
column5: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity in the central nervous system
column6: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist with central inhibition of motor neurons
column7: Inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptor blockade is correct because dantrolene directly interferes with ryanodine receptors in skeletal muscle, reducing calcium release and muscle contraction, making it effective for conditions like spasticity and malignant hyperthermia.
column8: Activates GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord is incorrect because this describes baclofen, which works via presynaptic and postsynaptic inhibition in the spinal cord.
column9: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity in the central nervous system is incorrect because this describes diazepam, a benzodiazepine that potentiates GABA-mediated inhibition at GABA-A receptors.
column10: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist with central inhibition of motor neurons is incorrect because this describes tizanidine, which reduces spasticity by increasing presynaptic inhibition of motor neurons.

column3: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity in the central nervous system
column4: Activates GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord
column5: Inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptor blockade
column6: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist with central inhibition of motor neurons
column7: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity in the central nervous system is correct because diazepam potentiates GABA-mediated inhibition at GABA-A receptors, leading to increased inhibitory neurotransmission, which reduces muscle hyperactivity and spasticity.
column8: Activates GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord is incorrect because this describes baclofen, which reduces excitatory neurotransmitter release and enhances postsynaptic inhibition in the spinal cord.
column9: Inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptor blockade is incorrect because this describes dantrolene, which directly reduces muscle contraction by interfering with calcium release in skeletal muscle.
column10: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist with central inhibition of motor neurons is incorrect because this describes tizanidine, which reduces spasticity through increased presynaptic inhibition of motor neurons in the spinal cord.

column2: What is the mechanism of action of tizanidine in the treatment of spasticity?
column3: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist with central inhibition of motor neurons
column4: Activates GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord
column5: Inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptor blockade
column6: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity in the central nervous system
column7: Alpha-2 adrenergic agonist with central inhibition of motor neurons is correct because tizanidine reduces spasticity by stimulating alpha-2 adrenergic receptors in the central nervous system, leading to decreased excitatory neurotransmitter release and increased presynaptic inhibition of motor neurons.
column8: Activates GABA-B receptors in the spinal cord is incorrect because this describes baclofen, which acts on GABA-B receptors to decrease excitatory neurotransmitter release and increase postsynaptic inhibition.
column9: Inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via ryanodine receptor blockade is incorrect because this describes dantrolene, which directly reduces muscle contraction by preventing calcium release in skeletal muscle.
column10: Enhances GABA-A receptor activity in the central nervous system is incorrect because this describes diazepam, which potentiates GABA-A receptor activity to increase inhibitory neurotransmission and reduce muscle spasticity.

column2: Which antispasmodic acts peripherally rather than centrally?
column7: Dantrolene is correct because it directly inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum in skeletal muscle, reducing muscle contraction without affecting central neurotransmission.
column8: Baclofen is incorrect because it acts centrally as a GABA-B receptor agonist, reducing excitatory neurotransmission in the spinal cord.
column9: Tizanidine is incorrect because it acts centrally as an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist, inhibiting motor neuron activity to reduce spasticity.
column10: Diazepam is incorrect because it enhances GABA-A receptor activity in the central nervous system, leading to increased inhibitory neurotransmission and muscle relaxation.

column2: A 32-year-old male with a severe traumatic brain injury is in a rehabilitation facility and has significant impairments in arousal and attention. His treating physician decides to initiate the first-line pharmacologic treatment for arousal enhancement. What is the primary mechanism of action of this medication?
column7: Dopaminergic modulation is correct because amantadine enhances arousal in patients with traumatic brain injury primarily through its dopaminergic modulation, increasing dopamine release and reducing its reuptake, which promotes wakefulness and cognitive function. Amantadine is typically viewed as first line in promoting arousal in patients with severe traumatic brain injury.
column8: Inhibition of NMDA receptors is incorrect because while amantadine does have NMDA receptor antagonistic properties, its primary mechanism for arousal enhancement is its effect on dopamine.
column9: Enhancement of GABAergic activity is incorrect because amantadine does not enhance GABAergic activity; instead, it has excitatory effects that increase wakefulness.
column10: Serotonin receptor blockade is incorrect because amantadine does not function through serotonin receptor blockade; instead, it works primarily by modulating dopamine.

column2: A 45-year-old male with a severe traumatic brain injury exhibits frequent episodes of agitation and aggression during his inpatient rehabilitation stay. His physician decides to start the first-line pharmacologic treatment for agitation in traumatic brain injury. Which of the following medications is the most appropriate choice?
column7: Propranolol is correct because it is the first-line pharmacologic treatment for agitation in traumatic brain injury, effectively reducing sympathetic overactivity through beta-adrenergic blockade without sedative or cognitive-impairing effects.
column8: Lorazepam is incorrect because benzodiazepines can worsen confusion, increase fall risk, and contribute to paradoxical agitation in traumatic brain injury.
column9: Haloperidol is incorrect because typical antipsychotics are associated with increased risks of sedation, extrapyramidal side effects, and prolonged recovery in patients with traumatic brain injury.
column10: Sertraline is incorrect because SSRIs are primarily used for mood disorders rather than acute agitation management in traumatic brain injury.

column2: A 45-year-old woman with complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) type I in her right foot has failed conservative treatment. She undergoes a spinal cord stimulator trial and reports 70% pain relief. Which of the following mechanisms best explains the effectiveness of spinal cord stimulation?
column3: Activation of descending inhibitory pathways from the brainstem
column4: Direct stimulation of nociceptive C fibers
column5: Inhibition of A-beta fibers in the dorsal horn
column6: Suppression of excitatory neurotransmitters like dopamine and norepinephrine
column7: Spinal cord stimulation (SCS) modulates pain by activating descending inhibitory pathways from the brainstem, which help regulate nociceptive processing and reduce pain perception. This aligns with the patient's 70% pain relief, as SCS influences both supraspinal mechanisms and local spinal circuitry to provide analgesia.
column8: Direct stimulation of nociceptive C fibers is incorrect because SCS inhibits C fiber activity rather than stimulating it, as C fibers are responsible for transmitting chronic pain.
column9: Inhibition of A-beta fibers in the dorsal horn is incorrect because SCS stimulates A-beta fibers, which suppress nociceptive input via the gate control mechanism.
column10: Suppression of excitatory neurotransmitters like dopamine and norepinephrine is incorrect because SCS primarily modulates GABAergic and serotonergic pathways rather than directly affecting dopamine or norepinephrine.
column12: he Gate Control Theory of Pain, proposed by Melzack and Wall in 1965, serves as the foundation for understanding spinal cord stimulation (SCS). Their landmark study suggested that pain perception is modulated at the spinal level, where large A-beta fibers inhibit nociceptive C fiber input in the dorsal horn, effectively "closing the pain gate" before signals reach higher brain centers. SCS works by stimulating A-beta fibers, reducing pain transmission via this mechanism, while also engaging descending inhibitory pathways from the brainstem, such as the periaqueductal gray and rostroventral medulla, which further enhance endogenous pain suppression. The clinical success of SCS in treating neuropathic pain, CRPS, and failed back surgery syndrome (FBSS) validates the Gate Control Theory, demonstrating how neuromodulation can effectively alter pain perception at both spinal and supraspinal levels.

column2: A woman with a complete C6 AISA A spinal cord injury is considering pregnancy and seeks counseling about her reproductive health. Which of the following statements is true regarding her condition?
column4: Her fertility is reduced due to her spinal cord injury
column5: She can safely proceed with labor and delivery without epidural anesthesia
column6: Vaginal lubrication will be completely lost
column7: Breastfeeding is impaired is correct because the neurohormonal reflex for lactation depends on nipple stimulation, which is mediated by T4 and above; a complete C6 injury disrupts this pathway, impairing the milk ejection reflex.
column8: Her fertility is reduced due to her spinal cord injury is incorrect because spinal cord injury does not typically impair ovulation or fertility in women, and most can conceive naturally.
column9: She can proceed with labor and delivery without epidural anesthesia is incorrect because autonomic dysreflexia, a life-threatening condition, can occur during labor in patients with injuries at or above T6, necessitating epidural anesthesia to prevent complications.
column10: Vaginal lubrication will be completely lost is incorrect because vaginal lubrication is mediated by parasympathetic fibers from the sacral spinal cord (S2-S4) via the pelvic splanchnic nerves, which stimulate blood flow and glandular secretion. The pudendal nerve (S2-S4) provides sensory feedback and somatic motor control to the external genitalia, allowing for reflexive responses even if supraspinal control is impaired.

column2: A woman with a complete C6 AISA A spinal cord injury is pregnant and asks whether she will be able to have a vaginal delivery. Which of the following best explains how labor progresses in her condition?
column3: Uterine contractions remain intact due to autonomic pathways below the injury
column4: Uterine contractions will be absent due to spinal cord injury
column5: She will be unable to labor due to loss of voluntary muscle control
column6: Her pregnancy will require a C-section due to autonomic dysfunction
column7: Uterine contractions remain intact due to autonomic pathways below the injury is correct because uterine contractions are controlled by autonomic pathways originating at T10-L2 (sympathetic via the hypogastric nerve) and S2-S4 (parasympathetic via the pelvic splanchnic nerves), which are unaffected by a cervical spinal cord injury.
column8: Uterine contractions will be absent due to spinal cord injury is incorrect because the uterus contracts independently of voluntary control, and the autonomic pathways responsible for labor remain intact.
column9: She will be unable to labor due to loss of voluntary muscle control is incorrect because although voluntary pushing is impaired, uterine contractions can still progress labor, and assisted delivery can be used if needed.
column10: Her pregnancy will require a C-section due to autonomic dysfunction is incorrect because while autonomic dysreflexia is a concern, it can be managed with epidural anesthesia, and vaginal delivery is still possible.

column2: A 32-year-old woman with a T6 spinal cord injury (SCI) presents with concerns about her sexual function. Which of the following sexual functions is most likely to be impaired due to her level of injury?
column3: Impaired psychogenic arousal and orgasm with preserved reflexive lubrication
column4: Complete loss of all genital sensation and reflexive lubrication
column5: Preserved psychogenic arousal with impaired reflexive lubrication and orgasm
column6: No significant changes in sexual function
column7: Impaired psychogenic arousal and orgasm with preserved reflexive lubrication is correct because a T6 SCI disrupts descending autonomic pathways necessary for psychogenic arousal and orgasm, but reflexive responses controlled by the sacral spinal cord (S2-S4) may remain intact.
column8: Complete loss of all genital sensation and reflexive lubrication is incorrect because sacral reflex arcs may allow for some preserved sensation and lubrication.
column9: Preserved psychogenic arousal with impaired reflexive lubrication and orgasm is incorrect because psychogenic arousal requires intact suprasacral pathways, which are disrupted at T6.
column10: No significant changes in sexual function is incorrect because SCI impacts multiple aspects of sexual function, including lubrication, orgasm, and sensation.

column2: Which of the following statements about male sexual function and fertility after spinal cord injury is most accurate?
column3: Erectile dysfunction is the most common sexual dysfunction, and men with SCI often experience impaired sperm motility and viability
column4: Premature ejaculation is common in men with SCI, and fertility is typically unaffected
column5: Men with SCI generally retain normal erectile function, but fertility is impaired due to retrograde ejaculation
column6: Men with SCI rarely experience issues with ejaculation, but sperm quality is significantly improved after injury
column7: Erectile dysfunction is the most common sexual dysfunction, and men with SCI often experience impaired sperm motility and viability is correct because spinal cord injury frequently disrupts erectile function and impairs sperm motility and viability due to autonomic dysfunction and prolonged seminal stasis, contributing to reduced fertility.
column8: Premature ejaculation is common in men with SCI, and fertility is typically unaffected is incorrect because premature ejaculation is not a common dysfunction after SCI, and fertility is frequently impaired due to poor sperm motility and ejaculation dysfunction.
column9: Men with SCI generally retain normal erectile function, but fertility is impaired due to retrograde ejaculation is incorrect because most men with SCI experience erectile dysfunction, and while retrograde ejaculation can occur, impaired sperm motility and poor semen quality are more significant contributors to infertility.
column10: Men with SCI rarely experience issues with ejaculation, but sperm quality is significantly improved after injury is incorrect because ejaculation dysfunction is common, and sperm quality is typically reduced rather than improved after SCI.

column2: Which assessment tool can be used to assess the sensory, affective, and evaluative aspects of pain?
column7: McGill Pain Questionnaire can be used to assess the sensory, affective, and evaluative aspects of pain, making it a valuable tool for evaluating pain-related experiences in patients with myofascial pain.
column8: Numeric rating scale is incorrect because it only provides a simple numerical rating of pain intensity without assessing the qualitative aspects of pain.
column9: Sickness Impact Profile is incorrect because it is a broad measure of health-related quality of life rather than a specific assessment of pain characteristics.
column10: Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory is incorrect because it is primarily a psychological assessment tool used to evaluate personality traits and psychopathology rather than pain evaluation.

column2: Which assessment tool is commonly used to quantify subjective pain intensity on a continuous scale?
column7: The Visual Analog Scale (VAS) score is a simple and widely used tool for assessing pain intensity by having patients mark their pain level on a continuous line, making it effective for quantifying subjective pain experiences.
column8: McGill Pain Questionnaire is incorrect because it assesses multiple dimensions of pain, including sensory, affective, and evaluative aspects, rather than providing a quick numerical rating.
column9: Oswestry Disability Index is incorrect because it evaluates disability related to low back pain rather than directly measuring pain intensity.
column10: Beck Depression Inventory is incorrect because it is a psychological assessment tool used to measure depressive symptoms rather than pain severity.

column2: Which questionnaire is specifically designed to assess functional limitations due to chronic low back pain?
column7: The Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) is a validated tool designed to measure the impact of chronic low back pain on a patient’s ability to perform daily activities and assess functional limitations.
column8: VAS score is incorrect because it only quantifies pain intensity without evaluating disability.
column9: McGill Pain Questionnaire is incorrect because it describes pain characteristics rather than functional impairment.
column10: WOMAC is incorrect because it is specific to osteoarthritis-related pain and function rather than general back pain disability.

column2: Which modality provides the deepest tissue heating using electromagnetic energy?
column7: Shortwave diathermy is correct because it uses electromagnetic waves at a frequency of 27.12 MHz to produce deep tissue heating while penetrating up to 5 cm, making it effective for treating musculoskeletal conditions.
column8: Microwave diathermy is incorrect because it operates at a higher frequency (915 MHz or 2450 MHz), resulting in more superficial heating with a penetration depth of 1-3 cm.
column9: Therapeutic ultrasound is incorrect because it uses mechanical sound waves rather than electromagnetic energy and provides focused deep heating, particularly for tendons and ligaments.
column10: Hot packs are incorrect because they provide only superficial heat through conduction and do not effectively reach deep tissues.

column2: Which modality uses electrical current to deliver ionized medication through the skin for localized treatment?
column7: Iontophoresis is correct because it uses a low-voltage electrical current to drive ionized medication through the skin, allowing for targeted drug delivery without the need for systemic administration.
column8: Phonophoresis is incorrect because it uses ultrasound to enhance transdermal drug absorption rather than electrical current.
column9: Therapeutic ultrasound is incorrect because it applies mechanical sound waves to produce deep tissue heating and does not facilitate medication delivery.
column10: TENS is incorrect because it uses electrical stimulation for pain modulation rather than medication administration.

column2: A patient with severe traumatic brain injury develops episodic tachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, and increased muscle tone. Infectious workup, including blood cultures, urinalysis, and chest X-ray, is negative. What is the initial step in management?
column7: Reducing external stimuli is correct because the initial step in managing these autonomic episodes involves minimizing triggers such as pain, suctioning, and excessive stimulation, which can provoke sympathetic overactivity.
column8: Beta-blockers are incorrect because they primarily reduce heart rate and blood pressure but do not treat the underlying dysregulated autonomic control.
column9: Benzodiazepines are incorrect because while they can blunt autonomic responses, they are not first-line and may lead to excessive sedation.
column10: Glycopyrrolate is incorrect because it helps reduce secretions but does not address the sympathetic overactivity characteristic of this condition.

column2: A 42-year-old male is found unresponsive with pinpoint pupils and shallow respirations. EMS administers a medication that rapidly reverses his respiratory depression, and he regains consciousness within minutes. Which of the following medications was administered, based on its mechanism of action?
column3: Naloxone, a competitive opioid receptor antagonist
column7: Naloxone, a competitive opioid receptor antagonist, is correct because it binds to opioid receptors with high affinity, displacing opioid agonists and rapidly reversing opioid-induced respiratory depression.
column8: Buprenorphine, a partial opioid agonist, is incorrect because it has both agonist and antagonist properties at opioid receptors and does not acutely reverse opioid overdose.
column9: Clonidine, an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist, is incorrect because it primarily reduces sympathetic outflow and is used for opioid withdrawal, not overdose reversal.
column10: Flumazenil, a benzodiazepine receptor antagonist, is incorrect because it reverses benzodiazepine effects, not opioid-induced respiratory depression.

column2: Which of the following opioid regimens has the highest total daily morphine milligram equivalent (MME)?
column7: Methadone 5 mg TID is correct because methadone has a nonlinear conversion factor, and at this dose, it contributes approximately 180 MME, which is higher than the other options.
column8: Oxycodone 20 mg BID is incorrect because oxycodone converts to 60 MME, which is significantly lower than methadone’s contribution.
column9: Hydrocodone 30 mg BID is incorrect because hydrocodone converts to 60 MME, which is lower than methadone.
column10: Tramadol 100 mg QID is incorrect because tramadol has a much lower MME conversion, contributing only about 40 MME.

column2: A 45-year-old male presents with a severe traumatic brain injury (TBI) following a motor vehicle collision. His Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score is 6, and a CT scan reveals a right temporal contusion with subarachnoid hemorrhage. According to current guidelines, what is the most appropriate approach for seizure prophylaxis in this patient?
column7: Levetiracetam for 7 days is correct because current guidelines recommend prophylactic antiseizure medication, typically phenytoin or levetiracetam, for up to 7 days in patients with severe TBI to reduce the risk of early post-traumatic seizures.
column8: Phenytoin for 6 months is incorrect because extended prophylaxis beyond 7 days does not reduce the risk of late post-traumatic seizures and is not guideline-recommended.
column9: No seizure prophylaxis needed is incorrect because severe TBI with intracranial hemorrhage increases the risk of early post-traumatic seizures, warranting short-term prophylaxis.
column10: Valproic acid for 14 days is incorrect because valproic acid is not a first-line agent for seizure prophylaxis in TBI, and prophylaxis should not exceed 7 days.

column2: In a patient with a history of epilepsy, discontinuation of antiepileptic drug therapy is considered appropriate after being seizure-free for at least how many months?
column7: 24 is correct because guidelines suggest that discontinuation of antiepileptic drugs is safest after at least two years of seizure freedom in patients with well-controlled epilepsy.
column8: 6 is incorrect because this duration is too short to ensure stable seizure remission before stopping therapy.
column9: 12 is incorrect because one year of seizure freedom is generally not considered sufficient for safely discontinuing antiepileptic medication.
column10: 18 is incorrect because while longer than 12 months, it still falls short of the two-year recommendation for seizure-free status before stopping therapy.
