column3: Lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve sensory nerve conduction study
column4: Stimulation of the brachial plexus at Erb’s point
column5: Radial nerve motor conduction at the spiral groove
column6: Axillary nerve conduction study in the quadrilateral space
column7: Lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve sensory nerve conduction study is correct because preserved SNAPs in this nerve suggest a pre-ganglionic lesion (root avulsion), while absent or reduced SNAPs indicate a post-ganglionic plexus injury.
column8: Stimulation of the brachial plexus at Erb’s point is incorrect because this assesses mixed motor responses of the plexus trunks but does not evaluate dorsal root integrity.
column9: Radial nerve motor conduction at the spiral groove is incorrect because this tests a distal peripheral nerve and does not differentiate root from plexus lesions.
column10: Axillary nerve conduction study in the quadrilateral space is incorrect because this tests a terminal branch of the posterior cord and cannot distinguish between pre- and post-ganglionic injury.

column2: A 3-week-old infant presents with left hand weakness and clawing of the fourth and fifth digits following a difficult delivery with arm hyperabduction. Needle EMG shows denervation in the left first dorsal interosseous and abductor digiti minimi muscles. To determine if the lesion is pre-ganglionic (root avulsion) or post-ganglionic (lower trunk plexus injury), which of the following is the most appropriate electrodiagnostic study to perform?
column3: Ulnar nerve sensory nerve conduction study across the wrist
column4: Stimulation of the lower trunk of the brachial plexus in the supraclavicular fossa
column5: Median nerve motor conduction study to the abductor pollicis brevis
column6: Radial nerve motor conduction study to the extensor indicis
column7: Ulnar nerve sensory nerve conduction study across the wrist is correct because preserved ulnar SNAPs suggest a pre-ganglionic lesion (C8/T1 root avulsion), while absent or reduced SNAPs indicate a post-ganglionic lower trunk plexus injury.
column8: Stimulation of the lower trunk of the brachial plexus in the supraclavicular fossa is incorrect because this assesses mixed motor responses and cannot differentiate pre- from post-ganglionic lesions.
column9: Median nerve motor conduction study to the abductor pollicis brevis is incorrect because this assesses motor function in a different terminal nerve and does not test sensory pathways involved in Klumpke’s palsy.
column10: Radial nerve motor conduction study to the extensor indicis is incorrect because radial nerve testing assesses a different nerve distribution and cannot distinguish root from plexus lesions.

column2: A 45-year-old woman presents with left hand weakness, numbness in the fourth and fifth fingers, and visible atrophy of the first dorsal interosseous muscle. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal abnormal ulnar sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) and normal median SNAPs. Which of the following best explains why the median SNAP is spared in neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome?
column3: The median sensory fibers primarily originate from the middle trunk (C6–C7), which is typically unaffected in neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome.
column4: The median nerve is protected anatomically by the scalene muscles and therefore cannot be compressed in the thoracic outlet.
column5: The median sensory fibers bypass the brachial plexus and arise directly from the spinal roots.
column6: The median sensory fibers are primarily C8–T1, but the thoracic outlet syndrome only affects motor fibers.
column7: The median sensory fibers primarily originate from the middle trunk (C6–C7), which is typically unaffected in neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome is correct because nTOS usually involves the lower trunk (C8–T1), sparing median sensory fibers that travel through the middle trunk, resulting in normal median SNAPs.
column8: The median nerve is protected anatomically by the scalene muscles and therefore cannot be compressed in the thoracic outlet is incorrect because the median nerve can be affected in brachial plexopathies but median sensory fibers are spared in nTOS due to lower trunk involvement, not muscle protection.
column9: The median sensory fibers bypass the brachial plexus and arise directly from the spinal roots is incorrect because median sensory fibers pass through the brachial plexus middle trunk.
column10: The median sensory fibers are primarily C8–T1, but the thoracic outlet syndrome only affects motor fibers is incorrect because the median sensory fibers are mainly from C6–C7, and neurogenic TOS affects both motor and sensory fibers in the lower trunk.

column2: A 50-year-old man presents with left hand weakness and numbness in the fourth and fifth digits. On exam, he has atrophy of the left first dorsal interosseous and abductor digiti minimi muscles, and he cannot flex the tip of his left thumb. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal abnormal ulnar SNAPs and normal median SNAPs. Needle EMG shows fibrillation potentials in the left abductor digiti minimi, first dorsal interosseous, and flexor pollicis longus, with normal findings in the cervical paraspinals and deltoid. Which of the following best explains this pattern of EMG findings?
column3: Lower trunk brachial plexopathy affecting C8–T1 motor fibers with sparing of C5–C7 muscles
column5: Radial nerve neuropathy at the spiral groove
column7: Lower trunk brachial plexopathy affecting C8–T1 motor fibers with sparing of C5–C7 muscles is correct because neurogenic thoracic outlet syndrome typically affects the lower trunk, causing denervation in both ulnar-innervated intrinsic hand muscles and median-innervated C8 muscles like the flexor pollicis longus, while sparing proximal muscles like the deltoid and paraspinals.
column8: C8/T1 pre-ganglionic root lesion is incorrect because a root lesion would typically show normal SNAPs and often involves paraspinal denervation.
column9: Radial nerve neuropathy at the spiral groove is incorrect because radial neuropathy would not involve the intrinsic hand muscles or the flexor pollicis longus.
column10: Ulnar neuropathy at the elbow is incorrect because ulnar neuropathy would not explain flexor pollicis longus denervation and would not involve median-innervated C8 muscles.

column2: A 38-year-old man presents with acute onset of severe left shoulder pain lasting several days, followed by weakness in shoulder abduction and external rotation. Needle EMG performed three weeks after symptom onset shows fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in the left supraspinatus, infraspinatus, and serratus anterior muscles, with normal findings in the left deltoid and cervical paraspinals. Which of the following best describes the electrodiagnostic pattern typical of Parsonage-Turner syndrome?
column3: Patchy involvement of multiple peripheral nerves from the upper brachial plexus region, often affecting the suprascapular and long thoracic nerves
column4: Isolated axillary neuropathy due to shoulder dislocation
column5: Diffuse involvement of all muscles in a C5/C6 radiculopathy pattern with paraspinal denervation
column6: Selective involvement of lower trunk plexus muscles affecting only the hand intrinsics
column7: Patchy involvement of multiple peripheral nerves from the upper brachial plexus region, often affecting the suprascapular and long thoracic nerves is correct because Parsonage-Turner syndrome presents with acute shoulder pain followed by selective denervation in muscles innervated by multiple upper plexus nerves while sparing the paraspinals.
column8: Isolated axillary neuropathy due to shoulder dislocation is incorrect because Parsonage-Turner syndrome typically involves multiple nerves, not just the axillary nerve.
column9: Diffuse involvement of all muscles in a C5/C6 radiculopathy pattern with paraspinal denervation is incorrect because Parsonage-Turner syndrome spares the paraspinals and has a patchy, plexus-level distribution rather than a root pattern.
column10: Selective involvement of lower trunk plexus muscles affecting only the hand intrinsics is incorrect because Parsonage-Turner syndrome primarily affects proximal upper extremity muscles in the shoulder girdle, not the hand intrinsics alone.

column2: A 58-year-old woman with a history of left breast cancer treated with surgery and radiation therapy presents with progressive weakness and numbness of the left upper limb over the past several months. On exam, she has atrophy of the intrinsic hand muscles and diminished sensation over the medial forearm and hand. Electrodiagnostic studies show reduced sensory nerve action potential amplitudes in the ulnar and medial antebrachial cutaneous nerves. Needle EMG reveals chronic neurogenic changes in the first dorsal interosseous and triceps muscles, with no fibrillation potentials or positive sharp waves. Cervical paraspinal muscles are normal. Which of the following best describes the typical EMG pattern seen in radiation-induced brachial plexopathy?
column3: Chronic denervation with large motor unit potentials and reduced recruitment without active fibrillation potentials
column4: Acute denervation with fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in a dermatomal pattern
column5: Diffuse involvement of all muscles in the upper limb including cervical paraspinals
column6: Isolated involvement of the lower trunk with preservation of sensory nerve action potentials
column7: Chronic denervation with large motor unit potentials and reduced recruitment without active fibrillation potentials is correct because radiation-induced plexopathy typically presents months after radiation with chronic denervation findings such as large polyphasic motor units and reduced recruitment, often without fibrillation potentials due to motor end-plate fibrosis.
column8: Acute denervation with fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in a dermatomal pattern is incorrect because this pattern is characteristic of acute radiculopathy, not radiation plexopathy.
column9: Diffuse involvement of all muscles in the upper limb including cervical paraspinals is incorrect because radiation plexopathy typically spares the paraspinals since it is a plexus lesion, not a root lesion.
column10: Isolated involvement of the lower trunk with preservation of sensory nerve action potentials is incorrect because radiation plexopathy usually causes sensory NCS abnormalities and is not limited to the lower trunk.

column2: A 42-year-old man presents with numbness in the thumb, index, and middle fingers, hand weakness, and difficulty with both pronation and grip strength. He also reports aching in the medial upper arm. On exam, there is mild atrophy of the thenar muscles, weakness of wrist flexion, forearm pronation, and impaired thumb opposition. Electrodiagnostic studies show slowed motor conduction across the distal humerus, reduced CMAP amplitude in the forearm, and reduced median SNAP amplitudes. The anterior interosseous branch shows abnormal motor responses, but the radial and ulnar nerves are normal. Which of the following is the most likely structure responsible for this patient’s nerve compression?
column7: Ligament of Struthers is correct because the combination of medial upper arm aching, median sensory loss, weakness in pronation and wrist flexion, slowed conduction across the distal humerus, and anterior interosseous involvement localizes the lesion to median nerve compression under the ligament of Struthers.
column8: Arcade of Struthers is incorrect because this structure affects the ulnar nerve proximal to the cubital tunnel, not the median nerve.
column9: Ligament of Frohse is incorrect because this site causes posterior interosseous syndrome, leading to radial nerve motor weakness in wrist and finger extension without median nerve findings.
column10: Anterior interosseous nerve fascicle is incorrect because anterior interosseous syndrome causes pure motor deficits in the forearm without sensory loss or conduction slowing at the elbow.

column2: A 50-year-old man presents with numbness in the ring and little fingers of his right hand and weakness with gripping objects. On exam, he has hypothenar atrophy, weak finger abduction, and clawing of the fourth and fifth digits. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal slowed motor conduction velocity across the elbow, reduced ulnar CMAP amplitude, and abnormal ulnar SNAPs. The flexor carpi ulnaris and first dorsal interosseous muscles show fibrillation potentials. Which of the following is the most likely structure causing his nerve compression?
column7: Arcade of Struthers is correct because it is a proximal ulnar nerve entrapment site above the elbow that causes both motor and sensory abnormalities, including forearm muscle involvement and slowed conduction across the elbow.
column8: Ligament of Osborne is incorrect because it compresses the ulnar nerve within the cubital tunnel but typically spares the proximal forearm muscles.
column9: Guyon’s canal is incorrect because compression here causes intrinsic hand weakness and sensory loss in the ulnar hand but spares forearm muscles like the flexor carpi ulnaris.
column10: Cubital tunnel retinaculum is incorrect because this is another name for the roof of the cubital tunnel and would not account for more proximal forearm muscle denervation.

column2: A 47-year-old woman presents with right wrist drop and weakness in finger extension after repetitive forearm use. There is no sensory loss in the hand. Electrodiagnostic studies show normal radial SNAPs, but needle EMG reveals fibrillation potentials in the extensor digitorum, extensor carpi ulnaris, and extensor indicis muscles. Brachioradialis and triceps are normal. Which of the following is the most likely site of compression?
column7: Ligament of Frohse is correct because it is the most common site of posterior interosseous nerve entrapment, leading to pure motor deficits in wrist and finger extension with sparing of the brachioradialis and triceps.
column8: Arcade of Struthers is incorrect because this structure compresses the ulnar nerve, not the radial nerve.
column9: Radial tunnel is incorrect because radial tunnel syndrome typically causes lateral forearm pain without motor weakness.
column10: Spiral groove of the humerus is incorrect because radial nerve compression here would cause weakness of the triceps and brachioradialis in addition to wrist drop.

column2: A 55-year-old woman presents with numbness and tingling in the right thumb, index, and middle fingers. Electrodiagnostic studies show a prolonged median distal motor latency of 6.2 milliseconds to the abductor pollicis brevis, absent median sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) in the index finger, and a median compound motor action potential (CMAP) amplitude of 2.5 mV. Ulnar sensory responses are normal. According to AANEM guidelines, how should the severity of her carpal tunnel syndrome be classified?
column7: Severe is correct because AANEM guidelines classify carpal tunnel syndrome as severe when there is both absent median SNAPs and evidence of axonal loss such as reduced CMAP amplitude or markedly prolonged distal motor latency.
column8: Mild is incorrect because mild CTS according to AANEM involves only prolonged median sensory latency with normal motor conduction and normal CMAP amplitude.
column9: Moderate is incorrect because moderate CTS includes prolonged or absent median SNAPs and prolonged distal motor latency, but with normal CMAP amplitude.
column10: Very mild (electrodiagnostically negative) is incorrect because AANEM guidelines do not recognize "very mild" as a formal category; normal or minimally abnormal studies may indicate subclinical or early CTS, but this is not part of the standard severity grading.

column2: A 49-year-old woman presents with numbness and tingling in the right hand affecting the thumb, index, and middle fingers, especially at night. On exam, there is no thenar atrophy and grip strength is normal. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal a median distal sensory latency of 4.2 milliseconds to the index finger (normal <3.5 ms), and a prolonged median distal motor latency of 5.4 milliseconds to the abductor pollicis brevis (normal <4.5 ms). The median compound motor action potential (CMAP) amplitude is normal at 6.5 mV. Ulnar sensory responses are normal. According to AANEM guidelines, how should the severity of her carpal tunnel syndrome be classified?
column7: Moderate is correct because AANEM guidelines classify CTS as moderate when there is prolonged median sensory latency, prolonged distal motor latency, but normal CMAP amplitude, indicating demyelination without axonal loss.
column8: Mild is incorrect because mild CTS involves only prolonged median sensory latency with normal motor conduction and CMAP amplitude.
column9: Severe is incorrect because severe CTS includes absent SNAPs and/or reduced CMAP amplitude indicating axonal loss, which is not present here.
column10: Very mild (electrodiagnostically negative) is incorrect because this patient's findings include both sensory and motor latency abnormalities, whereas very mild CTS (which is not an AANEM category) would have normal studies or only subtle comparative sensory differences.

column2: A 46-year-old woman presents with intermittent numbness and tingling in the right thumb, index, and middle fingers, especially when driving or holding her phone. On exam, there is no thenar atrophy, and grip strength is normal. Electrodiagnostic studies show a prolonged median distal sensory latency of 4.0 milliseconds to the index finger (normal <3.5 ms), but the median distal motor latency to the abductor pollicis brevis is normal at 3.9 milliseconds. The median compound motor action potential (CMAP) amplitude is normal at 7.0 mV. Ulnar sensory responses are normal. According to AANEM guidelines, how should the severity of her carpal tunnel syndrome be classified?
column7: Mild is correct because AANEM guidelines classify CTS as mild when there is prolonged median sensory latency but normal motor latency and CMAP amplitude, indicating purely sensory involvement without motor impairment.
column8: Moderate is incorrect because moderate CTS requires both prolonged sensory latency and prolonged distal motor latency, which is not present here.
column9: Severe is incorrect because severe CTS requires absent SNAPs and/or reduced CMAP amplitude, indicating axonal loss, which this patient does not have.
column10: Very mild (electrodiagnostically negative) is incorrect because AANEM does not formally define "very mild CTS"; this patient has abnormal sensory latency, which confirms mild CTS per standard criteria.

column2: A 50-year-old man with ulnar nerve palsy is asked to perform a lateral pinch test by holding a piece of paper between his thumb and the lateral aspect of his index finger. When the examiner pulls on the paper, the patient compensates by flexing the interphalangeal joint of the thumb. Which of the following best explains the biomechanical reason for this compensation?
column3: Loss of adductor pollicis function leads to recruitment of the flexor pollicis longus to generate pinch force
column4: Weakness of the opponens pollicis leads to recruitment of the abductor pollicis brevis for paper retention
column5: Loss of abductor pollicis brevis function causes substitution by the flexor pollicis brevis
column6: Weakness of the first dorsal interosseous causes the patient to recruit the palmaris longus during pinch
column7: Loss of adductor pollicis function leads to recruitment of the flexor pollicis longus to generate pinch force is correct because in ulnar nerve palsy, the weakened adductor pollicis cannot maintain lateral pinch, resulting in compensatory flexion of the thumb’s interphalangeal joint via the flexor pollicis longus (median nerve, anterior interosseous branch).
column8: Weakness of the opponens pollicis leads to recruitment of the abductor pollicis brevis for paper retention is incorrect because the opponens pollicis controls thumb opposition, not lateral pinch, and this substitution does not explain the observed thumb flexion.
column9: Loss of abductor pollicis brevis function causes substitution by the flexor pollicis brevis is incorrect because the abductor pollicis brevis controls thumb abduction, not adduction or pinch grip.
column10: Weakness of the first dorsal interosseous causes the patient to recruit the palmaris longus during pinch is incorrect because the first dorsal interosseous abducts the index finger, and the palmaris longus does not contribute to pinch grip.

column2: A 54-year-old man presents with numbness and tingling in the ring and little fingers of his left hand, along with hand weakness. Electrodiagnostic studies show slowed ulnar motor conduction velocity across the elbow segment (38 m/s), normal velocity across the forearm, and reduced ulnar CMAP amplitude recorded at the abductor digiti minimi. Sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs) in the ulnar distribution are reduced but present. Needle EMG demonstrates fibrillation potentials in the first dorsal interosseous and abductor digiti minimi muscles with normal findings in the flexor carpi ulnaris. Which of the following is the most likely localization?
column3: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the cubital tunnel
column4: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the Guyon’s canal
column5: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the arcade of Struthers
column7: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the cubital tunnel is correct because the combination of slowed conduction velocity across the elbow, reduced CMAP amplitude, abnormal ulnar SNAPs, and normal flexor carpi ulnaris EMG indicates a lesion at the cubital tunnel where the ulnar nerve passes behind the medial epicondyle.
column8: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the Guyon’s canal is incorrect because Guyon’s canal entrapment would not cause slowing across the elbow and typically spares the ulnar sensory fibers to the dorsal hand.
column9: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the arcade of Struthers is incorrect because entrapment at the arcade would involve the flexor carpi ulnaris due to its more proximal location.
column10: C8/T1 radiculopathy is incorrect because radiculopathy would typically show normal ulnar SNAPs and involve paraspinal denervation.

column2: A 60-year-old woman presents with numbness in the ring and little fingers of her right hand along with weakness in finger abduction. Electrodiagnostic studies show normal ulnar motor conduction velocity across the elbow and forearm, normal flexor carpi ulnaris and flexor digitorum profundus EMG, but reduced ulnar CMAP amplitude at the abductor digiti minimi. The ulnar sensory nerve action potential to the dorsal ulnar cutaneous branch is normal, but the digital ulnar SNAPs are absent. Which of the following is the most likely localization?
column3: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the Guyon’s canal
column4: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the cubital tunnel
column5: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the arcade of Struthers
column7: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the Guyon’s canal is correct because the combination of normal dorsal ulnar cutaneous SNAPs with absent digital SNAPs, intrinsic hand muscle denervation, and normal proximal forearm conduction localizes the lesion to the Guyon’s canal.
column8: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the cubital tunnel is incorrect because cubital tunnel syndrome would typically show slowing across the elbow and involve the dorsal ulnar cutaneous sensory fibers.
column9: Ulnar nerve entrapment at the arcade of Struthers is incorrect because proximal entrapment would involve both sensory and motor fibers in the forearm, including flexor carpi ulnaris.
column10: C8/T1 radiculopathy is incorrect because radiculopathy would present with normal SNAPs and often paraspinal denervation.

column2: A 45-year-old man presents with deep aching pain over the lateral proximal forearm worsened by resisted supination but has no sensory changes or wrist drop. Electrodiagnostic studies show normal radial motor and sensory conduction and normal EMG in the extensor digitorum, extensor indicis, and brachioradialis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column5: Radial nerve palsy at the spiral groove
column7: Radial tunnel syndrome is correct because it presents with lateral forearm pain without motor or sensory deficits, and EMG is typically normal, consistent with dynamic nerve compression.
column8: Posterior interosseous nerve syndrome is incorrect because it would present with weakness of wrist and finger extension and abnormal EMG in the extensor compartment.
column9: Radial nerve palsy at the spiral groove is incorrect because spiral groove lesions cause wrist drop and involve the brachioradialis.
column10: Lateral epicondylitis is incorrect because it presents with localized tendon pain over the lateral epicondyle, not nerve-related symptoms.

column2: A 48-year-old man presents with difficulty extending his fingers and wrist after weeks of repetitive forearm use while operating machinery. He denies pain or sensory changes. On examination, he has weakness of finger and wrist extension with preserved elbow extension and normal sensation in the hand. Electrodiagnostic studies show normal radial sensory nerve action potentials, normal triceps and brachioradialis EMG, but fibrillation potentials and reduced recruitment in the extensor digitorum, extensor indicis, and extensor carpi ulnaris muscles. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column5: Radial nerve palsy at the spiral groove
column7: Posterior interosseous nerve syndrome is correct because the patient has painless motor weakness of wrist and finger extension, normal sensation, and EMG evidence of denervation in radial-innervated extensor muscles distal to the supinator, consistent with PIN syndrome.
column8: Radial tunnel syndrome is incorrect because radial tunnel syndrome presents with lateral forearm pain without weakness or EMG abnormalities.
column9: Radial nerve palsy at the spiral groove is incorrect because a spiral groove lesion would involve the triceps and brachioradialis along with wrist drop, not isolated extensor weakness with sparing of elbow extension.
column10: C7 radiculopathy is incorrect because radiculopathy would typically involve multiple nerve territories and cause paraspinal denervation, not isolated posterior interosseous involvement with normal sensation.

column2: A 38-year-old man presents with numbness and tingling over the dorsoradial aspect of his right hand. He denies weakness, trauma, or neck pain. On examination, there is decreased sensation over the dorsum of the thumb and index finger near the wrist, but motor strength is normal. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal reduced right superficial radial sensory nerve action potential (SNAP) amplitude compared to the left, with normal radial motor responses. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management?
column3: Remove potential external compressive sources such as wristbands or tight devices
column4: Surgical decompression of the radial nerve at the spiral groove
column7: Remove potential external compressive sources such as wristbands or tight devices is correct because superficial radial neuropathy (cheiralgia paresthetica) is commonly due to external compression at the distal forearm or wrist, and initial treatment involves removing potential compressive sources even if the history is not explicitly provided.
column8: Surgical decompression of the radial nerve at the spiral groove is incorrect because the lesion is distal, affecting only the superficial sensory branch at the wrist, not the radial nerve at the spiral groove.
column9: Initiation of corticosteroid therapy is incorrect because superficial radial neuropathy is usually mechanical, not inflammatory, and often resolves with conservative management.
column10: Perform carpal tunnel release is incorrect because carpal tunnel syndrome involves median nerve dysfunction and affects palmar sensation, not the dorsoradial hand.

column2: A 35-year-old male tennis player presents with weakness in external rotation of the shoulder and atrophy of the infraspinatus muscle. He has no significant pain or history of trauma. Needle EMG reveals fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in the infraspinatus but normal findings in the supraspinatus and deltoid muscles. Nerve conduction studies are otherwise normal. Which of the following is the most likely site of nerve compression?
column3: Suprascapular nerve at the spinoglenoid notch
column4: Suprascapular nerve at the suprascapular notch
column5: Axillary nerve at the quadrilateral space
column7: Suprascapular nerve at the spinoglenoid notch is correct because compression at the spinoglenoid notch affects the branch to the infraspinatus while sparing the supraspinatus, resulting in isolated infraspinatus denervation on EMG.
column8: Suprascapular nerve at the suprascapular notch is incorrect because compression at this location would affect both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles.
column9: Axillary nerve at the quadrilateral space is incorrect because axillary nerve injury would involve the deltoid and teres minor, not the infraspinatus.
column10: C5/C6 radiculopathy is incorrect because radiculopathy would typically involve both the supraspinatus and infraspinatus, as well as paraspinal denervation and potentially deltoid involvement.

column2: A 34-year-old man presents with right scapular protrusion after a fall. On exam, the medial border of the scapula becomes prominent when pushing against a wall. Needle EMG reveals fibrillation potentials in the serratus anterior, with normal findings in the trapezius and rhomboids. Which of the following best explains this patient’s scapular abnormality?
column3: Medial scapular winging due to long thoracic nerve injury affecting the serratus anterior
column4: Lateral scapular winging due to spinal accessory nerve injury affecting the trapezius
column5: Medial scapular winging due to dorsal scapular nerve injury affecting the rhomboids
column6: Lateral scapular winging due to axillary nerve injury affecting the deltoid and teres minor
column7: Medial scapular winging due to long thoracic nerve injury affecting the serratus anterior is correct because the serratus anterior stabilizes the scapula against the thoracic wall, and denervation leads to medial border prominence on wall push.
column8: Lateral scapular winging due to spinal accessory nerve injury affecting the trapezius is incorrect because trapezius denervation causes lateral and inferior scapular displacement during abduction, not medial winging.
column9: Medial scapular winging due to dorsal scapular nerve injury affecting the rhomboids is incorrect because rhomboid weakness causes mild scapular displacement but does not produce classic medial winging during wall push.
column10: Lateral scapular winging due to axillary nerve injury affecting the deltoid and teres minor is incorrect because axillary nerve injury causes deltoid weakness and shoulder abduction deficits, not scapular winging.

column2: A 42-year-old man presents with right shoulder drooping and difficulty elevating his arm following neck surgery. On exam, the scapula is displaced laterally and inferiorly during attempted abduction. Needle EMG reveals denervation of the trapezius, with normal findings in the serratus anterior and rhomboids. Which of the following best explains this patient’s scapular abnormality?
column3: Lateral scapular winging due to spinal accessory nerve injury affecting the trapezius
column4: Medial scapular winging due to long thoracic nerve injury affecting the serratus anterior
column5: Medial scapular winging due to dorsal scapular nerve injury affecting the rhomboids
column6: Lateral scapular winging due to axillary nerve injury affecting the deltoid and teres minor
column7: Lateral scapular winging due to spinal accessory nerve injury affecting the trapezius is correct because trapezius denervation leads to lateral and inferior scapular displacement during arm elevation, consistent with lateral winging.
column8: Medial scapular winging due to long thoracic nerve injury affecting the serratus anterior is incorrect because serratus anterior weakness causes medial winging during forward flexion or wall push, not lateral winging.
column9: Medial scapular winging due to dorsal scapular nerve injury affecting the rhomboids is incorrect because dorsal scapular nerve injury causes mild scapular displacement but not pronounced lateral winging or shoulder droop.
column10: Lateral scapular winging due to axillary nerve injury affecting the deltoid and teres minor is incorrect because axillary nerve injury affects shoulder abduction but does not cause scapular winging.

column2: A 45-year-old man is diagnosed with meralgia paresthetica after presenting with numbness and burning pain over the anterolateral thigh. Electrodiagnostic studies confirm reduced lateral femoral cutaneous nerve sensory responses with normal motor findings. His symptoms are mild and primarily occur when he wears tight belts or stands for long periods. What is the most appropriate initial management?
column3: Advise avoidance of tight clothing and weight loss if applicable
column4: Schedule surgical decompression of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve
column6: Prescribe a rigid lumbar brace to stabilize the spine
column7: Advise avoidance of tight clothing and weight loss if applicable is correct because first-line treatment for meralgia paresthetica involves conservative management, including removal of compressive factors like tight belts or clothing and lifestyle modifications.
column8: Schedule surgical decompression of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve is incorrect because surgery is reserved for severe or refractory cases that do not improve with conservative management.
column9: Initiate high-dose corticosteroid therapy is incorrect because corticosteroids are not first-line treatment for this mechanical entrapment neuropathy.
column10: Prescribe a rigid lumbar brace to stabilize the spine is incorrect because meralgia paresthetica is a mononeuropathy of the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, not a spinal instability issue.

column2: A 58-year-old woman presents with right medial thigh pain and weakness when crossing her legs. On examination, she has decreased strength in thigh adduction and reduced sensation over the medial thigh. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in the right adductor longus, with normal findings in the iliopsoas, quadriceps, and paraspinal muscles. Sensory nerve action potentials in the saphenous nerve are normal. Which of the following is the most likely localization?
column7: Obturator nerve neuropathy is correct because the combination of medial thigh pain, weakness in hip adduction, sensory loss over the medial thigh, and EMG denervation in the adductor longus with normal iliopsoas, quadriceps, and paraspinals localizes the lesion to the obturator nerve.
column8: Femoral nerve neuropathy is incorrect because femoral neuropathy would involve quadriceps weakness, reduced patellar reflex, and anterior thigh sensory loss.
column9: L2/L3 radiculopathy is incorrect because radiculopathy would typically involve paraspinal denervation and potentially involve both the iliopsoas and quadriceps.
column10: Lumbar plexopathy is incorrect because a plexopathy would affect multiple nerves in the lumbar plexus, not just the obturator nerve, and would involve other muscles beyond the adductors.

column2: A 65-year-old man presents with right anterior thigh pain and difficulty climbing stairs. On examination, he has weakness in knee extension, decreased sensation over the anterior thigh and medial leg, and a reduced right patellar reflex. Electrodiagnostic studies show fibrillation potentials in the right vastus medialis and rectus femoris, with normal findings in the adductor longus and paraspinal muscles. The right saphenous sensory nerve action potential is absent. Which of the following is the most likely localization?
column7: Femoral nerve mononeuropathy is correct because the combination of quadriceps weakness, anterior thigh and medial leg sensory loss, reduced patellar reflex, absent saphenous SNAP, and normal paraspinal and adductor findings localizes the lesion to the femoral nerve.
column8: L2 radiculopathy is incorrect because radiculopathy would typically involve paraspinal denervation and might affect both iliopsoas and adductor muscles.
column9: Obturator nerve neuropathy is incorrect because obturator neuropathy would primarily affect the adductors, not the quadriceps, and would spare the saphenous nerve.
column10: Lumbar plexopathy is incorrect because a plexopathy would involve multiple lumbar plexus nerves, not just the femoral nerve, and typically affects both quadriceps and adductors.

column2: A 52-year-old woman presents with numbness and burning pain on the sole of her right foot, worse with prolonged standing. On examination, she has decreased sensation over the plantar surface of the foot, with normal sensation over the heel and no weakness in dorsiflexion or foot eversion. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal prolonged distal motor latency of the tibial nerve to the abductor hallucis and reduced plantar mixed sensory nerve action potentials. The medial calcaneal sensory response is normal. Peroneal motor and sensory studies are normal, and there are no fibrillation potentials in the anterior tibialis or paraspinal muscles. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column5: Peroneal neuropathy at the fibular head
column7: Tarsal tunnel syndrome is correct because plantar foot sensory loss with sparing of the heel, delayed tibial motor response, reduced plantar sensory responses, and normal peroneal and paraspinal findings localize the lesion to the tibial nerve in the tarsal tunnel.
column8: L5 radiculopathy is incorrect because L5 radiculopathy would involve dorsiflexion weakness, sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot, and paraspinal denervation.
column9: Peroneal neuropathy at the fibular head is incorrect because this would cause foot drop and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot, not the plantar surface.
column10: S1 radiculopathy is incorrect because S1 radiculopathy would affect plantarflexion and the Achilles reflex, and would not spare the heel’s sensation.

column2: A 60-year-old man presents with right foot drop and difficulty walking after prolonged squatting while gardening. On examination, he has weakness in ankle dorsiflexion and foot eversion, with decreased sensation over the dorsum of the foot and lateral shin. Electrodiagnostic studies show reduced peroneal CMAP amplitude recorded at the extensor digitorum brevis, slowed peroneal motor conduction velocity across the fibular head, and reduced superficial peroneal sensory responses. Tibial motor and sural sensory studies are normal. Needle EMG reveals fibrillation potentials in the tibialis anterior and peroneus longus muscles, with normal findings in the short head of the biceps femoris. Which of the following is the most likely localization?
column3: Common peroneal nerve injury at the fibular head
column5: Sciatic nerve injury in the thigh
column6: Deep peroneal nerve injury at the ankle
column7: Common peroneal nerve injury at the fibular head is correct because the combination of foot drop, dorsum of foot sensory loss, slowed peroneal conduction across the fibular head, and EMG denervation in both the tibialis anterior and peroneus longus with sparing of the short head of the biceps femoris localizes the lesion to the common peroneal nerve at the fibular neck.
column8: L5 radiculopathy is incorrect because L5 radiculopathy would typically involve paraspinal denervation and often spares the peroneal conduction velocity across the fibular head.
column9: Sciatic nerve injury in the thigh is incorrect because sciatic neuropathy would involve the short head of the biceps femoris and tibial-innervated muscles, not just peroneal-innervated muscles.
column10: Deep peroneal nerve injury at the ankle is incorrect because injury at this level would only affect toe extension and dorsum foot sensation between the first and second toes, not the entire peroneal distribution.
column12: This is the classic “strawberry picker's palsy” scenario!

column2: A 40-year-old man presents with right foot weakness after ankle surgery. On examination, he has difficulty dorsiflexing his great toe and extending his toes but has preserved foot eversion strength. Sensation is decreased in the first web space, but the rest of the dorsum of the foot is intact. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal fibrillation potentials in the extensor digitorum brevis and tibialis anterior with normal findings in the peroneus longus. The superficial peroneal sensory response is normal. Which of the following is the most likely localization?
column3: Deep peroneal nerve injury at the ankle or midfoot
column4: Common peroneal nerve injury at the fibular head
column7: Deep peroneal nerve injury at the ankle or midfoot is correct because weakness in toe extension, sensory loss in the first web space, normal foot eversion, normal peroneus longus EMG, and normal superficial peroneal SNAP localize the lesion to the deep peroneal nerve distally.
column8: Common peroneal nerve injury at the fibular head is incorrect because this would involve both deep and superficial peroneal branches, affecting foot eversion and dorsum foot sensation more broadly.
column9: L5 radiculopathy is incorrect because L5 radiculopathy would involve paraspinal denervation and possibly other muscles outside the peroneal distribution.
column10: Superficial peroneal nerve injury is incorrect because superficial peroneal neuropathy would cause sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot but would not affect toe extension or tibialis anterior function.

column2: A 50-year-old woman presents with numbness over the dorsum of her right foot after prolonged leg crossing. On examination, she has normal foot dorsiflexion and toe extension strength but decreased sensation over the lateral shin and dorsum of the foot, sparing the first web space. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal reduced superficial peroneal sensory responses with normal motor studies. Needle EMG shows no abnormalities in the tibialis anterior, extensor digitorum brevis, or peroneus longus. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column5: Common peroneal nerve injury at the fibular head
column7: Isolated superficial peroneal nerve injury is correct because sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot with normal strength, normal first web space sensation, and reduced superficial peroneal SNAP localizes the lesion to the superficial peroneal nerve.
column8: Deep peroneal nerve injury is incorrect because deep peroneal neuropathy would cause weakness in toe extension and sensory loss limited to the first web space.
column9: Common peroneal nerve injury at the fibular head is incorrect because this would cause both motor and sensory deficits, including foot drop.
column10: L5 radiculopathy is incorrect because L5 radiculopathy would often cause weakness in foot dorsiflexion and paraspinal EMG abnormalities, not isolated sensory loss.

column2: A 45-year-old man presents with difficulty climbing stairs and a waddling gait. On examination, he has weakness of hip abduction with a positive Trendelenburg sign. There is no hip extension weakness, and sensory examination is normal. Needle EMG reveals fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in the gluteus medius and minimus with normal findings in the gluteus maximus and paraspinal muscles. Which of the following is the most likely localization?
column7: Superior gluteal nerve neuropathy is correct because isolated weakness of hip abduction with denervation in the gluteus medius and minimus, normal gluteus maximus, and no sensory loss localizes to the superior gluteal nerve.
column8: Inferior gluteal nerve neuropathy is incorrect because the inferior gluteal nerve innervates the gluteus maximus, not the abductors.
column9: L5 radiculopathy is incorrect because L5 radiculopathy would typically involve additional muscles such as the tibialis anterior and paraspinals.
column10: Sciatic neuropathy is incorrect because sciatic nerve injury would cause leg weakness and sensory deficits below the knee, not isolated hip abduction weakness.

column2: A 50-year-old woman presents with difficulty rising from a seated position and climbing stairs. On examination, she has weakness of hip extension with preserved hip abduction strength and no sensory deficits. Needle EMG shows fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in the gluteus maximus, with normal findings in the gluteus medius, minimus, and paraspinal muscles. Which of the following is the most likely localization?
column7: Inferior gluteal nerve neuropathy is correct because isolated weakness of hip extension with denervation in the gluteus maximus and normal findings in the abductors localizes to the inferior gluteal nerve.
column8: Superior gluteal nerve neuropathy is incorrect because the superior gluteal nerve controls the gluteus medius and minimus, not the gluteus maximus.
column9: L5 radiculopathy is incorrect because radiculopathy would typically involve paraspinal denervation and may affect additional muscles such as the hamstrings or gastrocnemius.
column10: Sciatic neuropathy is incorrect because sciatic nerve injury would involve the posterior thigh and leg muscles, not just the gluteus maximus.

column2: A 62-year-old man with poorly controlled diabetes presents with numbness and burning pain in both feet. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal reduced sural and superficial peroneal sensory nerve action potential amplitudes, prolonged distal motor latencies in the tibial and peroneal nerves, and mildly reduced compound motor action potential (CMAP) amplitudes. Motor conduction velocities are within normal limits or only mildly slowed. Which of the following is the most likely electrodiagnostic diagnosis?
column3: Length-dependent axonal sensory and motor polyneuropathy consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy
column4: Demyelinating polyneuropathy consistent with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
column7: Length-dependent axonal sensory and motor polyneuropathy consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy is correct because the electrodiagnostic findings of reduced sensory amplitudes, mild distal motor latency prolongation, and mildly reduced CMAP amplitudes with preserved conduction velocities are typical of an axonal polyneuropathy such as diabetic peripheral neuropathy.
column8: Demyelinating polyneuropathy consistent with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is incorrect because CMT typically presents with markedly slowed conduction velocities (<38 m/s), not primarily axonal findings.
column9: Acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (AIDP) is incorrect because AIDP presents with uniformly slowed conduction velocities, conduction block, and temporal dispersion, which are not seen here.
column10: Motor-predominant polyneuropathy is incorrect because the findings include both sensory and motor involvement, not motor predominance.

column2: A 25-year-old man presents with a history of progressive foot drop since adolescence. On examination, he has bilateral pes cavus deformities and distal muscle atrophy. Electrodiagnostic studies reveal uniformly slowed motor conduction velocities in the upper and lower extremities (<35 m/s), prolonged distal motor latencies, and mildly reduced CMAP amplitudes. Sensory responses are reduced in all extremities. Which of the following is the most likely electrodiagnostic diagnosis?
column3: Hereditary demyelinating polyneuropathy consistent with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease
column4: Length-dependent axonal sensory and motor polyneuropathy consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy
column7: Hereditary demyelinating polyneuropathy consistent with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is correct because uniform, diffuse slowing of motor conduction velocities <35 m/s in all limbs with prolonged distal latencies and sensory involvement is characteristic of hereditary demyelinating polyneuropathy such as CMT.
column8: Length-dependent axonal sensory and motor polyneuropathy consistent with diabetic peripheral neuropathy is incorrect because diabetic neuropathy typically shows reduced amplitudes with preserved or only mildly slowed conduction velocities.
column9: Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) is incorrect because CIDP usually shows segmental demyelination with conduction block and temporal dispersion, not uniform diffuse slowing.
column10: Motor neuron disease is incorrect because motor neuron disease causes motor denervation without sensory involvement and typically normal nerve conduction velocities.

column2: A 36-year-old woman presents with fluctuating ptosis and diplopia that worsen throughout the day. Routine nerve conduction studies and needle EMG are normal. Repetitive nerve stimulation at 3 Hz shows a 15% decrement in compound motor action potential (CMAP) amplitude between the first and fourth stimuli in the right ulnar nerve. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Myasthenia gravis is correct because the combination of fluctuating weakness with normal routine nerve conduction and EMG, plus a greater than 10% CMAP decrement on low-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation, is consistent with a postsynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder such as myasthenia gravis.
column8: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is incorrect because LEMS shows low CMAP amplitudes at rest with an increment greater than 100% after exercise or high-frequency stimulation, not a decrement at low frequency.
column9: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis is incorrect because ALS shows signs of active denervation on EMG without significant decrement on repetitive nerve stimulation.
column10: Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy is incorrect because CIDP causes slowed conduction velocities and demyelinating features, not a decremental response.

column2: A 62-year-old man presents with gradually progressive proximal leg weakness. On examination, he has diminished deep tendon reflexes that improve immediately after muscle contraction. Electrodiagnostic studies show low CMAP amplitudes in the peroneal and ulnar nerves at rest. After brief exercise, the CMAP amplitude increases by 200%. Sensory nerve conduction studies are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is correct because the combination of low resting CMAPs, post-exercise facilitation, and reflex improvement is classic for a presynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder such as LEMS.
column8: Myasthenia gravis is incorrect because MG produces a decrement on low-frequency stimulation without post-exercise facilitation or reflex changes.
column9: Motor neuron disease is incorrect because ALS causes denervation on needle EMG but does not lead to CMAP facilitation or reflex improvement.
column10: Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy is incorrect because CIDP typically shows slowed conduction velocities and conduction block, not CMAP increment.

column2: A 30-year-old man presents with progressive blurred vision, dysarthria, and generalized weakness. On examination, pupils are sluggishly reactive, but sensation is intact. Electrodiagnostic studies show low CMAP amplitudes at rest, with more than a 100% increase in CMAP amplitude after 50 Hz repetitive nerve stimulation. Sensory studies are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Botulism is correct because the combination of bulbar weakness, autonomic features (pupil involvement), low CMAP amplitudes, and post-tetanic facilitation is consistent with presynaptic neuromuscular junction blockade due to botulism.
column8: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is incorrect because LEMS presents with limb weakness and facilitation but usually spares cranial nerves and pupillary function.
column9: Myasthenia gravis is incorrect because MG produces fluctuating weakness with normal CMAP amplitudes at rest and decrement at low-frequency stimulation, without facilitation.
column10: Guillain-Barré syndrome (AIDP) is incorrect because AIDP presents with demyelinating features and sensory involvement, not facilitation on repetitive nerve stimulation.

column2: A 29-year-old woman undergoes repetitive nerve stimulation for fluctuating weakness. Low-frequency (3 Hz) stimulation of the ulnar nerve shows a 15% decrement in CMAP amplitude from the first to fourth stimulus. After brief maximal voluntary contraction, CMAP amplitude transiently improves but rapidly deteriorates with continued stimulation. Which of the following best describes this physiological response?
column3: Post-activation exhaustion due to postsynaptic neuromuscular junction failure
column4: Post-tetanic potentiation due to presynaptic calcium buildup
column5: Facilitation with depletion due to reduced safety margin at the neuromuscular junction
column6: Pseudofacilitation from increased voluntary effort during testing
column7: Post-activation exhaustion due to postsynaptic neuromuscular junction failure is correct because transient improvement in CMAP amplitude followed by rapid decline is characteristic of post-activation exhaustion seen in disorders like myasthenia gravis, due to progressive acetylcholine receptor depletion at the NMJ.
column8: Post-tetanic potentiation due to presynaptic calcium buildup is incorrect because post-tetanic potentiation refers to sustained CMAP amplitude improvement after high-frequency stimulation, not failure after brief voluntary effort.
column9: Facilitation with depletion due to reduced safety margin at the neuromuscular junction is incorrect because although MG involves a reduced safety margin, true post-activation exhaustion involves receptor fatigue, not depletion after facilitated release.
column10: Pseudofacilitation from increased voluntary effort during testing is incorrect because this is an artifact of testing, not a true electrophysiologic phenomenon of NMJ transmission failure.

column2: A 68-year-old man presents with slowly progressive proximal lower extremity weakness and absent deep tendon reflexes. Electrodiagnostic studies show low CMAP amplitudes at rest in the peroneal and ulnar nerves. After 10 seconds of maximal voluntary contraction, the CMAP amplitude increases by 250% and remains elevated for several minutes. Which of the following best describes this physiological response?
column3: Post-activation facilitation due to presynaptic neuromuscular junction dysfunction
column4: Post-tetanic potentiation due to depletion of acetylcholine receptors
column5: Pseudofacilitation from increased muscle fiber recruitment during testing
column6: Reinnervation potentiation due to collateral sprouting in motor neuron disease
column7: Post-activation facilitation due to presynaptic neuromuscular junction dysfunction is correct because a sustained CMAP amplitude increase after brief maximal contraction is characteristic of presynaptic disorders such as Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome, due to enhanced calcium-mediated neurotransmitter release.
column8: Post-tetanic potentiation due to depletion of acetylcholine receptors is incorrect because post-tetanic potentiation is transient and related to presynaptic calcium buildup, not postsynaptic receptor depletion.
column9: Pseudofacilitation from increased muscle fiber recruitment during testing is incorrect because this would be a voluntary effort artifact, not a true electrophysiological facilitation pattern.
column10: Reinnervation potentiation due to collateral sprouting in motor neuron disease is incorrect because motor neuron disease causes chronic reinnervation changes, not exercise-dependent CMAP amplitude increments.

column2: A 38-year-old woman with fluctuating ptosis undergoes single fiber EMG of the frontalis muscle. The study reveals an average jitter of 75 microseconds and occasional blocking. Routine nerve conduction studies and standard needle EMG are normal. Which of the following best explains the physiological significance of this finding?
column3: Increased neuromuscular transmission variability consistent with impaired postsynaptic safety margin
column4: Segmental demyelination resulting in conduction block
column5: Collateral sprouting from reinnervation leading to increased fiber density without jitter changes
column6: Axonal degeneration causing reduced recruitment and decreased jitter
column7: Increased neuromuscular transmission variability consistent with impaired postsynaptic safety margin is correct because single fiber EMG showing increased jitter and blocking reflects transmission instability at the neuromuscular junction, typically due to postsynaptic dysfunction as seen in myasthenia gravis.
column8: Segmental demyelination resulting in conduction block is incorrect because conduction block is a nerve conduction study finding, not detected on single fiber EMG.
column9: Collateral sprouting from reinnervation leading to increased fiber density without jitter changes is incorrect because reinnervation increases fiber density but usually also causes increased jitter due to immature NMJ formation.
column10: Axonal degeneration causing reduced recruitment and decreased jitter is incorrect because axonal loss leads to reduced recruitment but often increases jitter due to reinnervation, not decreases it.

column2: A 60-year-old man presents with a history of slowly progressive distal limb weakness. Needle EMG reveals large-amplitude, long-duration, polyphasic motor unit potentials with increased fiber density on single fiber EMG. There are no fibrillation potentials or positive sharp waves. Which of the following best explains these findings?
column4: Acute denervation due to motor axon loss
column5: Primary demyelinating neuropathy with temporal dispersion
column6: Postsynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder with transmission failure
column7: Collateral reinnervation after chronic denervation is correct because large, long-duration, polyphasic motor unit potentials and increased fiber density on SFEMG reflect chronic axonal loss with collateral sprouting, leading to reinnervation of denervated fibers.
column8: Acute denervation due to motor axon loss is incorrect because acute denervation typically shows fibrillation potentials and reduced recruitment, not large motor units or increased fiber density.
column9: Primary demyelinating neuropathy with temporal dispersion is incorrect because demyelinating neuropathies affect conduction velocity and waveform shape in NCS, not motor unit morphology or fiber density on EMG.
column10: Postsynaptic neuromuscular junction disorder with transmission failure is incorrect because NMJ disorders cause increased jitter and blocking but do not lead to large, long-duration motor unit potentials or increased fiber density.

column2: A 62-year-old man presents with progressive weakness in his arms, legs, and shortness of breath over the past year. On examination, he has diffuse muscle atrophy, fasciculations, and brisk reflexes. Needle EMG shows fibrillation potentials and positive sharp waves in the biceps, tibialis anterior, and diaphragm, as well as large-amplitude, long-duration, polyphasic motor unit potentials in these same muscles with decreased recruitment. Sensory nerve conduction studies are normal. Which of the following best describes the electrodiagnostic significance of these findings?
column3: They confirm widespread active and chronic denervation in multiple regions, supporting the diagnosis of ALS.
column4: They are consistent with isolated cervical and lumbar radiculopathies with phrenic nerve involvement.
column5: They indicate a generalized myopathic process with early recruitment.
column6: They suggest a neuromuscular junction transmission disorder without axonal degeneration.
column7: They confirm widespread active and chronic denervation in multiple regions, supporting the diagnosis of ALS is correct because the combination of fibrillations (active denervation), large polyphasic MUAPs (chronic denervation), and normal sensory studies across bulbar, cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions is characteristic of ALS per El Escorial and Awaji criteria.
column8: They are consistent with isolated cervical and lumbar radiculopathies with phrenic nerve involvement is incorrect because radiculopathies do not cause simultaneous widespread denervation across multiple non-contiguous myotomes or involve the diaphragm without corresponding sensory root changes.
column9: They indicate a generalized myopathic process with early recruitment is incorrect because myopathies show small, short-duration motor units with early recruitment, not large polyphasic units or denervation.
column10: They suggest a neuromuscular junction transmission disorder without axonal degeneration is incorrect because NMJ disorders do not cause fibrillation potentials or chronic neurogenic MUAP changes.

column2: During overhead arm elevation, which of the following best describes the coordinated motion between the glenohumeral joint and the scapulothoracic articulation in a healthy adult?
column3: Approximately 120 degrees of glenohumeral motion combines with 60 degrees of scapulothoracic rotation to achieve full abduction.
column4: Glenohumeral motion accounts for 90% of overhead arm elevation, with minimal scapular contribution.
column5: Scapulothoracic motion initiates shoulder abduction, followed by delayed glenohumeral involvement after the first 60 degrees.
column6: Full shoulder abduction requires equal 90-degree contributions from both the glenohumeral and scapulothoracic joints.
column7: Approximately 120 degrees of glenohumeral motion combines with 60 degrees of scapulothoracic rotation to achieve full abduction is correct because this follows the classic 2:1 scapulohumeral rhythm, where 120 degrees of glenohumeral abduction pairs with 60 degrees of scapular upward rotation to complete 180 degrees of arm elevation.
column8: Glenohumeral motion accounts for 90% of overhead arm elevation, with minimal scapular contribution is incorrect because the scapulothoracic joint contributes significantly to overhead motion, providing about one-third of total abduction.
column9: Scapulothoracic motion initiates shoulder abduction, followed by delayed glenohumeral involvement after the first 60 degrees is incorrect because early shoulder abduction is primarily driven by the glenohumeral joint, not the scapula.
column10: Full shoulder abduction requires equal 90-degree contributions from both the glenohumeral and scapulothoracic joints is incorrect because the actual ratio is approximately 120 degrees of glenohumeral motion to 60 degrees of scapular rotation, not equal contributions.

column2: A 28-year-old man presents after a fall onto his shoulder during a mountain biking accident. He has pain over the acromioclavicular (AC) joint with visible elevation of the distal clavicle. Radiographs reveal a coracoclavicular (CC) distance greater than double the normal side, and CT imaging confirms posterior displacement of the distal clavicle into the trapezius muscle. According to the Rockwood classification, what is the grade of this AC joint injury?
column7: Grade IV is correct because Rockwood Grade IV injuries involve complete AC and CC ligament disruption with posterior displacement of the distal clavicle into or through the trapezius, as described in this scenario.
column8: Grade III is incorrect because Grade III involves complete ligament disruption with superior clavicular displacement but without posterior displacement into the trapezius.
column9: Grade II is incorrect because Grade II involves AC ligament tear with CC ligament sprain and mild clavicular elevation, not posterior displacement.
column10: Grade V is incorrect because Grade V involves exaggerated superior displacement with >100% CC interval increase and tenting of the skin, not posterior displacement into the trapezius.
column12: Rockwood classification of AC joint injuries: Type I – AC ligament sprain, no displacement; Type II – AC ligament rupture, CC ligament sprain, slight clavicle elevation (<25-50% CC distance increase); Type III – Complete AC and CC ligament rupture, clavicle displaced superiorly up to 100% of normal CC distance; Type IV – Complete AC and CC rupture with posterior clavicle displacement into trapezius; Type V – Complete AC and CC rupture with >100% CC distance increase and significant soft tissue disruption (skin tenting); Type VI – Inferior clavicle displacement under coracoid or acromion, rare.

column2: A 35-year-old construction worker falls directly onto his left shoulder from a ladder. On examination, the distal clavicle is markedly prominent with significant tenting of the skin but no open wound. Radiographs reveal complete AC and CC ligament disruption, with the clavicle displaced superiorly more than 100% of the normal coracoclavicular distance. There is no posterior displacement into the trapezius. According to the Rockwood classification, what is the grade of this AC joint injury?
column7: Grade V is correct because Rockwood Grade V injuries involve complete AC and CC ligament disruption with exaggerated superior displacement of the clavicle, typically >100% of the normal coracoclavicular distance, along with significant soft tissue disruption causing skin tenting, but without posterior or inferior displacement.
column8: Grade III is incorrect because Grade III involves complete ligament disruption with superior clavicle elevation, but the displacement is typically no more than 100% of the normal CC distance and there is no soft tissue tenting.
column9: Grade IV is incorrect because Grade IV involves posterior displacement of the clavicle into the trapezius muscle, which is not present in this case.
column10: Grade VI is incorrect because Grade VI is a rare injury where the clavicle is displaced inferiorly, usually beneath the coracoid or into the subacromial space.
column12: Rockwood classification of AC joint injuries: Type I – AC ligament sprain, no displacement; Type II – AC ligament rupture, CC ligament sprain, slight clavicle elevation (<25-50% CC distance increase); Type III – Complete AC and CC ligament rupture, clavicle displaced superiorly up to 100% of normal CC distance; Type IV – Complete AC and CC rupture with posterior clavicle displacement into trapezius; Type V – Complete AC and CC rupture with >100% CC distance increase and significant soft tissue disruption (skin tenting); Type VI – Inferior clavicle displacement under coracoid or acromion, rare.

column2: A 40-year-old man presents after falling directly onto his shoulder during a soccer game. He reports pain and swelling over the acromioclavicular (AC) joint. On physical exam, there is a palpable step-off at the distal clavicle without significant skin tenting. Radiographs show complete disruption of both the AC and coracoclavicular (CC) ligaments with the clavicle displaced superiorly approximately equal to the width of the acromion but less than twice the normal coracoclavicular distance. According to the Rockwood classification, what is the grade of this AC joint injury?
column7: Grade III is correct because Rockwood Grade III injuries involve complete disruption of both the AC and CC ligaments, resulting in superior clavicular displacement typically up to 100% of the normal coracoclavicular distance, with no posterior or inferior displacement and no significant soft tissue tenting.
column8: Grade II is incorrect because Grade II injuries involve partial CC ligament injury with only mild clavicular elevation and the CC distance is not increased by more than 25–50%.
column9: Grade IV is incorrect because Grade IV involves posterior displacement of the clavicle into the trapezius, which is not present in this case.
column10: Grade V is incorrect because Grade V involves exaggerated superior displacement of the clavicle with >100% CC distance increase and significant soft tissue disruption, which is not described here.
column12: Rockwood classification of AC joint injuries: Type I – AC ligament sprain, no displacement; Type II – AC ligament rupture, CC ligament sprain, slight clavicle elevation (<25-50% CC distance increase); Type III – Complete AC and CC ligament rupture, clavicle displaced superiorly up to 100% of normal CC distance; Type IV – Complete AC and CC rupture with posterior clavicle displacement into trapezius; Type V – Complete AC and CC rupture with >100% CC distance increase and significant soft tissue disruption (skin tenting); Type VI – Inferior clavicle displacement under coracoid or acromion, rare.

column2: A 32-year-old male wrestler lands directly on his shoulder during a match. He reports severe pain and an obvious deformity. Examination shows a palpable step-off at the AC joint with posterior displacement of the distal clavicle, which appears to be lodged in the trapezius muscle. Radiographs and CT imaging confirm complete disruption of the AC and CC ligaments, with the distal clavicle positioned posteriorly behind the acromion. According to the Rockwood classification, what is the next best step in management?
column3: Refer for surgical consultation due to the clavicle’s posterior displacement into the trapezius.
column4: Initiate conservative management with rest, ice, and physical therapy.
column5: Recommend activity modification and a sling for 2 weeks followed by gradual return to sport.
column6: Perform a corticosteroid injection into the AC joint to reduce inflammation.
column7: Refer for surgical consultation due to the clavicle’s posterior displacement into the trapezius is correct because Rockwood Grade IV AC joint injuries involve posterior displacement of the clavicle into or through the trapezius, making surgical intervention the standard of care to restore anatomy and prevent long-term dysfunction.
column8: Initiate conservative management with rest, ice, and physical therapy is incorrect because conservative management is not appropriate for Grade IV injuries due to the clavicle’s malposition.
column9: Recommend activity modification and a sling for 2 weeks followed by gradual return to sport is incorrect because this approach is standard for Grade I and II injuries, not for posteriorly displaced Grade IV injuries.
column10: Perform a corticosteroid injection into the AC joint to reduce inflammation is incorrect because injections are not indicated for acute Grade IV separations where structural instability and displacement are present.
column12: Rockwood classification of AC joint injuries: Type I – AC ligament sprain, no displacement; Type II – AC ligament rupture, CC ligament sprain, slight clavicle elevation (<25-50% CC distance increase); Type III – Complete AC and CC ligament rupture, clavicle displaced superiorly up to 100% of normal CC distance; Type IV – Complete AC and CC rupture with posterior clavicle displacement into trapezius; Type V – Complete AC and CC rupture with >100% CC distance increase and significant soft tissue disruption (skin tenting); Type VI – Inferior clavicle displacement under coracoid or acromion, rare.

column2: A 27-year-old man falls off his bicycle and lands directly on his shoulder. He presents with localized tenderness over the acromioclavicular (AC) joint but no significant deformity or skin tenting. Radiographs show mild elevation of the distal clavicle compared to the contralateral side, with the coracoclavicular (CC) distance increased by less than 25% from normal. According to the Rockwood classification, what is the most appropriate initial management for this injury?
column3: Conservative management with rest, ice, and a brief period of immobilization followed by gradual rehabilitation.
column4: Immediate surgical fixation to restore clavicular alignment.
column5: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection to prevent chronic AC joint arthritis.
column6: No treatment is necessary as all Grade II injuries spontaneously resolve without intervention.
column7: Conservative management with rest, ice, and a brief period of immobilization followed by gradual rehabilitation is correct because Rockwood Grade II injuries involve complete AC ligament tear with partial CC ligament sprain, leading to mild clavicular elevation, and are appropriately managed conservatively with symptomatic care and rehabilitation.
column8: Immediate surgical fixation to restore clavicular alignment is incorrect because surgery is not indicated for Grade II AC separations, which are stable and managed non-operatively.
column9: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection to prevent chronic AC joint arthritis is incorrect because steroid injections are not recommended acutely and have no role in the management of acute Grade II separations.
column10: No treatment is necessary as all Grade II injuries spontaneously resolve without intervention is incorrect because treatment with symptomatic management and rehabilitation is required to restore full function.
column12: Rockwood classification of AC joint injuries: Type I – AC ligament sprain, no displacement; Type II – AC ligament rupture, CC ligament sprain, slight clavicle elevation (<25-50% CC distance increase); Type III – Complete AC and CC ligament rupture, clavicle displaced superiorly up to 100% of normal CC distance; Type IV – Complete AC and CC rupture with posterior clavicle displacement into trapezius; Type V – Complete AC and CC rupture with >100% CC distance increase and significant soft tissue disruption (skin tenting); Type VI – Inferior clavicle displacement under coracoid or acromion, rare.

column2: A 22-year-old collegiate quarterback presents after a traumatic anterior shoulder dislocation during a game. He reports the shoulder was reduced in the emergency department but now feels unstable with overhead movements, especially in abduction and external rotation. He denies any prior shoulder instability or hypermobility. MRI reveals a Bankart lesion involving the anterior inferior labrum. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column3: Refer for surgical stabilization due to traumatic unidirectional instability with a Bankart lesion.
column4: Initiate rotator cuff and periscapular strengthening with physical therapy alone.
column5: Reassure the patient and recommend no further treatment unless instability recurs.
column6: Perform an intra-articular corticosteroid injection to reduce shoulder inflammation.
column7: Refer for surgical stabilization due to traumatic unidirectional instability with a Bankart lesion is correct because young athletes with first-time traumatic anterior dislocation and a Bankart lesion are at high risk for recurrence, and early surgical stabilization reduces the likelihood of repeated instability.
column8: Initiate rotator cuff and periscapular strengthening with physical therapy alone is incorrect because conservative management has a high failure rate in young contact athletes with traumatic anterior instability and Bankart lesions.
column9: Reassure the patient and recommend no further treatment unless instability recurs is incorrect because this approach neglects the high recurrence risk and structural injury associated with Bankart lesions.
column10: Perform an intra-articular corticosteroid injection to reduce shoulder inflammation is incorrect because injections do not address the underlying labral tear and are not first-line treatment for traumatic structural instability.
column12: TUBS refers to Traumatic onset, Unidirectional instability, associated with a Bankart lesion, and typically requires Surgery. It represents the classic pattern of traumatic anterior shoulder instability, often from forceful abduction and external rotation, with a Bankart lesion of the anterior inferior labrum. According to Rockwood et al., 1996 (The Shoulder, 2nd ed.), surgery is typically recommended due to the high recurrence rate in young active patients.

column2: A 19-year-old female swimmer presents with recurrent shoulder instability, reporting that her shoulder feels like it "slips out" during overhead strokes. She denies any history of trauma. Examination reveals symmetric bilateral shoulder laxity, a positive sulcus sign, and generalized joint hypermobility. MRI shows no labral tear. What is the most appropriate initial management for this patient’s shoulder instability?
column3: Initiate a structured rehabilitation program focused on rotator cuff strengthening and scapular stabilization.
column4: Schedule the patient for surgical capsular shift as first-line treatment.
column5: Perform corticosteroid injection into the glenohumeral joint to reduce inflammation.
column6: Recommend complete activity restriction and immobilization for six weeks.
column7: Initiate a structured rehabilitation program focused on rotator cuff strengthening and scapular stabilization is correct because the AMBRI pattern (Atraumatic, Multidirectional, Bilateral, Rehabilitation first, Inferior capsular shift if surgery needed) is best managed initially with conservative treatment targeting dynamic stabilizers like the rotator cuff and scapular muscles
column8: Schedule the patient for surgical capsular shift as first-line treatment is incorrect because surgery is reserved for failed conservative management, not first-line therapy in AMBRI instability.
column9: Perform corticosteroid injection into the glenohumeral joint to reduce inflammation is incorrect because this is not indicated in atraumatic multidirectional instability without inflammatory pathology.
column10: Recommend complete activity restriction and immobilization for six weeks is incorrect because immobilization leads to stiffness and weakness, worsening multidirectional instability.
column12: AMBRI refers to Atraumatic onset, Multidirectional instability, often Bilateral, with Rehabilitation as first-line treatment, and Inferior capsular shift surgery considered only if conservative management fails. According to Braddom (4th ed., Ch. 38), AMBRI is typically seen in patients with generalized ligamentous laxity and shoulder instability during overhead activities, and initial management focuses on strengthening the rotator cuff and scapular stabilizers.

column2: A 26-year-old weightlifter reports a history of shoulder discomfort and a feeling of the joint "slipping back" during bench presses or pushing movements. He denies any major trauma. On examination, there is a positive jerk test reproducing his symptoms and a noticeable clunk as the humeral head shifts posteriorly. Imaging shows minor posterior labral changes but no frank tear. What is the most appropriate initial treatment strategy for this patient?
column3: Start a rehabilitation program targeting posterior shoulder strength and dynamic joint stability.
column4: Recommend surgical repair of the posterior labrum to prevent further instability.
column5: Use a shoulder brace to limit internal rotation and posterior translation during daily activities.
column6: Prescribe prolonged rest from all upper body exercises for at least 8 weeks before initiating treatment.
