column7: C4 and C5 medial branches is correct because in the cervical spine, each facet joint is innervated by the medial branches of the dorsal rami from the same level and the level above, so the C5–C6 facet joint receives innervation from the C4 and C5 medial branches.
column8: C5 and C6 medial branches is incorrect because the C6 branch innervates the C6–C7 joint, not the C5–C6 joint.
column9: C3 and C4 medial branches is incorrect because these innervate the C4–C5 facet joint, not C5–C6.
column10: C6 and C7 medial branches is incorrect because these target the C6–C7 and C7–T1 joints, not C5–C6.

column2: A 48-year-old woman presents with suboccipital and upper cervical pain. Physical exam reproduces pain with extension and rotation of the upper cervical spine. She experiences relief following a third occipital nerve block. Which facet joint could be the likely source of her pain?
column7: C2–C3 is correct because the third occipital nerve, a branch of the C3 dorsal ramus, innervates the C2–C3 facet joint, which is a common pain generator in cervicogenic headaches and upper cervical facet arthropathy.
column8: C1–C2 is incorrect because this joint is innervated by the suboccipital nerve (C1 dorsal ramus), not the third occipital nerve.
column9: C3–C4 is incorrect because it is innervated by the C2 and C3 medial branches, not solely the third occipital nerve.
column10: C4–C5 is incorrect because it is innervated by the C3 and C4 medial branches, unrelated to the third occipital nerve.

column2: A 52-year-old woman presents with chronic upper neck pain and occipital headaches. She localizes the pain just inferior to the occiput and describes radiation over the parietal scalp. The pain is aggravated by cervical extension and contralateral rotation. On exam, palpation just below the occiput reproduces her symptoms. Third occipital nerve block provides near-complete relief. Which facet joint is most consistent with this presentation?
column7: C2–C3 is correct because third occipital nerve–mediated C2–C3 facet pain typically presents with suboccipital pain radiating to the occiput and parietal scalp, often mimicking cervicogenic headache, and is confirmed with diagnostic relief from a third occipital nerve block.
column8: C1–C2 is incorrect because pain from this joint is typically more restricted to the upper cervical region and does not reliably refer over the parietal scalp.
column9: C3–C4 is incorrect because this joint more often causes lower neck pain without typical referral to the occiput or scalp.
column10: C4–C5 is incorrect because it is unlikely to cause occipital or scalp radiation, typically presenting as lower posterior neck pain.

column2: A 47-year-old man presents with chronic axial low back pain that worsens with sitting and forward flexion. MRI reveals a high-intensity zone at the posterior aspect of the L5–S1 disc, without nerve root compression. You suspect discogenic pain. Based on Braddom’s discussion of intervertebral disc innervation, which of the following best explains the potential pain generator?
column3: Sensory innervation of the outer disc via the sinuvertebral nerve
column4: Chemical irritation of the nucleus pulposus due to its dense sensory innervation
column5: Uniform innervation of the intervertebral disc by the dorsal root ganglion at each level
column6: Sympathetic chain fibers supplying pain sensation to the nucleus pulposus
column7: Sensory innervation of the outer disc via the sinuvertebral nerve is correct because Braddom confirms that the outer third of the disc—particularly the posterior portion—is innervated by the sinuvertebral nerve and capable of generating pain when disrupted or inflamed, as seen in high-intensity zones.
column8: Chemical irritation of the nucleus pulposus due to its dense sensory innervation is incorrect because the nucleus pulposus is normally aneural and only becomes symptomatic when it leaks into innervated areas.
column9: Uniform innervation of the intervertebral disc by the dorsal root ganglion at each level is incorrect because disc innervation is segmental and limited, not uniform or direct from the dorsal root ganglia.
column10: Sympathetic chain fibers supplying pain sensation to the nucleus pulposus is incorrect because the sympathetic chain does not innervate the nucleus pulposus directly.

column2: A 64-year-old woman presents with worsening axial low back pain. MRI reveals disc height loss at L4–L5 with vacuum disc phenomenon and facet joint hypertrophy. According to the three-joint complex model, which of the following best explains how intervertebral disc degeneration contributes to facet-mediated pathology?
column3: Reduced anterior column support shifts compressive loading to the facet joints, leading to reactive hypertrophy
column4: Narrowed disc height reduces intradiscal pressure, thereby decompressing the posterior elements
column5: Desiccated discs decrease sagittal motion, protecting posterior structures from dynamic overload
column6: Vacuum phenomenon decompresses the motion segment, offloading both anterior and posterior structures
column7: Reduced anterior column support shifts compressive loading to the facet joints, leading to reactive hypertrophy is correct because disc height loss decreases anterior axial load-bearing, causing compensatory load transfer to the facet joints, which accelerates degeneration via increased mechanical stress.
column8: Narrowed disc height reduces intradiscal pressure, thereby decompressing the posterior elements is incorrect because although pressure decreases, mechanical stress is redistributed to the posterior elements, not away from them.
column9: Desiccated discs decrease sagittal motion, protecting posterior structures from dynamic overload is incorrect because while dehydration may reduce elasticity, it often leads to abnormal segmental motion and greater facet loading due to instability.
column10: Vacuum phenomenon decompresses the motion segment, offloading both anterior and posterior structures is incorrect because vacuum changes are a sign of instability, not decompression, and typically coincide with increased stress on posterior joints.

column2: A 65-year-old man is undergoing a lumbar interlaminar epidural steroid injection at the L4–L5 level using a midline approach. To accurately enter the epidural space, the needle must pass through several distinct structures. Which of the following correctly lists the order of structures encountered during this approach?
column3: Skin → subcutaneous tissue → supraspinous ligament → interspinous ligament → ligamentum flavum → epidural space
column4: Skin → subcutaneous tissue → interspinous ligament → supraspinous ligament → ligamentum flavum → epidural space
column5: Skin → supraspinous ligament → interspinous ligament → subcutaneous tissue → ligamentum flavum → epidural space
column6: Skin → subcutaneous tissue → ligamentum flavum → interspinous ligament → supraspinous ligament → epidural space
column7: Skin → subcutaneous tissue → supraspinous ligament → interspinous ligament → ligamentum flavum → epidural space is correct because this is the true sequential order of structures encountered during a midline lumbar epidural approach, proceeding from superficial to deep.
column8: Skin → subcutaneous tissue → interspinous ligament → supraspinous ligament → ligamentum flavum → epidural space is incorrect because the supraspinous ligament is superficial to the interspinous ligament and must be encountered first.
column9: Skin → supraspinous ligament → interspinous ligament → subcutaneous tissue → ligamentum flavum → epidural space is incorrect because the subcutaneous tissue is superficial to both ligaments and must be traversed immediately after the skin.
column10: Skin → subcutaneous tissue → ligamentum flavum → interspinous ligament → supraspinous ligament → epidural space is incorrect because both the supraspinous and interspinous ligaments must be passed before reaching the ligamentum flavum.

column2: A 50-year-old man undergoes lumbar spine MRI for radicular pain. Imaging reveals disc material that has broken through the annulus fibrosus and posterior longitudinal ligament and lies free in the spinal canal, no longer connected to the parent disc. Which of the following best describes this type of herniation?
column7: Sequestered disc is correct because a sequestered disc occurs when nuclear material extends through the annulus and posterior longitudinal ligament and is no longer continuous with the originating disc.
column8: Extruded disc is incorrect because although the nucleus pulposus has broken through the annulus, it remains connected to the parent disc.
column9: Prolapsed disc is incorrect because a prolapse refers to focal protrusion of disc material without rupture of the outer annulus.
column10: Bulging disc is incorrect because bulging refers to circumferential disc extension beyond the endplates without focal rupture.

column2: A 62-year-old woman undergoes lumbar MRI for chronic axial low back pain without radiculopathy. Imaging shows circumferential extension of the disc beyond the vertebral endplates without focal rupture. Which of the following best describes this finding?
column7: Bulging disc is correct because a bulging disc involves circumferential, symmetric extension of disc material beyond the vertebral margins without disruption of the annulus fibrosus.
column8: Extruded disc is incorrect because it involves focal herniation with loss of continuity in the annulus and extension of nuclear material beyond the disc space.
column9: Prolapsed disc is incorrect because it refers to focal displacement of disc material through the inner annulus but without full-thickness annular disruption.
column10: Sequestered disc is incorrect because it involves disc material that has broken off and migrated away from the parent disc.

column2: A 54-year-old man presents with intermittent radicular pain during lumbar flexion. MRI reveals focal herniation of disc material that has displaced through the inner annulus but remains contained by the outer annulus. Which of the following best describes this disc pathology?
column7: Prolapsed disc is correct because it refers to focal displacement of nucleus pulposus that breaches the inner annulus but remains contained by the outer annulus fibrosus.
column8: Sequestered disc is incorrect because it involves free disc fragments no longer connected to the parent disc.
column9: Extruded disc is incorrect because the nucleus pulposus in extrusion extends beyond the annulus with a wide neck or loss of containment.
column10: Bulging disc is incorrect because it involves circumferential disc expansion without focal herniation.

column2: A 59-year-old man presents with acute-onset radicular pain. MRI shows a focal herniation where disc material has extended beyond the annulus fibrosus and has a narrower connection to the parent disc than its extruded portion. Which of the following best describes this finding?
column7: Extruded disc is correct because an extruded disc occurs when nucleus pulposus extends beyond the annulus fibrosus and the neck is narrower than the herniated portion, though still connected to the disc.
column8: Bulging disc is incorrect because it is circumferential and symmetric without rupture of the annulus.
column9: Prolapsed disc is incorrect because it refers to a focal displacement through the inner annulus that remains contained by outer annular fibers.
column10: Sequestered disc is incorrect because the disc fragment is no longer attached to the parent disc.

column2: A 67-year-old man presents with bilateral lower extremity weakness and numbness that is worse when standing. MRI of the lumbar spine shows a central disc herniation at the L4–L5 level compressing the traversing nerve roots. Which nerve root is most likely affected?
column7: L5 is correct because a central disc herniation at L4–L5 compresses the traversing L5 nerve roots within the spinal canal.
column8: L4 is incorrect because it is the exiting nerve root at L4–L5 and lies more lateral.
column9: S1 is incorrect because it traverses the L5–S1 level, not L4–L5.
column10: L3 is incorrect because it exits the spine two levels above the herniation and is not in the canal at that level.

column2: A 55-year-old woman presents with left-sided leg pain and foot dorsiflexion weakness. MRI shows a posterolateral disc herniation at L4–L5. Which nerve root is most likely being impinged?
column7: L5 is correct because posterolateral herniations at L4–L5 typically compress the traversing L5 nerve root within the lateral recess.
column8: L4 is incorrect because it exits above the herniation and is usually not affected by a posterolateral disc at this level.
column9: S1 is incorrect because it traverses at L5–S1 and is not at risk at L4–L5.
column10: L3 is incorrect because it exits the spine more proximally and is not in proximity to an L4–L5 herniation.

column2: A 60-year-old man has isolated right anterior thigh pain and mild quadriceps weakness. MRI shows a foraminal disc herniation at L3–L4 compressing the exiting nerve root. Which nerve root is most likely affected?
column7: L3 is correct because a far lateral herniation at L3–L4 compresses the exiting L3 nerve root, which lies in the neural foramen.
column8: L4 is incorrect because it traverses the L3–L4 disc level and is affected only by more central or posterolateral herniations.
column9: L5 is incorrect because it is located further down in the spinal canal and not in the L3–L4 foramen.
column10: L2 is incorrect because it exits above the L3–L4 level and is not near the herniation.

column2: A 45-year-old man presents with right-sided C7 radicular pain. MRI reveals foraminal narrowing at C6–C7. He is referred for mechanical cervical traction. Which of the following cervical traction parameters is most appropriate to target the lower cervical nerve roots?
column3: 25 degrees of cervical flexion with 25 pounds of traction force
column4: 10 degrees of cervical flexion with 10 pounds of traction force
column5: 25 degrees of cervical extension with 25 pounds of traction force
column6: 10 degrees of cervical extension with 10 pounds of traction force
column7: 25 degrees of cervical flexion with 25 pounds of traction force is correct because this position opens the intervertebral foramina in the lower cervical spine and applies a therapeutic traction force (~7–10% body weight) to relieve nerve root compression.
column8: 10 degrees of cervical flexion with 10 pounds of traction force is incorrect because minimal flexion fails to adequately target the lower cervical levels, and 10 pounds is generally insufficient for therapeutic distraction in adults.
column9: 25 degrees of cervical extension with 25 pounds of traction force is incorrect because extension narrows the neural foramina and increases posterior element compression, worsening radicular symptoms.
column10: 10 degrees of cervical extension with 10 pounds of traction force is incorrect because both the position and force are suboptimal for decompressing the lower cervical spine and may aggravate foraminal narrowing.

column2: A 51-year-old man with lumbar radiculopathy due to a contained disc herniation is being evaluated for non-surgical intervention. He asks about historical options no longer widely used. Which of the following best describes the mechanism and clinical concerns associated with chymopapain injection?
column3: Chymopapain enzymatically digests nucleus pulposus but was discontinued due to severe anaphylactic reactions
column4: Chymopapain promotes disc hydration and was withdrawn for lack of efficacy
column5: Chymopapain reduces inflammation at nerve roots and is still commonly used in select cases
column6: Chymopapain mechanically disrupts the annulus and is preferred in extruded disc fragments
column7: Chymopapain enzymatically digests nucleus pulposus but was discontinued due to severe anaphylactic reactions is correct because chymopapain was a proteolytic enzyme injected into herniated discs to dissolve nucleus pulposus but fell out of favor due to risks including life-threatening hypersensitivity.
column8: Chymopapain promotes disc hydration and was withdrawn for lack of efficacy is incorrect because chymopapain dehydrates the nucleus via enzymatic breakdown rather than promoting hydration.
column9: Chymopapain reduces inflammation at nerve roots and is still commonly used in select cases is incorrect because chymopapain is no longer used clinically due to safety concerns.
column10: Chymopapain mechanically disrupts the annulus and is preferred in extruded disc fragments is incorrect because it acts enzymatically on contained herniations and is not used for extruded or sequestered discs.

column2: A 46-year-old man with chronic low back pain undergoes discography at L4–L5. Imaging reveals radial fissures extending from the nucleus pulposus to the outer third of the annulus fibrosus with concordant pain reproduction. Based on internal disc disruption (IDD) grading, how should this lesion be classified?
column7: Grade 3 is correct because Grade 3 internal disc disruption is defined by radial fissures that extend through all three zones of the annulus fibrosus, consistent with the fissures reaching the outer third and reproducing concordant pain during discography.
column8: Grade 1 is incorrect because it involves concentric tears within the inner annulus without radial extension.
column9: Grade 2 is incorrect because it includes radial fissures limited to the middle third of the annulus and does not reach the outer third.
column10: Grade 4 is incorrect because there is no officially recognized Grade 4 in the commonly used grading systems for internal disc disruption.

column2: A 68-year-old man reports progressive bilateral calf and buttock pain that occurs after walking several blocks. He states that the pain improves with sitting and leaning forward, but standing still provides little relief. He denies rest pain. On exam, his dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ bilaterally, and he has mild sensory loss over the lateral thighs. Treadmill testing shows earlier symptom onset with walking downhill than uphill. Which of the following features most strongly supports a neurogenic rather than vascular source of claudication?
column3: Earlier symptom onset with downhill ambulation
column4: Absence of rest pain and symmetric distal pulses
column5: Presence of bilateral calf and buttock pain
column6: Mild sensory loss over the lateral thighs
column7: Earlier symptom onset with downhill ambulation is correct because walking downhill places the lumbar spine in extension, narrowing the spinal canal and provoking symptoms in neurogenic claudication; this pattern is not typical of vascular disease.
column8: Absence of rest pain and symmetric distal pulses is incorrect because these findings are supportive but not specific enough to distinguish neurogenic from mild vascular claudication.
column9: Presence of bilateral calf and buttock pain is incorrect because both neurogenic and vascular claudication can cause bilateral leg pain, especially with more proximal stenosis or aortoiliac disease.
column10: Mild sensory loss over the lateral thighs is incorrect because while it may suggest a neurologic component, it could also reflect unrelated meralgia paresthetica and does not localize specifically to spinal stenosis.

column2: A 72-year-old man presents with bilateral calf pain after walking 2–3 blocks. The pain improves with sitting but not while standing still. He also notes numbness in his feet and occasional difficulty initiating urination. On physical exam, he has 2+ dorsalis pedis pulses bilaterally and no skin changes. Ankle-brachial index (ABI) is 1.05 at rest and 1.00 after exercise. Which of the following findings most strongly supports a diagnosis of neurogenic claudication?
column3: Normal resting and post-exercise ABI despite exertional leg symptoms
column4: Symmetrical distal pulses and lack of skin breakdown
column5: Bilateral calf pain that improves with sitting
column6: Absence of foot pallor or dependent rubor
column7: Normal resting and post-exercise ABI despite exertional leg symptoms is correct because a normal or near-normal post-exercise ABI in the setting of exertional leg pain argues strongly against peripheral arterial disease and supports neurogenic claudication.
column8: Symmetrical distal pulses and lack of skin breakdown is incorrect because these findings are nonspecific and can be seen in early or moderate vascular disease as well as neurogenic claudication.
column9: Bilateral calf pain that improves with sitting is incorrect because both vascular and neurogenic claudication can improve with sitting, though flexion helps in neurogenic claudication.
column10: Absence of foot pallor or dependent rubor is incorrect because these signs suggest vascular insufficiency only in advanced disease, and their absence does not definitively distinguish between the two causes.

column2: A 16-year-old male gymnast presents with several months of low back pain worsened by hyperextension activities. Lumbar spine imaging reveals bilateral pars interarticularis defects at L5 without vertebral slippage. Neurologic exam is normal. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment?
column3: Activity modification and physical therapy focused on core stabilization
column4: Immediate surgical referral for posterior fusion
column5: Lumbar extension strengthening exercises and return to full sport
column7: Activity modification and physical therapy focused on core stabilization is correct because initial treatment for spondylolysis in adolescents without spondylolisthesis includes rest from aggravating activities and PT to strengthen core and avoid extension loading.
column8: Immediate surgical referral for posterior fusion is incorrect because surgery is reserved for cases with instability, neurologic deficits, or failed conservative treatment.
column9: Lumbar extension strengthening exercises and return to full sport is incorrect because early extension loading may delay healing of the pars defect and worsen symptoms.
column10: Epidural steroid injection at L5–S1 is incorrect because this targets nerve root inflammation, not mechanical stress at the pars, and is not first-line therapy for isolated spondylolysis.

column2: A 14-year-old girl presents with low back pain and lumbar spine radiographs reveal anterior displacement of the L5 vertebral body over S1. The displacement measures approximately 35% of the width of the S1 vertebral body. Based on the standard grading system for spondylolisthesis, how should this be classified?
column7: Grade II is correct because a slip measuring 26%–50% of the vertebral body width is classified as Grade II spondylolisthesis, which includes 35%.
column8: Grade I is incorrect because it includes only 0%–25% anterior translation.
column9: Grade III is incorrect because it includes slips between 51%–75%.
column10: Grade IV is incorrect because it refers to slippage of 76%–100%.

column2: A 16-year-old female soccer player has been treated conservatively for 8 months for symptomatic isthmic spondylolisthesis. Follow-up imaging now shows 60% anterior displacement of L5 on S1. She continues to have pain with activity despite adherence to physical therapy, bracing, and cessation of sports. Neurologic exam is normal. Based on current guidelines, what is the most appropriate next step?
column3: Referral for surgical evaluation due to high-grade slippage and failed conservative management
column4: Continue non-operative management since neurologic exam is normal
column5: Prescribe NSAIDs and resume sport with close monitoring
column6: Begin lumbar extension strengthening and discontinue bracing
column7: Referral for surgical evaluation due to high-grade slippage and failed conservative management is correct because slippage >50% (Grade III or higher) with persistent symptoms after adequate conservative therapy is an indication for surgical referral, even in the absence of neurologic deficits.
column8: Continue non-operative management since neurologic exam is normal is incorrect because persistent symptoms with high-grade slippage are an accepted surgical indication, regardless of neurologic findings.
column9: Prescribe NSAIDs and resume sport with close monitoring is incorrect because returning to sport with ongoing symptoms and instability risks progression and is not appropriate in high-grade slips.
column10: Begin lumbar extension strengthening and discontinue bracing is incorrect because extension exercises may worsen symptoms in spondylolytic spondylolisthesis, especially at higher grades.

column2: A 13-year-old girl is evaluated for scoliosis. Standing PA radiograph reveals a right thoracic spinal curve. To quantify the curve, you decide to measure the Cobb angle. Which of the following best describes the correct method for Cobb angle measurement?
column3: Draw lines along the superior endplate of the uppermost tilted vertebra and the inferior endplate of the lowermost tilted vertebra, then measure the angle where perpendiculars to these lines intersect
column4: Draw lines parallel to the spinous processes above and below the apex of the curve and measure the acute angle formed
column5: Connect the vertebral bodies at the apex of the curve and base of the curve and measure the angle at the intersection
column6: Measure the angle between the transverse processes of the upper and lower end vertebrae of the curve
column7: Draw lines along the superior endplate of the uppermost tilted vertebra and the inferior endplate of the lowermost tilted vertebra, then measure the angle where perpendiculars to these lines intersect is correct because this is the standard method for Cobb angle measurement used to quantify scoliosis on radiographs.
column8: Draw lines parallel to the spinous processes above and below the apex of the curve and measure the acute angle formed is incorrect because Cobb angle is based on endplates, not spinous processes.
column9: Connect the vertebral bodies at the apex of the curve and base of the curve and measure the angle at the intersection is incorrect because this approach does not account for the tilt of the most angulated vertebrae.
column10: Measure the angle between the transverse processes of the upper and lower end vertebrae of the curve is incorrect because transverse processes are not used in Cobb angle measurement and would yield inaccurate results.

column2: A 12-year-old girl is referred for scoliosis evaluation. Standing PA spinal radiographs reveal a 28-degree right thoracic curve. She is premenarchal and has Risser stage 1. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
column5: Observe and repeat imaging in 12 months
column6: Discharge from follow-up as the curve is mild
column7: Recommend thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) bracing is correct because patients with curves between 25–40 degrees, open growth plates (e.g., Risser 0–2), and remaining skeletal immaturity are candidates for bracing to prevent curve progression.
column8: Schedule surgical consultation is incorrect because surgery is typically reserved for curves >45–50 degrees or failed bracing.
column9: Observe and repeat imaging in 12 months is incorrect because the curve is already in the moderate range, and the patient is at high risk for progression due to skeletal immaturity.
column10: Discharge from follow-up as the curve is mild is incorrect because 28 degrees is not considered mild, and continued follow-up or bracing is indicated in skeletally immature patients.

column2: A 15-year-old male presents with mid-thoracic back pain and worsening posture. Physical exam reveals exaggerated thoracic kyphosis that does not correct with forward flexion. Standing lateral spine radiograph shows anterior wedging greater than 5 degrees in at least three adjacent vertebrae and irregularity of vertebral endplates. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Scheuermann's disease is correct because this adolescent presents with rigid thoracic hyperkyphosis, pain, and radiographic findings of ≥3 vertebrae with >5° anterior wedging, consistent with the diagnostic criteria for Scheuermann’s disease also known as juvinile kyphosis.
column8: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis is incorrect because scoliosis involves lateral curvature of the spine, typically without anterior vertebral wedging or rigid kyphosis.
column9: Postural kyphosis is incorrect because it is flexible and corrects with voluntary movement, unlike the rigid deformity seen in Scheuermann's.
column10: Ankylosing spondylitis is incorrect because it is typically associated with sacroiliitis and stiffness, not localized thoracic vertebral wedging in adolescents.

column2: A 76-year-old woman presents with acute mid-back pain following a minor fall. Imaging reveals a compression fracture at T10 involving only the posterior third of the vertebral body, with subtle retropulsion of bone into the spinal canal. According to Denis’ three-column model, how is this fracture best classified?
column3: Potentially unstable due to isolated middle column involvement
column4: Stable fracture because the anterior and posterior columns are intact
column5: Unstable fracture due to disruption of the posterior elements
column7: Potentially unstable due to isolated middle column involvement is correct because according to Denis’ three-column model, the middle column is key to spinal stability; injury to this column alone, especially with retropulsion, may render the spine unstable.
column8: Stable fracture because the anterior and posterior columns are intact is incorrect because injury to the middle column alone may compromise stability.
column9: Unstable fracture due to disruption of the posterior elements is incorrect because the posterior elements (spinous process, lamina, and ligaments) are not involved; the injury is limited to the middle column.
column10: Stable wedge compression fracture is incorrect because wedge fractures affect the anterior column, whereas this case involves the posterior third of the vertebral body.

column2: A 68-year-old man with osteoporosis presents with acute mid-back pain after lifting a heavy object. Imaging shows a wedge-shaped compression fracture at T8 involving only the anterior third of the vertebral body. The posterior wall is intact, with no retropulsion, and the posterior elements are unremarkable. According to Denis’ three-column model, how is this fracture best classified?
column3: Stable fracture involving only the anterior column
column4: Unstable fracture because anterior column height loss may lead to kyphotic deformity
column5: Unstable fracture due to microinstability of the middle column
column6: Stable fracture because there is no spinal cord compression
column7: Stable fracture involving only the anterior column is correct because the Denis three-column model defines stability based on how many columns are disrupted; isolated anterior column injuries, like wedge compression fractures, are considered stable when the middle and posterior columns remain intact.
column8: Unstable fracture because anterior column height loss may lead to kyphotic deformity is incorrect because deformity risk does not determine acute mechanical stability; the fracture is stable if only the anterior column is involved.
column9: Unstable fracture due to microinstability of the middle column is incorrect because the middle column is not disrupted on imaging; microinstability is not supported by the three-column model without structural failure.
column10: Stable fracture because there is no spinal cord compression is incorrect because neurologic involvement is not required to determine stability; column integrity is the primary determinant in the Denis model.

column2: A 40-year-old man presents after a high-speed motor vehicle crash in which he was wearing a lap-only seatbelt. He complains of severe lower back pain but is neurologically intact. Imaging reveals horizontal fractures through the spinous process, lamina, and pedicles of L2, along with widening of the interspinous space. The anterior vertebral body is preserved. According to Denis’ three-column model, how is this fracture best classified?
column3: Unstable fracture due to posterior and middle column disruption
column4: Stable fracture because the anterior vertebral body is preserved
column5: Unstable fracture due to anterior and middle column injury
column6: Stable fracture because there is no spinal cord or nerve root injury
column7: Unstable fracture due to posterior and middle column disruption is correct because, under the Denis three-column model, injuries disrupting two columns are unstable; this pattern is consistent with a flexion-distraction mechanism (Chance fracture), in which the posterior and middle columns fail in tension.
column8: Stable fracture because the anterior vertebral body is preserved is incorrect because the integrity of the anterior column alone does not ensure spinal stability when the posterior and middle columns are clearly disrupted.
column9: Unstable fracture due to anterior and middle column injury is incorrect because this fracture pattern involves posterior and middle column failure, not the anterior column.
column10: Stable fracture because there is no spinal cord or nerve root injury is incorrect because mechanical stability is based on column involvement, not neurologic findings.

column2: A 72-year-old woman with osteoporosis undergoes lateral spine radiographs after reporting mid-back pain. Imaging reveals a T11 vertebral body deformity with anterior height loss of 30%, preservation of posterior height, and no cortical disruption. According to the Genant semiquantitative grading system, how should this deformity be classified?
column7: Grade 2 (moderate) wedge deformity is correct because a 30% anterior height loss falls between 25–40%, consistent with a moderate deformity according to the Genant grading scale.
column8: Grade 1 (mild) wedge deformity is incorrect because mild deformities involve 20–25% height loss; this case exceeds that range.
column9: Grade 3 (severe) biconcave deformity is incorrect because severe deformities require >40% height loss and this case is wedge-shaped, not biconcave.
column10: Normal vertebral body height is incorrect because anterior height is clearly reduced by 30%, which exceeds the threshold for normal.
column12: The Genant semiquantitative grading system classifies vertebral deformities based on the percentage of height loss (anterior, middle, or posterior) as Grade 0 (normal), Grade 1 (mild, 20–25%), Grade 2 (moderate, >25–40%), and Grade 3 (severe, >40%), using morphologic patterns (wedge, biconcave, or crush) to describe the shape of the deformity.

column2: A 75-year-old man with known osteoporosis presents after a ground-level fall. Lateral spine radiograph reveals a T12 vertebral deformity with 42% loss of both anterior and middle vertebral height, while posterior height is preserved. There is no step-off or cortical break. According to the Genant semiquantitative method, how should this deformity be graded?
column7: Grade 3 (severe) wedge deformity is correct because the Genant grading system defines severe vertebral deformities as >40% height loss in any region; with anterior and middle height reduced >40% and preserved posterior height, this represents a severe wedge deformity.
column8: Grade 2 (moderate) biconcave deformity is incorrect because moderate deformities have 25–40% height loss, and this is >40%; also, the pattern is wedge-shaped (anterior/middle loss) not centrally concave.
column9: Grade 1 (mild) wedge deformity is incorrect because mild deformities are limited to 20–25% height loss, which this case exceeds.
column10: Normal vertebral body height is incorrect because the 42% loss of anterior and middle height exceeds normal limits and clearly represents a fracture deformity.
column12: The Genant semiquantitative grading system classifies vertebral deformities based on the percentage of height loss (anterior, middle, or posterior) as Grade 0 (normal), Grade 1 (mild, 20–25%), Grade 2 (moderate, >25–40%), and Grade 3 (severe, >40%), using morphologic patterns (wedge, biconcave, or crush) to describe the shape of the deformity.

column2: A 70-year-old woman with known osteopenia undergoes routine lateral spine imaging. Radiographs show a T9 vertebral body with 22% anterior height loss, preserved middle and posterior height, and no cortical break or endplate step-off. She denies trauma or back pain. According to the Genant semiquantitative grading system, how should this vertebral deformity be classified?
column7: Grade 1 (mild) is correct because the Genant semiquantitative grading system defines Grade 1 deformities as involving 20–25% height loss in one region (anterior, middle, or posterior) with preserved structure otherwise, which matches the 22% anterior height loss described.
column8: Grade 0 (normal) is incorrect because height loss >20% exceeds the threshold for normal vertebral morphology.
column9: Grade 2 (moderate) is incorrect because moderate deformities require height loss greater than 25% and up to 40%.
column10: Grade 3 (severe) is incorrect because severe deformities involve >40% height loss, which is not present here.
column12: The Genant semiquantitative grading system classifies vertebral deformities based on the percentage of height loss (anterior, middle, or posterior) as Grade 0 (normal), Grade 1 (mild, 20–25%), Grade 2 (moderate, >25–40%), and Grade 3 (severe, >40%), using morphologic patterns (wedge, biconcave, or crush) to describe the shape of the deformity.

column2: An 82-year-old woman with osteoporosis presents with acute mid-thoracic pain after a ground-level fall. Imaging reveals a stable wedge compression fracture at T7 involving only the anterior column, with 25% anterior height loss, intact posterior vertebral wall, and no retropulsion. She is neurologically intact, ambulatory with assistance, and has well-controlled pain with oral medications. What is the most appropriate initial management?
column3: Conservative treatment with bracing, analgesia, and gradual mobilization
column4: Urgent surgical stabilization due to risk of neurologic deterioration
column5: Percutaneous vertebroplasty within 48 hours to prevent deformity
column6: Bed rest until complete radiographic healing is observed
column7: Conservative treatment with bracing, analgesia, and gradual mobilization is correct because this fracture represents a stable wedge deformity involving only the anterior column, consistent with a Genant Grade 2 (moderate) deformity (25% height loss); the absence of neurologic compromise, retropulsion, or instability supports nonoperative treatment.
column8: Urgent surgical stabilization due to risk of neurologic deterioration is incorrect because there is no evidence of posterior element injury or canal compromise requiring surgery.
column9: Percutaneous vertebroplasty within 48 hours to prevent deformity is incorrect because vertebroplasty is not indicated in a stable Genant Grade 2 fracture with manageable pain.
column10: Bed rest until complete radiographic healing is observed is incorrect because prolonged bed rest increases morbidity and is not recommended in stable vertebral compression fractures.
column12: The Genant semiquantitative grading system classifies vertebral deformities based on the percentage of height loss (anterior, middle, or posterior) as Grade 0 (normal), Grade 1 (mild, 20–25%), Grade 2 (moderate, >25–40%), and Grade 3 (severe, >40%), using morphologic patterns (wedge, biconcave, or crush) to describe the shape of the deformity.

column2: A pain physician is planning a lumbar medial branch radiofrequency ablation for a patient with bilateral facetogenic pain at L4–L5 and L5–S1. Which medial branches must be targeted on each side to effectively denervate these two joints?
column3: L3, L4, and L5 medial branches
column4: L4, L5, and S1 medial branches
column5: Only L4 and L5 medial branches
column6: L2, L3, and L4 medial branches
column7: L3, L4, and L5 medial branches is correct because each lumbar facet joint is innervated by the medial branches from the dorsal rami at the same level and the level above; thus, L4–L5 is innervated by L3 and L4, and L5–S1 is innervated by L4 and L5 medial branches.
column8: L4, L5, and S1 medial branches is incorrect because the S1 medial branch does not exist; the L5–S1 facet is innervated by the L4 and L5 medial branches, not S1.
column9: Only L4 and L5 medial branches is incorrect because L3 must also be ablated to denervate the L4–L5 joint, which receives dual innervation.
column10: L2, L3, and L4 medial branches is incorrect because these branches do not innervate the L5–S1 facet joint.

column2: A 58-year-old man presents with unilateral low back pain localized near the posterior superior iliac spine. The pain worsens with standing and improves with sitting. Provocative maneuvers including FABER, Gaenslen, and thigh thrust are positive. Neurologic exam and lumbar MRI are unremarkable. What is the most appropriate next step to confirm sacroiliac (SI) joint–mediated pain?
column3: Perform a fluoroscopically guided intra-articular SI joint block with local anesthetic
column4: Order MRI of the sacroiliac joints to identify erosions or sclerosis
column5: Refer for radiofrequency ablation of the lateral sacral branches
column6: Order CT of the sacroiliac joints to assess for bony abnormalities
column7: Perform a fluoroscopically guided intra-articular SI joint block with local anesthetic is correct because the gold standard for confirming SI joint–mediated pain is ≥75% relief following a precisely placed diagnostic injection under image guidance.
column8: Order MRI of the sacroiliac joints to identify erosions or sclerosis is incorrect because imaging has low sensitivity for mechanical SI joint pain and is more useful for inflammatory arthropathy.
column9: Refer for radiofrequency ablation of the lateral sacral branches is incorrect because RFA is a treatment reserved for cases with confirmed SI joint–mediated pain based on block response.
column10: Order CT of the sacroiliac joints to assess for bony abnormalities is incorrect because CT may show degenerative changes, but these often do not correlate with pain and are not diagnostic for SI joint dysfunction.

column2: A 67-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled diabetes presents with worsening back pain and low-grade fevers over several weeks. He denies recent trauma. MRI of the lumbar spine shows increased T2 signal and enhancement in the L3 vertebral body and adjacent disc space. Blood cultures are pending. Based on current epidemiology, which organism is most likely responsible for his condition?
column7: Staphylococcus aureus is correct because it is the most common cause of vertebral body osteomyelitis in adults, particularly in hematogenous spread associated with chronic conditions such as diabetes.
column8: Escherichia coli is incorrect because although it can cause vertebral osteomyelitis, it is more often associated with urinary tract source or immunosuppression and is far less common than Staphylococcus aureus.
column9: Mycobacterium tuberculosis is incorrect because it causes Pott disease, which is less common in developed countries and presents more insidiously.
column10: Pseudomonas aeruginosa is incorrect because it is most often associated with IV drug use or direct inoculation, not the typical community-acquired hematogenous spread in diabetics.

column2: A 62-year-old man with diabetes presents with worsening lumbar pain and low-grade fevers. MRI of the lumbar spine is ordered to evaluate for vertebral osteomyelitis. Which MRI finding is most characteristic of acute vertebral osteomyelitis?
column3: Low signal intensity on T1 and high signal intensity on T2 within the vertebral body
column4: High signal intensity on both T1 and T2 within the vertebral body
column5: Low signal intensity on T2 and high signal intensity on T1 within the intervertebral disc
column6: Normal T1 and T2 signal with isolated endplate irregularity
column7: Low signal intensity on T1 and high signal intensity on T2 within the vertebral body is correct because this is the classic pattern seen in acute vertebral osteomyelitis due to marrow edema and inflammation.
column8: High signal intensity on both T1 and T2 within the vertebral body is incorrect because marrow edema typically causes low T1 and high T2 signal, not increased signal on both sequences.
column9: Low signal intensity on T2 and high signal intensity on T1 within the intervertebral disc is incorrect because these findings are not consistent with infection and suggest chronic degeneration or calcification.
column10: Normal T1 and T2 signal with isolated endplate irregularity is incorrect because vertebral osteomyelitis usually alters marrow signal and often affects both the endplate and adjacent disc.
column12: T1-weighted MRI sequences provide detailed anatomic images and show fat as bright and fluid as dark, making them useful for evaluating normal marrow and structural detail. T2-weighted sequences highlight fluid and inflammation, showing fluid and edema as bright, which helps detect pathology like infection or tumors.

column2: A 42-year-old man presents with disabling low back pain for 9 months following a motor vehicle accident. On exam, he visibly flinches and withdraws when you gently move your palm across his lumbar skin without applying pressure. However, he does not react when firm pressure is applied to the paraspinal muscles during palpation. What is the best interpretation of this finding?
column7: Superficial tenderness is correct because this Waddell sign is defined as pain elicited by light touch or superficial palpation over a wide area that is not consistent with known anatomy or deep tissue irritation.
column8: Overreaction is incorrect because it refers to exaggerated facial expressions, verbalizations, or other behaviors during the exam, not pain to light touch.
column9: Regional disturbances is incorrect because that Waddell sign involves non-anatomic or inconsistent motor or sensory deficits.
column10: Simulation is incorrect because this Waddell sign involves maneuvers that mimic spinal stress without actually applying force, such as axial loading.

column2: A 37-year-old warehouse worker with chronic low back pain reports severe lumbar discomfort with all bending and lifting activities. During physical exam, you gently press down on the top of his head while he is standing. He reports sharp low back pain. No neurologic deficits are present. What Waddell sign does this maneuver best represent?
column7: Simulation is correct because this Waddell sign involves responses to maneuvers that should not cause back pain, such as axial loading or en bloc trunk rotation, which simulate but do not stress the lumbar spine.
column8: Superficial tenderness is incorrect because that sign involves pain from light touch over a wide superficial area of the back.
column9: Distraction is incorrect because it refers to differing responses between focused and distracted exam maneuvers.
column10: Regional disturbances is incorrect because it involves non-dermatomal or diffuse weakness or sensory loss that does not follow neurologic patterns.

column2: A 49-year-old man with low back pain winces and stops you from raising his leg beyond 30 degrees during a formal supine straight leg raise test. However, minutes later, while seated and putting on his shoes, he fully extends the same leg without apparent discomfort. Which Waddell sign is demonstrated?
column7: Distraction is correct because this Waddell sign refers to inconsistency in pain response between formal tests and functionally equivalent movements when the patient is unaware, such as seated vs supine straight leg raise.
column8: Simulation is incorrect because it involves maneuvers like axial loading or trunk rotation that simulate lumbar loading without actually stressing the spine.
column9: Overreaction is incorrect because the patient does not display excessive emotional or behavioral responses during the exam.
column10: Superficial tenderness is incorrect because this sign requires pain to light touch rather than movement-based tests.

column2: A 45-year-old man with chronic low back pain shows "give-way" weakness in the right lower extremity during manual muscle testing. However, the weakness is inconsistent and not confined to any single myotome. Sensation is decreased throughout the entire leg in a stocking-glove pattern. These findings are best classified as which Waddell sign?
column7: Regional disturbances is correct because this Waddell sign includes motor or sensory findings that do not conform to known neuroanatomic distributions, such as diffuse weakness or non-dermatomal sensory loss.
column8: Simulation is incorrect because no simulated spinal stress maneuver was performed in this scenario.
column9: Distraction is incorrect because there is no evidence of changing responses between distracted and focused testing.
column10: Overreaction is incorrect because no exaggerated emotional or behavioral responses were observed during the exam.

column2: A 40-year-old man being evaluated for back pain frequently moans, breathes heavily, and grips the exam table tightly during standard lumbar range-of-motion testing. He demonstrates exaggerated facial expressions and resists all movement with minimal provocation, though no objective deficits are found. These behaviors are most consistent with which Waddell sign?
column7: Overreaction is correct because this Waddell sign is defined as an excessive or disproportionate emotional response (such as grimacing, verbal outbursts, or dramatic movements) during routine physical examination.
column8: Distraction is incorrect because no comparison between distracted and formal testing was made.
column9: Superficial tenderness is incorrect because the behavior was not in response to light touch or superficial palpation.
column10: Simulation is incorrect because no axial loading or other non-physiologic maneuver was used to provoke symptoms.

column2: A 66-year-old woman with chronic axial low back pain receives diagnostic medial branch blocks at L3, L4, and L5 with 0.5 mL of 2% lidocaine per level. She reports 90% pain relief lasting 2 hours, then complete return of symptoms. What is the most appropriate next step?
column3: Repeat the medial branch blocks using a longer-acting anesthetic
column4: Schedule the patient for radiofrequency ablation of the medial branches
column5: Refer the patient for spinal cord stimulation
column6: Order lumbar facet joint corticosteroid injections under fluoroscopy
column7: Repeat the medial branch blocks using a longer-acting anesthetic is correct because concordant relief with short-acting lidocaine suggests facet-mediated pain, and guidelines recommend a confirmatory block with a longer-acting agent before proceeding to RFA.
column8: Schedule the patient for radiofrequency ablation of the medial branches is incorrect because only one diagnostic block has been performed, and confirmatory testing with a different anesthetic is recommended before lesioning.
column9: Refer the patient for spinal cord stimulation is incorrect because SCS is indicated for neuropathic pain, not for axial mechanical pain consistent with facet arthropathy.
column10: Order lumbar facet joint corticosteroid injections under fluoroscopy is incorrect because intra-articular injections have lower diagnostic specificity than medial branch blocks and are not the next step in this algorithm.

column2: A 64-year-old man with lumbar spinal stenosis undergoes a fluoroscopically guided interlaminar epidural corticosteroid injection at L4–L5. Three days later, he presents with worsening low back pain, fever, and difficulty walking. Exam reveals tenderness over the lumbar spine and mildly decreased strength in the bilateral lower extremities. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column3: Order emergent MRI of the lumbar spine with contrast
column4: Initiate a course of oral prednisone and schedule physical therapy
column5: Order lumbar spine X-rays and monitor symptoms outpatient
column6: Prescribe empiric oral antibiotics and re-evaluate in 48 hours
column7: Order emergent MRI of the lumbar spine with contrast is correct because epidural abscess is a serious post-injection complication characterized by back pain, fever, and neurologic signs, and contrast-enhanced MRI is the diagnostic modality of choice to confirm or rule out this condition.
column8: Initiate a course of oral prednisone and schedule physical therapy is incorrect because steroids could worsen infection and delay appropriate diagnosis and treatment of a potential spinal abscess.
column9: Order lumbar spine X-rays and monitor symptoms outpatient is incorrect because plain radiographs are not sensitive for early infection and outpatient monitoring is unsafe in the setting of systemic and neurologic signs.
column10: Prescribe empiric oral antibiotics and re-evaluate in 48 hours is incorrect because this delays definitive imaging and does not adequately assess for a space-occupying lesion requiring possible surgical decompression.

column2: A 59-year-old woman receives a fluoroscopically guided lumbar interlaminar epidural corticosteroid injection for spinal stenosis. Twelve hours later, she presents with headache, photophobia, nausea, and neck stiffness. Her temperature is 99.2°F, and neurologic exam is normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Aseptic meningitis is correct because symptoms of meningismus developing within 24 hours after injection without fever or focal neurologic deficits are characteristic of sterile meningeal irritation.
column8: Intracranial hemorrhage is incorrect because it typically presents with focal deficits, altered mental status, or sudden severe headache.
column9: Post-dural puncture headache is incorrect because it usually presents with postural worsening and lacks neck stiffness or photophobia.
column10: Epidural abscess is incorrect because it presents with fever, focal neurologic signs, and evolves over days, not hours.

column2: A 63-year-old man presents 2 weeks after a lumbar epidural injection with worsening bilateral leg pain, numbness, and new-onset bowel urgency. MRI of the lumbar spine shows clumped nerve roots within the thecal sac without enhancement or mass lesion. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Arachnoiditis is correct because nerve root clumping with delayed progressive neurologic symptoms and bowel involvement is typical of inflammatory arachnoiditis following spinal intervention.
column8: Cauda equina syndrome is incorrect because it is a surgical emergency with sudden onset and usually compressive etiology.
column9: Lumbar disc herniation is incorrect because it causes focal root compression, not diffuse nerve root clumping.
column10: Spinal epidural hematoma is incorrect because it presents acutely with dense motor deficits and mass effect on imaging.

column2: A 70-year-old man with lumbar radiculopathy undergoes a transforaminal epidural steroid injection at L3–L4. Minutes later, he develops bilateral leg weakness and loss of pain and temperature sensation, while proprioception and vibration are preserved. What is the most likely cause of these findings?
column3: Inadvertent injury to the artery of Adamkiewicz
column7: Inadvertent injury to the artery of Adamkiewicz is correct because anterior spinal artery syndrome may result from vascular insult during transforaminal injections, leading to motor paralysis and loss of pain/temperature with preserved dorsal column function.
column8: Needle-induced dural puncture is incorrect because it would typically present with postural headache, not acute spinal cord ischemia.
column9: Arachnoiditis from contrast material is incorrect because it would cause delayed symptoms and nerve root clumping, not acute focal deficits.
column10: Intrathecal opioid administration is incorrect because it may cause delayed respiratory depression or sedation, but not selective spinal cord infarction.

column2: A 68-year-old woman is scheduled for a lumbar interlaminar epidural corticosteroid injection. She takes apixaban twice daily for atrial fibrillation, with her last dose taken this morning. What is the most appropriate next step?
column3: Delay the injection and hold apixaban for at least 72 hours before proceeding
column4: Proceed with the injection if INR is within normal limits
column5: Hold apixaban for 24 hours and proceed with the injection
column6: Switch the patient to low-dose aspirin and proceed immediately
column7: Delay the injection and hold apixaban for at least 72 hours before proceeding is correct because ASRA guidelines recommend a minimum 72-hour hold of apixaban before neuraxial procedures to minimize risk of spinal hematoma.
column8: Proceed with the injection if INR is within normal limits is incorrect because INR monitoring does not reflect apixaban activity.
column9: Hold apixaban for 24 hours and proceed with the injection is incorrect because this is insufficient time for clearance of apixaban for a high-risk neuraxial procedure.
column10: Switch the patient to low-dose aspirin and proceed immediately is incorrect because switching antiplatelet for anticoagulant therapy without guidance risks under- or over-anticoagulation and does not justify immediate injection.

column2: Which of the following is not an FDA-approved indication for spinal cord stimulation?
column7: Phantom limb pain is correct because SCS is not currently FDA-approved for phantom limb pain, although it may be used off-label in select cases.
column8: Post-laminectomy syndrome is incorrect because SCS is FDA-approved for chronic intractable pain of the trunk and limbs, including failed back surgery syndrome.
column9: Painful diabetic neuropathy is incorrect because the FDA approved SCS for painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy in 2021 based on randomized trial evidence.
column10: Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) is incorrect because SCS is FDA-approved for CRPS (formerly known as reflex sympathetic dystrophy), especially in the upper or lower extremities.
column12: FDA-approved indications for spinal cord stimulation (SCS) include chronic intractable pain of the trunk and/or limbs, such as failed back surgery syndrome (post-laminectomy syndrome), complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS I and II), and painful diabetic peripheral neuropathy. Other uses such as phantom limb pain, post-herpetic neuralgia, spinal cord injury pain, or visceral abdominal pain are investigational or off-label and not FDA-approved indications, though they may be supported in clinical literature or used in specialized settings.

column2: A 46-year-old woman presents with severe, localized burning pain in the right foot following an ankle fracture one year ago. She has been diagnosed with CRPS type I and has failed conservative treatments, including medications, physical therapy, and sympathetic blocks. Her pain is sharply demarcated to the dorsum of the foot and lateral ankle. Which neuromodulation option offers the most targeted relief in this scenario?
column6: Peripheral nerve stimulation of the peroneal nerve
column7: Dorsal root ganglion stimulation is correct because it is FDA-approved for CRPS and provides highly targeted pain relief for focal neuropathic pain syndromes like CRPS type I, especially in hard-to-treat areas such as the foot and ankle.
column8: High-frequency spinal cord stimulation (HF10) is incorrect because while effective for diffuse axial and radicular pain, it does not provide dermatomal precision like DRG stimulation.
column9: Burst waveform spinal cord stimulation is incorrect because although paresthesia-free, it is not specifically designed for focal pain targeting and is less effective for discrete areas like the foot.
column10: Peripheral nerve stimulation of the peroneal nerve is incorrect because it may not adequately address deep neuropathic pain localized to multiple sensory branches as seen in CRPS.

column2: What is the most common overall cause of spinal cord injury in the United States?
column7: Motor vehicle crash is correct because it remains the leading cause of spinal cord injury across all age groups, especially among young adults.
column8: Falls is incorrect because although falls are the most common cause in older adults, they are not the leading cause overall.
column9: Sports injuries is incorrect because they contribute to a smaller proportion of spinal cord injuries overall.
column10: Gunshot wounds is incorrect because they account for a minority of spinal cord injuries.

column2: What is the most common cause of spinal cord injury in the elderly population?
column7: Falls is correct because they are the leading cause of spinal cord injury in the elderly due to age-related factors such as balance impairment and osteoporosis.
column8: Motor vehicle crash is incorrect because although it is the leading cause overall, it is less common than falls in older adults.
column9: Sports injuries is incorrect because they are rare in the elderly and contribute minimally to spinal cord injury in this age group.
column10: Gunshot wounds is incorrect because they are an uncommon cause of spinal cord injury in older adults.

column2: Which of the following represents the most common neurologic classification of spinal cord injury?
column7: Incomplete tetraplegia is correct because it is the most frequent neurologic outcome of spinal cord injury, accounting for the highest proportion of cases, particularly due to the prevalence of cervical-level injuries with some preserved function.
column8: Incomplete paraplegia is incorrect because it is less common than incomplete tetraplegia among all spinal cord injuries.
column9: Complete paraplegia is incorrect because complete injuries overall are less common than incomplete injuries and are less likely to involve the cervical spine.
column10: Complete tetraplegia is incorrect because it is the least frequent classification among the four listed.

column2: What is the most common cause of death in the acute phase following a spinal cord injury?
column7: Respiratory complications is correct because respiratory failure, most often due to pneumonia from impaired cough and diaphragmatic weakness in cervical and high thoracic injuries, is the leading cause of death in the acute phase post-SCI.
column8: Pulmonary embolism is incorrect because although it is a major concern in spinal cord injury, it more commonly causes death in the subacute and chronic phases.
column9: Sepsis is incorrect because it typically results from chronic infections and is a more frequent cause of death in the later stages.
column10: Cardiac arrhythmia is incorrect because although autonomic dysfunction may lead to bradyarrhythmias, these are not the most common cause of acute mortality.

column2: What is the most common cause of death in the chronic phase following a spinal cord injury?
column7: Cardiovascular disease is correct because ischemic heart disease is the leading long-term cause of mortality in individuals with chronic spinal cord injury due to altered autonomic function, decreased physical activity, and metabolic changes.
column8: Pulmonary embolism is incorrect because while it significantly contributes to mortality, it is more prominent in the early subacute period than in long-term follow-up.
column9: Sepsis is incorrect because it typically arises from chronic infections but is not the most common overall cause of death in the chronic phase.
column10: Respiratory complications is incorrect because they are more frequently associated with early mortality post-injury, particularly from pneumonia in high-level injuries.

column2: A patient with a traumatic spinal cord injury exhibits preserved proprioception and vibration sense but complete loss of motor function and pain/temperature sensation below the level of injury. Which spinal tract was most likely spared?
column7: Dorsal columns is correct because these tracts carry proprioception and vibration sense, and their preservation in this spinal cord injury pattern is clinically consistent with anterior cord syndrome, making the dorsal columns the most likely spared pathway.
column8: Lateral corticospinal tract is incorrect because this descending motor pathway is typically involved in anterior cord syndrome, which presents with motor loss below the level of injury.
column9: Spinothalamic tract is incorrect because loss of pain and temperature sensation indicates involvement of this ascending tract, which is commonly affected in anterior cord injuries.
column10: Anterior corticospinal tract is incorrect because although it carries motor fibers, its limited role in voluntary movement and involvement in axial muscle control make it insufficient to explain the clinical picture.

column2: A 58-year-old man with known cervical spondylotic myelopathy is started on physical therapy. After three weeks, he reports progressive bilateral hand weakness and new difficulty with balance. What is the next best step in management?
column4: Continue physical therapy with closer monitoring
column7: Refer for surgical decompression is correct because this patient has progressive motor deficits and gait dysfunction in the setting of cervical myelopathy, which indicates spinal cord compression that requires urgent surgical intervention.
column8: Continue physical therapy with closer monitoring is incorrect because worsening myelopathic signs despite therapy reflects failed conservative management and risks permanent neurologic damage if not escalated.
column9: Cervical epidural steroid injection is incorrect because epidural steroids are indicated for radiculopathy, not for cervical myelopathy, and would not address underlying cord compression.
column10: Order repeat cervical MRI is incorrect because although imaging may be appropriate, it should not delay referral for surgical evaluation in a patient with worsening cervical myelopathy.

column2: A 47-year-old woman presents with chronic, burning pain localized to the perineum and coccyx that worsens with sitting. She has failed oral medications and pelvic floor physical therapy. Which of the following is the most appropriate interventional option?
column7: Ganglion impar block is correct because it targets the terminal sympathetic ganglion responsible for nociceptive transmission from the perineum, and is specifically indicated for coccydynia and chronic perineal pain unresponsive to conservative therapy.
column8: Lumbar sympathetic block is incorrect because it is used for lower extremity sympathetically mediated pain, such as CRPS, rather than perineal or coccygeal pain.
column9: Pudendal nerve block is incorrect because it targets somatic sensory fibers and is more appropriate for pudendal neuralgia, not broad perineal pain including coccyx involvement.
column10: Caudal epidural steroid injection is incorrect because it may provide nonspecific analgesia to sacral nerve roots but does not effectively target the sympathetic system involved in chronic perineal pain.

column2: A 35-year-old woman reports sharp, shooting pain in the vulvar region radiating to the rectum, worsened by sitting and relieved when standing or lying down. She describes a feeling of a foreign object in the vagina and has failed oral neuropathic agents and pelvic floor therapy. What is the most appropriate next interventional step?
column7: Pudendal nerve block is correct because the patient’s symptoms are characteristic of pudendal neuralgia, including positional perineal pain and altered genital sensation. A diagnostic block can help confirm the diagnosis, and when combined with steroid, may reduce inflammation and provide temporary symptom relief, enabling better participation in rehabilitation.
column8: Ganglion impar block is incorrect because it targets visceral sympathetic fibers involved in coccygeal or diffuse perineal pain, not the somatic pudendal nerve.
column9: Lumbar sympathetic block is incorrect because it treats lower extremity sympathetically mediated pain, not somatic pelvic nerve syndromes.
column10: Sacroiliac joint injection is incorrect because sacroiliac joint pathology does not present with perineal or genital pain and is unrelated to pudendal nerve territory.

column2: A 70-year-old woman undergoes repair of a descending thoracic aortic aneurysm. Twelve hours later, she is unable to move either leg and has diminished pinprick and temperature sensation below the umbilicus. Joint position sense and vibration are intact. Injury to which vascular structure most likely caused this complication?
column6: Radicular branch of the internal iliac artery
column7: Artery of Adamkiewicz is correct because it provides the primary blood supply to the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord, and its injury during thoracic aortic surgery can result in anterior cord syndrome with bilateral motor loss and impaired pain/temperature sensation while sparing dorsal column function.
column8: Posterior spinal artery is incorrect because it supplies the dorsal columns, which are preserved in this patient, and its infarction would impair proprioception and vibration rather than motor function.
column9: Vertebral artery is incorrect because it supplies the upper cervical spinal cord and brainstem, not the thoracolumbar cord at risk during aortic repair.
column10: Radicular branch of the internal iliac artery is incorrect because it supplies the sacral spinal roots and cauda equina, and its compromise would not cause a thoracic-level cord syndrome.

column2: A patient undergoes thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm repair involving prolonged aortic cross-clamping. Postoperatively, they develop flaccid paralysis of both lower extremities with loss of pain and temperature sensation, while proprioception and vibration remain intact. Which spinal cord region is most likely to have suffered ischemic injury?
column3: Midthoracic spinal cord between T4 and T6
column7: Midthoracic spinal cord between T4 and T6 is correct because this region represents a watershed zone with limited collateral blood flow, making it the most vulnerable segment to ischemia during aortic cross-clamping, especially when the artery of Adamkiewicz is compromised.
column8: Cervicomedullary junction (C1–C2) is incorrect because it is supplied by the vertebral and anterior spinal arteries from the subclavian system and is not typically affected by thoracoabdominal aortic procedures.
column9: Conus medullaris (L1–L2) is incorrect because this region is supplied by branches from the iliolumbar and lateral sacral arteries and is generally spared in mid-thoracic ischemia.
column10: Upper thoracic spinal cord (T1–T2) is incorrect because this segment has more robust collateral circulation and is less frequently involved in ischemic injury related to aortic surgery.

column2: A patient undergoes thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm repair involving prolonged aortic cross-clamping. Postoperatively, they develop flaccid paralysis of both lower extremities with loss of pain and temperature sensation, while proprioception and vibration remain intact. Which spinal cord region is most likely to have suffered ischemic injury?
column3: Midthoracic spinal cord between T4 and T6
column7: Midthoracic spinal cord between T4 and T6 is correct because this region represents a watershed zone with limited collateral blood flow, making it the most vulnerable segment to ischemia during aortic cross-clamping, especially when the artery of Adamkiewicz is compromised.
column8: Cervicomedullary junction (C1–C2) is incorrect because it is supplied by the vertebral and anterior spinal arteries from the subclavian system and is not typically affected by thoracoabdominal aortic procedures.
column9: Conus medullaris (L1–L2) is incorrect because this region is supplied by branches from the iliolumbar and lateral sacral arteries and is generally spared in mid-thoracic ischemia.
column10: Upper thoracic spinal cord (T1–T2) is incorrect because this segment has more robust collateral circulation and is less frequently involved in ischemic injury related to aortic surgery.

column2: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a motor vehicle collision. He is found to have a flexion-distraction injury with bilateral facet dislocation resulting in anterior displacement of one cervical vertebra over another. At which cervical level is this type of injury most commonly observed?
column7: C5–C6 is correct because this is the most common level of cervical facet joint dislocation due to the high mobility and mechanical stress experienced at this junction during flexion injuries.
column8: C3–C4 is incorrect because although part of the mobile cervical spine, it is less commonly involved in facet dislocations compared to lower levels.
column9: C1–C2 is incorrect because injuries at this level usually involve rotational instability or odontoid fractures rather than facet dislocation.
column10: C7–T1 is incorrect because this junction is relatively stable and less frequently subject to bilateral facet dislocation injuries.

column2: A 34-year-old woman develops bilateral leg weakness, a sensory level at T8, and urinary retention over three days. Spine MRI shows a T2-hyperintense lesion spanning three vertebral segments without compression. Which of the following is required to confirm the diagnosis of transverse myelitis?
column3: Objective evidence of spinal cord inflammation via MRI enhancement or CSF pleocytosis
column4: Presence of oligoclonal bands in the cerebrospinal fluid
column6: Involvement of both gray and white matter on spinal MRI
column7: Objective evidence of spinal cord inflammation via MRI enhancement or CSF pleocytosis is correct because the diagnosis of transverse myelitis requires confirmation of active inflammation either radiographically or via cerebrospinal fluid analysis.
column8: Presence of oligoclonal bands in the cerebrospinal fluid is incorrect because this finding supports a diagnosis of multiple sclerosis but is not required for transverse myelitis.
column9: Absence of brain lesions is incorrect because brain lesions may be present in transverse myelitis and do not exclude the diagnosis or confirm alternative causes like MS.
column10: Involvement of both gray and white matter on spinal MRI is incorrect because while this is a common finding in transverse myelitis, it is not required for diagnosis.

column2: A 41-year-old woman presents with right optic neuritis followed by severe transverse myelitis two months later. MRI shows a spinal cord lesion extending over five vertebral segments. Serum is positive for aquaporin-4 IgG antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder is correct because the patient has a core clinical feature (optic neuritis and longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis) along with a positive aquaporin-4 antibody, which confirms the diagnosis of NMOSD.
column8: Multiple sclerosis is incorrect because MS typically causes shorter spinal cord lesions (<3 vertebral segments), and AQP4-IgG positivity is not a feature of MS.
column9: Acute disseminated encephalomyelitis is incorrect because ADEM is typically monophasic, affects children, and presents with diffuse encephalopathy rather than sequential optic and spinal attacks.
column10: Transverse myelitis is incorrect because this diagnosis is reserved for cases without a known cause; the presence of AQP4-IgG confirms NMOSD.

column2: A 41-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of optic neuritis and transverse myelitis. MRI shows a spinal cord lesion extending over five vertebral segments. Which of the following serum findings would most strongly support the diagnosis of neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder?
column7: Positive aquaporin-4 IgG antibody is correct because AQP4-IgG is a disease-specific autoantibody that, in the presence of core clinical features, confirms the diagnosis of neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder.
column8: Positive oligoclonal bands is incorrect because this finding is more typical of multiple sclerosis and is not diagnostic for NMOSD.
column9: Positive myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (MOG) antibody is incorrect because MOG antibody–associated disease is a separate demyelinating condition with distinct features from NMOSD.
column10: Elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate is incorrect because it is a nonspecific marker of inflammation and not sufficient to diagnose NMOSD.

column2: Which of the following is NOT characteristic of delayed radiation myelopathy?
column5: T2 hyperintensity in the spinal cord without mass effect
column6: Symptom onset several months after completing radiation therapy
column7: CSF pleocytosis is correct because this is NOT characteristic of delayed radiation myelopathy; CSF is typically normal and pleocytosis suggests an inflammatory cause like transverse myelitis or NMOSD.
column8: Gradual progression of spastic paraparesis is incorrect because this is a hallmark clinical feature of delayed radiation-induced spinal cord injury.
column9: T2 hyperintensity in the spinal cord without mass effect is incorrect because this is a common MRI finding in radiation myelopathy.
column10: Symptom onset several months after completing radiation therapy is incorrect because delayed onset (6–24 months) is typical of radiation myelopathy.

column2: A 62-year-old man presents with several months of worsening gait instability, lower extremity paresthesias, and hand clumsiness. Examination reveals spasticity, hyperreflexia, impaired proprioception, and a positive Romberg sign. MRI of the cervical spine shows bilateral T2 hyperintensity of the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts without enhancement. Which of the following is the most common cause of this condition?
column7: Vitamin B12 deficiency is correct because it is the most common cause of subacute combined degeneration, which affects the dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts and presents with proprioceptive loss and upper motor neuron signs.
column8: Copper deficiency is incorrect because although it can mimic SCD, it is far less common and typically occurs with prior gastric surgery or excessive zinc intake.
column9: Nitrous oxide toxicity is incorrect because it causes B12 inactivation and similar neurologic findings, but is a less frequent etiology overall.
column10: HIV-associated vacuolar myelopathy is incorrect because it is a rare cause of dorsal column dysfunction and usually occurs in patients with advanced HIV/AIDS and additional systemic signs.

column2: A 45-year-old woman presents with progressive lower extremity weakness and sensory changes. MRI of the thoracic spine reveals an enhancing mass within the spinal cord parenchyma. Based on location, which of the following tumor types is most commonly associated with this finding?
column7: Ependymoma is correct because it is the most common intramedullary spinal cord tumor in adults, arising within the parenchyma and typically found in the cervical or thoracic cord.
column8: Schwannoma is incorrect because schwannomas are typically intradural extramedullary and arise from dorsal sensory roots, not within the cord itself.
