column9: Meningioma is incorrect because meningiomas are intradural extramedullary tumors that originate from the meninges, not from within the cord parenchyma.
column10: Metastatic epidural lesion is incorrect because these typically compress the cord from the outside and are located in the extradural space.

column2: A 68-year-old man with a history of prostate cancer presents with acute onset mid-back pain, bilateral leg weakness, and urinary retention. Examination reveals hyperreflexia and a sensory level at T8. MRI shows a compressive mass in the epidural space extending from the posterior vertebral body, with sparing of the intradural structures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Vertebral metastasis with epidural extension is correct because this is the most common cause of extradural spinal cord compression, typically from cancers such as prostate, breast, or lung.
column8: Ependymoma is incorrect because it arises from within the spinal cord and is classified as intramedullary.
column9: Schwannoma is incorrect because it is an intradural extramedullary tumor arising from the nerve roots, not the epidural space.
column10: Meningioma is incorrect because it is a dural-based tumor that is typically intradural extramedullary, not extradural.

column2: A 64-year-old woman presents with slowly progressive bilateral lower extremity weakness, numbness, and urinary hesitancy. MRI of the thoracic spine shows a homogenously enhancing lesion located inside the dura but outside the spinal cord, with a broad dural base compressing the cord. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Meningioma is correct because it is a common intradural extramedullary tumor, often seen in older women, and appears as a dural-based, homogenously enhancing mass that compresses the cord.
column8: Ependymoma is incorrect because it is an intramedullary tumor that arises from the ependymal cells within the spinal cord.
column9: Astrocytoma is incorrect because it is also an intramedullary tumor and more commonly affects children and young adults.
column10: Epidural lymphoma is incorrect because it arises outside the dura, typically in the vertebral column or epidural space, and causes compression from the extradural compartment.

column2: A 32-year-old man presents after a motorcycle crash with cervical spine trauma. On ISNCSCI exam, he has intact light touch and pinprick sensation at C5 and C6 bilaterally, with motor function 3/5 at C5 and C6, but 0/5 below. No sacral sparing is present for motor or sensory function. What is the neurologic level of injury?
column7: C6 complete spinal cord injury is correct because C6 is the most caudal level with normal sensory and at least antigravity (3/5) motor function bilaterally, and the absence of sacral sparing confirms a complete injury (AIS A).
column8: C5 incomplete spinal cord injury is incorrect because C6 motor function is partially preserved, making C6 the correct level, and sacral sparing is required to call it incomplete.
column9: C7 complete spinal cord injury is incorrect because motor and sensory function do not reach the C7 level.
column10: C6 incomplete spinal cord injury is incorrect because there is no sacral sparing, which is required for classification as incomplete.

column2: A 27-year-old man presents after a fall from scaffolding. ISNCSCI exam reveals normal sensory and 5/5 motor strength through C7 bilaterally. At C8, he has intact pinprick and light touch but motor is 1/5. No motor or sensory function is present below that level, including the sacral segments. What is the neurologic level of injury?
column7: C7 complete spinal cord injury is correct because C7 is the lowest level with normal sensory and motor bilaterally, and the absence of sacral sparing indicates a complete (AIS A) injury.
column8: C8 complete spinal cord injury is incorrect because motor function at C8 is not normal.
column9: C8 incomplete spinal cord injury is incorrect because there is no sacral sparing to classify the injury as incomplete.
column10: T1 complete spinal cord injury is incorrect because the motor and sensory functions do not extend to T1.

column2: A 39-year-old woman presents after a car crash. On ISNCSCI exam, she has normal sensation and 5/5 strength through T1 bilaterally. At T2, sensation is preserved but no motor function is testable. Anal sensation and voluntary anal contraction are both intact. What is the injury classification?
column7: T1 incomplete spinal cord injury is correct because T1 is the last level with normal sensory and motor, and sacral sparing confirms the injury is incomplete.
column8: T2 complete spinal cord injury is incorrect because T1 is the neurologic level and the injury is not complete due to sacral sparing.
column9: T2 incomplete spinal cord injury is incorrect because T1 is the most caudal normal level, not T2.
column10: T1 complete spinal cord injury is incorrect because sacral sparing excludes a complete injury.

column2: A 45-year-old man has 5/5 strength and normal sensation through L2. He has 1/5 strength at L3, 0/5 at L4-S1, and no sacral sensation or voluntary anal contraction. What is the zone of partial preservation?
column7: L3 is correct because it is the most caudal level below the neurologic level of injury (L2) with preserved motor or sensory function, and there is no sacral sparing (making it a complete injury with a ZPP).
column8: L2 is incorrect because it is the neurologic level, not the most caudal level with some function.
column9: L4 is incorrect because there is no preserved function at L4.
column10: L5 is incorrect because there is no motor or sensory function at L5.

column2: A 50-year-old woman with a thoracic spinal cord injury has 5/5 motor strength at C5-T1 and normal sensory through T6. Sensory is reduced below T7, and motor is not testable below that level. Sacral sensation is absent, but voluntary anal contraction is present. What is her AIS grade?
column7: AIS B is correct because there is preserved sacral motor function (voluntary anal contraction) without motor function more than three levels below the motor level, classifying it as sensory incomplete.
column8: AIS C is incorrect because this grade requires some preserved motor function below the neurologic level.
column9: AIS D is incorrect because this grade requires most key muscles below the injury to be at least 3/5.
column10: AIS A is incorrect because the presence of voluntary anal contraction rules out a complete injury.

column2: During an ISNCSCI exam, a patient demonstrates 5/5 strength in C5-C6, 3/5 strength at C7, and 0/5 strength below. Sensation is intact through T1. What defines the motor level of injury?
column3: The lowest key muscle function that is at least grade 3 with all muscles above graded 5
column4: The most caudal dermatome with intact light touch and pinprick
column5: The level with both motor and sensory function graded at least 3/5
column6: The lowest level with preserved voluntary anal contraction
column7: The lowest key muscle function that is at least grade 3 with all muscles above graded 5 is correct because this defines the motor level of injury according to ISNCSCI standards.
column8: The most caudal dermatome with intact light touch and pinprick is incorrect because that defines the sensory level, not motor.
column9: The level with both motor and sensory function graded at least 3/5 is incorrect because motor level requires all key muscles above to be 5/5, not just both at 3/5.
column10: The lowest level with preserved voluntary anal contraction is incorrect because this is used in sacral sparing assessment, not to determine motor level.

column2: A patient undergoing ISNCSCI evaluation has intact light touch and pinprick through T6 bilaterally. At T7, light touch is present on the left but absent on the right, and pinprick is absent bilaterally. What is the sensory level of injury?
column7: T6 is correct because the sensory level is defined as the most caudal dermatome with normal sensation (light touch and pinprick) on both sides of the body.
column8: T7 is incorrect because sensation is impaired on the right and absent for pinprick bilaterally, so it does not qualify as normal.
column9: T8 is incorrect because sensory function is not intact at T7, so T8 cannot be the sensory level.
column10: L1 is incorrect because intact sensation does not extend to this level.

column2: A patient undergoing ISNCSCI exam has normal sensory (light touch and pinprick) and 5/5 motor function through C6 bilaterally. At C7, motor strength is 3/5 and sensory is intact bilaterally. At C8, sensory is impaired and motor is 1/5. What is the neurologic level of injury?
column7: C6 is correct because the neurologic level of injury is defined as the most caudal segment with normal motor and sensory function bilaterally, which in this case is C6.
column8: C7 is incorrect because although sensory is intact, motor is only 3/5 at that level, and all levels above must have full (5/5) strength.
column9: C8 is incorrect because both motor and sensory are impaired at this level.
column10: T1 is incorrect because motor and sensory levels do not extend to T1.

column2: A 43-year-old woman presents with a T6 SCI after a fall. On exam, she has no motor function below T6 but intact pinprick sensation in the perianal region. Voluntary anal contraction is absent. She has no motor function preserved more than three levels below the motor level. What is her AIS grade?
column7: AIS B is correct because she has preserved sensory but no motor function below the injury, including preserved sacral sensation without voluntary anal contraction or significant motor recovery.
column8: AIS A is incorrect because the presence of sacral sensory function excludes a complete injury.
column9: AIS C is incorrect because there is no motor function preserved below the level of injury.
column10: AIS D is incorrect because it requires most key muscles below the injury to be 3/5 or better, which is not present.

column2: On ISNCSCI exam, a patient has normal motor and sensory function through C6 bilaterally. At C7, motor is 5/5 on the right and 3/5 on the left; sensation is intact bilaterally. All motor levels above are 5/5. What is the neurologic level of injury?
column7: C6 is correct because the NLI is the most caudal segment with normal sensory and motor function bilaterally, and C7 has asymmetric motor strength.
column8: C7 is incorrect because motor function is not normal bilaterally — the left side is only 3/5.
column9: C8 is incorrect because strength was not tested or preserved at that level.
column10: T1 is incorrect because it is below the sensory and motor findings.

column2: A patient has 5/5 motor strength in C5 and C6 bilaterally. At C7, wrist flexion is 3/5, but elbow extension is not testable due to pain. Finger extensors are 0/5. Sensory testing shows normal light touch and pinprick through C7 bilaterally. What is the neurologic level of injury?
column7: C6 is correct because elbow extension (C7 key muscle) is not testable, and non-key muscle function (e.g., wrist flexors) cannot be used to define the motor level, so the motor level defaults to the last level with fully testable 5/5 function — C6.
column8: C7 is incorrect because the key muscle (elbow extension) is not testable, and strength in non-key muscles cannot substitute for key muscles in determining the motor level.
column9: C8 is incorrect because key muscles below C6 do not show preserved function.
column10: T1 is incorrect because there is no data supporting motor or sensory integrity at that level.

column2: A 35-year-old woman has an SCI with no voluntary anal contraction or sacral sensation. She has no motor function in any key muscles below T1, but shows trace contraction in a non-key muscle (hip adductors). Which of the following best describes the classification?
column7: AIS A is correct because there is no motor or sensory sacral sparing, and preservation of non-key muscle activity alone is not sufficient to classify the injury as incomplete.
column8: AIS B is incorrect because sensory sparing in the sacral segments (S4–S5) is required, which is absent.
column9: AIS C is incorrect because it requires sacral sparing and motor preservation below the neurologic level.
column10: AIS D is incorrect because it requires the criteria for AIS C plus most key muscles below the level to be at least 3/5.

column2: A 29-year-old man presents after a motorcycle accident with flaccid paralysis, areflexia, and absent bulbocavernosus reflex. Sensory and motor function are absent below the T6 level. MRI shows spinal cord contusion at T6. Which of the following findings would best indicate the end of spinal shock?
column4: Onset of spasticity below the lesion
column7: Return of the bulbocavernosus reflex is correct because it is the earliest sign of the end of spinal shock and marks the return of reflex arc function, even in the absence of voluntary movement.
column8: Onset of spasticity below the lesion is incorrect because it typically occurs later and does not define the end of spinal shock.
column9: Positive Babinski sign is incorrect because it reflects upper motor neuron damage but not necessarily resolution of spinal shock.
column10: Return of voluntary anal contraction is incorrect because it reflects sacral motor function, not reflex recovery, and may remain absent even after spinal shock resolves.

column2: A 41-year-old man presents with a T10 spinal cord injury. On exam, he has flaccid paralysis and areflexia below the level of injury, consistent with spinal shock. The plantar response is flexor bilaterally. Which of the following best explains the clinical relevance of a delayed plantar extensor response during recovery?
column3: It indicates resolution of spinal shock and emergence of upper motor neuron signs
column4: It confirms intact corticospinal tract conduction
column5: It reflects preserved lower motor neuron function at S1
column6: It is a normal variant without clinical significance in SCI
column7: It indicates resolution of spinal shock and emergence of upper motor neuron signs is correct because a delayed Babinski sign (extensor plantar response) often emerges after spinal shock ends and reflects reemergence of UMN signs.
column8: It confirms intact corticospinal tract conduction is incorrect because an extensor plantar response actually reflects disruption of the corticospinal tract, not integrity.
column9: It reflects preserved lower motor neuron function at S1 is incorrect because it is a pathological upper motor neuron sign and does not require intact S1 LMNs.
column10: It is a normal variant without clinical significance in SCI is incorrect because a delayed plantar extensor response is a meaningful indicator of UMN pathology during recovery.

column2: A 27-year-old man presents with right-sided flaccid paralysis, weakness, and loss of proprioception below the level of a right-sided thoracic hemisection. Which of the following additional findings is expected based on the spinal cord anatomy involved?
column3: Contralateral loss of pain and temperature due to damage to the spinothalamic tract
column4: Ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature due to dorsal column involvement
column5: Contralateral weakness due to corticospinal tract disruption
column6: Bilateral loss of proprioception from spinocerebellar tract injury
column7: Contralateral loss of pain and temperature due to damage to the spinothalamic tract is correct because Brown-Séquard syndrome results from hemisection of the cord, which causes contralateral loss of pain and temperature 1–2 segments below the lesion due to crossing of spinothalamic fibers.
column8: Ipsilateral loss of pain and temperature due to dorsal column involvement is incorrect because the dorsal column does not carry pain and temperature, and the spinothalamic tract crosses early.
column9: Contralateral weakness due to corticospinal tract disruption is incorrect because the corticospinal tract has already decussated in the medulla and produces ipsilateral weakness below the lesion.
column10: Bilateral loss of proprioception from spinocerebellar tract injury is incorrect because hemisection does not typically affect both dorsal columns or spinocerebellar tracts.

column2: A 34-year-old man presents with left-sided flaccid paralysis and loss of proprioception after a penetrating injury causing hemisection of the left thoracic spinal cord. He also has diminished pain and temperature sensation on the right beginning a few segments below the injury. Which of the following best explains the right-sided sensory deficit?
column3: Disruption of the spinothalamic tract crossing contralaterally
column4: Injury to the left dorsal column at the level of the lesion
column5: Compression of the anterior corticospinal tract on the right
column6: Bilateral involvement of the medial lemniscus pathway
column7: Disruption of the spinothalamic tract crossing contralaterally is correct because in Brown-Séquard syndrome, hemisection damages spinothalamic fibers that have crossed to the contralateral side, resulting in pain and temperature loss 1–2 levels below the lesion.
column8: Injury to the left dorsal column at the level of the lesion is incorrect because the dorsal column carries ipsilateral vibration and proprioception, not pain and temperature.
column9: Compression of the anterior corticospinal tract on the right is incorrect because motor deficits in hemisection occur ipsilaterally below the lesion, not contralaterally.
column10: Bilateral involvement of the medial lemniscus pathway is incorrect because this pathway is located in the brainstem and not affected by spinal cord hemisection.

column2: A 30-year-old woman sustains a left-sided hemisection of the cervical spinal cord after a knife injury. On exam, she has weakness with spasticity in the left lower extremity and loss of vibration sense on the same side. Pain and temperature sensation are diminished on the right beginning several levels below the injury. Which of the following tracts is responsible for her left-sided motor deficits?
column7: Lateral corticospinal tract is correct because it controls voluntary movement, has already crossed in the medullary pyramids, and damage in a spinal cord hemisection results in ipsilateral upper motor neuron signs below the lesion, consistent with Brown-Séquard syndrome.
column8: Spinothalamic tract is incorrect because it mediates pain and temperature and causes contralateral deficits below the level of the lesion.
column9: Dorsal column is incorrect because it carries proprioception and vibration, not motor function.
column10: Rubrospinal tract is incorrect because it plays a minor role in humans and does not account for the observed motor weakness and spasticity.

column2: A 35-year-old man sustains a penetrating injury resulting in hemisection of the left thoracic spinal cord. Which of the following findings is most consistent with involvement of the dorsal column?
column3: Loss of light touch and proprioception on the left starting at the level of the lesion
column4: Loss of light touch and proprioception on the right beginning two levels below the lesion
column5: Loss of pain and temperature on the left two levels below the lesion
column6: Weakness and spasticity on the right below the lesion
column7: Loss of light touch and proprioception on the left starting at the level of the lesion is correct because the dorsal column ascends ipsilaterally in the spinal cord and carries proprioception and fine touch; a left hemisection would produce deficits on the left side, beginning at the level of injury.
column8: Loss of light touch and proprioception on the right beginning two levels below the lesion is incorrect because dorsal column fibers do not cross in the spinal cord, and right-sided deficits would require right-sided injury.
column9: Loss of pain and temperature on the left two levels below the lesion is incorrect because these sensations are mediated by the spinothalamic tract, which crosses shortly after entry and would cause contralateral loss.
column10: Weakness and spasticity on the right below the lesion is incorrect because corticospinal tract injury in a left hemisection would cause left-sided upper motor neuron signs.

column2: A 42-year-old man presents with weakness, spasticity, and hyperreflexia in the right lower extremity after a stab wound that caused left-sided hemisection of the thoracic spinal cord at T10. He also has loss of proprioception on the right and loss of pain and temperature on the left. Which spinal tract injury best explains his motor findings?
column3: Disruption of the left lateral corticospinal tract
column4: Disruption of the right anterior corticospinal tract
column5: Compression of the left medial lemniscus
column6: Transection of the left spinothalamic tract
column7: Disruption of the left lateral corticospinal tract is correct because the corticospinal tract decussates in the medulla and descends ipsilaterally in the spinal cord; a left hemisection at T10 causes ipsilateral (left-sided) UMN signs including spasticity, weakness, and hyperreflexia below the lesion.
column8: Disruption of the right anterior corticospinal tract is incorrect because the anterior corticospinal tract has minimal contribution to lower limb motor control and primarily influences axial muscles.
column9: Compression of the left medial lemniscus is incorrect because this tract is located in the brainstem and not affected by spinal cord injury.
column10: Transection of the left spinothalamic tract is incorrect because spinothalamic tract injury causes contralateral loss of pain and temperature, not motor symptoms.

column2: A 27-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after diving headfirst into shallow water. He has profound motor weakness below the level of the lesion and is unable to move his legs or arms but retains preserved vibration and proprioception in all extremities. Sensory examination reveals loss of pain and temperature sensation below the lesion. Which spinal cord syndrome is most likely?
column7: Anterior cord syndrome is correct because the patient's injury mechanism (hyperflexion or axial load from diving) is associated with injury to the anterior two-thirds of the spinal cord, commonly affecting the corticospinal tracts (motor loss) and spinothalamic tracts (pain and temperature), while sparing the dorsal columns (vibration and proprioception).
column8: Central cord syndrome is incorrect because it typically presents with greater motor impairment in the upper extremities than lower and often spares proprioception and motor function in the legs, which is not consistent with this presentation.
column9: Brown-Séquard syndrome is incorrect because it results from hemisection of the spinal cord and presents with ipsilateral motor and proprioception loss and contralateral pain and temperature loss, which this patient does not exhibit.
column10: Posterior cord syndrome is incorrect because it causes loss of vibration and proprioception with preserved motor and pain/temperature function, which is opposite this patient’s findings.

column2: An 82-year-old man falls forward while walking and strikes his chin on the ground. He is brought to the emergency department where neurologic examination reveals profound weakness in the upper extremities with only mild weakness in the lower extremities. He has decreased pain and temperature sensation in the upper extremities but preserved proprioception throughout. Which spinal cord syndrome is most likely?
column7: Central cord syndrome is correct because this syndrome often results from hyperextension injuries in older adults with cervical spondylosis and causes disproportionately greater motor impairment in the upper extremities, often with preserved lower extremity strength and dorsal column function.
column8: Anterior cord syndrome is incorrect because it causes motor and pain/temperature loss below the lesion but typically spares the upper extremities preferentially.
column9: Posterior cord syndrome is incorrect because it presents with loss of proprioception and vibration, not with the motor-predominant pattern described here.
column10: Conus medullaris syndrome is incorrect because it affects the lower extremities and bowel/bladder function more prominently, which is not consistent with this case.

column2: An 80-year-old man with a history of cervical spondylosis falls and hits his forehead. He is evaluated in the emergency department. On examination, he has decreased pain and temperature sensation in the upper limbs, while proprioception remains intact. Which pattern of motor impairment is most characteristic of this spinal cord syndrome?
column3: Greater weakness in the upper extremities than the lower extremities
column4: Equal motor impairment in all extremities
column5: Greater weakness in the lower extremities than the upper extremities
column6: Absent motor impairment with isolated sensory loss
column7: Greater weakness in the upper extremities than the lower extremities is correct because central cord syndrome, commonly due to hyperextension injuries in patients with cervical stenosis, causes preferential involvement of the upper extremity motor tracts located centrally within the cord.
column8: Equal motor impairment in all extremities is incorrect because central cord syndrome causes a disproportionate pattern of weakness.
column9: Greater weakness in the lower extremities than the upper extremities is incorrect because this pattern is more typical of anterior cord syndrome or conus medullaris syndrome.
column10: Absent motor impairment with isolated sensory loss is incorrect because motor weakness is a core feature of central cord syndrome.

column2: A 35-year-old man with a history of untreated neurosyphilis presents with an unsteady gait and frequent falls. He reports difficulty feeling the ground under his feet, especially in the dark. Neurologic examination reveals preserved motor strength and intact pain and temperature sensation, but severely impaired proprioception and vibration sense in the lower extremities. Which spinal cord syndrome is most likely?
column7: Posterior cord syndrome is correct because the patient’s loss of proprioception and vibration sense with preserved motor strength and pain/temperature sensation is classic for a dorsal column lesion, commonly seen in neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis).
column8: Central cord syndrome is incorrect because it typically causes motor weakness more severe in the upper extremities and does not primarily affect vibration or proprioception.
column9: Anterior cord syndrome is incorrect because it causes motor and pain/temperature deficits while sparing the dorsal columns.
column10: Brown-Séquard syndrome is incorrect because it presents with a hemicord pattern of ipsilateral proprioception and motor loss with contralateral pain and temperature loss, which is not consistent with this patient’s bilateral posterior column findings.

column2: A 64-year-old man presents with progressive saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, and new-onset constipation. On examination, he has symmetric lower extremity weakness, brisk patellar reflexes, and absent ankle reflexes. Rectal tone is decreased. MRI shows a lesion at the L1 vertebral level. Which diagnosis is most likely?
column7: Conus medullaris syndrome is correct because symmetric lower extremity weakness with mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs, early bowel/bladder involvement, and a lesion at L1 is characteristic of conus involvement.
column8: Cauda equina syndrome is incorrect because it typically presents with asymmetric lower extremity weakness, absent reflexes, and later-onset bowel/bladder dysfunction due to involvement of individual nerve roots.
column9: Anterior cord syndrome is incorrect because it presents with bilateral motor and pain/temperature loss below the lesion, usually without early bowel/bladder symptoms or saddle anesthesia.
column10: Central cord syndrome is incorrect because it typically causes greater weakness in the upper extremities and is associated with cervical spine pathology, not lumbar lesions.

column2: A 58-year-old man presents with severe lower back pain radiating down the right leg, worsening over 3 days. He has asymmetric weakness in the right leg, absent right ankle reflex, and new onset urinary retention. On examination, he has decreased sensation in the perianal region and posterior right thigh. MRI reveals a large central disc herniation compressing the lower spinal canal. Which diagnosis is most likely?
column7: Cauda equina syndrome is correct because this patient presents with asymmetric leg weakness, dermatomal sensory loss, urinary retention, areflexia, and a large central disc herniation compressing the lower spinal canal — all characteristic of cauda equina syndrome due to nerve root compression.
column8: Conus medullaris syndrome is incorrect because it more often causes symmetric lower extremity findings with earlier bowel/bladder involvement and typically includes mixed UMN and LMN signs.
column9: Lateral medullary syndrome is incorrect because it is a brainstem stroke syndrome causing crossed findings, vertigo, and dysphagia, not isolated lower extremity symptoms.
column10: Brown-Séquard syndrome is incorrect because it results from a hemicord lesion producing ipsilateral motor/proprioceptive loss and contralateral pain/temperature loss, which is not described here.

column2: A 26-year-old man sustains a complete spinal cord injury in a motor vehicle collision. He is motivated, cognitively intact, and otherwise healthy. On exam, he has intact elbow extension and wrist extension, but lacks fine motor control in the hands. Which neurologic level is most consistent with the threshold for achieving independence in ADLs and wheelchair mobility without attendant care?
column7: C7 is correct because this level provides triceps and wrist extensor function, allowing independence in transfers, dressing, and wheelchair mobility with the aid of tenodesis grip, marking the typical threshold for functional independence without the need for attendant care.
column8: C8 is incorrect because although it provides improved hand function, independence in ADLs is already typically achieved by C7.
column9: C6 is incorrect because lack of triceps function makes transfers and dressing more difficult, often requiring adaptive equipment or assistance.
column10: C5 is incorrect because individuals at this level lack triceps and wrist extensors, relying heavily on assistance for mobility and self-care.

column2: A 29-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury experiences sudden hypertension, pounding headache, facial flushing, and bradycardia during a routine bladder irrigation. His injury is known to affect sympathetic regulation of the splanchnic vasculature. At what spinal cord level does the risk of autonomic dysreflexia become clinically significant?
column7: T6 is correct because autonomic dysreflexia is typically seen in lesions at or above T6, where supraspinal control of the greater splanchnic outflow (T6–L2) is lost, allowing unregulated sympathetic discharge in response to stimuli below the lesion.
column8: T4 is incorrect because although patients with injuries above T6 are at risk, T6 marks the lowest level at which this risk becomes clinically significant.
column9: T8 is incorrect because injuries below T6 usually maintain enough descending autonomic control to prevent dysreflexia.
column10: T10 is incorrect because injuries at this level do not typically disrupt splanchnic sympathetic regulation sufficiently to trigger autonomic dysreflexia.

column2: A 33-year-old woman with a complete spinal cord injury reports frequent episodes of overheating in warm environments and cannot sweat below her level of injury. She also experiences difficulty maintaining body heat in cold weather. Which neurologic level of injury is most commonly associated with impaired thermoregulation due to disruption of sympathetic control?
column7: T8 is correct because injuries at or above T8 interrupt descending sympathetic pathways responsible for vasomotor and sudomotor control over a large surface area, impairing thermoregulation.
column8: T4 is incorrect because although it is above T8 and would also impair thermoregulation, the question specifically asks for the most common threshold level, which is T8.
column9: T10 is incorrect because it preserves a greater portion of sympathetic control, allowing for partial thermoregulatory capacity.
column10: L2 is incorrect because it is below the key sympathetic pathways responsible for body temperature regulation, which originate in the thoracic spinal cord.

column2: A 35-year-old man with a recent complete cervical spinal cord injury reports lightheadedness and near-syncope when transitioning from supine to sitting. On examination, his blood pressure drops significantly upon sitting without compensatory tachycardia. What is the most likely explanation for his symptoms?
column3: Impaired sympathetic outflow to splanchnic vasculature.
column4: Volume depletion due to autonomic dysreflexia
column5: Excess parasympathetic tone from vagal stimulation
column7: Impaired sympathetic outflow to splanchnic vasculature is correct because spinal cord injury above the sympathetic thoracolumbar outflow disrupts vasoconstriction and venous return upon positional change, resulting in orthostatic hypotension. This is orthostatic hypotension that is potentiated from the disruption of the the sympathetic outflow secondary to spinal cord injury.
column8: Volume depletion due to autonomic dysreflexia is incorrect because autonomic dysreflexia typically causes hypertension, not volume loss or orthostatic hypotension.
column9: Excess parasympathetic tone from vagal stimulation is incorrect because parasympathetic tone is not increased post-SCI; the issue is unopposed parasympathetic influence due to loss of sympathetic control.
column10: Bradycardia due to baroreceptor overcompensation is incorrect because the baroreceptor reflex is impaired in SCI, and bradycardia is not the primary cause of the hypotension in this setting.

column2: A 35-year-old man with a complete C6 spinal cord injury reports dizziness, lightheadedness, and fatigue after transitioning from supine to sitting. His blood pressure is 84/52 mmHg while sitting, and heart rate remains in the 60s. He denies headache, flushing, or nasal congestion. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing his symptoms?
column4: Administer antihypertensives and check for noxious stimuli
column5: Order cranial imaging for suspected posterior circulation stroke
column7: Compression stockings and abdominal binder is correct because the patient has orthostatic hypotension (low BP, no sympathetic compensation), not autonomic dysreflexia, and nonpharmacologic strategies to increase venous return are the first-line intervention.
column8: Administer antihypertensives and check for noxious stimuli is incorrect because this is the treatment for autonomic dysreflexia, which typically presents with hypertension, headache, and flushing—not hypotension and lightheadedness.
column9: Order cranial imaging for suspected posterior circulation stroke is incorrect because there are no focal neurologic deficits or stroke signs, and symptoms are clearly triggered by position change.
column10: Start beta-blockers to manage bradycardia is incorrect because bradycardia is expected in SCI-related orthostatic hypotension and beta-blockers would further lower blood pressure.

column2: A 38-year-old man with a complete T4 spinal cord injury presents with sudden-onset pounding headache, facial flushing, nasal congestion, and profuse sweating above the level of injury. His blood pressure is 198/108 mmHg and heart rate is 52 bpm. He is undergoing a bladder scan due to difficulty voiding. What is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?
column3: Sit the patient upright and remove noxious stimuli
column5: Apply nitroglycerin paste to lower blood pressure
column7: Sit the patient upright and remove noxious stimuli is correct because first-line treatment for autonomic dysreflexia involves lowering blood pressure through upright positioning and eliminating the triggering stimulus, typically bladder or bowel-related.
column8: Administer intravenous hydralazine is incorrect because antihypertensive medications like hydralazine should only be used if blood pressure remains elevated after physical measures and stimulus removal.
column9: Apply nitroglycerin paste to lower blood pressure is incorrect because nitroglycerin is reserved for refractory cases, not for initial management prior to addressing positioning and the underlying trigger.
column10: Administer intravenous beta blocker is incorrect because beta blockers can worsen bradycardia in autonomic dysreflexia and do not address the sympathetic overactivity driving the condition.

column2: A 40-year-old man with a complete T3 spinal cord injury presents with a pounding headache, facial flushing, and diaphoresis above the level of injury. His blood pressure is 200/110 mmHg and heart rate is 54 bpm. He was immediately sat upright and had a distended bladder catheterized, but his blood pressure remains dangerously elevated. What is the most appropriate next step?
column3: Apply nitroglycerin paste to lower blood pressure
column5: Order emergent head CT to rule out hemorrhage
column7: Apply nitroglycerin paste to lower blood pressure is correct because the patient is experiencing persistent autonomic dysreflexia despite initial physical measures and trigger removal, and nitroglycerin paste is a fast-acting, easily titratable agent to reduce blood pressure.
column8: Administer intravenous hydralazine is incorrect because although effective, it is less predictable in onset and duration compared to topical nitrates and may cause hypotension.
column9: Order emergent head CT to rule out hemorrhage is incorrect because the clinical picture is classic for autonomic dysreflexia, and imaging is not warranted unless symptoms are atypical or refractory.
column10: Administer intravenous beta blocker is incorrect because beta blockers may worsen bradycardia and do not address the sympathetic overactivity characteristic of autonomic dysreflexia.

column2: A 28-year-old woman with a complete spinal cord injury at the T4 level is considering pregnancy. She is otherwise healthy and has no voluntary or sensory function below the level of injury. She asks about any special considerations during pregnancy and delivery. What is the most important counseling point to include in your discussion?
column3: Spinal or epidural anesthesia will be necessary during labor
column4: Pregnancy is contraindicated in women with SCI above T6 due to risk of preterm labor
column5: She will not be able to carry a pregnancy due to impaired uterine contractility
column6: Cesarean delivery is always required in women with SCI above T6
column7: Spinal or epidural anesthesia will be necessary during labor is correct because women with SCI at or above T6 may not feel uterine contractions and are at risk for hypertensive episodes during labor; regional anesthesia is recommended for safe and controlled delivery.
column8: Pregnancy is contraindicated in women with SCI above T6 due to risk of preterm labor is incorrect because pregnancy is not contraindicated after SCI, though additional monitoring and care are required.
column9: She will not be able to carry a pregnancy due to impaired uterine contractility is incorrect because uterine contractility is preserved as it is hormonally controlled and does not depend on voluntary innervation.
column10: Cesarean delivery is always required in women with SCI above T6 is incorrect because vaginal delivery is often possible, and cesarean is reserved for standard obstetric indications.

column2: A 30-year-old woman with a complete T5 spinal cord injury is in her third trimester of pregnancy and is meeting with her rehabilitation physician to discuss expectations for postpartum care. She expresses interest in breastfeeding and asks whether it will be possible given her injury. What is the most accurate counseling point?
column3: Yes, she can breastfeed because nipple stimulation (at T4) is intact and sufficient to trigger the neuroendocrine reflex for lactation
column4: No, breastfeeding is not possible because sensory input from the uterus is required to stimulate milk production
column5: Breastfeeding is only possible if sensation below the injury level is preserved
column6: No, her injury prevents activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis required for lactation
column7: Yes, she can breastfeed because nipple stimulation (at T4) is intact and sufficient to trigger the neuroendocrine reflex for lactation is correct because the neurohormonal release of prolactin and oxytocin depends on intact nipple stimulation, which she retains at T4 and above.
column8: No, breastfeeding is not possible because sensory input from the uterus is required to stimulate milk production is incorrect because uterine sensation is unrelated to the hormonal regulation of lactation.
column9: Breastfeeding is only possible if sensation below the injury level is preserved is incorrect because lactation does not require sensation below the level of injury and can occur with intact sensation at the nipple (T4 dermatome).
column10: No, her injury prevents activation of the hypothalamic-pituitary axis required for lactation is incorrect because spinal cord injury does not disrupt central neuroendocrine function.

column2: A 32-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury at T10 presents for rehabilitation follow-up. He reports requiring intermittent catheterization for bladder management. Which of the following best explains the neurophysiologic basis for his voiding dysfunction?
column3: Disruption of supraspinal input to the pontine micturition center and sacral cord reflex arc
column4: Direct injury to the sacral micturition center (S2–S4)
column5: Damage to peripheral pudendal nerves leading to flaccid detrusor and sphincter
column6: Complete loss of cortical awareness of bladder fullness
column7: Disruption of supraspinal input to the pontine micturition center and sacral cord reflex arc is correct because spinal cord injury above the sacral segments interrupts descending pathways that coordinate bladder contraction and sphincter relaxation, resulting in detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia.
column8: Direct injury to the sacral micturition center (S2–S4) is incorrect because his injury is at T10, which is above the sacral cord and would not directly damage the sacral micturition center.
column9: Damage to peripheral pudendal nerves leading to flaccid detrusor and sphincter is incorrect because peripheral nerve injury would result in areflexic bladder, which is not typical of isolated T10 cord lesions.
column10: Complete loss of cortical awareness of bladder fullness is incorrect because cortical perception may be impaired, but this alone does not account for the reflexive dyssynergia seen after suprasacral SCI.

column2: A 29-year-old woman with a complete spinal cord lesion at T11 is being evaluated for neurogenic bladder. Urodynamic testing reveals detrusor contractions with sustained internal sphincter tone during attempted voiding, leading to high bladder pressures. Which spinal cord region gives rise to the sympathetic control of the internal urethral sphincter?
column6: Dorsal column of the sacral spinal cord
column7: Thoracolumbar intermediolateral cell column (T11–L2) is correct because sympathetic fibers to the internal urethral sphincter travel via the hypogastric nerve, which originates from the T11–L2 intermediolateral cell column; this pathway facilitates sphincter contraction during storage and explains why a lesion at this level may disrupt coordinated voiding.
column8: Sacral parasympathetic nucleus (S2–S4) is incorrect because it provides parasympathetic input to the detrusor muscle, not sympathetic input to the internal sphincter.
column9: Pontine micturition center is incorrect because although it coordinates detrusor and sphincter activity, it is not the origin of the spinal autonomic pathways.
column10: Dorsal column of the sacral spinal cord is incorrect because it carries sensory input, not autonomic efferent control for the urinary system.

column2: A 55-year-old man with a tumor compressing the conus medullaris presents with urinary retention and a flaccid, distended bladder. He also has decreased rectal tone and absent bulbocavernosus reflex. Which of the following spinal cord structures is most directly responsible for detrusor contraction and is likely impaired?
column6: Dorsal columns of the sacral spinal cord
column7: Intermediolateral gray matter at S2–S4 is correct because this region contains the sacral parasympathetic nucleus, whose preganglionic neurons send fibers via the pelvic nerves to innervate the detrusor muscle; damage results in an areflexic (flaccid) bladder and urinary retention.
column8: Thoracolumbar intermediolateral cell column (T11–L2) is incorrect because it provides sympathetic outflow to the internal sphincter and bladder base, promoting storage rather than bladder emptying.
column9: Pontine micturition center is incorrect because it coordinates bladder and sphincter function but does not directly send autonomic output to the detrusor muscle.
column10: Dorsal columns of the sacral spinal cord are incorrect because they transmit sensory modalities like vibration and proprioception, not autonomic efferent signals for bladder contraction.

column2: A 35-year-old man with a T6 spinal cord injury develops detrusor overactivity and bladder-sphincter dyssynergia. Pharmacologic management is initiated to relax the bladder and improve storage. Which of the following receptor targets is responsible for promoting detrusor relaxation during bladder filling?
column7: Beta-3 adrenergic receptor is correct because stimulation of beta-3 receptors on detrusor smooth muscle promotes bladder relaxation and facilitates urine storage, which is desirable in patients with detrusor overactivity after spinal cord injury.
column8: Muscarinic M3 receptor is incorrect because activation of M3 receptors causes detrusor contraction, which would worsen bladder overactivity.
column9: Alpha-1 adrenergic receptor is incorrect because alpha-1 stimulation promotes contraction of the bladder neck and internal sphincter, impairing voiding.
column10: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor is incorrect because nicotinic receptors are involved in somatic neuromuscular transmission, not autonomic control of bladder smooth muscle.

column2: A 35-year-old man with a T6 spinal cord injury develops detrusor overactivity and bladder-sphincter dyssynergia. Pharmacologic management is initiated to relax the bladder and improve storage. Which of the following neurotransmitters is primarily responsible for activating the receptor that promotes detrusor relaxation during bladder filling?
column7: Norepinephrine is correct because it activates beta-3 adrenergic receptors on the detrusor muscle, promoting relaxation and bladder filling, which is a therapeutic target in neurogenic bladder after spinal cord injury.
column8: Acetylcholine is incorrect because it stimulates muscarinic M3 receptors, causing detrusor contraction rather than relaxation.
column9: Dopamine is incorrect because it does not play a direct role in bladder smooth muscle relaxation.
column10: Serotonin is incorrect because it modulates central pathways but does not directly act on bladder smooth muscle receptors to promote detrusor relaxation.

column2: A 40-year-old woman with a C7 spinal cord injury experiences episodes of urinary incontinence due to uninhibited detrusor contractions. Her physician prescribes a medication that targets the receptor responsible for detrusor contraction. Which of the following receptors is most directly responsible for this activity?
column7: Muscarinic M3 receptor is correct because activation of this cholinergic receptor by acetylcholine leads to detrusor smooth muscle contraction, and antimuscarinic agents are commonly used to manage detrusor overactivity after spinal cord injury.
column8: Beta-3 adrenergic receptor is incorrect because stimulation of this receptor causes detrusor relaxation to facilitate bladder filling, not contraction.
column9: Alpha-1 adrenergic receptor is incorrect because it promotes contraction of the bladder neck and internal sphincter, contributing to storage, not bladder contraction.
column10: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor is incorrect because it mediates somatic motor function at the external sphincter and skeletal muscles, not detrusor activity.

column2: A 40-year-old woman with a C7 spinal cord injury experiences episodes of involuntary detrusor contractions and urinary incontinence. Her condition is attributed to activation of receptors responsible for detrusor contraction. Which of the following neurotransmitters is primarily responsible for this action?
column7: Acetylcholine is correct because it binds to muscarinic M3 receptors on the detrusor muscle to trigger contraction, which contributes to bladder overactivity in spinal cord injury.
column8: Norepinephrine is incorrect because it acts on beta-3 adrenergic receptors to promote detrusor relaxation, not contraction.
column9: Glutamate is incorrect because it is a major excitatory neurotransmitter in the CNS but does not directly mediate bladder smooth muscle contraction.
column10: Dopamine is incorrect because it modulates central autonomic functions but does not play a direct role in detrusor contraction.

column2: A 62-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury at T12 is undergoing bladder management in the rehabilitation unit. Urodynamic testing reveals a flaccid detrusor muscle with a large post-void residual volume and only mild bladder contractions. His team considers pharmacologic management to stimulate detrusor activity. Which of the following agents is most appropriate to initiate in this setting?
column7: Bethanechol is correct because it is a muscarinic agonist that stimulates M3 receptors on detrusor smooth muscle, and is used in spinal cord injury to enhance weak bladder contractions in the setting of areflexic or hypocontractile bladder.
column8: Oxybutynin is incorrect because it is an antimuscarinic agent that reduces detrusor contractions and is used for detrusor overactivity, not hypocontractility.
column9: Tamsulosin is incorrect because it is an alpha-1 antagonist that reduces bladder outlet resistance but does not directly increase detrusor strength.
column10: Mirabegron is incorrect because it is a beta-3 agonist that promotes bladder relaxation, which would worsen retention in a hypocontractile bladder.

column2: A 58-year-old man with a spinal cord injury is prescribed bethanechol for persistent urinary retention due to a hypocontractile bladder. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of this medication?
column7: Direct muscarinic receptor agonist is correct because bethanechol directly stimulates muscarinic receptors, especially M3 receptors in the detrusor muscle, to promote bladder contraction in the setting of reduced parasympathetic output.
column8: Muscarinic receptor antagonist is incorrect because this class blocks cholinergic stimulation and would inhibit detrusor contraction, worsening urinary retention.
column9: Beta-3 adrenergic receptor agonist is incorrect because these agents promote bladder relaxation, not contraction, and are used to treat overactive bladder.
column10: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor is incorrect because this class increases endogenous acetylcholine levels indirectly but does not selectively stimulate muscarinic receptors like bethanechol does.

column2: A 43-year-old woman with a C6 spinal cord injury reports urinary incontinence due to detrusor overactivity confirmed on urodynamic testing. She is started on oxybutynin to manage her symptoms. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of this medication?
column7: Muscarinic receptor antagonist is correct because oxybutynin blocks muscarinic receptors, particularly M3 receptors, on the detrusor muscle, reducing involuntary bladder contractions in patients with detrusor overactivity.
column8: Beta-3 adrenergic receptor agonist is incorrect because this class promotes bladder relaxation but works through adrenergic, not cholinergic, pathways.
column9: Muscarinic receptor agonist is incorrect because stimulating muscarinic receptors would worsen detrusor overactivity by increasing bladder contractions.
column10: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor is incorrect because it increases endogenous acetylcholine and would exacerbate detrusor overactivity, not reduce it.

column2: A 43-year-old woman with a C6 spinal cord injury reports urinary urgency and incontinence. Urodynamic testing confirms detrusor overactivity. Which of the following medications is most appropriate to reduce detrusor contractions in this patient?
column7: Oxybutynin is correct because it is an antimuscarinic agent that blocks M3 receptors on the detrusor muscle, reducing involuntary contractions and improving bladder storage in patients with detrusor overactivity.
column8: Bethanechol is incorrect because it is a muscarinic agonist that stimulates detrusor contractions and is used for areflexic bladder, not detrusor overactivity.
column9: Tamsulosin is incorrect because it relaxes the bladder neck and prostate to improve voiding but does not reduce detrusor muscle activity.
column10: Pyridostigmine is incorrect because it increases acetylcholine levels and would enhance bladder contractions, worsening detrusor overactivity.

column2: A 60-year-old man with a T4 spinal cord injury is being treated for neurogenic detrusor overactivity. He previously experienced dry mouth and constipation while on anticholinergic therapy. His physician decides to switch him to a medication that reduces bladder overactivity without causing anticholinergic side effects. Which of the following is the most appropriate medication?
column7: Mirabegron is correct because it is a beta-3 adrenergic agonist that promotes detrusor relaxation during bladder filling without the anticholinergic side effects associated with muscarinic antagonists such as oxybutynin, which is the most commonly used agent in this class.
column8: Bethanechol is incorrect because it stimulates muscarinic receptors and increases detrusor contractions, which would worsen bladder overactivity.
column9: Prazosin is incorrect because it is an alpha-1 blocker that reduces bladder outlet resistance but does not address detrusor overactivity.
column10: Pyridostigmine is incorrect because it is a cholinesterase inhibitor that increases acetylcholine levels, potentially exacerbating detrusor contractions.

column2: A 68-year-old woman with incomplete tetraplegia is prescribed mirabegron for neurogenic detrusor overactivity. She previously had difficulty tolerating anticholinergic medications due to dry mouth and cognitive fog. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of mirabegron?
column7: Beta-3 adrenergic receptor agonist is correct because mirabegron activates beta-3 receptors on detrusor smooth muscle, promoting relaxation during bladder filling and improving storage capacity.
column8: Muscarinic M3 receptor antagonist is incorrect because this describes the action of anticholinergic medications such as oxybutynin, which the patient could not tolerate.
column9: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor is incorrect because it increases acetylcholine levels and may worsen bladder overactivity by stimulating detrusor contractions.
column10: Alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist is incorrect because these agents reduce bladder outlet resistance but do not directly relax the detrusor muscle.

column2: A 46-year-old man with a C6 ASIA B spinal cord injury is found to have detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia on urodynamic testing. He has not yet started treatment. If left untreated, which of the following is the most likely complication to develop?
column7: Vesicoureteral reflux is correct because DSD leads to elevated bladder pressures during voiding against a closed sphincter, which can push urine backward into the ureters, increasing the risk for hydronephrosis and renal damage.
column8: Urethral stricture is incorrect because it is more commonly caused by catheter trauma or infection and is not a direct consequence of bladder-sphincter dyssynergia.
column9: Bladder diverticulum is incorrect because it can occur due to chronically elevated bladder pressures but is less immediately threatening than reflux into the upper tracts.
column10: Urinary frequency is incorrect because it is a bothersome symptom, not the most clinically significant consequence of untreated DSD.

column2: A 33-year-old man with a T2 complete spinal cord injury is found to have detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia. His rehabilitation physician prescribes a medication to reduce urethral outlet resistance and improve bladder emptying. Which of the following best describes the mechanism of action of the prescribed drug?
column7: Alpha-1 adrenergic receptor antagonist is correct because tamsulosin blocks alpha-1 receptors in the bladder neck and internal urethral sphincter, reducing smooth muscle tone and relieving functional obstruction from sphincter dyssynergia.
column8: Muscarinic M3 receptor antagonist is incorrect because this mechanism applies to drugs like oxybutynin, which reduce detrusor overactivity but do not affect sphincter tone.
column9: Beta-3 adrenergic receptor agonist is incorrect because this mechanism promotes detrusor relaxation and bladder filling, not sphincter relaxation.
column10: Acetylcholinesterase inhibitor is incorrect because it increases acetylcholine levels and may worsen detrusor-sphincter dyssynergia by promoting detrusor contraction.

column2: A 28-year-old man with C6 ASIA A spinal cord injury is undergoing routine annual surveillance. Urinalysis reveals >100,000 colony-forming units of E. coli on culture, but the patient denies any urinary symptoms. He performs clean intermittent catheterization every 6 hours and has had no recent fevers, autonomic dysreflexia, or change in continence. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column7: Observe without antibiotic treatment is correct because asymptomatic bacteriuria is common in SCI patients using intermittent catheterization, and treatment does not improve outcomes and may increase resistance.
column8: Begin empiric oral ciprofloxacin is incorrect because treating asymptomatic bacteriuria increases antibiotic resistance without clinical benefit in this population.
column9: Start nitrofurantoin prophylactically for 6 months is incorrect because long-term antibiotic prophylaxis is not indicated for asymptomatic bacteriuria and may increase adverse events and resistance.
column10: Repeat urine culture in 3 days is incorrect because further testing is unnecessary in asymptomatic patients and may lead to inappropriate treatment.

column2: A 42-year-old man with T4 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents for routine follow-up. He performs clean intermittent catheterization and has no urinary complaints. Urinalysis reveals alkaline urine, and urine culture grows Proteus mirabilis with >100,000 CFU. He is afebrile and has had no recent episodes of autonomic dysreflexia, change in continence, or malaise. What is the most appropriate next step?
column3: Treat with antibiotics to prevent struvite stone formation
column6: Repeat urine culture in 1 week to confirm findings
column7: Treat with antibiotics to prevent struvite stone formation is correct because Proteus mirabilis is a urease-producing organism that alkalinizes urine and increases the risk of struvite stone formation, which justifies treatment in asymptomatic patients with SCI; other urease-producing organisms that warrant treatment include Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, Providencia, Morganella, Staphylococcus epidermidis, and Ureaplasma urealyticum—E. coli, although common, does not typically warrant treatment in asymptomatic cases.
column8: Observe without antibiotic treatment is incorrect because urease-producing organisms such as Proteus increase the risk of struvite calculi and require treatment even in the absence of symptoms.
column9: Initiate chronic suppressive antibiotic therapy is incorrect because suppressive therapy is not indicated unless the patient has frequent symptomatic infections or complications.
column10: Repeat urine culture in 1 week to confirm findings is incorrect because prompt treatment is required when urease-producing organisms are found in high-risk patients.

column2: A 19-year-old woman with spina bifida and a thoracic-level spinal cord injury is evaluated during routine urologic follow-up. She uses clean intermittent catheterization, has no urinary symptoms, and denies fevers or autonomic dysreflexia. Urinalysis reveals alkaline urine, and urine culture shows >100,000 CFU of E. coli. Renal ultrasound demonstrates mild bilateral hydronephrosis, and a voiding cystourethrogram confirms bilateral grade III vesicoureteral reflux. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column3: Treat with antibiotics due to increased risk of renal damage
column4: Observe without treatment, as E. coli is not a urease-producing organism
column6: Begin long-term suppressive antibiotics to prevent future infections
column7: Treat with antibiotics due to increased risk of renal damage is correct because vesicoureteral reflux is a recognized exception where treatment of asymptomatic bacteriuria is warranted in SCI patients due to the elevated risk of renal scarring and long-term renal dysfunction.
column8: Observe without treatment, as E. coli is not a urease-producing organism is incorrect because vesicoureteral reflux itself, not the organism type, drives the decision to treat in this scenario.
column9: Repeat urine culture in 2 weeks is incorrect because delaying treatment in the setting of reflux increases the risk of upper tract injury.
column10: Begin long-term suppressive antibiotics to prevent future infections is incorrect because suppressive therapy is not first-line and should only be considered after recurrent infections or based on urologist recommendation.

column2: A 25-year-old man with C7 ASIA A spinal cord injury is scheduled for a urodynamic study as part of his annual evaluation. He is asymptomatic, denies fevers or autonomic dysreflexia, and performs clean intermittent catheterization. Routine urine culture reveals >100,000 CFU of Escherichia coli, but he has no urinary complaints. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column3: Treat with antibiotics to reduce risk of procedure-related infection
column4: Observe without treatment, as E. coli does not require treatment when asymptomatic
column5: Proceed with urodynamic study and monitor for symptoms
column6: Delay the urodynamic study and repeat the urine culture in 1 week
column7: Treat with antibiotics to reduce risk of procedure-related infection is correct because urodynamic testing is an invasive procedure that may introduce bacteria into the upper urinary tract; treatment of asymptomatic bacteriuria is recommended before urologic instrumentation.
column8: Observe without treatment, as E. coli does not require treatment when asymptomatic is incorrect because upcoming urologic instrumentation is an exception to the general rule and warrants antibiotic therapy.
column9: Proceed with urodynamic study and monitor for symptoms is incorrect because this increases the risk of iatrogenic upper urinary tract infection in a high-risk patient.
column10: Delay the urodynamic study and repeat the urine culture in 1 week is incorrect because immediate antibiotic treatment is appropriate in preparation for the scheduled urologic procedure.

column2: A 45-year-old woman with a chronic T8 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents with increased tone in her legs over the past two days. She uses clean intermittent catheterization. Her temperature is 98.6°F. Urinalysis shows pyuria and positive nitrites, and urine culture grows >100,000 CFU of Escherichia coli. She denies flank pain, dysuria, or suprapubic tenderness. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column3: Initiate a course of antibiotics based on culture and sensitivity
column4: Do not treat because E. coli is not a urease-producing organism
column5: Observe and repeat urine culture in one week
column6: Begin long-term suppressive antibiotics to prevent recurrence
column7: Initiate a course of antibiotics based on culture and sensitivity is correct because the patient has signs of a symptomatic UTI in the setting of spinal cord injury, including new-onset increased spasticity, which is a recognized presentation of infection even in the absence of fever.
column8: Do not treat because E. coli is not a urease-producing organism is incorrect because the presence of symptoms, not organism type, dictates treatment in symptomatic cases.
column9: Observe and repeat urine culture in one week is incorrect because symptomatic UTIs in SCI patients must be treated promptly to prevent serious complications like autonomic dysreflexia or sepsis.
column10: Begin long-term suppressive antibiotics to prevent recurrence is incorrect because suppressive therapy is not indicated after a single symptomatic episode and carries risks of resistance.
column12: Treatment of urinary tract infection (UTI) in patients with spinal cord injury (SCI) is indicated when the patient develops clinical symptoms such as fever, malaise, increased spasticity, cloudy or foul-smelling urine, autonomic dysreflexia, or worsening incontinence.

column2: A 31-year-old man with a T12 ASIA A spinal cord injury is undergoing evaluation for fertility. He reports preserved erection with stimulation but inability to ejaculate. Which of the following best explains the impaired ejaculation in this patient?
column3: Ejaculation is primarily mediated by sympathetic fibers from T11–L2 via the hypogastric nerve
column4: Ejaculation is primarily controlled by parasympathetic fibers from S2–S4 via the pelvic nerve
column5: Ejaculation is mediated by somatic efferents from S2–S4 via the pudendal nerve
column6: Ejaculation depends on supraspinal control from the pontine micturition center
column7: Ejaculation is primarily mediated by sympathetic fibers from T11–L2 via the hypogastric nerve is correct because the emission phase of ejaculation is controlled by sympathetic output from the thoracolumbar cord (T11–L2) that travels through the hypogastric nerve, and injury at T12 disrupts this pathway, impairing emission.
column8: Ejaculation is primarily mediated by sympathetic fibers from T11–L2 via the hypogastric nerve is correct because the emission phase of ejaculation is controlled by sympathetic output from the thoracolumbar cord (T11–L2) that travels through the hypogastric nerve, and injury at T12 disrupts this pathway, impairing emission despite preserved reflexogenic erection.
column9: Ejaculation is primarily controlled by parasympathetic fibers from S2–S4 via the pelvic nerve is incorrect because parasympathetic fibers from S2–S4 primarily mediate erection rather than ejaculation.
column10: Ejaculation depends on supraspinal control from the pontine micturition center is incorrect because ejaculation is mediated by spinal reflex pathways and does not require supraspinal input, particularly in the presence of complete spinal cord injury.

column2: A 30-year-old man with a T11 ASIA A spinal cord injury is evaluated for anejaculation. He has preserved reflex erections but is unable to ejaculate with stimulation. Which of the following spinal cord levels is most critical for the sympathetic control of emission during ejaculation?
column7: T11–L2 is correct because the emission phase of ejaculation is mediated by sympathetic fibers originating in the thoracolumbar spinal cord (T11–L2), which travel via the hypogastric nerve and are commonly disrupted in injuries at or above this level.
column8: S2–S4 is incorrect because these sacral parasympathetic levels are primarily involved in erection, not the sympathetic emission phase.
column9: C5–T1 is incorrect because these levels are associated with upper limb innervation and have no direct role in ejaculation.
column10: L4–S1 is incorrect because these segments contribute to lower limb motor control and are not part of the sympathetic pathways governing ejaculation.

column2: A 36-year-old man with a complete spinal cord injury localized to the sacral spinal cord (S2–S4) is evaluated for sexual dysfunction. He cannot achieve erections with direct genital stimulation but reports occasional erections in response to visual sexual stimuli. Which of the following best explains his erectile function?
column3: Reflex erections are absent due to sacral segment injury; psychogenic erections are preserved due to intact descending sympathetic pathways
column4: Reflex erections are preserved due to intact brainstem control; psychogenic erections are absent due to sacral injury
column5: Both reflex and psychogenic erections are preserved due to intact conus function
column6: Reflex erections are absent due to loss of thoracolumbar sympathetic input; psychogenic erections are preserved due to intact pudendal nerve
column7: Reflex erections are absent due to sacral segment injury; psychogenic erections are preserved due to intact descending sympathetic pathways is correct because S2–S4 controls reflex erections through parasympathetic innervation, while descending supraspinal input to T11–L2 sympathetic centers allows psychogenic erections to remain intact.
column8: Reflex erections are preserved due to intact brainstem control; psychogenic erections are absent due to sacral injury is incorrect because reflex erections require intact sacral segments (S2–S4), not brainstem control.
column9: Both reflex and psychogenic erections are preserved due to intact conus function is incorrect because the conus (which contains S2–S4) is affected in this case, leading to loss of reflex erections.
column10: Reflex erections are absent due to loss of thoracolumbar sympathetic input; psychogenic erections are preserved due to intact pudendal nerve is incorrect because reflex erections rely on parasympathetic S2–S4, not thoracolumbar sympathetic output, and the pudendal nerve mediates somatic function, not psychogenic erections.

column2: A 32-year-old man with a T5 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents for evaluation of infertility. He is able to achieve reflex erections but has been unable to ejaculate. Semen analysis obtained via electroejaculation reveals low sperm motility and high leukocyte concentration. Which of the following best explains the cause of infertility in men with spinal cord injury?
column3: Impaired ejaculation and poor semen quality due to altered seminal fluid composition
column4: Low testosterone production due to disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis
column5: Azoospermia due to testicular atrophy from prolonged immobility
column6: Failure of spermatogenesis due to absent cremasteric reflex
column7: Impaired ejaculation and poor semen quality due to altered seminal fluid composition is correct because men with SCI often have difficulty with ejaculation and exhibit inflammatory changes in semen, including high white blood cells and poor motility, both of which contribute to infertility.
column8: Low testosterone production due to disruption of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal axis is incorrect because testosterone levels are typically normal in SCI, as the axis remains intact.
column9: Azoospermia due to testicular atrophy from prolonged immobility is incorrect because sperm production generally continues after SCI, and azoospermia is uncommon.
column10: Failure of spermatogenesis due to absent cremasteric reflex is incorrect because the cremasteric reflex is unrelated to spermatogenesis, which occurs independently within the testes.

column2: Which of the following statements about sexual function after spinal cord injury is false?
column3: Psychogenic erections are typically preserved in men with complete cervical injuries
column4: Men with upper motor neuron injuries often retain reflexogenic erections
column5: Women with SCI are typically able to conceive and carry pregnancies to term
column6: Semen quality is often abnormal in men with SCI, even if ejaculation is achieved
column7: Psychogenic erections are typically preserved in men with complete cervical injuries is correct because this statement is false — complete cervical spinal cord injuries interrupt descending pathways needed for psychogenic erections.
column8: Men with upper motor neuron injuries often retain reflexogenic erections is incorrect because this is a true statement; the sacral reflex arc is intact in UMN injuries, allowing reflex erections with genital stimulation.
column9: Women with SCI are typically able to conceive and carry pregnancies to term is incorrect because this is a true statement; although pregnancy requires specialized care, fertility is generally preserved.
column10: Semen quality is often abnormal in men with SCI, even if ejaculation is achieved is incorrect because this is a true statement; semen often shows elevated leukocytes and reduced motility post-injury.

column2: A 28-year-old woman with a complete T6 spinal cord injury is being counseled on sexual and reproductive health. Which of the following statements about female function after spinal cord injury is false?
column3: Women with SCI cannot become pregnant due to disruption of hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian signaling
column4: Most women with SCI are capable of achieving orgasm through genital or non-genital stimulation
column5: Menstruation typically resumes within 6 months after injury in premenopausal women
column6: Vaginal lubrication may be impaired due to autonomic nervous system disruption
column7: Women with SCI cannot become pregnant due to disruption of hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian signaling is correct because this statement is false — the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis remains intact after SCI, so fertility is generally preserved.
column8: Most women with SCI are capable of achieving orgasm through genital or non-genital stimulation is incorrect because this is a true statement; many women retain orgasmic ability despite altered sensation.
column9: Menstruation typically resumes within 6 months after injury in premenopausal women is incorrect because this is a true statement; transient amenorrhea is common but menstruation generally returns.
column10: Vaginal lubrication may be impaired due to autonomic nervous system disruption is incorrect because this is a true statement; loss of parasympathetic input from S2–S4 can reduce lubrication.

column2: A 30-year-old man with a complete T8 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents for evaluation of sexual dysfunction. He reports the ability to achieve erections with genital stimulation but denies erections in response to visual or mental arousal. Which of the following best explains this pattern?
column3: Reflex erections are preserved via intact sacral segments; psychogenic erections are lost due to interrupted descending pathways
column4: Reflex erections are lost due to thoracic cord injury; psychogenic erections are preserved due to intact brainstem circuits
column5: Both reflex and psychogenic erections are absent due to loss of sympathetic and parasympathetic input
column6: Reflex erections are preserved by pudendal nerve stimulation; psychogenic erections are lost due to hypothalamic dysfunction
column7: Reflex erections are preserved via intact sacral segments; psychogenic erections are lost due to interrupted descending pathways is correct because reflex erections arise from S2–S4 and remain functional with direct stimulation, while psychogenic erections require descending input disrupted by complete thoracic SCI.
column8: Reflex erections are lost due to thoracic cord injury; psychogenic erections are preserved due to intact brainstem circuits is incorrect because reflex erections depend on sacral circuits, not the thoracic cord or brainstem.
column9: Both reflex and psychogenic erections are absent due to loss of sympathetic and parasympathetic input is incorrect because parasympathetic reflex arcs remain intact below a thoracic lesion.
column10: Reflex erections are preserved by pudendal nerve stimulation; psychogenic erections are lost due to hypothalamic dysfunction is incorrect because psychogenic erections are lost due to spinal cord interruption, not hypothalamic dysfunction.

column2: A 29-year-old woman with a T4 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents for counseling on reproductive health. She has had amenorrhea since her injury 3 months ago. She notes reduced genital sensation and lack of vaginal lubrication during sexual activity. She expresses concern about her ability to conceive. Which of the following statements best explains her current and future reproductive potential?
column3: She likely has preserved fertility but may continue to have impaired vaginal lubrication due to disruption of descending autonomic input to S2–S4
column4: She is infertile due to loss of hypothalamic-pituitary signaling from supraspinal injury
column5: She is unlikely to resume menstruation because sacral cord function is impaired
column6: She cannot become pregnant naturally due to interruption of the pelvic splanchnic nerves
column7: She likely has preserved fertility but may continue to have impaired vaginal lubrication due to disruption of descending autonomic input to S2–S4 is correct because T4 injuries preserve the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis (fertility), but disrupt descending input to parasympathetic centers, affecting vaginal lubrication.
column8: She is infertile due to loss of hypothalamic-pituitary signaling from supraspinal injury is incorrect because the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian axis remains intact in SCI unless there is direct brain injury.
column9: She is unlikely to resume menstruation because sacral cord function is impaired is incorrect because sacral cord function is not impaired in a T4 injury; menstruation typically resumes within 6 months.
column10: She cannot become pregnant naturally due to interruption of the pelvic splanchnic nerves is incorrect because these nerves are intact at S2–S4, and even if lubrication is affected, ovulation and conception are still possible.

column2: A 26-year-old woman with a complete T4 ASIA A spinal cord injury is 38 weeks pregnant and presents with a sudden pounding headache, nasal congestion, facial flushing, and elevated blood pressure. She denies any abdominal pain or uterine contractions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
column3: Assess for uterine contractions as a potential trigger for autonomic dysreflexia due to labor
column4: Administer antihypertensives and discharge the patient with close outpatient follow-up
column5: Rule out eclampsia given the elevated blood pressure and proceed with magnesium sulfate
column6: Obtain head imaging to evaluate for intracranial hemorrhage given the new-onset headache
