column7: Assess for uterine contractions as a potential trigger for autonomic dysreflexia due to labor is correct because women with SCI above T6 may not feel labor pain; autonomic dysreflexia can be the only presenting sign of labor due to loss of ascending sensory input.
column8: Administer antihypertensives and discharge the patient with close outpatient follow-up is incorrect because it misses the underlying trigger of autonomic dysreflexia, which must be identified and treated — labor is an emergency if unrecognized.
column9: Rule out eclampsia given the elevated blood pressure and proceed with magnesium sulfate is incorrect because she has no seizures, proteinuria, or other signs of preeclampsia/eclampsia; the symptoms are better explained by autonomic dysreflexia in a patient with high-level SCI.
column10: Obtain head imaging to evaluate for intracranial hemorrhage given the new-onset headache is incorrect because the constellation of headache, flushing, nasal congestion, and high BP in a T4 SCI patient is classic for autonomic dysreflexia, not intracranial pathology.

column2: A 34-year-old woman with a complete C7 ASIA A spinal cord injury is in active labor at 40 weeks gestation. PM&R was consulted prior to delivery and recommended early epidural anesthesia, which was initiated appropriately. Despite a functioning epidural catheter, she now develops facial flushing, pounding headache, bradycardia, and a blood pressure of 200/110 mmHg. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
column3: Administer short-acting antihypertensive medication and evaluate for uterine or bladder triggers
column4: Discontinue epidural anesthesia and switch to systemic opioids for pain control
column5: Perform emergent cesarean section to remove the provoking stimulus
column6: Observe and recheck blood pressure in 15 minutes as symptoms are likely to self-resolve
column7: Administer short-acting antihypertensive medication and evaluate for uterine or bladder triggers is correct because this is refractory autonomic dysreflexia despite a functioning epidural; immediate BP control and identification of triggers (e.g., uterine contractions, bladder distension) are essential, as emphasized in PM&R consultation.
column8: Discontinue epidural anesthesia and switch to systemic opioids for pain control is incorrect because the epidural reduces AD risk and opioids won’t treat the hypertensive crisis.
column9: Perform emergent cesarean section to remove the provoking stimulus is incorrect because cesarean delivery is not first-line unless obstetrically indicated; treat AD medically first.
column10: Observe and recheck blood pressure in 15 minutes as symptoms are likely to self-resolve is incorrect because AD is a hypertensive emergency and requires immediate treatment to prevent stroke or seizure.

column2: A 34-year-old woman with a complete C7 ASIA A spinal cord injury is in active labor at 40 weeks gestation. PM&R was consulted prior to delivery and recommended early epidural anesthesia, which was initiated. Despite a functioning epidural catheter and multiple doses of short-acting antihypertensive medications, she continues to experience severe autonomic dysreflexia with systolic blood pressures >200 mmHg, facial flushing, headache, and bradycardia. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column3: Proceed with emergent cesarean section to safely deliver the baby and resolve the triggering stimulus
column4: Increase the epidural dose and administer another antihypertensive
column5: Observe and monitor closely, as symptoms will likely resolve with labor progression
column6: Administer intravenous opioids for central sympathetic inhibition
column7: Proceed with emergent cesarean section to safely deliver the baby and resolve the triggering stimulus is correct because this patient has refractory AD despite optimal epidural and antihypertensive treatment, and continued uterine contractions are the likely trigger; cesarean section is now indicated to protect both mother and fetus.
column8: Increase the epidural dose and administer another antihypertensive is incorrect because this has already been attempted; continued delay increases risk of stroke, seizure, and fetal compromise.
column9: Observe and monitor closely, as symptoms will likely resolve with labor progression is incorrect because AD is a hypertensive emergency; passive observation risks maternal and fetal harm.
column10: Administer intravenous opioids for central sympathetic inhibition is incorrect because opioids are not effective for treating AD and may obscure vital signs or cause respiratory depression.

column2: A 30-year-old woman at 37 weeks gestation with a complete T3 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents with sudden-onset hypertension (BP 190/100 mmHg), pounding headache, nasal congestion, facial flushing, and bradycardia. She denies visual changes. Urinalysis is negative for proteinuria, and she has no peripheral edema. PM&R was consulted during pregnancy for autonomic dysreflexia risk. Which of the following findings most reliably distinguishes autonomic dysreflexia from preeclampsia?
column6: Systolic blood pressure greater than 160 mmHg
column7: Hypertension accompanied by bradycardia is correct because paradoxical bradycardia with severe hypertension is a hallmark of autonomic dysreflexia, due to intact baroreceptor reflexes acting on the vagus nerve in the context of uncontrolled sympathetic discharge below the lesion.
column8: Absence of proteinuria on urinalysis is incorrect because preeclampsia with severe features can occur without proteinuria; thus, this finding is not specific for distinguishing AD.
column9: Headache triggered by uterine contractions is incorrect because this can be seen in either condition — uterine contractions can trigger AD, but labor itself can also exacerbate preeclampsia symptoms.
column10: Systolic blood pressure greater than 160 mmHg is incorrect because this BP threshold is common to both disorders, and does not help differentiate between them.

column2: A 32-year-old woman with a complete T2 ASIA A spinal cord injury is admitted at 39 weeks gestation for labor induction. She has an epidural catheter in place. During labor, she experiences episodic facial flushing, severe headache, and systolic blood pressure spikes to 200 mmHg. The obstetric team notes that these hypertensive episodes consistently occur during uterine contractions on the fetal monitor. She denies abdominal pain or visual changes. Urinalysis is negative for proteinuria. Which of the following findings most strongly supports autonomic dysreflexia as the cause of her symptoms?
column3: Temporal association of blood pressure spikes with uterine contractions
column5: Absence of abdominal or pelvic pain despite active labor
column6: Facial flushing and headache during labor
column7: Temporal association of blood pressure spikes with uterine contractions is correct because this pattern is characteristic of autonomic dysreflexia, in which visceral noxious stimuli (e.g., uterine contractions) below the level of injury trigger a sympathetic surge leading to episodic hypertension.
column8: Negative urinalysis for proteinuria is incorrect because preeclampsia may present without proteinuria, so this finding does not reliably exclude it.
column9: Absence of abdominal or pelvic pain despite active labor is incorrect because this is expected in a complete SCI above T10 and does not distinguish between AD and other conditions.
column10: Facial flushing and headache during labor is incorrect because these are nonspecific symptoms that can occur in both AD and preeclampsia.

column2: A 29-year-old man with a T6 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents for bowel program optimization. He has constipation and relies on digital stimulation and suppositories to induce bowel movements. His sacral reflexes are intact. Which neural pathway is most likely disrupted in this patient’s neurogenic bowel syndrome?
column3: Supraspinal input to the sacral defecation reflex arc
column4: Parasympathetic outflow from the pelvic splanchnic nerves
column5: Enteric nervous system control within the colonic wall
column6: Somatic efferents through the pudendal nerve
column7: Supraspinal input to the sacral defecation reflex arc is correct because a complete spinal cord injury above the sacral cord disrupts voluntary brain control over bowel function, resulting in a reflexive (UMN) bowel where reflex defecation can still occur via sacral pathways. This is a hyperreflexic neurogenic bowel.
column8: Parasympathetic outflow from the pelvic splanchnic nerves is incorrect because a T6 lesion spares the sacral cord (S2–S4), so parasympathetic innervation remains intact.
column9: Enteric nervous system control within the colonic wall is incorrect because the enteric nervous system operates independently of the spinal cord and remains functional after SCI.
column10: Somatic efferents through the pudendal nerve is incorrect because the pudendal nerve arises from S2–S4, which is below the lesion and remains structurally intact in this case.
column12: In a complete spinal cord injury, the spinal cord tissue below the level of injury is anatomically intact but functionally disconnected from the brain. This means that while voluntary control is lost, local reflex arcs (such as those governing bowel, bladder, and sexual reflexes) may remain functional if the reflex center itself is below the lesion and undamaged. For example, in a T6 complete injury, the sacral spinal cord (S2–S4) remains intact, allowing reflex bowel movements triggered by stimulation, even though the patient cannot consciously initiate defecation. In contrast, injuries affecting the sacral cord or cauda equina damage the reflex centers themselves, resulting in areflexic (lower motor neuron) bowel and bladder.

column2: A 64-year-old man presents with severe low back pain, saddle anesthesia, and fecal incontinence following a large L5–S1 disc herniation. He has flaccid anal tone, absent bulbocavernosus reflex, and no urge to defecate. Bowel programs using suppositories and digital stimulation have been ineffective. Which neural structure is most likely impaired in this patient’s bowel dysfunction?
column3: Sacral nerve roots contributing to pelvic splanchnic nerves
column4: Corticospinal tracts descending from the motor cortex
column5: Sympathetic fibers from the thoracolumbar spinal cord
column6: Enteric ganglia in the submucosal and myenteric plexus
column7: Sacral nerve roots contributing to pelvic splanchnic nerves is correct because this patient's L5–S1 disc herniation has caused lower motor neuron bowel dysfunction by injuring the S2–S4 nerve roots, which carry the parasympathetic and somatic innervation responsible for reflex defecation and external sphincter control. This is how  cauda equina syndrome affects the bowel causing an areflexic neurogenic bowel.
column8: Corticospinal tracts descending from the motor cortex is incorrect because these tracts are not involved in lower motor neuron lesions involving peripheral nerve roots.
column9: Sympathetic fibers from the thoracolumbar spinal cord is incorrect because the lesion is below the level of T12–L2, so sympathetic outflow is unaffected.
column10: Enteric ganglia in the submucosal and myenteric plexus is incorrect because the enteric nervous system functions independently of spinal innervation and remains intact in this setting.
column12: In a complete spinal cord injury, the spinal cord tissue below the level of injury is anatomically intact but functionally disconnected from the brain. This means that while voluntary control is lost, local reflex arcs (such as those governing bowel, bladder, and sexual reflexes) may remain functional if the reflex center itself is below the lesion and undamaged. For example, in a T6 complete injury, the sacral spinal cord (S2–S4) remains intact, allowing reflex bowel movements triggered by stimulation, even though the patient cannot consciously initiate defecation. In contrast, injuries affecting the sacral cord or cauda equina damage the reflex centers themselves, resulting in areflexic (lower motor neuron) bowel and bladder.

column2: A 64-year-old man with bowel incontinence and flaccid anal tone following a large L5–S1 disc herniation is diagnosed with lower motor neuron (areflexic) bowel due to cauda equina syndrome. He has no rectal sensation or urge to defecate. Which of the following is the most appropriate component of his bowel program?
column3: Scheduled manual evacuation at the same time daily
column4: Digital stimulation to trigger reflex defecation
column5: Bisacodyl suppositories to initiate sacral reflexes
column6: Avoidance of rectal interventions and reliance on bulk-forming fiber alone
column7: Scheduled manual evacuation at the same time daily is correct because patients with LMN bowel lack sacral reflexes, requiring manual removal of stool to prevent impaction, best done on a regular schedule using stool softeners and timing based on colonic motility.
column8: Digital stimulation to trigger reflex defecation is incorrect because this technique depends on an intact sacral reflex arc, which is disrupted in LMN bowel.
column9: Bisacodyl suppositories to initiate sacral reflexes is incorrect because stimulant suppositories are ineffective when the sacral cord or its nerve roots are damaged and cannot mediate reflex activity.
column10: Avoidance of rectal interventions and reliance on bulk-forming fiber alone is incorrect because fiber without active evacuation methods may worsen impaction in patients with areflexic bowel.
column12: In a complete spinal cord injury, the spinal cord tissue below the level of injury is anatomically intact but functionally disconnected from the brain. This means that while voluntary control is lost, local reflex arcs (such as those governing bowel, bladder, and sexual reflexes) may remain functional if the reflex center itself is below the lesion and undamaged. For example, in a T6 complete injury, the sacral spinal cord (S2–S4) remains intact, allowing reflex bowel movements triggered by stimulation, even though the patient cannot consciously initiate defecation. In contrast, injuries affecting the sacral cord or cauda equina damage the reflex centers themselves, resulting in areflexic (lower motor neuron) bowel and bladder.

column2: A 27-year-old man with a complete C6 spinal cord injury is admitted to the ICU 24 hours after his injury. He reports persistent nausea and abdominal bloating. His abdomen is distended with minimal bowel sounds. A nasogastric tube placed for decompression drains over 800 mL of bilious fluid in the first 8 hours. Abdominal X-ray shows no evidence of obstruction. What is the most likely explanation for his gastrointestinal findings?
column3: Gastric atony due to impaired vagal and sympathetic coordination
column4: Mechanical small bowel obstruction from spinal shock
column5: Bowel ischemia due to autonomic dysreflexia
column6: Gastroparesis from opioid analgesic use alone
column7: Gastric atony due to impaired vagal and sympathetic coordination is correct because acute cervical SCI disrupts autonomic control of gastric motility, leading to gastric stasis, abdominal distension, and bilious NG drainage in the absence of obstruction — all signs consistent with gastric atony in spinal shock.
column8: Mechanical small bowel obstruction from spinal shock is incorrect because spinal shock does not cause physical obstruction, and imaging rules it out.
column9: Bowel ischemia due to autonomic dysreflexia is incorrect because autonomic dysreflexia typically develops later in chronic SCI and presents with paroxysmal hypertension, not early gastric stasis.
column10: Gastroparesis from opioid analgesic use alone is incorrect because while opioids reduce GI motility, the timing and severity of this presentation are more consistent with neurogenic gastric atony due to acute autonomic disruption.

column2: A 31-year-old man with a T6 ASIA A spinal cord injury is developing a bowel program due to persistent constipation. He has intact sacral reflexes and no voluntary bowel control. Currently, digital stimulation and a bisacodyl suppository are administered before breakfast each day, but bowel movements remain inconsistent. Which of the following changes would most effectively utilize his preserved reflexes to enhance bowel program success?
column4: Add an osmotic laxative in the evening
column5: Perform digital stimulation both before and after meals
column7: Administer bowel care after breakfast is correct because the gastrocolic reflex, which promotes colonic motility in response to gastric filling, remains intact in upper motor neuron bowel and can be optimized by timing care to follow meals.
column8: Add an osmotic laxative in the evening is incorrect because this may help soften stool but does not improve coordination with reflex-mediated colonic activity.
column9: Perform digital stimulation both before and after meals is incorrect because increasing stimulation frequency adds care burden without addressing the underlying timing issue with the gastrocolic reflex.
column10: Increase dietary fiber is incorrect because while it may improve stool bulk, it does not compensate for poor synchronization with colonic motility reflexes.

column2: A 29-year-old man with a complete T4 ASIA A spinal cord injury is undergoing evaluation for a bowel program. He has no voluntary bowel control but retains reflex activity below the lesion. His PM&R team recommends performing digital rectal stimulation to initiate defecation. The colonic motor response to this intervention is primarily mediated by which mechanism?
column3: Parasympathetic reflex pathway activated by rectal wall afferents
column4: Stretch of intrinsic rectal ganglia triggering local peristalsis
column5: Vagal afferent feedback loop via the dorsal motor nucleus
column6: Enteric pacemaker cell activation near the rectosigmoid junction
column7: Parasympathetic reflex pathway activated by rectal wall afferents is correct because digital stimulation engages afferents in the rectal wall that synapse in the sacral spinal cord, activating parasympathetic efferents (S2–S4) to trigger colonic contractions — the core mechanism behind the rectocolic reflex.
column8: Stretch of intrinsic rectal ganglia triggering local peristalsis is incorrect because intrinsic ganglia (enteric nervous system) mediate local motility but cannot produce coordinated defecation in the absence of suprasegmental or reflex input.
column9: Vagal afferent feedback loop via the dorsal motor nucleus is incorrect because the vagus nerve supplies upper GI tract control and is not involved in sacral defecation reflexes.
column10: Enteric pacemaker cell activation near the rectosigmoid junction is incorrect because these pacemakers drive baseline slow waves, not reflexively triggered coordinated contractions for evacuation.

column2: A 22-year-old man is 4 weeks post–T3 ASIA A spinal cord injury after a motorcycle crash. He presents with nausea, diffuse abdominal pain, and worsening confusion. Labs reveal: Serum calcium: 12.8 mg/dL (normal: 8.5–10.5), Phosphate: low, PTH: suppressed, Creatinine: normal, 25-OH vitamin D: normal. Which of the following best explains the etiology of his current condition?
column3: Osteoclastic activation secondary to immobilization-induced bone loss
column4: PTH-mediated calcium mobilization from unmasked primary hyperparathyroidism
column5: Iatrogenic vitamin D toxicity from rehabilitation supplementation
column6: Sympathetic denervation causing renal calcium retention
column7: Osteoclastic activation secondary to immobilization-induced bone loss is correct because in the subacute phase of spinal cord injury, particularly in young men, immobilization rapidly stimulates bone resorption via increased osteoclastic activity, resulting in hypercalcemia with suppressed PTH and low phosphate.
column8: PTH-mediated calcium mobilization from unmasked primary hyperparathyroidism is incorrect because the patient’s PTH is suppressed, ruling out a PTH-driven cause.
column9: Iatrogenic vitamin D toxicity from rehabilitation supplementation is incorrect because his vitamin D levels are normal, and there is no history of high-dose supplementation.
column10: Sympathetic denervation causing renal calcium retention is incorrect because while autonomic disruption can affect renal function, it does not explain increased total body calcium with suppressed PTH and low phosphate.

column2: A 19-year-old man is 3 weeks post–C5 ASIA A spinal cord injury after a diving accident. He is noted to be lethargic, anorexic, and intermittently confused. Physical exam reveals dry mucous membranes and reduced muscle tone. His lab results reveal: Serum calcium: 13.0 mg/dL, Phosphate: low, PTH: suppressed, Creatinine: normal. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for his current symptoms?
column4: Acute renal insufficiency secondary to autonomic dysfunction
column5: Unrecognized malignancy producing parathyroid hormone–related peptide
column6: Vitamin D toxicity from high-dose supplementation during rehabilitation
column7: Immobilization-induced bone resorption is correct because Braddom notes that hypercalcemia may occur within 1 to 4 weeks after SCI due to rapid osteoclastic bone loss, especially in young males.
column8: Acute renal insufficiency secondary to autonomic dysfunction is incorrect because the patient has normal creatinine, and this mechanism does not account for suppressed PTH and high calcium.
column9: Unrecognized malignancy producing parathyroid hormone–related peptide is incorrect because there is no history or exam findings suggesting malignancy, and SCI is a well-known cause of hypercalcemia in this timeframe.
column10: Vitamin D toxicity from high-dose supplementation during rehabilitation is incorrect because Braddom does not cite vitamin D toxicity as a common cause in SCI-related hypercalcemia.
column12: “Bones, stones, groans, and psychiatric overtones” is a mnemonic summarizing hypercalcemia symptoms: bone pain(from resorption), kidney stones (from hypercalciuria), abdominal groans (GI symptoms like constipation and nausea), and psychiatric overtones (neurocognitive changes such as lethargy, confusion, or depression).

column2: Which of the following is the leading cause of death in patients with chronic spinal cord injury?
column7: Pneumonia is correct because it is the leading cause of death in individuals with chronic spinal cord injury, due to impaired cough, decreased vital capacity, and reduced pulmonary clearance mechanisms.
column8: Cardiovascular disease is incorrect because although it is a significant contributor to morbidity and mortality in SCI, it is not the leading cause of death.
column9: Pulmonary embolism is incorrect because it is more commonly a cause of death in the acute phase of SCI rather than in t
column10: Renal failure is incorrect because advances in bladder management, infection control, and urologic surveillance have markedly reduced renal-related mortality in patients with chronic spinal cord injury.

column2: A 29-year-old man with a C6 ASIA A spinal cord injury undergoes pulmonary function testing six months after injury. He has no prior history of pulmonary disease. Which of the following findings is most consistent with his pulmonary physiology?
column3: Reduced total lung capacity with preserved FEV1/FVC ratio
column4: Normal total lung capacity with mildly reduced FEV1 and reduced FEV1/FVC ratio
column5: Reduced total lung capacity with reduced FEV1/FVC ratio
column6: Normal total lung capacity with normal FEV1 and mildly increased FEV1/FVC ratio
column7: Reduced total lung capacity with preserved FEV1/FVC ratio is correct because tetraplegia causes a restrictive ventilatory defect due to weakness of respiratory musculature, resulting in decreased lung volumes with preserved airflow ratios.
column8: Normal total lung capacity with mildly reduced FEV1 and reduced FEV1/FVC ratio is incorrect because this obstructive pattern is not seen in spinal cord injury.
column9: Reduced total lung capacity with reduced FEV1/FVC ratio is incorrect because spinal cord injury produces a restrictive ventilatory defect with preserved or increased FEV1/FVC ratio rather than an obstructive pattern.
column10: Normal total lung capacity with normal FEV1 and mildly increased FEV1/FVC ratio is incorrect because tetraplegia reduces lung volumes due to respiratory muscle weakness, making a normal total lung capacity inconsistent with this injury.

column2: A 33-year-old man with a C5 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents with ineffective secretion clearance despite routine suctioning. He retains partial diaphragmatic function but lacks effective abdominal and intercostal muscle control. Which of the following best describes the manual technique used to simulate an effective cough in this patient?
column3: Apply an abdominal thrust in synchrony with the patient’s active exhalation following assisted maximal inhalation
column4: Use glossopharyngeal breathing to increase inspiratory volume before a voluntary cough
column5: Trigger mechanical insufflation-exsufflation at the end of a spontaneous inspiration
column6: Initiate suctioning during inspiratory flow to remove secretions before airway collapse
column7: Apply an abdominal thrust in synchrony with the patient’s active exhalation following assisted maximal inhalation is correct because manual cough assist (quad cough) mimics natural coughing by combining lung inflation with a timed abdominal thrust during expiration to generate expulsive force.
column8: Use glossopharyngeal breathing to increase inspiratory volume before a voluntary cough is incorrect because this technique is used for breath stacking in high cervical injuries but does not substitute for an abdominal thrust.
column9: Trigger mechanical insufflation-exsufflation at the end of a spontaneous inspiration is incorrect because the device uses alternating positive and negative pressures and does not require patient-initiated timing.
column10: Initiate suctioning during inspiratory flow to remove secretions before airway collapse is incorrect because suctioning is not synchronized with inspiration and does not mimic the mechanics of a cough.

column2: A 25-year-old man with a C1 ASIA A spinal cord injury is dependent on mechanical ventilation but has intact cranial nerves and is undergoing a rehabilitation program aimed at increasing his independence. He is learning a technique that allows him to take unaided breaths by using his mouth and throat to draw in small boluses of air. Which of the following statements best characterizes the technique he is using?
column3: It uses sequential tongue and pharyngeal movements to push boluses of air past the glottis and into the lungs
column4: It uses abdominal thrusts timed to expiration to simulate a forceful cough
column5: It requires the use of a mechanical device to alternate positive and negative airway pressure
column6: It involves passive lung inflation by a caregiver using a self-inflating bag and a one-way valve
column7: It uses sequential tongue and pharyngeal movements to push boluses of air past the glottis and into the lungs is correct because this describes glossopharyngeal breathing, which enables high cervical SCI patients to temporarily breathe without mechanical support by actively gulping air into their lungs using oropharyngeal muscle contractions.
column8: It uses abdominal thrusts timed to expiration to simulate a forceful cough is incorrect because this refers to manually assisted cough (quad cough), not inspiratory augmentation.
column9: It requires the use of a mechanical device to alternate positive and negative airway pressure is incorrect because glossopharyngeal breathing is an active, patient-driven technique that does not rely on mechanical ventilation or external pressure devices.
column10: It involves passive lung inflation by a caregiver using a self-inflating bag and a one-way valve is incorrect because this describes manual resuscitation or lung volume recruitment performed by a caregiver rather than an independent breathing technique using oropharyngeal muscles.

column2: Which of the following clinical scenarios best supports candidacy for phrenic nerve pacing?
column3: High cervical spinal cord injury with preserved phrenic nerve and diaphragm function requiring full-time ventilatory support
column4: Thoracic spinal cord injury with reduced exercise tolerance but no respiratory assist device
column5: High cervical spinal cord injury with absent diaphragmatic compound muscle action potentials
column6: High cervical spinal cord injury with ventilator dependence due to bilateral vocal cord paralysis
column7: High cervical spinal cord injury with preserved phrenic nerve and diaphragm function requiring full-time ventilatory support is correct because phrenic nerve pacing is indicated in patients with high cervical SCI who lack voluntary control of breathing but retain intact lower motor neuron pathways and viable diaphragm muscles.
column8: Thoracic spinal cord injury with reduced exercise tolerance but no respiratory assist device is incorrect because these patients typically have intact diaphragmatic function and do not require pacing.
column9: High cervical spinal cord injury with absent diaphragmatic compound muscle action potentials is incorrect because phrenic nerve pacing requires a functional phrenic nerve and excitable diaphragm muscle.
column10: High cervical spinal cord injury with ventilator dependence due to bilateral vocal cord paralysis is incorrect because upper airway obstruction precludes the effectiveness of pacing despite ventilator dependence.

column2: A 34-year-old man with a C5 ASIA B spinal cord injury presents to outpatient clinic reporting unrefreshing sleep, frequent morning headaches, and excessive daytime fatigue. His spouse reports loud snoring and occasional witnessed apneas. His injury occurred 8 months ago, and he uses a power wheelchair. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of his symptoms?
column5: Order daytime pulmonary function testing including forced vital capacity
column7: Order a diagnostic overnight polysomnography is correct because patients with cervical spinal cord injury are at high risk for sleep-disordered breathing, and polysomnography is the gold standard to diagnose obstructive sleep apnea and guide appropriate therapy.
column8: Begin empiric CPAP therapy is incorrect because treatment should not be initiated without first confirming the diagnosis and characterizing the type and severity of sleep-disordered breathing.
column9: Order daytime pulmonary function testing including forced vital capacity is incorrect because pulmonary function testing assesses respiratory mechanics but does not evaluate nocturnal breathing abnormalities or apneic events.
column10: Initiate nocturnal pulse oximetry monitoring is incorrect because overnight oximetry alone lacks sufficient sensitivity and specificity to diagnose sleep apnea and does not distinguish obstructive from central events.

column2: Which joint is most commonly affected by heterotopic ossification in patients with spinal cord injury?
column7: Hip is correct because it is the most frequently involved joint in heterotopic ossification following spinal cord injury, often due to prolonged immobility and positioning.
column8: Shoulder is incorrect because although it may be affected, it is less commonly involved than the hip in SCI-related HO.
column9: Knee is incorrect because while it is a common site in traumatic brain injury, it is less commonly affected in SCI.
column10: Elbow is incorrect because it is more frequently involved in patients with burns or neurologic injury other than SCI.

column2: Heterotopic ossification most commonly develops within what time frame following spinal cord injury?
column7: 1 to 4 months is correct because heterotopic ossification following spinal cord injury typically begins during the subacute phase, most commonly between 1 and 4 months, which is later than the 2–4 week window often seen in TBI or burn-related HO.
column8: 2 to 4 weeks is incorrect because this earlier onset is more characteristic of patients with traumatic brain injury or severe burns, not spinal cord injury.
column9: 6 to 12 months is incorrect because while HO may continue to mature during this time, the initial onset in SCI usually occurs earlier in the recovery course.
column10: After 1 year is incorrect because HO in SCI has typically formed and stabilized well before this point, and new ossification at this stage is unusual.

column2: A patient with spinal cord injury is suspected to have early heterotopic ossification. Which of the following findings on triple-phase bone scan is most consistent with the early inflammatory stage?
column3: Increased uptake in the flow and blood pool phases with normal or mildly increased delayed phase uptake
column4: Normal flow and blood pool phases with increased uptake in the delayed phase
column5: Increased uptake in all three phases
column6: Photopenia in the blood pool and delayed phases with mildly increased flow
column7: Increased uptake in the flow and blood pool phases with normal or mildly increased delayed phase uptake is correct because early HO is marked by hyperemia and vascular leakage without significant bone mineralization, leading to increased early-phase activity but delayed-phase changes lagging behind.
column8: Normal flow and blood pool phases with increased uptake in the delayed phase is incorrect because this pattern reflects mature HO or other chronic bone-forming processes, not early inflammatory changes.
column9: Increased uptake in all three phases is incorrect because this reflects a more advanced stage of HO with active bone formation already present in the delayed phase.
column10: Photopenia in the blood pool and delayed phases with mildly increased flow is incorrect because this pattern may suggest avascular necrosis or soft tissue infarct, not the hyperemic and vascular pattern of early HO.

column2: Which of the following medications is FDA-approved specifically for the treatment of neuropathic pain associated with spinal cord injury?
column7: Pregabalin is correct because it is the only medication with FDA approval specifically for the treatment of neuropathic pain associated with spinal cord injury.
column8: Gabapentin is incorrect because although commonly used off-label for neuropathic pain in SCI, it is not FDA-approved for this specific indication.
column9: Amitriptyline is incorrect because it is used for neuropathic pain more generally, especially in diabetic neuropathy and postherpetic neuralgia, but lacks FDA approval for SCI-related neuropathic pain.
column10: Duloxetine is incorrect because it is FDA-approved for diabetic peripheral neuropathy and fibromyalgia, but not for neuropathic pain specifically in SCI.

column2: A 29-year-old man with a chronic T6 ASIA B spinal cord injury presents with progressive upper extremity weakness, increased tone, and decreased pinprick and temperature sensation in both arms and shoulders. His lower extremity exam remains unchanged. What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
column3: MRI of the spinal cord with and without contrast
column7: MRI of the spinal cord with and without contrast is correct because post-traumatic syringomyelia often presents years after SCI with new bilateral central cord symptoms, and MRI is the gold standard to detect intramedullary cystic changes or syrinx formation, especially when upper extremity weakness and dissociated sensory loss are present.
column8: EMG and nerve conduction studies is incorrect because while helpful for peripheral nerve assessment, they cannot visualize central cord lesions; bilateral and proximal involvement favors a central process best evaluated by MRI.
column9: MRI of the lumbar spine is incorrect because post-traumatic syringomyelia typically occurs at or rostral to the level of injury, most often in the cervical or thoracic cord, not the lumbar region.
column10: Somatosensory evoked potentials (SSEPs) is incorrect because although they assess sensory pathway function, they lack anatomic specificity and cannot confirm or localize a syrinx.

column2: A 26-year-old man with a C7 ASIA A spinal cord injury has intact elbow flexion and wrist extension but lacks active thumb flexion. He is being considered for tendon transfer to restore key pinch. Which of the following tendon transfers is most appropriate?
column4: Deltoid to extensor carpi radialis longus
column5: Extensor carpi ulnaris to flexor digitorum profundus
column7: Brachioradialis to flexor pollicis longus is correct because this transfer restores key pinch in individuals with C7 tetraplegia by redirecting a preserved, volitionally controlled muscle to power thumb flexion.
column8: Deltoid to extensor carpi radialis longus is incorrect because this transfer is neither functionally necessary nor anatomically compatible, and the ECRL is already functional at the C6 level.
column9: Extensor carpi ulnaris to flexor digitorum profundus is incorrect because the ECU provides critical wrist stability and is not typically sacrificed for grasp restoration.
column10: Biceps to triceps is incorrect because this transfer is aimed at restoring elbow extension, not key pinch, and is used more for shoulder positioning than hand function.

column2: A 24-year-old man with a C5 ASIA A spinal cord injury has intact shoulder abduction and elbow flexion but lacks active elbow extension. He is being evaluated for tendon transfer surgery to improve upper extremity function, particularly for improved positioning and wheelchair propulsion. Which tendon transfer is most appropriate to restore elbow extension?
column6: Extensor carpi radialis longus to triceps
column7: Posterior deltoid to triceps is correct because in C5 tetraplegia, the posterior deltoid is under volitional control and can be transferred to the triceps to restore elbow extension, improving reach, transfers, and propulsion.
column8: Brachioradialis to flexor pollicis longus is incorrect because this transfer restores key pinch and is used in C6 SCI, not C5, where wrist extension is typically absent.
column9: Pectoralis major to triceps is incorrect because while this transfer has been described, it is less commonly used than the posterior deltoid and can compromise other functional movements like forward pushing.
column10: Extensor carpi radialis longus to triceps is incorrect because ECRL may not be available or strong enough in C5 injuries, and its sacrifice would compromise future reconstructive options if wrist extension develops.

column2: A 25-year-old man with a C5 ASIA A spinal cord injury is unable to extend his wrist but has full elbow flexion and shoulder abduction. He is undergoing evaluation for tendon transfer surgery to improve hand function. Which of the following tendon transfers is most appropriate to restore active wrist extension and facilitate tenodesis grasp?
column3: Brachioradialis to extensor carpi radialis brevis
column7: Brachioradialis to extensor carpi radialis brevis is correct because in C5 tetraplegia, this transfer restores active wrist extension, which is essential for tenodesis grasp and allows functional hand use even in the absence of finger flexion.
column8: Posterior deltoid to triceps is incorrect because it is used to restore elbow extension, not wrist extension, and is not sufficient alone to enable tenodesis grasp.
column9: Brachioradialis to flexor digitorum profundus is incorrect because finger flexor transfers require intact wrist extensors (C6), which are absent in C5 SCI.
column10: Pectoralis major to triceps is incorrect because it is an alternative for elbow extension restoration and does not assist with wrist function.

column2: A 25-year-old man with a C5 ASIA A spinal cord injury undergoes tendon transfer surgery in which his brachioradialis is rerouted to the extensor carpi radialis brevis. What functional ability does this procedure most directly help restore?
column7: Tenodesis grasp is correct because transferring the brachioradialis to the extensor carpi radialis brevis restores active wrist extension, allowing passive finger flexion through tenodesis mechanics in individuals with C5 tetraplegia.
column8: Lateral (key) pinch is incorrect because this requires active thumb flexion, typically restored with a brachioradialis to flexor pollicis longus transfer in C6 injuries, not C5.
column9: Thumb opposition is incorrect because it requires thenar muscle function, which is not addressed by BR to ECRB and is typically absent in C5 SCI.
column10: Elbow extension is incorrect because this function is restored using transfers such as posterior deltoid to triceps, not BR to ECRB.

column2: In patients with spinal cord injury during the first two years post-injury, what is the most common site of pressure injury?
column7: Sacrum is correct because it is the most common site of pressure injury in patients with spinal cord injury, especially in the first two years, due to prolonged supine positioning and loss of sensation over the sacral area.
column8: Ischial tuberosity is incorrect because ischial pressure injuries are more commonly associated with prolonged sitting and tend to occur later in the chronic phase of spinal cord injury.
column9: Greater trochanter is incorrect because trochanteric pressure injuries are less common early after injury and are typically related to side-lying positioning rather than supine immobility.
column10: Heel is incorrect because heel pressure injuries are more frequently seen in hospitalized or immobilized patients but are less common than sacral ulcers in the early post–spinal cord injury period.

column2: In patients with spinal cord injury more than two years post-injury, what is the most common site of pressure injury?
column7: Ischium is correct because in chronic spinal cord injury patients, especially those who use wheelchairs regularly, the ischial tuberosities are the most common site for pressure injuries due to prolonged sitting.
column8: Sacrum is incorrect because although common early after SCI, sacral pressure injuries are less frequent in chronic wheelchair users compared to ischial injuries.
column9: Heel is incorrect because it is more associated with supine immobility rather than chronic seated pressure.
column10: Occiput is incorrect because this site is more commonly affected in infants or acutely immobilized patients, not in long-term wheelchair users.

column2: What is the most common site of pressure injury in children with immobility?
column7: Occiput is correct because in children, particularly infants and young children with limited mobility, the occiput is the most common site of pressure injury due to their proportionally larger head size and time spent lying supine.
column8: Sacrum is incorrect because although common in adults with immobility, it is less frequently involved than the occiput in pediatric populations.
column9: Heel is incorrect because while it is a pressure point in both children and adults, it is not the most commonly affected site in pediatric patients.
column10: Ischium is incorrect because pressure injuries at this site typically occur in older wheelchair-bound patients, not in younger children who spend more time lying than sitting.

column2: A 72-year-old nursing home resident presents with an area of persistent, non-blanchable redness over the left greater trochanter. The skin is intact, and the patient reports mild tenderness. What is the appropriate pressure injury stage?
column7: Stage 1 is correct because the area shows intact skin with non-blanchable erythema, consistent with Stage 1 pressure injury.
column8: Stage 2 is incorrect because there is no loss of skin integrity or blister formation.
column9: Stage 3 is incorrect because there is no full-thickness skin loss or visible subcutaneous fat.
column10: Deep tissue pressure injury is incorrect because the skin is intact and not discolored purple or maroon.

column2: A 55-year-old woman with paraplegia develops a shallow open ulcer over the sacrum with a red wound bed and no slough. The surrounding skin is intact. What is the most appropriate staging?
column7: Stage 2 is correct because there is partial-thickness skin loss with exposed dermis and no slough.
column8: Stage 1 is incorrect because the skin is no longer intact.
column9: Stage 3 is incorrect because there is no visible subcutaneous fat or full-thickness skin loss.
column10: Unstageable is incorrect because the wound bed is visible and does not contain obscuring slough or eschar.

column2: A 64-year-old patient with limited mobility has a pressure ulcer over the ischium with full-thickness skin loss and visible subcutaneous fat. There is no exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. What is the correct stage?
column7: Stage 3 is correct because the ulcer involves full-thickness skin loss with visible adipose tissue but no deeper structures.
column8: Stage 2 is incorrect because the damage extends beyond the dermis.
column9: Stage 4 is incorrect because there is no exposure of muscle, tendon, or bone.
column10: Unstageable is incorrect because the wound bed is fully visible and not covered by slough or eschar.

column2: A 58-year-old man with quadriplegia has a sacral ulcer with exposed bone and tunneling. What is the appropriate pressure injury stage?
column7: Stage 4 is correct because the ulcer involves full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone.
column8: Stage 3 is incorrect because there is visible bone, which exceeds the depth criteria for Stage 3.
column9: Unstageable is incorrect because the depth and structures are clearly visualized.
column10: Deep tissue pressure injury is incorrect because the wound is open and the tissue layers are already exposed.

column2: A 70-year-old patient with a heel wound has intact skin covered in dark, dry eschar. What is the best classification?
column7: Unstageable is correct because the wound bed is completely covered by eschar, preventing visualization of the extent of tissue damage.
column8: Stage 3 is incorrect because staging requires visualization of the wound bed.
column9: Stage 4 is incorrect because there is no visibility of deep structures.
column10: Deep tissue pressure injury is incorrect because the lesion is not a dark purple/maroon discoloration of intact skin.

column2: A 68-year-old ICU patient has a maroon-colored area over the sacrum with skin that is intact but firm and cool to the touch. What is the most appropriate pressure injury classification?
column7: Deep tissue pressure injury is correct because the intact skin shows persistent dark discoloration, typical of deep tissue involvement.
column8: Stage 1 is incorrect because non-blanchable erythema is present but not purple or maroon.
column9: Unstageable is incorrect because there is no eschar or slough obscuring the wound.
column10: Stage 2 is incorrect because the skin is intact and there is no open ulcer or blister.

column2: A 79-year-old woman admitted after a hip fracture repair is evaluated for pressure injury risk. She is alert but confused, requires assistance to reposition in bed, is incontinent of urine, and has minimal nutritional intake. Which assessment tool best quantifies her risk for developing a pressure injury?
column7: Braden Scale is correct because it evaluates six domains—sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition, and friction/shear—to quantify pressure injury risk in at-risk patients like this elderly woman with poor mobility and nutrition.
column8: Norton Scale is incorrect because it uses fewer domains and is less predictive than the Braden Scale in modern clinical practice.
column9: Barthel Index is incorrect because it evaluates activities of daily living but does not quantify risk factors for pressure injuries.
column10: Waterlow Functional Risk Index is incorrect because it is not a validated or recognized tool for assessing pressure injury risk despite sounding plausible.

column2: Why is skeletal muscle more susceptible to pressure-induced ischemia than skin in immobilized patients?
column3: Muscle has higher metabolic demand and lower perfusion pressure than skin
column4: Skin has fewer lymphatic vessels and traps more interstitial fluid
column5: Skin is thinner and less elastic than muscle
column6: Muscle has greater capillary density and better oxygen diffusion
column7: Muscle has higher metabolic demand and lower perfusion pressure than skin is correct because skeletal muscle requires more oxygen at baseline and is more vulnerable to ischemic injury under sustained pressure due to its lower tolerance for reduced perfusion.
column8: Skin has fewer lymphatic vessels and traps more interstitial fluid is incorrect because this affects edema, not susceptibility to ischemia.
column9: Skin is thinner and less elastic than muscle is incorrect because tissue thickness and elasticity do not determine ischemic vulnerability.
column10: Muscle has greater capillary density and better oxygen diffusion is incorrect because although capillary density is high, this does not protect against ischemia during prolonged compression.

column2: A 32-year-old man with a complete T4 spinal cord injury is wheelchair-dependent. To prevent pressure injuries, how often should he perform pressure relief maneuvers while seated?
column7: Every 15 to 30 minutes is correct because wheelchair users with spinal cord injury should perform pressure relief maneuvers at least every 15 to 30 minutes to reduce sustained tissue ischemia and prevent pressure injury formation.
column8: Every hour is incorrect because waiting an hour allows prolonged pressure over bony prominences, increasing the risk of tissue ischemia and pressure injury.
column9: Every 2 hours is incorrect because this interval is appropriate for repositioning in bed rather than for seated pressure relief in wheelchair users.
column10: Only when discomfort is noticed is incorrect because patients with spinal cord injury often have impaired sensation and cannot rely on discomfort as a cue for pressure relief.

column2: A 35-year-old man with a T4 ASIA A spinal cord injury presents for follow-up after healing a stage 3 ischial pressure injury. He denies any discomfort, and his skin appears intact. Which of the following best explains the utility of pressure mapping in this case?
column3: It identifies asymmetrical pressure loads that are not evident by inspection or patient report
column4: It determines the depth of prior ulcers using surface pressure gradients
column5: It monitors temperature changes that signal skin breakdown risk
column6: It confirms muscle atrophy beneath the skin contributing to pressure injury risk
column7: It identifies asymmetrical pressure loads that are not evident by inspection or patient report is correct because pressure mapping is especially valuable in SCI patients who lack sensation and may have normal-appearing skin, allowing clinicians to detect offloading deficits and guide preventive strategies.
column8: It determines the depth of prior ulcers using surface pressure gradients is incorrect because pressure mapping does not evaluate wound depth or healing stage.
column9: It monitors temperature changes that signal skin breakdown risk is incorrect because temperature monitoring is not a function of standard pressure mapping systems.
column10: It confirms muscle atrophy beneath the skin contributing to pressure injury risk is incorrect because pressure mapping does not assess muscle mass or tissue thickness.

column2: A 68-year-old man with paraplegia presents with a chronic sacral wound that has been present for 20 years and has recently failed to heal despite appropriate pressure offloading and wound care. The ulcer has heaped-up margins, friable granulation tissue, and occasional bleeding. What is the most appropriate next step?
column3: Perform a biopsy of the wound edge
column4: Begin empiric antibiotics and monitor for 2 more weeks
column5: Order MRI to evaluate for osteomyelitis
column6: Apply silver-impregnated dressing and reassess in 1 month
column7: Perform a biopsy of the wound edge is correct because the chronicity, raised margins, and friable tissue raise concern for Marjolin ulcer, a malignant transformation (most often squamous cell carcinoma) that arises in long-standing wounds, requiring biopsy for diagnosis.
column8: Begin empiric antibiotics and monitor for 2 more weeks is incorrect because there is no evidence of acute infection and further delay risks missing malignancy.
column9: Order MRI to evaluate for osteomyelitis is incorrect because while useful for evaluating bone involvement, it does not assess for malignant transformation.
column10: Apply silver-impregnated dressing and reassess in 1 month is incorrect because ongoing conservative management risks delayed diagnosis of a potentially aggressive skin cancer.

column2: A 63-year-old man with diabetes presents with a stage 4 pressure injury over the right heel that probes to bone. MRI confirms osteomyelitis. A wound swab has been obtained, and empiric antibiotics have been started. What is the most appropriate next step to guide definitive antibiotic therapy?
column4: Continue empiric antibiotics based on wound culture results
column5: Repeat wound swab after 72 hours of antibiotics
column6: Begin broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for 6 weeks
column7: Perform bone biopsy for culture is correct because in the setting of a stage 4 pressure injury with confirmed osteomyelitis, bone biopsy is necessary to accurately identify the pathogen and tailor antibiotic therapy.
column8: Continue empiric antibiotics based on wound culture results is incorrect because wound swabs often reflect surface contaminants and may not represent the organisms infecting the bone.
column9: Repeat wound swab after 72 hours of antibiotics is incorrect because prior antibiotic exposure reduces culture reliability, and swab results still do not confirm bone involvement.
column10: Begin broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for 6 weeks is incorrect because empiric therapy should not be prolonged without culture-directed guidance from bone biopsy.

column2: A 62-year-old woman is undergoing evaluation after a left temporoparietal stroke. During conversation, she speaks fluently but frequently substitutes incorrect words that are close in sound or meaning to the intended word—for example, calling a "pen" a "pencil" or a "watch" a "clock." Her articulation and prosody are otherwise intact. What is the term that best describes this language disturbance?
column7: Paraphasia is correct because the patient shows fluent speech with substitution of semantically or phonemically related words, which is typical of language disturbance from dominant temporoparietal lesions.
column8: Neologism is incorrect because it involves the production of made-up, non-existent words that are unrecognizable to listeners.
column9: Circumlocution is incorrect because it refers to using descriptive language to convey meaning when the specific word is inaccessible, rather than substituting it with a similar real word.
column10: Apraxia is incorrect because it refers to impaired motor planning that affects purposeful movements, including speech articulation, not word substitution.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 70-year-old man is being evaluated after a left middle cerebral artery stroke. His speech is nonfluent, effortful, and consists mostly of content words while omitting function words and grammatical markers. For instance, he says “want food” instead of “I want the food” and “go store” instead of “I am going to the store.” Which term best describes this pattern of language impairment?
column7: Agrammatism is correct because the patient demonstrates nonfluent, effortful speech with omission of grammatical elements such as auxiliary verbs and prepositions, a hallmark feature of agrammatism, often seen in Broca’s aphasia.
column8: Telegraphic speech is incorrect because it is a descriptive term that refers to the style of speech seen in agrammatism but is not the diagnostic term for the language disturbance.
column9: Broca's aphasia is incorrect because it refers to the broader syndrome that includes agrammatism, apraxia of speech, and impaired repetition, rather than the specific grammatical speech deficit described.
column10: Phonemic paraphasia is incorrect because it involves substitution of incorrect sounds (e.g., “gat” for “cat”), not omission of grammatical structure.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 68-year-old right-handed woman is undergoing evaluation for persistent language difficulties after a left temporo-occipital stroke. Her speech is fluent, grammatically correct, and well-articulated, but she frequently pauses mid-sentence when attempting to name common objects. Comprehension and repetition are intact. What is the most likely language disturbance?
column7: Anomia is correct because the patient demonstrates fluent, grammatically intact speech with impaired naming and frequent word-retrieval pauses, while comprehension and repetition remain preserved, consistent with anomic aphasia from dominant temporo-occipital damage.
column8: Semantic dementia is incorrect because it involves fluent speech with progressive loss of word and object meaning, often accompanied by impaired single-word comprehension.
column9: Transcortical sensory aphasia is incorrect because it features fluent speech and preserved repetition but impaired auditory comprehension, which is not present here.
column10: Apraxia of speech is incorrect because it results in effortful, inconsistent speech with disrupted prosody due to motor planning deficits, not fluent speech with naming impairment.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 72-year-old man with advanced frontotemporal dementia is noted to repeat phrases immediately after they are spoken to him. For example, when asked “How are you feeling today?” he responds with “How are you feeling today?” instead of generating an original answer. His speech is otherwise fluent but lacks meaningful content. What is the term that best describes this language disturbance?
column7: Echolalia is correct because the patient repetitively echoes others’ speech verbatim and immediately, which is a hallmark of echolalia often seen in neurodegenerative conditions such as frontotemporal dementia.
column8: Palilalia is incorrect because it involves involuntary repetition of the patient’s own words or syllables, not repetition of someone else’s speech.
column9: Perseveration is incorrect because it refers to the inappropriate repetition of a previous response or behavior, regardless of a new prompt.
column10: Wernicke’s aphasia is incorrect because it involves fluent but nonsensical speech with impaired comprehension, not direct repetition of others' statements.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 66-year-old woman recovering from a left temporoparietal stroke is undergoing language assessment. When shown common objects, she is unable to name them but provides descriptive phrases instead. For example, when shown a “watch,” she says “the thing you wear on your wrist to tell time.” Her speech is fluent and grammatically correct, and both comprehension and repetition are preserved. What is the term that best describes this pattern of speech?
column7: Circumlocution is correct because the patient avoids using specific words by describing their function or characteristics, a strategy often used to compensate for word-finding difficulty in the setting of preserved fluency and comprehension.
column8: Anomia is incorrect because it refers to the inability to name objects, but does not specifically describe the use of descriptive phrases as a compensatory mechanism.
column9: Paraphasia is incorrect because it involves substitution of incorrect words (semantic or phonemic), not descriptive workarounds.
column10: Semantic dementia is incorrect because it involves progressive loss of word and concept meaning, often with impaired single-word comprehension, not just word-retrieval circumvention.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 59-year-old man with a history of left posterior superior temporal lobe stroke is undergoing a language evaluation. His speech is fluent but filled with made-up words that are unintelligible to the listener. For example, when handed a comb, he says “This is a flinter.” He appears unaware that his word choice is incorrect. Comprehension is impaired, and repetition is poor. What is the term that best describes this type of language disturbance?
column7: Neologism is correct because the patient produces non-real, invented words that do not resemble the target word, which is characteristic of neologistic errors often associated with severe receptive aphasia.
column8: Phonemic paraphasia is incorrect because it involves word errors with sound substitutions or rearrangements (e.g., “lable” for “table”), not invented, meaningless words.
column9: Wernicke’s aphasia is incorrect because it describes a broader syndrome involving impaired comprehension and fluent but nonsensical speech, not the specific production of neologisms.
column10: Jargon aphasia is incorrect because it refers to fluent, unintelligible speech with a mixture of neologisms, paraphasias, and normal words, rather than isolated invented words.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 64-year-old man with a recent left posterior superior temporal lobe stroke is undergoing a language evaluation. His speech is fluent and well-articulated but completely unintelligible, consisting of a continuous stream of normal words, made-up words, and word substitutions. For example, when asked to describe a picture, he responds, “The grintle was splinding the flanter when the kabber flew.” He is unaware of his deficits, and both comprehension and repetition are impaired. What is the term that best describes this language disturbance?
column7: Jargon aphasia is correct because the patient demonstrates fluent but unintelligible speech composed of neologisms, paraphasias, and real words in a disorganized stream, with poor awareness and impaired comprehension.
column8: Neologism is incorrect because it refers to the use of isolated invented words, not sustained speech filled with mixed language errors.
column9: Paraphasia is incorrect because it refers to occasional word-level substitutions, not pervasive unintelligibility from a combination of neologisms and real words.
column10: Logorrhea is incorrect because it refers to excessive verbosity, often with tangential content, not the nonsensical mixture of errors seen in jargon aphasia.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 71-year-old man with global aphasia after a large left MCA stroke exhibits severely limited speech output. He produces the same single word or syllable repeatedly, regardless of the context. For example, when asked his name, to describe a picture, or to count, he responds with “dee dee dee.” His articulation is preserved, but he cannot produce meaningful or variable language. What is the term that best describes this speech behavior?
column7: Stereotype is correct because the patient produces a non-meaningful, fixed utterance repeatedly across different contexts, a classic feature of stereotyped verbal output often seen in severe aphasias such as global aphasia.
column8: Logorrhea is incorrect because it refers to excessive, often meaningless speech, not restricted repetition of a fixed phrase or syllable.
column9: Echolalia is incorrect because it involves repetition of others' spoken phrases, not internally generated stereotyped utterances.
column10: Perseveration is incorrect because it involves inappropriate continuation of a previous response or behavior, not a fixed phrase used across all responses.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 55-year-old man with severe nonfluent aphasia following a dominant hemisphere stroke is enrolled in speech therapy. He is able to sing familiar phrases fluently, such as “Happy birthday to you,” but struggles to produce the same words during normal conversation. His speech-language pathologist begins a therapy approach that emphasizes exaggerated rhythm and pitch variation to facilitate verbal expression. What is the name of this therapeutic technique?
column7: Melodic intonation is correct because the patient demonstrates improved verbal output through exaggerated melodic and rhythmic patterns, which this therapy leverages by activating preserved right-hemisphere networks in patients with severe nonfluent aphasia.
column8: Constraint-induced language therapy is incorrect because it focuses on forcing verbal output by constraining alternative modalities, not through use of melody or rhythm.
column9: Transcranial magnetic stimulation is incorrect because it is a neuromodulation technique, not a behavioral therapy involving prosody or music-like elements.
column10: Script training is incorrect because it involves rehearsing specific functional dialogues, not using melodic and rhythmic cues to promote speech production.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A speech-language pathologist is designing a treatment plan for a patient with nonfluent aphasia following a left MCA stroke. The patient has severely limited speech output but is able to sing familiar melodies with relatively preserved fluency. Therapy will focus on using exaggerated rhythm and melody to facilitate verbal expression. For which type of aphasia is this treatment approach most appropriate?
column7: Broca’s aphasia is correct because patients with nonfluent speech, impaired repetition, and relatively intact comprehension benefit most from melodic intonation therapy, which leverages right hemisphere prosodic processing to improve expressive language.
column8: Wernicke’s aphasia is incorrect because these patients have fluent but nonsensical speech and impaired comprehension, making them poor candidates for a prosody-based expressive therapy.
column9: Anomic aphasia is incorrect because these patients have fluent speech with isolated word-finding difficulty and typically do not require melody-based facilitation of output.
column10: Conduction aphasia is incorrect because it is characterized by impaired repetition with relatively preserved fluency and comprehension, and does not primarily involve nonfluent output.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 74-year-old woman is admitted to inpatient rehabilitation following a right-sided ischemic stroke resulting in left hemiparesis and mild dysphagia. The rehabilitation team plans to monitor her progress in basic self-care skills such as feeding, bathing, dressing, and transfers. Which of the following standardized assessment tools is most appropriate for tracking her independence in activities of daily living during her stay?
column7: Barthel Index is correct because it specifically measures performance in basic activities of daily living such as toileting, feeding, bathing, dressing, and ambulation, and is widely used to track functional progress in stroke rehabilitation.
column8: Fugl-Meyer Assessment is incorrect because it evaluates motor recovery and sensorimotor function post-stroke, not independence in daily tasks.
column9: Mini-Mental State Examination is incorrect because it screens for cognitive impairment and does not assess physical functional status or ADL performance.
column10: Functional Independence Measure (FIM) is incorrect because although it assesses ADL performance, it is more comprehensive and no longer used in IRFs after being replaced by Section GG in CMS reporting.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 67-year-old man is admitted to an inpatient rehabilitation facility after a left thalamic hemorrhagic stroke. As part of Medicare-mandated documentation, the rehabilitation team must assess his functional status upon admission and discharge using a standardized scale that evaluates self-care and mobility. Which of the following tools fulfills this CMS reporting requirement?
column7: Section GG is correct because it is the current standardized assessment required by CMS for IRF quality reporting, evaluating functional status across self-care and mobility domains at both admission and discharge.
column8: Functional Independence Measure (FIM) is incorrect because it was phased out of CMS quality reporting requirements as of 2019 and replaced by Section GG.
column9: Barthel Index is incorrect because it measures ADL independence but is not used for Medicare IRF reporting.
column10: Disability Rating Scale is incorrect because it is used primarily in traumatic brain injury to track recovery from coma, not general stroke rehabilitation or Medicare documentation.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 78-year-old woman is admitted to inpatient rehabilitation after sustaining a traumatic brain injury (TBI) following a ground-level fall in her home. She has no history of substance use or recent motor vehicle accidents. Which of the following is the most common mechanism of TBI across all age groups?
column7: Falls is correct because falls are the leading cause of traumatic brain injury overall, accounting for nearly half of all TBI-related emergency visits, hospitalizations, and deaths in the United States.
column8: Motor vehicle collisions is incorrect because although they are a major cause of TBI in younger adults, they do not account for the majority of cases across all age groups.
column9: Assaults is incorrect because they are a significant cause of TBI in specific populations (e.g., young men) but are far less common overall than falls.
column10: Sports-related injuries is incorrect because they are a frequent cause of TBI in youth and athletes but represent a small fraction of cases across all ages.

column2: Which of the following best explains the biomechanical force responsible for diffuse axonal injury?
column5: Massive cerebral edema from traumatic swelling
column7: Rotational acceleration of the brain is correct because diffuse axonal injury results from angular forces that shear axons at the gray-white junction, corpus callosum, and brainstem, often in the absence of focal injury on CT.
column8: Direct impact to the skull is incorrect because this causes focal contusions or skull fractures, not diffuse axonal shearing.
column9: Massive cerebral edema from traumatic swelling is incorrect because edema contributes to secondary injury but is not the initiating biomechanical force in DAI.
column10: Linear acceleration is incorrect because it typically leads to translational forces causing focal injuries, not the rotational forces responsible for widespread axonal disruption.

column2: A 19-year-old man presents in a coma after a high-speed rollover crash. Head CT is unremarkable. Brain MRI reveals multiple punctate hemorrhages consistent with diffuse axonal injury. Which of the following brain regions is most commonly affected in this condition?
column7: Gray-white matter junction is correct because it is the most common site of axonal shearing in diffuse axonal injury due to differences in tissue density between gray and white matter, making it highly susceptible to rotational forces.
column8: Basal ganglia is incorrect because although they may be involved in severe brain injury, they are not the most common site of axonal injury in DAI.
column9: Cerebellar peduncles is incorrect because they may be affected in advanced or severe cases, but they are not the most frequent initial site.
column10: Pituitary stalk is incorrect because it is not a typical site of axonal shearing and is more commonly associated with endocrine complications after TBI.

column2: A 27-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay after a high-speed motorcycle collision. He is comatose with a GCS of 4. CT of the head is normal. Brain MRI shows multiple punctate hemorrhages, including involvement of the interhemispheric white matter. In diffuse axonal injury, which of the following is one of the most commonly affected deep structures?
column7: Corpus callosum is correct because it is one of the most commonly affected deep structures in diffuse axonal injury, especially the splenium and body, due to its midline location and susceptibility to rotational shear forces.
column8: Thalamus is incorrect because it may be affected in severe trauma but is not a typical early target in DAI.
column9: Cerebral cortex is incorrect because cortical contusions result from focal impact, not shearing forces as seen in DAI.
column10: Cerebellum is incorrect because it is not a frequent site of DAI-related hemorrhages or axonal disruption.

column2: A 19-year-old man is struck in the temporal region with a baseball during a game. He initially loses consciousness, then awakens briefly but later deteriorates neurologically. Head CT shows a biconvex (lentiform) hyperdensity that does not cross suture lines. Which of the following vessels is most commonly responsible for this type of intracranial hemorrhage?
column7: Middle meningeal artery is correct because it is the vessel most often injured in temporal bone trauma, leading to epidural hematoma, which presents with a lucid interval and a lens-shaped hemorrhage on CT that does not cross suture lines.
column8: Bridging veins is incorrect because tearing of bridging veins causes subdural hematomas, which appear crescent-shaped and cross suture lines.
column9: Basilar artery is incorrect because injury here leads to subarachnoid or brainstem hemorrhage, not epidural hematoma.
column10: Superior sagittal sinus is incorrect because damage to this structure causes venous sinus thrombosis or subdural hemorrhage, not the classic epidural presentation.

column2: An 82-year-old man with a history of cerebral atrophy falls and strikes the back of his head. He is initially alert but becomes progressively somnolent over several hours. Head CT shows a crescent-shaped hyperdensity that crosses suture lines. Which of the following structures is most likely responsible for this type of hemorrhage?
column7: Bridging veins is correct because subdural hematomas result from tearing of these veins as they traverse the subdural space, particularly in elderly patients with brain atrophy, and appear crescent-shaped and cross suture lines on CT.
column8: Middle meningeal artery is incorrect because injury to this vessel causes epidural hematomas, which appear lentiform and do not cross suture lines.
column9: Basilar artery is incorrect because basilar injury causes subarachnoid hemorrhage or brainstem infarction, not subdural collections.
column10: Posterior cerebral artery is incorrect because PCA injury typically causes occipital infarcts or subarachnoid hemorrhage, not subdural hematomas.

column2: Which of the following locations would be the most likely site of a cerebral contusion?
column7: Anterior frontal lobe is correct because it is the most commonly contused region in head trauma due to its proximity to the bony ridges of the anterior cranial fossa.
column8: Splenium of the corpus callosum is incorrect because this area is typically affected in diffuse axonal injury, not focal cortical contusions.
column9: Periventricular white matter is incorrect because it is a deep structure not commonly impacted by cortical contusion forces.
column10: Occipital cortex is incorrect because it is less frequently involved in contusions compared to the frontal and temporal poles.
column12: The most common sites of cerebral contusion are the inferior frontal lobes, anterior temporal lobes, and orbitofrontal cortex, due to their proximity to bony ridges of the skull.

column2: Which of the following locations is most commonly affected by a cerebral contusion following blunt head trauma?
column7: Inferior frontal lobe is correct because it is frequently contused in blunt trauma due to its location near the irregular bony floor of the anterior cranial fossa.
column8: Cerebellar vermis is incorrect because this structure is not typically impacted in cortical contusions resulting from blunt head trauma.
column9: Thalamus is incorrect because it is a deep gray matter structure usually affected in anoxic injury or severe central trauma, not focal cortical contusion.
column10: Parietal white matter is incorrect because contusions typically involve cortical gray matter, especially in the frontal and temporal poles.
column12: The most common sites of cerebral contusion are the inferior frontal lobes, anterior temporal lobes, and orbitofrontal cortex, due to their proximity to bony ridges of the skull.

column2: A 45-year-old man is undergoing neurorehabilitation after a moderate traumatic brain injury sustained 8 weeks ago. Despite no evidence of structural regeneration on imaging, he continues to improve in speech and coordination. Which of the following mechanisms best explains neuronal plasticity in this setting?
column5: Restoration of myelin by Schwann cells in the CNS
column6: Regeneration of cortical neurons from neural stem cells
column7: Axonal sprouting and synaptic reorganization is correct because functional recovery after TBI is primarily driven by plasticity mechanisms such as new synapse formation and reorganization of existing neural circuits, rather than regrowth of neurons.
column8: Neuronal mitosis and proliferation is incorrect because mature CNS neurons are post-mitotic and do not divide after injury.
column9: Restoration of myelin by Schwann cells in the CNS is incorrect because Schwann cells are part of the peripheral nervous system, not the central nervous system.
column10: Regeneration of cortical neurons from neural stem cells is incorrect because this process is extremely limited in the adult human brain and not the primary driver of post-TBI recovery.

column2: A 55-year-old man is undergoing inpatient rehabilitation following a moderate traumatic brain injury. He demonstrates cognitive and motor impairments that extend beyond the area of the original lesion. Imaging shows no new structural abnormalities. Which of the following best explains this observation?
column3: Functional depression of a remote but connected brain region
column4: Expansion of the original lesion through axonal degeneration
column5: Unmasking of a pre-existing developmental cortical malformation
column7: Functional depression of a remote but connected brain region is correct because this pattern of dysfunction reflects diaschisis, a phenomenon in which neural activity is reduced in a distant but functionally connected brain area due to disrupted connectivity following injury.
column8: Expansion of the original lesion through axonal degeneration is incorrect because this refers to Wallerian degeneration, which causes downstream tract damage, not remote network suppression.
column9: Unmasking of a pre-existing developmental cortical malformation is incorrect because this would typically present earlier in life and is unrelated to acute TBI mechanisms.
