column7: 6 months is correct because this is the ideal age for prosthetic fitting in congenital unilateral transradial limb deficiency, as most infants have achieved sitting balance by this time and are beginning to engage in bilateral reaching and object exploration.
column8: 3 months is incorrect because the child typically has not yet achieved sitting balance or developed sufficient trunk control for effective prosthetic use.
column9: 9 months is incorrect because delaying prosthetic fitting beyond 6 months may reduce prosthesis acceptance and interfere with early bimanual skill acquisition.
column10: 12 months is incorrect because fitting at this age misses the developmental window when sitting balance and bilateral upper extremity use are first emerging.

column2: A 16-year-old male from a low-resource setting presents for prosthetic evaluation after traumatic bilateral forearm amputations sustained in early childhood. He expresses a desire for improved independence in activities of daily living, especially for tasks like feeding and toileting, but his family cannot afford advanced myoelectric prostheses. Which of the following surgical options would most directly restore functional prehension without the use of a prosthetic device?
column7: Krukenberg procedure is correct because it surgically separates the radius and ulna to create a functional pincer grasp that allows prehension without a prosthetic device, making it particularly useful in bilateral forearm amputees from low-resource settings.
column8: Volkmann contracture release is incorrect because this procedure addresses ischemic muscle contractures of the forearm and does not restore prehension or replace lost hand function.
column9: Rotationplasty is incorrect because it is a limb-salvage procedure primarily used for lower extremity tumors that reorients the ankle to function as a knee, not to restore hand function.
column10: Van Nes procedure is incorrect because it is a type of rotationplasty used for proximal femoral deficiencies and does not provide functional prehension for upper extremity amputations.

column2: A 6-month-old infant with congenital left terminal transradial deficiency is being evaluated for first-time prosthetic fitting. The child has achieved sitting balance and is beginning to demonstrate bilateral reaching. Which of the following is the most appropriate type of prosthesis to initiate at this stage?
column5: Body-powered prosthesis with voluntary opening terminal device
column7: Passive prosthesis is correct because passive prostheses are lightweight devices that improve body symmetry, encourage bilateral use, provide proprioceptive input, and facilitate early acceptance of prosthetic wear during critical periods of motor development.
column8: Myoelectric prosthesis is incorrect because these are typically introduced later, once the child has the cognitive maturity and motor control necessary to operate electromyographic signals, usually around 18–24 months.
column9: Body-powered prosthesis with voluntary opening terminal device is incorrect because this type of prosthesis requires greater strength and coordination than is developmentally appropriate at 6 months of age.
column10: Cosmetic silicone prosthesis is incorrect because although it provides a lifelike appearance, it offers no functional benefit or proprioceptive stimulation important for motor development in infancy.

column2: A 6-year-old child with a congenital transradial limb deficiency is evaluated for limited ability to perform tasks requiring pincer grasp, such as picking up paper or small toys. The child uses the residual limb effectively but lacks a structure to oppose against. The parents are interested in a permanent surgical option that could improve functional grasp for daily activities. Which procedure is most appropriate?
column6: Microvascular toe transfer with tendon anastomosis
column7: Vilkké procedure is correct because it transfers the second toe to the end of a congenitally deficient limb to create a passive, sensate post that enables key and pincer grasp in children.
column8: Krukenberg procedure is incorrect because it is primarily indicated for bilateral below-elbow amputees and results in a pincer mechanism between radius and ulna, not applicable to congenital transverse deficiencies.
column9: Pollicization of the index finger is incorrect because it is used for thumb hypoplasia or absence, not for mid-forearm limb deficiencies lacking a distal structure entirely.
column10: Microvascular toe transfer with tendon anastomosis is incorrect because it aims to restore active prehension rather than a passive post, and is more complex with different indications than the Vilkké procedure.

column2: What is the most common congenital lower limb deficiency according to international classification standards?
column6: Complete transverse deficiency of the femur
column7: Fibular longitudinal deficiency is correct because it is the most common congenital lower limb deficiency, classified by ISPO as a longitudinal absence of the fibula, often associated with limb length discrepancy and foot deformity.
column8: Tibial longitudinal deficiency is incorrect because while it is a recognized ISPO-classified deficiency, it is significantly less common than fibular deficiency.
column9: Proximal femoral focal deficiency is incorrect because although it is a well-recognized congenital limb deficiency, it occurs less frequently than fibular longitudinal deficiency.
column10: Complete transverse deficiency of the femur is incorrect because transverse limb deficiencies are overall less common than longitudinal deficiencies and are not the most frequent lower limb congenital absence.

column2: What is the most appropriate age for initial prosthetic fitting in a child with a congenital lower extremity deficiency?
column7: 9 months is correct because initial prosthetic fitting is recommended around the time a child begins pulling to stand, which allows the prosthesis to be incorporated into early weight-bearing, balance, and upright mobility development.
column8: 6 months is incorrect because most infants have not yet achieved sufficient trunk control or readiness for upright mobility to benefit from a lower extremity prosthesis.
column9: 12 months is incorrect because delaying prosthetic fitting until this age may hinder early motor development and adaptation to the prosthesis.
column10: 18 months is incorrect because fitting at this age is unnecessarily delayed and may interfere with normal gait development and balance acquisition.

column2: A parent of a 4-year-old child with a congenital transtibial amputation asks how often their child will need a new prosthesis. The child is active, growing normally, and uses their prosthesis daily. What is the most appropriate guidance to provide?
column7: Every 12 months is correct because children under age 5 typically require annual prosthetic replacement due to rapid growth and high activity levels.
column8: Every 6 months is incorrect because this is more frequent than necessary and would place unnecessary burden on the family and provider.
column9: Every 24 months is incorrect because this interval is more appropriate for children aged 5 to 12, not a 4-year-old.
column10: Every 36 months is incorrect because this replacement schedule is used for adolescents and adults, not young children undergoing rapid growth.

column2: During a clinic visit, the parent of a 6-year-old child with a congenital transfemoral amputation asks how often their child will need a new prosthesis. The child is growing steadily and uses the prosthesis daily for school and play. What is the most appropriate guidance to provide?
column7: Every 24 months is correct because children aged 5 to 12 typically require prosthetic replacement every two years to accommodate steady growth and activity.
column8: Every 12 months is incorrect because this replacement schedule is more appropriate for children under 5 years of age.
column9: Every 6 months is incorrect because this frequency is excessive for a 6-year-old with stable growth.
column10: Every 48 months is incorrect because this interval is too long and may lead to discomfort or biomechanical issues due to outgrowing the prosthesis.

column2: A 13-year-old with a history of traumatic below-knee amputation presents for routine follow-up. The teenager has completed most of their linear growth and wants to know how often they will need a new prosthesis moving forward. What is the most appropriate replacement interval?
column7: Every 36 months is correct because adolescents and adults typically require prosthetic replacement every 3 to 4 years unless there are major functional changes or damage.
column8: Every 12 months is incorrect because this is more frequent than necessary in adolescents with completed growth.
column9: Every 24 months is incorrect because this replacement schedule is more appropriate for school-aged children, not adolescents.
column10: Every 60 months is incorrect because this interval is too long and may result in worn components or poor fit over time.

column2: A 10-year-old child with a history of congenital limb deficiency presents with localized pain and a firm prominence at the end of the residual limb. On examination, there is tenderness over the distal femur. Radiographs reveal a sharp, spike-like projection from the distal femoral stump. Which of the following best explains this complication?
column7: Terminal bony overgrowth is correct because this condition occurs in growing children due to active growth plates, leading to spike-like projections from the bone end visible on radiographs and associated with pain and soft tissue irritation.
column8: Neuroma formation is incorrect because neuromas originate from nerve tissue and are not visible as bony projections on imaging.
column9: Heterotopic ossification is incorrect because it presents as cloud-like, ectopic bone in soft tissues, not as a sharp extension from the bone stump.
column10: Poor socket fit is incorrect because it does not account for radiographic evidence of a pointed bony projection from the residual limb.

column2: A 10-year-old with a congenital transfemoral amputation presents with pain and skin breakdown over the residual limb. Imaging reveals a spike-like projection from the distal femur consistent with terminal bony overgrowth. Conservative measures have failed. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column3: Surgical revision with capping of the bone end
column5: Local steroid injection to the area of tenderness
column7: Surgical revision with capping of the bone end is correct because definitive treatment for terminal bony overgrowth involves removing the spike and surgically reshaping the bone, often using a cap (e.g., autogenous bone, cartilage, or synthetic material) to reduce recurrence.
column8: Increase prosthetic socket padding is incorrect because it may provide temporary relief but does not address the underlying structural cause.
column9: Local steroid injection to the area of tenderness is incorrect because this approach is used for soft tissue inflammation or neuromas, not bony projections.
column10: Targeted muscle reinnervation is incorrect because this is a technique for managing neuroma pain and prosthetic control in adults, not used to address bony overgrowth in children.

column2: A 9-year-old boy undergoes a transtibial amputation after a traumatic lawnmower injury. Two weeks postoperatively, he reports intermittent cramping and burning sensations in the missing foot. His incision is well-healed, without erythema, warmth, drainage, or fever. What is the most appropriate interpretation of this symptom?
column3: Phantom limb pain is a common and usually self-limited experience in children following amputation
column4: Phantom limb pain is uncommon in children and should prompt urgent imaging
column5: This presentation is consistent with stump infection and requires immediate antibiotics
column6: These symptoms suggest complex regional pain syndrome
column7: Phantom limb pain is a common and usually self-limited experience in children following amputation is correct because children often experience burning or cramping phantom pain within weeks after surgery, and in the absence of infection signs such as erythema, warmth, drainage, or fever, this is most consistent with expected postoperative phantom pain.
column8: Phantom limb pain is uncommon in children and should prompt urgent imaging is incorrect because phantom limb pain is well-recognized in pediatric amputees, particularly after trauma.
column9: This presentation is consistent with stump infection and requires immediate antibiotics is incorrect because the clinical picture lacks hallmark signs of infection such as redness, warmth, discharge, or systemic symptoms.
column10: These symptoms suggest complex regional pain syndrome is incorrect because CRPS typically affects intact limbs and does not usually present as early postoperative phantom pain in an amputated extremity.

column2: A 2-month-old infant is brought in for evaluation of a curved foot noted since birth. On examination, the forefoot is adducted relative to the hindfoot, a deep medial crease is present, and the heel is in a neutral position. The deformity is flexible on passive manipulation. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Metatarsus varus is correct because it presents with forefoot adduction, a deep medial crease, and a neutral heel; while sometimes used interchangeably with metatarsus adductus, metatarsus varus refers to the more rigid, structural variant with subluxation at the tarsometatarsal joint and is more likely to require treatment.
column8: Talipes equinovarus is incorrect because it involves hindfoot equinus and varus in addition to forefoot adduction, and the deformity is rigid.
column9: Congenital vertical talus is incorrect because it presents as a rigid rocker-bottom foot with hindfoot equinus and forefoot dorsiflexion.
column10: Calcaneovalgus foot is incorrect because it features a dorsiflexed forefoot and everted heel, typically due to intrauterine positioning and is usually flexible.

column2: A newborn presents with a rigid foot deformity noted at birth. Examination reveals forefoot adduction, hindfoot varus, hindfoot equinus, and a deep medial crease. Passive manipulation does not correct the position. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Talipes equinovarus is correct because the combination of forefoot adduction, hindfoot varus, hindfoot equinus, a deep medial crease, and rigidity on exam are characteristic features of clubfoot or talipes equinovarus.
column8: Metatarsus adductus is incorrect because it presents with forefoot adduction but lacks hindfoot varus and equinus, and is usually flexible.
column9: Congenital vertical talus is incorrect because it features a rigid rocker-bottom deformity with hindfoot equinus and forefoot dorsiflexion.
column10: Calcaneovalgus foot is incorrect because it typically presents as a flexible deformity with dorsiflexed forefoot and everted heel.
column12: Clubfoot Mnemonic: CAVE – Cavus, Adductus, Varus, Equinus

column2: What is the most appropriate initial treatment of congenital clubfoot in a newborn?
column3: Serial manipulation and casting using the Ponseti method
column5: Surgical lengthening of the Achilles tendon
column7: Serial manipulation and casting using the Ponseti method is correct because the Ponseti method is the gold standard initial treatment for congenital clubfoot or talies equinovarus and should begin soon after birth to gradually correct the deformity and minimize the need for surgery.
column8: Observation until 6 months of age is incorrect because untreated clubfoot does not resolve spontaneously and delayed treatment reduces the success of nonoperative correction.
column9: Surgical lengthening of the Achilles tendon is incorrect because surgery is reserved for resistant cases after failure of serial casting.
column10: Use of custom-molded orthotic shoes is incorrect because orthotics cannot correct the fixed structural abnormalities seen in congenital clubfoot.
column12: Clubfoot Mnemonic: CAVE – Cavus, Adductus, Varus, Equinus

column2: A 2-week-old infant is brought in for evaluation of bilateral curved feet. Examination reveals adduction of the forefoot with neutral hindfoot position. The deformities are flexible and correct to neutral with passive manipulation. What is the most appropriate initial management?
column4: Serial casting using the Ponseti technique
column5: Surgical correction at 9 months of age
column7: Parental stretching exercises and observation is correct because flexible metatarsus adductus in a newborn typically resolves spontaneously, and the initial management is parental stretching during diaper changes along with monitoring.
column8: Serial casting using the Ponseti technique is incorrect because this is the treatment for clubfoot, not flexible metatarsus adductus.
column9: Surgical correction at 9 months of age is incorrect because surgery is rarely indicated and only considered for severe rigid deformities unresponsive to conservative treatment.
column10: Custom-molded orthotic shoes is incorrect because orthoses are not needed in the early management of flexible deformities and do not improve outcomes over stretching.

column2: A full-term newborn presents with a foot deformity noted at birth. On examination, the foot is dorsiflexed against the anterior shin, the heel is everted, and the foot can be passively brought to a neutral position. The infant shows no discomfort on manipulation, and there are no other musculoskeletal abnormalities. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Talipes calcaneovalgus is correct because the dorsiflexed, everted foot that passively returns to neutral in an otherwise healthy newborn is characteristic of this positional deformity, often related to intrauterine positioning.
column8: Talipes equinovarus is incorrect because it presents with rigid plantarflexion, hindfoot varus, and forefoot adduction.
column9: Congenital vertical talus is incorrect because it presents with a rigid rocker-bottom foot and hindfoot equinus.
column10: Metatarsus adductus is incorrect because it involves forefoot adduction with a neutral hindfoot and does not involve dorsiflexion or eversion.

column2: A newborn is diagnosed with talipes calcaneovalgus. The foot is dorsiflexed and everted but can be passively brought to a normal position. The infant is otherwise healthy. What is the most appropriate initial management?
column7: Observation and parental stretching exercises is correct because talipes calcaneovalgus is typically a flexible positional deformity that resolves spontaneously with simple stretching and does not require formal intervention.
column8: Immediate referral for serial casting is incorrect because casting is not needed for a flexible deformity that passively returns to neutral.
column9: Surgical correction at 6 months is incorrect because this condition nearly always resolves without surgery.
column10: Custom orthotic shoes is incorrect because orthotics offer no benefit in the early management of a flexible positional deformity.

column2: A 16-year-old patient with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease presents with complaints of chronic foot discomfort. On exam, the patient has pes cavus, distal muscle atrophy, and weakness of intrinsic foot muscles. Inspection reveals hyperextension at the metatarsophalangeal joints with flexion at the proximal and distal interphalangeal joints. Which toe deformity is most commonly associated with this condition?
column7: Claw toes is correct because Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease leads to weakness of intrinsic foot muscles, resulting in claw toe deformity characterized by MTP hyperextension and PIP/DIP flexion.
column8: Hammer toes is incorrect because they involve flexion at the PIP joint with extension at the MTP and DIP joints.
column9: Mallet toes is incorrect because they involve isolated flexion of the DIP joint, not the combined pattern described.
column10: Overlapping toes is incorrect because this term refers to positional overlap, not joint-specific contractures.

column2: At what age does persistent genu varum typically warrant further evaluation?
column7: After 2 years of age is correct because physiologic genu varum is considered normal until approximately age 2; persistence beyond this age raises concern for pathologic causes such as Blount disease or rickets and warrants further workup.
column8: After 6 months of age is incorrect because genu varum is expected in infants and still within the range of normal at this age.
column9: After 4 years of age is incorrect because most children exhibit physiologic genu valgum by this age, and concerns would have already been addressed.
column10: After 1 year of age is incorrect because genu varum is still a normal physiologic finding in children up to 2 years old.

column2: At what age does persistent genu valgus typically warrant further evaluation?
column7: After 7 years of age is correct because physiologic genu valgus typically peaks around ages 3–4 and resolves by age 7; persistence beyond this age may indicate a pathologic cause such as skeletal dysplasia or metabolic bone disease.
column8: After 3 years of age is incorrect because mild genu valgus is still physiologic at this age and typically peaks around this time.
column9: After 18 months of age is incorrect because genu valgus typically has not yet developed significantly by this age; genu varum is more common.
column10: After 5 years of age is incorrect because valgus alignment often persists into early school age and usually resolves by age 7.

column2: A 2-year-old child is brought in for evaluation of worsening bowing of the legs. The parents report the deformity has become more pronounced over the past 6 months. On exam, there is asymmetric genu varum, internal tibial torsion, and a waddling gait. Radiographs reveal medial beaking and fragmentation of the proximal tibial metaphysis. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Blount disease is correct because progressive, asymmetric genu varum with internal tibial torsion in a toddler, along with radiographic findings of medial metaphyseal beaking and fragmentation of the proximal tibia, are classic features of Blount disease.
column8: Physiologic genu varum is incorrect because this typically improves by age 2 and is usually symmetric and non-progressive.
column9: Rickets is incorrect because while it can present with bowing, it is typically associated with systemic signs such as poor growth, rachitic rosary, and lab abnormalities like low vitamin D.
column10: Developmental dysplasia of the hip is incorrect because it presents with leg length discrepancy, asymmetric thigh folds, and limited hip abduction—not progressive tibial bowing.

column2: A 3-year-old child with infantile Blount disease presents with progressive unilateral genu varum. Bracing has failed after 12 months of use. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column5: Epiphysiodesis of the lateral tibial physis
column6: Physical therapy to strengthen hip abductors
column7: Tibial osteotomy is correct because this child with progressive infantile Blount disease has failed 12 months of bracing, making surgical correction with a valgus-producing proximal tibial osteotomy the next step in management.
column8: Continue bracing for another 6 months is incorrect because failure to improve after 12 months of bracing indicates poor response, and further delay risks worsening deformity.
column9: Epiphysiodesis of the lateral tibial physis is incorrect because this is not the preferred initial surgical treatment in young children with Blount disease.
column10: Physical therapy to strengthen hip abductors is incorrect because muscular strengthening alone cannot correct pathologic tibial varus.

column2: A 10-month-old infant is brought to clinic for concern of asymmetric leg lengths. On examination, the child is supine with hips and knees flexed, and one knee appears lower than the other. Which physical exam test is being performed?
column7: Galeazzi test is correct because the examiner observes for knee height asymmetry in a supine infant with hips and knees flexed, which can indicate unilateral hip dislocation or femoral length discrepancy.
column8: Ortolani maneuver is incorrect because it involves abducting the hips to reduce a dislocated femoral head, not comparing knee heights.
column9: Barlow maneuver is incorrect because it assesses hip stability by attempting to dislocate the hip posteriorly, not by checking for leg length asymmetry.
column10: Thomas test is incorrect because it is used to assess hip flexion contractures, not leg length differences in infants.

column2: During a newborn examination, the physician flexes the infant’s hips and knees to 90 degrees and applies gentle posterior pressure while adducting each hip. A subtle clunk is felt in the left hip. Which physical exam maneuver is being performed?
column7: Barlow maneuver is correct because this maneuver involves posterior pressure and adduction of the hip to assess for dislocatable hips, with a positive test producing a palpable clunk as the femoral head slips out of the acetabulum.
column8: Ortolani maneuver is incorrect because it involves abduction and anterior pressure to reduce a dislocated hip rather than dislocate it.
column9: Galeazzi test is incorrect because it compares knee heights in a flexed supine position and does not involve dynamic hip manipulation.
column10: Trendelenburg test is incorrect because it evaluates hip abductor strength and is performed in a standing patient, not a newborn exam.

column2: A newborn with suspected developmental dysplasia of the hip undergoes examination. The physician flexes the infant’s hips and knees to 90 degrees, then gently abducts the hips while applying anterior pressure on the greater trochanters. A palpable clunk is appreciated on the right. Which test is being performed?
column7: Ortolani maneuver is correct because this maneuver involves hip abduction with anterior pressure to reduce a dislocated femoral head into the acetabulum, indicated by a palpable clunk.
column8: Barlow maneuver is incorrect because it involves adduction and posterior pressure to attempt dislocation of a reducible hip.
column9: Galeazzi test is incorrect because it compares knee heights in a supine infant, not hip stability during dynamic movement.
column10: Thomas test is incorrect because it assesses hip flexion contracture in older children or adults, not hip dislocation in newborns.

column2: A 6-week-old infant is noted to have asymmetric thigh folds and limited abduction of the left hip. The Barlow and Ortolani maneuvers are equivocal. What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation?
column4: Observation with repeat physical exam in 1 month
column7: Bilateral hip ultrasound is correct because this infant is under 6 months old with clinical signs concerning for developmental dysplasia of the hip, and ultrasound is the imaging modality of choice before femoral head ossification.
column8: Observation with repeat physical exam in 1 month is incorrect because persistent physical findings warrant imaging rather than watchful waiting.
column9: Anteroposterior pelvis radiograph is incorrect because radiographs are not reliable before 4–6 months due to lack of ossification of the femoral head.
column10: Immediate referral for surgical correction is incorrect because early nonoperative interventions, such as a Pavlik harness, are typically first-line after imaging confirmation.

column2: A 2-month-old infant is diagnosed with unilateral developmental dysplasia of the hip confirmed by ultrasound. The femoral head is reducible and no bony abnormalities are present. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
column7: Pavlik harness is correct because this is the first-line treatment for infants under 6 months with reducible developmental dysplasia of the hip and no structural bony abnormalities.
column8: Closed reduction under general anesthesia is incorrect because this is reserved for cases where the Pavlik harness fails or the hip is not reducible.
column9: Open surgical reduction with osteotomy is incorrect because this is typically reserved for older children or failed closed reduction.
column10: Hip spica casting without reduction is incorrect because casting alone without a prior reduction is not effective and not standard practice.

column2: A 4-week-old infant presents with right-sided congenital muscular torticollis. Which head and chin position would most likely be observed on physical examination?
column3: Head tilted right, chin rotated left
column4: Head tilted left, chin rotated right
column5: Head tilted right, chin rotated right
column6: Head tilted left, chin rotated left
column7: Head tilted right, chin rotated left is correct because right-sided congenital muscular torticollis involves contracture of the right sternocleidomastoid, which tilts the head toward the affected side and rotates the chin to the opposite side.
column8: Head tilted left, chin rotated right is incorrect because this pattern suggests left-sided torticollis, not right.
column9: Head tilted right, chin rotated right is incorrect because simultaneous tilt and rotation to the same side is not consistent with SCM contracture.
column10: Head tilted left, chin rotated left is incorrect because this presentation does not reflect the typical biomechanics of unilateral SCM shortening.

column2: A 4-week-old infant presents with left-sided congenital muscular torticollis. Which head and chin position would most likely be observed on physical examination?
column3: Head tilted left, chin rotated right
column4: Head tilted right, chin rotated left
column5: Head tilted left, chin rotated left
column6: Head tilted right, chin rotated right
column7: Head tilted left, chin rotated right is correct because left-sided congenital muscular torticollis involves contracture of the left sternocleidomastoid, which tilts the head toward the affected side and rotates the chin to the opposite side.
column8: Head tilted right, chin rotated left is incorrect because this pattern corresponds to right-sided SCM involvement.
column9: Head tilted left, chin rotated left is incorrect because this motion pattern does not align with unilateral SCM shortening.
column10: Head tilted right, chin rotated right is incorrect because both tilt and rotation to the same side are inconsistent with SCM biomechanics.

column2: What is the most appropriate initial management for congenital torticollis caused by cervical hemivertebra?
column7: Referral to pediatric spine surgeon is correct because congenital torticollis due to cervical hemivertebra is a bony abnormality that often requires surgical evaluation rather than conservative stretching or strengthening alone.
column8: Initiation of passive range-of-motion stretching is incorrect because stretching is appropriate for muscular torticollis but ineffective in structural bony deformities.
column9: Trial of soft cervical collar is incorrect because collars do not address the underlying vertebral anomaly and have no role in treating structural torticollis.
column10: Physical therapy with strengthening only is incorrect because strengthening alone does not correct a fixed deformity caused by hemivertebra.

column2: A 6-week-old infant is diagnosed with congenital muscular torticollis. There is no evidence of cervical spine anomalies on imaging. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
column7: Passive stretching exercises is correct because the first-line treatment for congenital muscular torticollis without underlying skeletal abnormalities is a structured program of passive range-of-motion stretching initiated early in infancy.
column8: Soft cervical collar use is incorrect because collars are not effective in improving muscle length or correcting positional preference.
column9: Surgical release of the sternocleidomastoid is incorrect because this is reserved for children who do not respond to conservative treatment over several months.
column10: Chemodenervation of the sternocleidomastoid is incorrect because it is not considered first-line and is typically used in refractory or older cases.

column2: A 2-year-old child is brought to clinic refusing to move her left arm after being lifted by the hand. She holds the arm slightly flexed and pronated. What is the most likely underlying mechanism?
column3: Displacement and entrapment of the annular ligament
column4: Radial head fracture with joint effusion
column5: Dislocation of the radial head from the humerus
column7: Displacement and entrapment of the annular ligament is correct because nursemaid’s elbow occurs when axial traction on a pronated forearm causes the annular ligament to slip over the radial head and become entrapped between the radial head and capitellum.
column8: Radial head fracture with joint effusion is incorrect because fractures typically present with localized swelling and point tenderness, which are absent in classic nursemaid’s elbow.
column9: Dislocation of the radial head from the humerus is incorrect because the radial head remains anatomically aligned and is not fully dislocated in this condition.
column10: Subluxation of the radial head is incorrect because although transient subluxation facilitates the condition, the symptoms result from annular ligament entrapment, not from persistent radial head malalignment.

column2: What is the first-line treatment for nursemaid’s elbow?
column3: Supination with flexion of the elbow
column6: Surgical release of the annular ligament
column7: Supination with flexion of the elbow is correct because it is the most commonly used and effective first-line technique to reduce the entrapped annular ligament in nursemaid’s elbow.
column8: Hyperpronation of the forearm is incorrect because although it is an accepted alternative technique, it is typically used if supination-flexion fails.
column9: Extension with axial traction is incorrect because this maneuver does not facilitate reduction and may worsen the annular ligament entrapment.
column10: Surgical release of the annular ligament is incorrect because nursemaid’s elbow is almost always treated successfully with closed reduction and does not require surgery.

column2: A 5-year-old boy presents with a limp and right hip pain for two days. He had a mild upper respiratory illness one week ago. He is afebrile, and his labs show a normal white blood cell count, ESR, and CRP. On exam, he has mild limitation in internal rotation of the right hip but no tenderness. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Transient synovitis of the hip is correct because this child has acute, atraumatic hip pain following a recent viral illness with normal inflammatory markers and mild limitation in motion, which are classic features.
column8: Septic arthritis is incorrect because it typically presents with high fever, inability to bear weight, and elevated inflammatory markers.
column9: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is incorrect because it presents insidiously with chronic symptoms and often shows radiographic changes in the femoral head.
column10: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis is incorrect because it occurs in older, typically overweight children and causes external rotation deformity and limited flexion.

column2: A 7-year-old boy presents with a painless limp that has gradually worsened over the past few weeks. He denies recent trauma or illness. On examination, there is limited abduction and internal rotation of the hip. Pelvic X-ray reveals increased density and flattening of the femoral head. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is correct because this child has a gradual, painless limp with limited hip motion and X-ray findings of avascular necrosis of the femoral head, which are classic for this condition.
column8: Transient synovitis of the hip is incorrect because it typically presents acutely after a viral illness and resolves within a week or two without radiographic changes.
column9: Septic arthritis is incorrect because it presents acutely with fever, pain, and elevated inflammatory markers, not with a gradual painless limp.
column10: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis is incorrect because it typically affects obese adolescents and presents with hip or knee pain and externally rotated posture of the leg.

column2: What is the underlying mechanism responsible for Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease?
column3: Idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head
column4: Displacement of the femoral epiphysis due to shear stress
column5: Infection of the hip joint leading to cartilage destruction
column7: Idiopathic avascular necrosis of the femoral head is correct because Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease results from interrupted blood supply to the capital femoral epiphysis, leading to ischemia and bone necrosis.
column8: Displacement of the femoral epiphysis due to shear stress is incorrect because this describes slipped capital femoral epiphysis, not Perthes disease.
column9: Infection of the hip joint leading to cartilage destruction is incorrect because this describes septic arthritis, which is acute and infectious in origin.
column10: Post-viral inflammation of the synovium is incorrect because this describes transient synovitis, not the structural ischemic process of Perthes disease.

column2: A 13-year-old obese boy presents with several weeks of left knee pain and a limp. He has no history of trauma or recent illness. On exam, his left leg rests in external rotation, and there is obligatory external rotation with passive hip flexion. Internal rotation of the hip is limited. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Slipped capital femoral epiphysis is correct because this condition presents in overweight adolescents with referred knee pain, limited internal rotation, and obligatory external rotation with passive hip flexion.
column8: Legg-Calvé-Perthes disease is incorrect because it typically presents in younger children with gradual painless limping and shows avascular necrosis on imaging.
column9: Transient synovitis of the hip is incorrect because it usually presents acutely in younger children with recent viral illness and resolves quickly without mechanical restriction.
column10: Femoral shaft stress fracture is incorrect because it causes localized femoral pain without hip rotation abnormalities or external leg positioning.

column2: A 6-year-old girl presents for evaluation of a swollen right knee. She has no history of trauma, but her parents report that she has been limping intermittently for the past two months. On examination, the right knee is warm and mildly swollen, with decreased range of motion. She has no systemic symptoms. ANA is positive. Which of the following juvenile idiopathic arthritis (JIA) subtypes is most associated with the highest risk of developing iridocyclitis?
column7: Oligoarticular JIA is correct because this subtype commonly affects young girls under age 6, often presents with a single swollen joint (commonly the knee), and is strongly associated with ANA positivity and increased risk of chronic asymptomatic iridocyclitis.
column8: Systemic JIA is incorrect because it is associated with systemic features like fever and rash, and has a low risk of iridocyclitis.
column9: Enthesitis-related arthritis is incorrect because it typically affects older boys and is more associated with HLA-B27 than with iridocyclitis.
column10: Polyarticular RF-positive JIA is incorrect because it resembles adult RA and has a low association with uveitis.

column2: A 13-year-old boy presents with heel pain, morning stiffness, and swelling at the Achilles tendon insertion. He also has intermittent low back pain and limited lumbar flexion. HLA-B27 is positive, and his father has ankylosing spondylitis. On examination, there is tenderness at multiple entheses. Which subtype of juvenile idiopathic arthritis is most consistent with this presentation?
column7: Enthesitis-related arthritis is correct because this subtype commonly affects HLA-B27–positive adolescent boys, especially those with enthesitis, axial symptoms, and a family history of spondyloarthropathy.
column8: Oligoarticular JIA is incorrect because it usually affects young ANA-positive girls with large joint arthritis but without axial or enthesitis findings.
column9: Systemic JIA is incorrect because it presents with daily fevers and rash, which are not seen in this case.
column10: Psoriatic arthritis is incorrect because it is associated with psoriasis, dactylitis, or nail pitting, none of which are present.

column2: A 9-year-old boy presents with intermittent high fevers for two weeks, a transient salmon-colored rash, and generalized joint swelling. His parents note that the rash worsens during fevers. He also has hepatosplenomegaly on exam. What is the most likely juvenile idiopathic arthritis subtype?
column7: Systemic JIA is correct because it presents with daily (quotidian) fevers, evanescent rash, and systemic features such as hepatosplenomegaly, with or without arthritis initially.
column8: Polyarticular RF-negative JIA is incorrect because it lacks systemic symptoms like fever or rash.
column9: Psoriatic arthritis is incorrect because there is no evidence of psoriasis, dactylitis, or nail changes.
column10: Oligoarticular JIA is incorrect because it does not present with systemic illness or multi-organ involvement.

column2: A 6-year-old girl presents with swelling of the right knee and dactylitis of the second toe. Her mother has psoriasis. On exam, she also has nail pitting. She has had symptoms for over six weeks. What is the most likely juvenile idiopathic arthritis subtype?
column7: Psoriatic arthritis is correct because diagnosis can be made in the presence of arthritis plus dactylitis, nail pitting, and a family history of psoriasis.
column8: Oligoarticular JIA is incorrect because it does not typically present with dactylitis or nail pitting.
column9: Polyarticular RF-negative JIA is incorrect because it requires ≥5 joints and lacks psoriatic features.
column10: Systemic JIA is incorrect because it requires systemic symptoms like fever and rash.

column2: A 6-year-old girl is diagnosed with oligoarticular juvenile idiopathic arthritis involving a single swollen knee. Symptoms began three weeks ago. She has no systemic involvement. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
column7: NSAIDs is correct because newly diagnosed oligoarticular JIA without systemic features should be initially treated with NSAIDs to reduce inflammation and monitor response.
column8: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection is incorrect because it is used after failure of NSAIDs or when rapid local control is needed.
column9: Methotrexate is incorrect because it is reserved for refractory oligoarticular or polyarticular disease.
column10: TNF-alpha inhibitor therapy is incorrect because it is not first-line and is used after DMARD failure.

column2: A 28-year-old man presents with chronic low back pain and stiffness for over six months that improves with exercise and worsens with rest. He reports morning stiffness lasting over 45 minutes. Physical exam reveals decreased lumbar flexion and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. HLA-B27 is positive. What imaging finding is required to confirm the diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis?
column5: Bamboo spine on plain lumbar X-ray
column6: Bone marrow edema on sacroiliac MRI
column7: Bilateral sacroiliitis on plain radiographs is correct because radiographic sacroiliitis is required to confirm a diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis according to the modified New York criteria.
column8: Syndesmophytes on lumbar spine MRI is incorrect because these are characteristic findings but not required for diagnosis.
column9: Bamboo spine on plain lumbar X-ray is incorrect because it reflects advanced disease and is not necessary for diagnosis.
column10: Bone marrow edema on sacroiliac MRI is incorrect because it may support early disease (non-radiographic axial spondyloarthritis) but does not meet criteria for ankylosing spondylitis.

column2: A 17-year-old girl presents with a malar rash, oral ulcers, arthritis in both wrists and knees, and a positive ANA. She has no renal or neurologic symptoms. Which of the following best supports the diagnosis of systemic lupus erythematosus based on the 1997 ACR classification criteria?
column3: Presence of four of eleven established clinical and laboratory criteria
column5: One clinical criterion plus a biopsy-proven lupus nephritis
column7: Presence of four of eleven established clinical and laboratory criteria is correct because the 1997 ACR criteria require at least 4 of the 11 criteria to be met for classification of SLE.
column8: Positive ANA plus anti-dsDNA antibodies is incorrect because this combination alone does not meet the threshold for classification.
column9: One clinical criterion plus a biopsy-proven lupus nephritis is incorrect because this is a component of newer classification systems (e.g., SLICC), not ACR 1997.
column10: Photosensitivity, arthritis, and positive ANA is incorrect because only three of the required four criteria are met.

column2: An 8-year-old girl presents with progressive proximal muscle weakness, difficulty climbing stairs, and frequent falls. On exam, she has a heliotrope rash on her eyelids and erythematous papules over the extensor surfaces of her metacarpophalangeal and interphalangeal joints. Her CK is elevated. Which of the following is most supportive of the diagnosis of juvenile dermatomyositis?
column3: Symmetric proximal muscle weakness with classic skin findings
column7: Symmetric proximal muscle weakness with classic skin findings is correct because the hallmark of juvenile dermatomyositis is proximal muscle weakness in combination with pathognomonic rashes such as heliotrope rash and Gottron papules.
column8: Positive ANA with elevated CK is incorrect because these are nonspecific findings seen in multiple autoimmune conditions.
column9: Muscle biopsy showing perifascicular inflammation is incorrect because although supportive, biopsy is not required when characteristic clinical features are present.
column10: Abnormal EMG with myopathic changes is incorrect because EMG findings are nonspecific and not diagnostic on their own.

column2: A 7-year-old girl is diagnosed with juvenile dermatomyositis after presenting with symmetric proximal muscle weakness, a heliotrope rash, and Gottron papules. Her creatine kinase is elevated, and MRI shows muscle edema. She has difficulty standing from the floor and climbing stairs. What is the most appropriate initial treatment?
column7: High-dose corticosteroids is correct because the first-line treatment for juvenile dermatomyositis is high-dose systemic corticosteroids to rapidly suppress inflammation and halt disease progression.
column8: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) is incorrect because it is used for refractory disease or in patients with contraindications to steroids.
column9: Methotrexate monotherapy is incorrect because it is typically added as a steroid-sparing agent but not used as initial monotherapy.
column10: Azathioprine is incorrect because it is a second-line immunosuppressant used when there is poor response to first-line agents.

column2: A 4-year-old child presents with acute right hip pain, fever, refusal to bear weight, and a joint aspirate showing gram-positive cocci in clusters. What is the most likely cause of septic arthritis?
column7: Staphylococcus aureus is correct because it is the most common cause of septic arthritis in children and presents with acute monoarthritis and gram-positive cocci in clusters on gram stain.
column8: Kingella kingae is incorrect because it usually does not show organisms on gram stain and is more likely culture-negative.
column9: Streptococcus pyogenes is incorrect because although it may cause septic arthritis, it presents as chains of cocci on gram stain, not clusters.
column10: Haemophilus influenzae type b is incorrect because its incidence has drastically decreased due to vaccination and it appears as gram-negative rods.
column12:
In neonates, the most common causes of septic arthritis are Staphylococcus aureus, Group B Streptococcus, and gram-negative organisms like E. coli.
In infants under 1 year, Staphylococcus aureus remains most common, with Kingella kingae and Haemophilus influenzae (if unvaccinated) also possible.
In toddlers and young children, Staphylococcus aureus and Kingella kingae are the leading pathogens.
In older children, Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes are most common.
In adolescents, consider Staphylococcus aureus and Neisseria gonorrhoeae if sexually active.

column2: A 10-year-old boy from Connecticut presents with an intermittently swollen right knee that has persisted for several weeks but is not painful. He is otherwise well and has full range of motion. There is no history of trauma. Labs show a mildly elevated ESR and negative ANA. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Lyme disease arthritis is correct because it commonly presents in children as intermittent, nonpainful monoarthritis (especially in the knee) in endemic regions, with few systemic symptoms.
column8: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis is incorrect because it typically causes morning stiffness and persistent joint inflammation with functional limitation.
column9: Septic arthritis is incorrect because it usually causes acute onset of pain, fever, and limited motion.
column10: Transient synovitis is incorrect because it usually affects the hip, is self-limited, and resolves within a week or two.

column2: A 9-year-old boy from Pennsylvania presents with painless swelling of his right knee for the past two weeks. He has no fever, no trauma, and is otherwise well. Joint aspiration reveals an inflammatory profile with negative Gram stain and culture. ELISA and Western blot are positive for Lyme disease. Which organism is responsible for this presentation?
column7: Borrelia burgdorferi is correct because it is a spirochete transmitted by Ixodes ticks and is the causative agent of Lyme disease, which often presents with painless monoarthritis of large joints in children.
column8: Treponema pallidum is incorrect because it causes syphilis, which has systemic and neurologic manifestations rather than isolated arthritis.
column9: Rickettsia rickettsii is incorrect because it causes Rocky Mountain spotted fever, typically with rash and systemic symptoms.
column10: Mycoplasma pneumoniae is incorrect because it causes atypical pneumonia and is not associated with Lyme arthritis.

column2: A 2-week-old newborn presents with fever, irritability, and decreased spontaneous movement of the right leg. Exam reveals swelling and warmth over the right knee. Joint aspiration reveals purulent fluid. What is the most common cause of septic arthritis in neonates?
column7: Staphylococcus aureus is correct because it is the most common overall cause of septic arthritis in neonates, even though gram-negative and group B streptococcal organisms are also more prevalent in this age group than in older children.
column8: Escherichia coli is incorrect because it is a known cause in neonates but not the most common overall.
column9: Group B Streptococcus is incorrect because it is a leading cause of neonatal sepsis but is slightly less common than Staphylococcus aureus for isolated septic arthritis.
column10: Kingella kingae is incorrect because it typically affects toddlers and is rare in neonates.
column12:
In neonates, the most common causes of septic arthritis are Staphylococcus aureus, Group B Streptococcus, and gram-negative organisms like E. coli.
In infants under 1 year, Staphylococcus aureus remains most common, with Kingella kingae and Haemophilus influenzae (if unvaccinated) also possible.
In toddlers and young children, Staphylococcus aureus and Kingella kingae are the leading pathogens.
In older children, Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes are most common.
In adolescents, consider Staphylococcus aureus and Neisseria gonorrhoeae if sexually active.

column2: A 2-year-old boy presents with a swollen and warm right knee. He has no history of trauma or fever but has had two prior episodes of joint swelling that resolved without intervention. His maternal uncle had a bleeding disorder. Joint aspiration reveals bloody fluid without signs of infection. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column5: Schedule joint MRI to evaluate for osteomyelitis
column6: Send ANA and RF for juvenile idiopathic arthritis workup
column7: Refer to pediatric hematology is correct because spontaneous hemarthrosis, family history of bleeding, and bloody aspirate in a male child strongly suggest hemophilia, warranting hematology evaluation for definitive diagnosis and factor replacement.
column8: Begin NSAIDs and physical therapy is incorrect because treatment should not begin before confirming the diagnosis and could increase bleeding risk.
column9: Schedule joint MRI to evaluate for osteomyelitis is incorrect because there are no signs of infection, and bloody aspirate points toward a bleeding disorder, not bone infection.
column10: Send ANA and RF for juvenile idiopathic arthritis workup is incorrect because the presentation is more consistent with a bleeding disorder than autoimmune arthritis.

column2: What is the most common type of burn injury in children?
column7: Scald burn is correct because young children are most frequently burned by hot liquids due to accidental spills or immersion.
column8: Flame burn is incorrect because these typically occur in older children and adults and are less common in toddlers.
column9: Contact burn is incorrect because these are less frequent than scalds and usually involve direct contact with hot objects.
column10: Electrical burn is incorrect because they are uncommon and usually result from specific hazardous exposures.

column2: What is the most common cancer diagnosed in children?
column7: Leukemia is correct because it is the most common childhood cancer, accounting for about 30% of pediatric malignancies, with acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) being the most prevalent subtype.
column8: Central nervous system tumor is incorrect because although it is the most common solid tumor in children, it occurs less frequently than leukemia overall.
column9: Lymphoma is incorrect because it is more common in adolescents than young children and overall less common than leukemia.
column10: Germ cell tumor is incorrect because these are rare pediatric cancers and much less frequent than leukemia.

column2: What is the most common primary brain tumor in children?
column7: Pilocytic astrocytoma is correct because it is the most common pediatric brain tumor, typically presenting in the cerebellum and associated with a relatively favorable prognosis.
column8: Medulloblastoma is incorrect because although it is the most common malignant brain tumor in children, it is overall less common than pilocytic astrocytoma.
column9: Ependymoma is incorrect because it is less common and often arises in the posterior fossa, typically presenting with hydrocephalus.
column10: Craniopharyngioma is incorrect because it is rare and usually presents with visual changes and growth delay due to hypothalamic-pituitary axis involvement.

column2: What is the most common malignant brain tumor in children?
column7: Medulloblastoma is correct because it is the most common malignant brain tumor in children and typically arises in the cerebellar vermis, often presenting with ataxia and signs of increased intracranial pressure.
column8: Pilocytic astrocytoma is incorrect because it is more common overall but is classified as a low-grade, non-malignant tumor.
column9: Ependymoma is incorrect because it is a less common malignant tumor that arises from the ventricular system and may cause obstructive hydrocephalus.
column10: Craniopharyngioma is incorrect because it is a benign tumor that arises from remnants of Rathke’s pouch and is not considered malignant.

column2: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to progressive unsteadiness while walking, frequent morning vomiting, and irritability. On exam, he demonstrates truncal ataxia and a wide-based gait. MRI reveals a midline posterior fossa mass compressing the fourth ventricle. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Medulloblastoma is correct because this malignant tumor commonly arises in the midline cerebellar vermis in children, causing hydrocephalus and presenting with truncal ataxia, morning vomiting, and posterior fossa mass effect.
column8: Pilocytic astrocytoma is incorrect because it usually arises in the cerebellar hemispheres, not the midline vermis, and presents with more lateralizing signs.
column9: Ependymoma is incorrect because although it can occur in the posterior fossa, it typically arises from the floor of the fourth ventricle and presents later with more obstructive hydrocephalus.
column10: Craniopharyngioma is incorrect because it typically presents with visual disturbances and endocrine abnormalities, not cerebellar signs.

column2: Which part of the bone is Ewing sarcoma most commonly found in?
column7: Diaphysis is correct because Ewing sarcoma most commonly arises in the diaphysis (shaft) of long bones such as the femur, tibia, and humerus.
column8: Metaphysis is incorrect because the metaphysis is the more common site of osteosarcoma.
column9: Epiphysis is incorrect because epiphyseal lesions are typically associated with tumors like chondroblastoma or subacute osteomyelitis.
column10: Articular surface is incorrect because bone tumors do not originate from the articular cartilage.

column2: Which primary bone tumor most commonly arises in the diaphysis of long bones?
column7: Ewing sarcoma is correct because it typically originates in the diaphysis of long bones such as the femur and is characterized by aggressive growth and periosteal reaction.
column8: Osteosarcoma is incorrect because it usually arises in the metaphysis of long bones, especially around the knee.
column9: Chondroblastoma is incorrect because it typically arises in the epiphysis of long bones in adolescents.
column10: Giant cell tumor is incorrect because it commonly affects the epiphyseal region in skeletally mature individuals.

column2: Which primary bone tumor most commonly arises in the metaphysis of long bones?
column7: Osteosarcoma is correct because it typically arises in the metaphysis of long bones, especially near the distal femur and proximal tibia, during periods of rapid growth.
column8: Ewing sarcoma is incorrect because it arises most commonly in the diaphysis of long bones.
column9: Chondroblastoma is incorrect because it typically involves the epiphysis, not the metaphysis.
column10: Multiple myeloma is incorrect because it affects the medullary cavity in older adults, not the metaphysis of growing bones.

column2: Which primary bone tumor most commonly arises in the epiphysis of long bones in adolescents?
column7: Chondroblastoma is correct because it characteristically arises in the epiphyseal region of long bones, especially the proximal humerus and femur, during adolescence.
column8: Osteosarcoma is incorrect because it typically affects the metaphysis, not the epiphysis.
column9: Ewing sarcoma is incorrect because it originates in the diaphysis of long bones.
column10: Osteoid osteoma is incorrect because it usually arises in the cortex of the diaphysis or metaphysis and causes nocturnal pain relieved by NSAIDs.

column2: A 6-year-old girl presents with a three-week history of a swollen right knee. There is no history of trauma. She recently returned from a family camping trip in Maine and recalls a red, expanding rash on her leg that resolved without treatment. She is afebrile and has no neurologic symptoms. Joint aspiration reveals inflammatory synovial fluid, and Lyme disease is confirmed by ELISA and Western blot. What is the most appropriate antibiotic treatment?
column7: Amoxicillin is correct because it is the first-line treatment for Lyme arthritis in children under 8 years old without neurologic involvement, according to the 2020 IDSA guidelines.
column8: Doxycycline is incorrect because it is generally avoided in children under 8 due to risks of dental staining and bone effects.
column9: Ceftriaxone is incorrect because it is used for refractory arthritis or cases with neurologic involvement.
column10: Azithromycin is incorrect because it is less effective and not recommended as a first-line agent for Lyme arthritis.

column2: A 13-year-old boy presents with a swollen, mildly painful left knee that developed gradually over the past three weeks. He denies trauma. He recently returned from a hiking trip in Connecticut and recalls a circular rash on his thigh that resolved without treatment. Synovial fluid analysis shows inflammatory characteristics with negative Gram stain and culture. Lyme disease is confirmed by ELISA and Western blot. He has no neurologic symptoms and is otherwise well. What is the most appropriate antibiotic treatment?
column7: Doxycycline is correct because it is the first-line treatment for Lyme arthritis in children over 8 years old without neurologic involvement, as recommended by the 2020 IDSA guidelines.
column8: Ceftriaxone is incorrect because it is reserved for refractory arthritis or cases with neurologic involvement.
column9: Azithromycin is incorrect because it is less effective for Lyme arthritis and not recommended as first-line therapy.
column10: Amoxicillin is incorrect because it is used in younger children or those who cannot take doxycycline, but not typically first-line in adolescents.

column2: A 10-month-old infant is brought to the ED after being found unresponsive in his crib. On exam, he opens his eyes to pain, exhibits flexor posturing, and cries in response to painful stimulation but is not soothed. As you prepare to assign a coma score, which of the following accurately reflects a key distinction between the pediatric and adult Glasgow Coma Scales?
column3: Verbal scoring accounts for preverbal behaviors such as consolability and crying
column4: Eye opening is scored from 0 to 4 in infants instead of 1 to 4 in adults
column5: Motor scoring in infants excludes abnormal posturing as a response category
column6: Total score ranges from 0 to 15 in children versus 3 to 15 in adults
column7: Verbal scoring accounts for preverbal behaviors such as consolability and crying is correct because the pediatric GCS modifies the verbal domain to reflect developmental norms, including responses such as cooing, crying, or being inconsolable.
column8: Eye opening is scored from 0 to 4 in infants instead of 1 to 4 in adults is incorrect because eye opening is scored identically in adults and children, from 1 to 4.
column9: Motor scoring in infants excludes abnormal posturing as a response category is incorrect because abnormal posturing (flexion or extension) is still part of the pediatric motor score.
column10: Total score ranges from 0 to 15 in children versus 3 to 15 in adults is incorrect because both scales share the same scoring range of 3 to 15.

column2: Which of the following is the strongest known risk factor for cerebral palsy?
column7: Prematurity is correct because birth before 32 weeks is the most strongly associated risk factor for cerebral palsy due to the high vulnerability of the developing brain, especially the periventricular white matter.
column8: Acquired congenital infection is incorrect because while infections like CMV or toxoplasmosis can cause CP, they are less common causes compared to prematurity.
column9: Perinatal stroke is incorrect because it is a known cause of hemiplegic CP but is less frequently responsible for CP overall than prematurity.
column10: Maternal smoking during pregnancy is incorrect because although it may contribute to poor fetal growth and other complications, it is not among the leading direct risk factors for cerebral palsy.

column2: Which of the following is the most common type of cerebral palsy?
column7: Spastic diplegia is correct because it is the most common type of cerebral palsy, particularly in children born preterm, and is characterized by predominant lower extremity spasticity and periventricular white matter injury.
column8: Spastic hemiplegia is incorrect because although common, it is more frequently associated with term infants and perinatal stroke, affecting only one side of the body.
column9: Dyskinetic cerebral palsy is incorrect because it is less common and typically results from profound hypoxic-ischemic injury to the basal ganglia.
column10: Spastic tetraplegia is incorrect because it is a severe form of CP involving all limbs and often associated with extensive cortical and subcortical injury, but it is less common than spastic diplegia.

column2: Which of the following gait patterns is most characteristic of cerebral palsy with spastic diplegia?
column3: Excessive hip adduction, toe-walking, and progressive knee flexion during stance
column4: Foot drop with high stepping and increased hip and knee flexion during swing phase
column5: Wide-based gait with lumbar lordosis and pelvic instability
column6: Unilateral leg circumduction with hip hiking and plantarflexion
column7: Excessive hip adduction, toe-walking, and progressive knee flexion during stance is correct because this describes the classic crouch gait with scissoring and equinus seen in spastic diplegia, due to bilateral lower extremity spasticity.
column8: Foot drop with high stepping and increased hip and knee flexion during swing phase is incorrect because it describes a steppage gait, associated with peripheral neuropathy rather than cerebral palsy.
column9: Wide-based gait with lumbar lordosis and pelvic instability is incorrect because this describes a waddling gait, commonly seen in muscular dystrophy with proximal muscle weakness.
column10: Unilateral leg circumduction with hip hiking and plantarflexion is incorrect because this describes a hemiparetic gait, typical of spastic hemiplegia rather than diplegia.

column2: A 5-year-old child with dyskinetic cerebral palsy is undergoing a comprehensive evaluation in a multidisciplinary clinic. The family reports that the child does not respond to spoken instructions unless face-to-face. Audiologic testing confirms bilateral sensorineural hearing loss. Which of the following best explains the association between dyskinetic cerebral palsy and sensorineural hearing loss?
column5: Ischemic infarct to the primary auditory cortex
column6: Birth trauma causing ossicular chain disruption
column7: Cochlear injury from kernicterus is correct because dyskinetic cerebral palsy is commonly associated with kernicterus, which damages the basal ganglia in additon to the auditory pathways including the cochlear nuclei, leading to sensorineural hearing loss.
column8: Periventricular leukomalacia involving auditory tracts is incorrect because PVL primarily affects periventricular white matter and is associated with spastic diplegia, not dyskinetic CP.
column9: Ischemic infarct to the primary auditory cortex is incorrect because it causes cortical hearing loss rather than sensorineural hearing loss and is not typically associated with dyskinetic CP.
column10: Birth trauma causing ossicular chain disruption is incorrect because it would result in conductive, not sensorineural, hearing loss.

column2: A 6-year-old child with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy ambulates independently on level surfaces but has difficulty with stairs and uneven terrain. She does not use a gait aid and is able to walk in most settings, though running and jumping are limited. Which of the following is the most appropriate Gross Motor Function Classification System (GMFCS) level for this child?
column7: Level II is correct because the child walks without a mobility device in most settings, but has difficulty with more advanced motor skills such as running and jumping, consistent with GMFCS Level II.
column8: Level I is incorrect because it describes children who can walk, run, and jump without limitations in most settings, which this child cannot do.
column9: Level III is incorrect because it applies to children who require a handheld mobility device for ambulation.
column10: Level IV is incorrect because it is used for children with severely limited self-mobility who typically use powered mobility or are pushed in a manual wheelchair.
column12:
GMFCS I = walks without limitations  
GMFCS II = walks without devices but has difficulty with uneven terrain, stairs, or running  
GMFCS III = walks with handheld mobility device; uses wheelchair for long distances  
GMFCS IV = limited self-mobility; uses powered mobility or is pushed in wheelchair  
GMFCS V = transported in wheelchair; severely limited head and trunk control

column2: A 7-year-old child with spastic cerebral palsy uses forearm crutches to ambulate short distances at school but uses a wheelchair for longer distances in the community. She is able to sit independently with little support and performs most transfers with assistance. Which of the following is the most appropriate Gross Motor Function Classification System (GMFCS) level for this child?
column7: Level III is correct because the child uses a handheld mobility device for ambulation in indoor settings and relies on wheeled mobility for longer distances, consistent with the definition of GMFCS Level III.
column8: Level II is incorrect because it describes children who walk in most settings without assistive devices, which does not apply here.
column9: Level IV is incorrect because it describes children with severely limited mobility who require powered mobility or are transported in a manual wheelchair.
column10: Level I is incorrect because it refers to children who can walk, run, and jump without limitations or need for assistive devices.
column12:
GMFCS I = walks without limitations  
GMFCS II = walks without devices but has difficulty with uneven terrain, stairs, or running  
GMFCS III = walks with handheld mobility device; uses wheelchair for long distances  
GMFCS IV = limited self-mobility; uses powered mobility or is pushed in wheelchair  
GMFCS V = transported in wheelchair; severely limited head and trunk control

column2: An 8-year-old child with spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy is nonambulatory and uses a power wheelchair for mobility. He can maintain an upright sitting posture with trunk support but requires physical assistance for transfers. He does not walk in any environment. Which GMFCS level best describes this child?
column7: Level IV is correct because this child has limited self-mobility, requires trunk support for sitting, and uses powered mobility, which aligns with GMFCS Level IV.
column8: Level III is incorrect because it describes children who ambulate using handheld mobility devices and only use wheelchairs for longer distances.
column9: Level V is incorrect because it applies to children who lack voluntary control of movement and require total assistance for mobility and posture.
column10: Level II is incorrect because it describes ambulatory children who walk independently without assistive devices, which is not applicable here.
column12:
GMFCS I = walks without limitations  
GMFCS II = walks without devices but has difficulty with uneven terrain, stairs, or running  
GMFCS III = walks with handheld mobility device; uses wheelchair for long distances  
GMFCS IV = limited self-mobility; uses powered mobility or is pushed in wheelchair  
GMFCS V = transported in wheelchair; severely limited head and trunk control

column2: Which of the following statements is least accurate regarding predictors of future walking ability in children with cerebral palsy?
column3: Lack of crawling during infancy reliably predicts non-ambulatory status
column4: Persistence of three or more primitive reflexes at 18–24 months is associated with poor ambulatory outcomes
column5: Independent sitting by age 2 is a strong predictor of future walking ability
column6: Most children who achieve ambulation do so by 7 years of age
column7: Lack of crawling during infancy reliably predicts non-ambulatory status is incorrect because many children who never crawl are still able to walk independently, making crawling an unreliable prognostic marker.
column8: Persistence of three or more primitive reflexes at 18–24 months is associated with poor ambulatory outcomes is correct because ongoing primitive reflexes suggest severe motor impairment and correlate with reduced likelihood of walking.
column9: Independent sitting by age 2 is a strong predictor of future walking ability is correct because this milestone reflects foundational trunk control necessary for eventual ambulation.
column10: Most children who achieve ambulation do so by 7 years of age is correct because walking ability usually develops by that age in children with cerebral palsy who will eventually walk.

column2: A 6-year-old child with spastic quadriplegic cerebral palsy is evaluated in your outpatient clinic. He uses a wheelchair, has significant communication impairments, and requires a classroom aide. His parents report that the school is developing an Individualized Education Program (IEP) to support his academic and functional needs. Which federal law specifically mandates the development of IEPs and guarantees access to free and appropriate public education in the least restrictive environment?
column7: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act is correct because IDEA specifically mandates the creation of IEPs and ensures that children with disabilities receive a free and appropriate public education in the least restrictive environment.
column8: Americans with Disabilities Act is incorrect because ADA broadly prohibits discrimination based on disability but does not mandate IEPs.
column9: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act is incorrect because while it ensures access and accommodations in schools, it does not require formal IEPs.
column10: No Child Left Behind Act is incorrect because it was focused on school accountability and standardized testing, not disability-specific educational rights.

column2: A 12-year-old student with a history of traumatic brain injury has difficulties with attention and executive functioning. Although he performs at grade level, he requires extended test time and quiet testing environments. He does not qualify for special education services but still needs classroom accommodations. Under which federal law is he entitled to a written accommodation plan?
column7: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act is correct because it ensures access and accommodations for students with disabilities who do not require special education but need support in the general classroom.
column8: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act is incorrect because IDEA only applies to students who qualify for special education services and mandates IEPs, not 504 plans.
column9: Americans with Disabilities Act is incorrect because while it prohibits disability discrimination, it does not specifically mandate school accommodation plans.
column10: Every Student Succeeds Act is incorrect because it governs school accountability and performance but does not directly establish rights for students with disabilities.

column2: A 25-year-old man with T10 paraplegia is applying for a customer service job. He meets all qualifications but is told by the employer that the office is not wheelchair accessible and no changes can be made. Which law protects this individual from such discrimination in the workplace?
column7: Americans with Disabilities Act is correct because the ADA prohibits discrimination in employment, public accommodations, and transportation, and requires reasonable modifications unless they cause undue hardship.
column8: Section 504 of the Rehabilitation Act is incorrect because it applies only to entities receiving federal funding, not all private employers.
column9: Individuals with Disabilities Education Act is incorrect because IDEA applies only to school-age children and does not cover employment.
column10: Social Security Act is incorrect because it governs financial benefits, not anti-discrimination protections in employment.

column2: You are consulted for a 14-year-old with a history of perinatal stroke resulting in mild right hemiparesis. He has normal cognitive function and is in general education classes but requires extra time for writing assignments and a keyboard for note-taking. He does not need specialized instruction. Which of the following best describes the most appropriate plan to support him at school?
column3: 504 Plan: Provides classroom accommodations for students with disabilities who do not require special education
column4: IEP: Provides accommodations only for physical impairments without cognitive involvement
column5: 504 Plan: Required only for students in private schools receiving federal funding
column6: IEP: Mandates school-wide disability awareness training for all students
column7: 504 Plan: Provides classroom accommodations for students with disabilities who do not require special education is correct because a 504 Plan supports students with disabilities impacting a major life activity (such as writing) who do not need special education services.
column8: IEP: Provides accommodations only for physical impairments without cognitive involvement is incorrect because IEPs are for students needing specialized instruction regardless of physical or cognitive domains.
column9: 504 Plan: Required only for students in private schools receiving federal funding is incorrect because 504 Plans apply to public schools and any institution receiving federal funds, not exclusively private schools.
column10: IEP: Mandates school-wide disability awareness training for all students is incorrect because IEPs are individualized education plans and do not impose school-wide training requirements.

column2: A 10-year-old boy with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy is evaluated in clinic. He has mild cognitive impairment, receives physical therapy and occupational therapy during the school day, and struggles academically despite classroom accommodations. He is in a public school and his team is considering how to best support his learning. Which of the following best meets this student’s educational needs?
column3: Individualized Education Program: Provides individualized special education services and related therapies for eligible students
column4: Section 504 Plan: Offers specialized instruction and therapy services to improve educational performance
column5: Americans with Disabilities Act: Requires development of a legal education plan for students with physical disabilities
column6: Title I Services: Provides funding for students with disabilities to receive therapy in school
column7: Individualized Education Program: Provides individualized special education services and related therapies for eligible students is correct because it is the legal mechanism under the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act that delivers both individualized instruction and related services like physical and occupational therapy in public schools.
