column8: Section 504 Plan: Offers specialized instruction and therapy services to improve educational performance is incorrect because Section 504 Plans provide accommodations but do not mandate specialized instruction or therapies.
column9: Americans with Disabilities Act: Requires development of a legal education plan for students with physical disabilities is incorrect because the Americans with Disabilities Act prohibits discrimination but does not create or require individualized education plans.
column10: Title I Services: Provides funding for students with disabilities to receive therapy in school is incorrect because Title I funds support academic programs for disadvantaged students, not specifically for students with disabilities or therapy services.

column2: A 12-month-old child presents for evaluation of developmental delay. On exam, the child demonstrates a strong asymmetric tonic neck reflex (ATNR) and persistent palmar grasp reflex. There is no history of recent neurological insult. Which of the following best reflects the expected age range by which these primitive reflexes should normally be suppressed?
column7: 4 to 6 months is correct because primitive reflexes such as ATNR and the palmar grasp are expected to integrate by 4 to 6 months of age in typical development; persistence beyond this age may indicate neurologic dysfunction.
column8: At birth is incorrect because these reflexes are typically present at birth and are not expected to be suppressed until several months later.
column9: 18 to 24 months is incorrect because persistence of primitive reflexes this late suggests an abnormal neurologic condition such as cerebral palsy.
column10: 12 to 15 months is incorrect because most primitive reflexes, including ATNR and palmar grasp, are expected to resolve well before this age.
column12: The asymmetric tonic neck reflex (ATNR) is elicited when an infant’s head is turned to one side, causing extension of the limbs on the face side and flexion on the opposite side; it typically integrates by 4 to 6 months of age. The palmar grasp reflex involves involuntary finger flexion in response to pressure on the palm and normally disappears by 4 to 6 months.

column2: Which of the following best reflects the expected age range for suppression of the plantar grasp reflex?
column7: 9 to 12 months is correct because the plantar grasp reflex, which involves involuntary toe flexion in response to pressure on the sole of the foot, is present at birth and normally integrates by 9 to 12 months of age; persistence beyond this range may interfere with standing balance and early walking and suggests abnormal neurologic development.
column8: 4 to 6 months is incorrect because this is the typical suppression window for reflexes like the palmar grasp reflex (involuntary finger flexion when the palm is stimulated) and the asymmetric tonic neck reflex (extension of limbs on the face side when the head is turned), not the plantar grasp reflex.
column9: At birth is incorrect because the plantar grasp reflex is normally present at birth as a primitive reflex and is not expected to be suppressed until later in infancy.
column10: 18 to 24 months is incorrect because the plantar grasp reflex should already be integrated by 12 months; persistence into the second year of life is abnormal and may be seen in upper motor neuron disorders such as spastic cerebral palsy.

column2: A 17-week pregnant woman with no prior prenatal screening undergoes amniocentesis after a routine ultrasound reveals a possible neural tube defect. Which of the following amniotic fluid findings would most specifically support the diagnosis of an open neural tube defect?
column6: Presence of fetal hemoglobin in amniotic fluid
column7: Elevated amniotic fluid acetylcholinesterase (AChE) is correct because AChE is not normally present in amniotic fluid unless there is a neural tube defect causing an open connection between the neural tissue and amniotic fluid, making it highly specific for open neural tube defects.
column8: Elevated amniotic fluid alpha-fetoprotein (AFP) is incorrect because although AFP is a sensitive marker for open neural tube defects, it lacks specificity and can be elevated in other conditions such as ventral wall defects or multiple gestation.
column9: Decreased amniotic fluid estriol is incorrect because this is more associated with chromosomal abnormalities or placental insufficiency, not with neural tube defects.
column10: Presence of fetal hemoglobin in amniotic fluid is incorrect because it may occur with fetal bleeding or trauma during the procedure and does not specifically indicate a neural tube defect.

column2: Which of the following best describes the underlying pathogenesis of open neural tube defects?
column3: Failure of the neural folds to fuse during the third to fourth week of embryogenesis
column4: Arrested differentiation of neural crest cells during the second trimester
column5: Incomplete ossification of the vertebral arches during fetal development
column6: Vascular disruption of the spinal cord during the late second trimester
column7: Failure of the neural folds to fuse during the third to fourth week of embryogenesis is correct because open neural tube defects, such as myelomeningocele, result from failed closure of the neural tube, typically occurring between days 21 and 28 of gestation.
column8: Arrested differentiation of neural crest cells during the second trimester is incorrect because neural crest cell abnormalities contribute to other conditions (e.g., Hirschsprung disease, neurocristopathies) rather than NTDs.
column9: Incomplete ossification of the vertebral arches during fetal development is incorrect because this may contribute to closed spinal dysraphism, not open neural tube defects, and it is not the primary mechanism.
column10: Vascular disruption of the spinal cord during the late second trimester is incorrect because such events are more likely to cause acquired spinal cord injury, not congenital neural tube defects.

column2: A newborn has a small dimple with a tuft of hair over the lower lumbar spine, but no visible protrusion. Neurologic exam is normal, and ultrasound reveals a closed posterior vertebral arch. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Spina bifida occulta is correct because the lesion is closed, asymptomatic, and there is no protruding sac, only a superficial skin marker like a dimple or tuft of hair.
column8: Meningocele is incorrect because it involves a visible cystic sac containing meninges, which is not present here.
column9: Myelomeningocele is incorrect because it causes neurologic deficits and has exposed neural elements, which this case lacks.
column10: Myelocele is incorrect because it presents with an open placode at the skin surface and significant neurologic impairment.

column2: A term infant is born with a cystic midline sac over the lower lumbar spine. The sac is transilluminant and covered with skin. The infant has normal strength, reflexes, and bladder function. Which is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Meningocele is correct because the cystic sac contains only meninges and cerebrospinal fluid, is covered with skin, and neurologic function is preserved.
column8: Myelomeningocele is incorrect because it typically involves exposed neural tissue and neurologic deficits.
column9: Spina bifida occulta is incorrect because it lacks any external sac and is often detected only by imaging.
column10: Myelocele is incorrect because it is not covered by skin and presents with more severe deficits.

column2: A neonate presents with a lumbosacral mass that is not covered by skin. The sac contains neural elements and cerebrospinal fluid. Examination reveals flaccid lower extremities and no anal wink. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Myelomeningocele is correct because the sac contains both meninges and neural tissue, lacks skin covering, and presents with motor and sensory deficits.
column8: Meningocele is incorrect because it spares neurologic function and does not include neural tissue in the sac.
column9: Myelocele is incorrect because the spinal cord is completely flush with the skin surface, not elevated within a sac.
column10: Spina bifida occulta is incorrect because it is non-cystic and typically asymptomatic.

column2: A neonate is found to have a large, cystic lesion at the lumbosacral region. The cystic cavity lies anterior to the exposed spinal cord, which remains flush with the skin surface and is not covered by meninges or skin. Neurologic exam reveals flaccid legs and absent deep tendon reflexes. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Myelocele is correct because this defect consists of a flat neural placode exposed at the surface, with the cystic cavity situated anterior to the spinal cord and not enclosing it, resulting in severe neurologic dysfunction.
column8: Myelomeningocele is incorrect because the cystic sac typically encloses both meninges and spinal cord tissue, and the neural elements protrude within the sac.
column9: Meningocele is incorrect because the sac contains only meninges and CSF and is covered by skin.
column10: Spina bifida occulta is incorrect because it presents as a closed defect without a visible mass or neurologic impairment.

column2: A neonate is born with a large, uncovered lumbosacral myelomeningocele. The infant has flaccid legs, an absent anal wink, and urinary incontinence. Cranial ultrasound reveals a small posterior fossa with downward displacement of the cerebellar vermis and medulla through the foramen magnum. Which of the following is the most likely associated central nervous system malformation?
column7: Arnold-Chiari malformation type II is correct because it is classically associated with myelomeningocele and is defined by herniation of the cerebellar vermis and brainstem through the foramen magnum with a small posterior fossa, often detected on prenatal or neonatal imaging.
column8: Arnold-Chiari malformation type I is incorrect because it typically presents later in life with headaches or syringomyelia and involves only tonsillar herniation, not vermis or brainstem herniation.
column9: Dandy-Walker malformation is incorrect because it involves an enlarged posterior fossa, cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle, and cerebellar vermis hypoplasia, not herniation.
column10: Holoprosencephaly is incorrect because it results from failure of forebrain division and is not associated with myelomeningocele or posterior fossa abnormalities.

column2: A 6-year-old girl presents with progressive lower limb weakness, frequent falls, and new-onset urinary incontinence. Physical exam reveals spasticity, asymmetric leg lengths, and a midline lumbosacral skin dimple. MRI of the spine shows a longitudinal split of the spinal cord with a bony septum separating the two hemicords. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Diastematomyelia is correct because this patient has progressive motor and bladder symptoms, a midline cutaneous marker, and MRI showing a longitudinal split of the spinal cord with a bony or fibrous septum, all classic features of this condition.
column8: Tethered cord syndrome is incorrect because although it may present similarly, it does not involve a split cord or bony septum on imaging.
column9: Myelomeningocele is incorrect because it presents at birth with an open sac and exposed neural tissue, not a delayed progressive presentation with a split cord.
column10: Lipomyelomeningocele is incorrect because it involves a fatty mass and tethering, but does not produce two hemicords or a bony septum.

column2: Two children with spina bifida and shunted hydrocephalus undergo neurocognitive testing. One has a thoracic-level myelomeningocele; the other has a sacral-level lesion. Which of the following best describes the expected difference in cognitive outcomes?
column3: The child with the thoracic-level lesion will have lower overall intellectual function, especially in nonverbal domains
column4: The child with the thoracic-level lesion will have better visuospatial reasoning than the child with the sacral lesion
column5: Both children are expected to have similar cognitive profiles due to hydrocephalus
column6: The child with the thoracic-level lesion will have normal intelligence with isolated language delay
column7: The child with the thoracic-level lesion will have lower overall intellectual function, especially in nonverbal domains is correct because higher-level lesions are strongly associated with greater cognitive impairment, particularly in visual-spatial and executive functioning, even when hydrocephalus is present in both children.
column8: The child with the thoracic-level lesion will have better visuospatial reasoning than the child with the sacral lesion is incorrect because visuospatial reasoning is typically worse in those with higher-level lesions.
column9: Both children are expected to have similar cognitive profiles due to hydrocephalus is incorrect because spinal lesion level is an independent predictor of cognitive outcome.
column10: The child with the thoracic-level lesion will have normal intelligence with isolated language delay is incorrect because verbal skills are relatively preserved, while deficits more commonly affect nonverbal reasoning and problem-solving.

column2: A 12-year-old child with a history of myelomeningocele and multiple prior surgeries presents with hives and wheezing during a routine urologic catheterization. Which of the following best explains the likely cause?
column7: Sensitization to latex is correct because patients with spina bifida are frequently exposed to latex-containing products—such as gloves, catheters, and surgical supplies—from birth, especially during early and repeated surgeries and clean intermittent catheterization, leading to increased risk of IgE-mediated hypersensitivity reactions including hives, wheezing, and anaphylaxis.
column8: Allergic reaction to polyethylene is incorrect because polyethylene is biologically inert and rarely causes allergic responses.
column9: IgE-mediated reaction to iodine is incorrect because iodine is an uncommon allergen and is less likely to provoke an acute systemic response during catheterization.
column10: Cross-reactivity to antibiotic coating is incorrect because this is a rare cause of allergic reaction and not commonly associated with spina bifida.

column2: A 5-year-old boy with Duchenne muscular dystrophy demonstrates Gower sign when rising from the floor, using his hands to push off his thighs to stand upright. Weakness of which muscle groups most directly accounts for this compensatory movement?
column7: Gluteus maximus and quadriceps is correct because Gower sign reflects proximal muscle weakness—especially of the hip and knee extensors—which impairs the ability to rise from the floor without using the arms; this sign is classically seen in Duchenne muscular dystrophy but can also occur in other proximal myopathies such as Becker muscular dystrophy, limb-girdle muscular dystrophy, and inflammatory myopathies.
column8: Hamstrings and tibialis anterior is incorrect because these are not primary extensors and do not account for difficulty in trunk elevation during rising.
column9: Gastrocnemius and soleus is incorrect because these distal muscles are involved in ankle plantarflexion during gait, not floor-to-stand transitions.
column10: Hip adductors and internal rotators is incorrect because these muscles assist with stabilization, not the extension necessary for standing from the floor.

column2: Which of the following best explains the biomechanical cause of myopathic gait in a patient with Duchenne muscular dystrophy?
column3: Weakness of proximal hip and thigh extensors causes posterior trunk shift and decreased knee flexion to keep the center of gravity behind the hip
column4: Weakness of abdominal and hip flexor muscles causes anterior pelvic tilt and forward trunk lean during stance
column5: Imbalance between hip abductors and adductors leads to scissoring and narrow-based gait
column6: Weakness of ankle plantarflexors causes inability to push off during terminal stance, leading to shortened stride length
column7: Weakness of proximal hip and thigh extensors causes posterior trunk shift and decreased knee flexion to keep the center of gravity behind the hip is correct because in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, gluteus maximus and quadriceps weakness prevents upright stabilization of the trunk. Patients compensate by leaning their trunk backward, minimizing knee flexion, and increasing anterior pelvic tilt and lumbar lordosis to keep their center of gravity posterior to the hip joint. This reduces the flexion moment at the knee and hip, allowing them to remain upright during stance and preventing collapse.
column8: Weakness of abdominal and hip flexor muscles causes anterior pelvic tilt and forward trunk lean during stance is incorrect because these muscles do not produce the posterior trunk lean or stiff-legged compensation seen in myopathic gait.
column9: Imbalance between hip abductors and adductors leads to scissoring and narrow-based gait is incorrect because this pattern is more consistent with spastic diplegia, not muscular dystrophy.
column10: Weakness of ankle plantarflexors causes inability to push off during terminal stance, leading to shortened stride length is incorrect because this distal impairment affects propulsion, not the compensatory mechanics used to stabilize during initial contact and mid-stance.

column2: In a patient with Duchenne muscular dystrophy, gait analysis reveals a myopathic gait. Which of the following best explains a compensatory strategy commonly seen in patients with a myopathic gait?
column3: Plantarflexion at initial contact stabilizes the knee by limiting forward tibial translation, compensating for quadriceps weakness
column4: Forward trunk lean during stance compensates for quadriceps weakness by moving the ground reaction force anterior to the knee
column5: Contralateral vaulting assists with foot clearance in the setting of weak hip or knee flexors
column6: Pelvic hiking assists swing phase clearance in the presence of ankle dorsiflexor weakness
column7: Plantarflexion at initial contact stabilizes the knee by limiting forward tibial translation, compensating for quadriceps weakness is correct because in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, quadriceps weakness leads to stance phase instability. Plantarflexion helps shift the ground reaction force anterior to the knee, decreasing the flexion moment and preventing collapse.
column8: Forward trunk lean during stance compensates for quadriceps weakness by moving the ground reaction force anterior to the knee is incorrect because while this strategy is used in isolated quadriceps weakness (e.g., post-polio), it requires intact hip extensors, which are also weak in Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Therefore, patients with myopathic gait use posterior trunk lean and plantarflexion instead.
column9: Contralateral vaulting assists with foot clearance in the setting of weak hip or knee flexors is incorrect because vaulting compensates for swing-phase clearance problems, not stance-phase instability caused by proximal extensor weakness.
column10: Pelvic hiking assists swing phase clearance in the presence of ankle dorsiflexor weakness is incorrect because this is a compensation for foot drop, not for the proximal weakness seen in myopathic gait.

column2: A 5-year-old boy presents with difficulty rising from the floor and frequent falls. Examination reveals pseudohypertrophy of the calves and a waddling gait. Genetic testing confirms Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Which of the following best describes the underlying pathophysiologic mechanism?
column3: Absence of dystrophin leading to sarcolemmal instability
column4: Deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase leading to glycogen accumulation
column5: Autoimmune destruction of acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction
column6: Mutation in the survival motor neuron 1 (SMN1) gene leading to anterior horn cell degeneration
column7: Absence of dystrophin leading to sarcolemmal instability is correct because Duchenne muscular dystrophy results from mutations in the DMD gene, leading to complete absence of dystrophin. This weakens the dystrophin-glycoprotein complex, destabilizing the sarcolemma and causing progressive muscle fiber necrosis.
column8: Deficiency of acid alpha-glucosidase leading to glycogen accumulation is incorrect because this is the mechanism of Pompe disease, a glycogen storage disorder.
column9: Autoimmune destruction of acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction is incorrect because this is the mechanism of myasthenia gravis.
column10: Mutation

column2: A 4-year-old boy is brought in for delayed motor milestones and frequent falls. Examination reveals Gowers sign and calf pseudohypertrophy. His maternal uncle was wheelchair-bound in adolescence. What is the most likely pattern of inheritance for this patient’s condition?
column7: X-linked recessive is correct because Duchenne muscular dystrophy is caused by mutations in the DMD gene on the X chromosome, primarily affecting males, with maternal inheritance often revealing a family history in maternal male relatives.
column8: Autosomal recessive is incorrect because this pattern is more consistent with limb-girdle muscular dystrophy subtypes, not Duchenne.
column9: Autosomal dominant is incorrect because dominant inheritance is typical of myotonic dystrophy or facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
column10: Mitochondrial is incorrect because mitochondrial disorders are inherited maternally and typically affect high-energy systems like the CNS or eyes.

column2: A 7-year-old boy presents with progressive difficulty rising from the floor, frequent falls, and calf pseudohypertrophy. Examination reveals a positive Gowers sign and waddling gait. Genetic testing confirms Duchenne muscular dystrophy. The gene responsible for this disorder is located at which of the following chromosomal loci?
column7: Xp21 is correct because Duchenne muscular dystrophy is caused by mutations in the DMD gene, which is located on the short arm of the X chromosome at position p21.
column8: 17p11.2 is incorrect because this locus is associated with Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease type 1A due to PMP22 duplication.
column9: 5q13 is incorrect because this is the location of the SMN1 gene responsible for spinal muscular atrophy.
column10: 4q35 is incorrect because this chromosomal region is linked to facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.

column2: A 6-year-old boy presents with delayed motor milestones and frequent falls. Physical exam reveals a waddling gait, Gowers sign, and calf pseudohypertrophy. Genetic testing is pending, but a muscle biopsy was performed and reviewed. Which of the following histologic findings is most consistent with Duchenne muscular dystrophy?
column3: Muscle fiber size variation with degeneration, regeneration, fibrosis, and fatty infiltration
column4: Endoneurial onion bulb formations with Schwann cell proliferation
column6: Ragged red fibers on modified Gomori trichrome stain
column7: Muscle fiber size variation with degeneration, regeneration, fibrosis, and fatty infiltration is correct because Duchenne muscular dystrophy typically shows ongoing myofiber necrosis and regeneration with increased connective and fatty tissue replacement.
column8: Endoneurial onion bulb formations with Schwann cell proliferation is incorrect because this finding is characteristic of hereditary motor and sensory neuropathies like Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease.
column9: Rimmed vacuoles and intranuclear inclusions is incorrect because these are features of inclusion body myositis.
column10: Ragged red fibers on modified Gomori trichrome stain is incorrect because this histologic appearance is associated with mitochondrial myopathies.

column2: A 3-year-old boy is brought in for evaluation due to difficulty keeping up with peers and a tendency to fall. Examination reveals a waddling gait and a positive Gowers sign. Calf pseudohypertrophy is noted. Based on the typical pattern of muscle involvement in Duchenne muscular dystrophy, which of the following muscle groups is most likely to show weakness first?
column7: Neck flexors is correct because neck flexor weakness is typically the earliest clinical sign of Duchenne muscular dystrophy, even before involvement of the pelvic girdle muscles.
column8: Hip extensors is incorrect because although proximal lower extremity weakness is a hallmark of early gait disturbance, it generally follows neck flexor weakness.
column9: Deltoids is incorrect because upper extremity involvement appears later in the disease course.
column10: Distal lower leg muscles is incorrect because these are usually preserved until late in disease progression.

column2: Based on the natural progression of Duchenne muscular dystrophy, ambulation is typically lost closest to which of the following ages?
column7: 12 years old is correct because most children with Duchenne muscular dystrophy lose independent ambulation between ages 10 and 13, with 12 being the average age without disease-modifying treatment.
column8: 6 years old is incorrect because most children are still ambulatory at this age despite early signs of weakness.
column9: 16 years old is incorrect because ambulation is typically lost several years earlier unless corticosteroids are used to prolong walking.
column10: 20 years old is incorrect because by this age, most patients are wheelchair-bound and showing signs of respiratory and cardiac decline.

column2: A 14-year-old boy with Duchenne muscular dystrophy presents with new symptoms of fatigue, decreased exercise tolerance, early satiety, and abdominal bloating. He is non-ambulatory and has been on corticosteroids since age 6. On examination, he has hepatomegaly. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column7: Order an echocardiogram is correct because fatigue, GI symptoms, and hepatomegaly in a teenage patient with Duchenne muscular dystrophy raise suspicion for early cardiomyopathy, which should be assessed with cardiac imaging.
column8: Begin diuretic therapy is incorrect because a structural cardiac evaluation is necessary before initiating treatment.
column9: Refer to gastroenterology is incorrect because the constellation of symptoms is more consistent with cardiac dysfunction than primary GI disease.
column10: Increase corticosteroid dosage is incorrect because this does not address the potential cardiac etiology and may exacerbate fluid retention.

column2: Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology of Becker’s muscular dystrophy?
column3: In-frame DMD mutation causing partially functional dystrophin
column4: Frameshift DMD mutation causing absent dystrophin
column5: Acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency causing glycogen buildup
column6: SMN1 mutation causing motor neuron degeneration
column7: In-frame DMD mutation causing partially functional dystrophin is correct because Becker muscular dystrophy results from in-frame mutations that preserve the reading frame, producing an abnormal but partially functional dystrophin protein.
column8: Frameshift DMD mutation causing absent dystrophin is incorrect because this leads to Duchenne muscular dystrophy, where dystrophin is typically absent.
column9: Acid alpha-glucosidase deficiency causing glycogen buildup is incorrect because this describes the mechanism of Pompe disease, not a dystrophinopathy.
column10: SMN1 mutation causing motor neuron degeneration is incorrect because this underlies spinal muscular atrophy, not a muscular dystrophy.

column2: A 19-year-old man presents with difficulty lifting objects overhead and trouble smiling. Examination reveals bilateral scapular winging, asymmetric facial weakness, and sparing of the deltoid muscles. He reports that his father had similar symptoms beginning in his 20s. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is correct because the patient’s asymmetric facial weakness, scapular winging with preserved deltoids, and autosomal dominant family history are classic for FSHD.
column8: Becker muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it typically presents with symmetric proximal lower extremity weakness and preserved facial muscles.
column9: Limb-girdle muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it involves symmetric weakness of the hip and shoulder girdles without facial involvement.
column10: Myotonic dystrophy is incorrect because it usually includes distal weakness, ptosis, and systemic features like cataracts or cardiac conduction defects.

column2: A 20-year-old woman reports gradually worsening difficulty getting up from the floor, lifting grocery bags, and brushing her hair. She denies facial droop, trouble smiling, or frequent hand cramping. Her CK level is 8,000 U/L. A cousin was diagnosed with a similar condition in adolescence. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Limb-girdle muscular dystrophy is correct because difficulty with rising from the floor and overhead tasks, preserved facial strength, elevated CK, and positive family history are classic features of LGMD.
column8: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it often involves facial weakness such as difficulty smiling or closing the eyes.
column9: Myotonic dystrophy is incorrect because it typically includes distal weakness, myotonia, and systemic findings like cataracts or cardiac issues.
column10: Pompe disease is incorrect because it more commonly presents in infancy or childhood with cardiomyopathy or respiratory failure, not isolated proximal weakness in young adulthood.

column2: What is the inheritance pattern of facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy?
column7: Autosomal dominant is correct because facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is classically inherited in an autosomal dominant fashion, with variable penetrance and onset.
column8: Autosomal recessive is incorrect because this inheritance pattern is more typical of many forms of limb-girdle muscular dystrophy, not FSHD.
column9: X-linked recessive is incorrect because this pattern is characteristic of Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophies.
column10: Mitochondrial is incorrect because mitochondrial inheritance is maternal and typically associated with multi-system involvement and variable muscle patterns.

column2: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is most commonly associated with a contraction of the D4Z4 repeat array on which chromosome?
column7: Chromosome 4q35 is correct because facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is most commonly caused by a contraction of D4Z4 repeat units at the 4q35 locus, leading to aberrant expression of the DUX4 gene.
column8: Chromosome Xp21 is incorrect because this locus is associated with Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophies.
column9: Chromosome 5q31 is incorrect because this region is associated with spinal muscular atrophy type IV and certain immune-related genes, not FSHD.
column10: Chromosome 1p36 is incorrect because this region is associated with limb-girdle muscular dystrophy type 1 and certain developmental disorders.

column2: A 10-year-old boy presents with difficulty running, mild upper arm weakness, and recent falls. On exam, he has elbow contractures and rigid spine. His uncle had early heart block requiring pacemaker placement in his twenties. Which of the following diagnoses is most consistent with this clinical presentation?
column7: Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy is correct because the combination of early joint contractures, humeroperoneal weakness, cardiac conduction abnormalities, and spine rigidity is classic for EDMD.
column8: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it presents earlier with more severe proximal weakness, calf hypertrophy, and lacks early joint contractures or conduction defects.
column9: Becker muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it typically presents later with milder proximal weakness and without prominent contractures or cardiac conduction abnormalities.
column10: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it involves facial and scapular weakness with deltoid sparing and does not typically include early joint contractures or cardiomyopathy.

column2: Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology of Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy?
column3: Mutation in genes encoding nuclear envelope proteins such as emerin or lamin A/C
column4: Frameshift mutation in the DMD gene causing absence of dystrophin
column5: Expansion of CTG repeats in the DMPK gene
column6: Deletion of D4Z4 repeats on chromosome 4q35
column7: Mutation in genes encoding nuclear envelope proteins such as emerin or lamin A/C is correct because EDMD results from mutations in EMD or LMNA, leading to structural defects in the nuclear envelope that affect muscle and cardiac tissue.
column8: Frameshift mutation in the DMD gene causing absence of dystrophin is incorrect because this describes Duchenne muscular dystrophy.
column9: Expansion of CTG repeats in the DMPK gene is incorrect because this is the underlying mechanism of myotonic dystrophy type 1.
column10: Deletion of D4Z4 repeats on chromosome 4q35 is incorrect because this is associated with facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.

column2: What is the most common inheritance pattern of Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy?
column7: X-linked recessive is correct because the most common form of EDMD is caused by mutations in the EMD gene encoding emerin, which is inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern.
column8: Autosomal dominant is incorrect because this pattern is more commonly seen in LMNA-related EDMD but is not the most frequent form overall.
column9: Autosomal recessive is incorrect because this inheritance pattern is rare in EDMD and typically associated with mutations in other nuclear envelope proteins.
column10: Mitochondrial is incorrect because EDMD is not inherited through mitochondrial DNA, which is maternally transmitted.

column2: Which of the following gene loci is most commonly associated with Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy?
column7: Xq28 is correct because the most common form of EDMD involves mutations in the EMD gene, which encodes emerin and is located on chromosome Xq28.
column8: 4q35 is incorrect because this is the locus for the D4Z4 repeat contraction in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
column9: 19q13.3 is incorrect because this locus is associated with the DMPK gene involved in myotonic dystrophy type 1.
column10: Xp21 is incorrect because this region is where the DMD gene is located, responsible for Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophies.

column2: What is the most common inheritance pattern of limb-girdle muscular dystrophy?
column7: Autosomal recessive is correct because the majority of LGMD subtypes are inherited in an autosomal recessive fashion, especially those presenting in childhood or adolescence.
column8: Autosomal dominant is incorrect because while some LGMD subtypes are dominantly inherited, they are less common than recessive forms.
column9: X-linked recessive is incorrect because this pattern is characteristic of Duchenne and Becker muscular dystrophies, not LGMD.
column10: Mitochondrial is incorrect because mitochondrial inheritance is not seen in LGMD and is more typical of disorders involving multisystem mitochondrial dysfunction.

column2: A mutation in the CAPN3 gene, which causes a common subtype of limb-girdle muscular dystrophy, is located on which chromosome?
column7: 15q15 is correct because the CAPN3 gene is located on chromosome 15q15 and is associated with autosomal recessive LGMD R1.
column8: 4q35 is incorrect because this locus is associated with the D4Z4 contraction in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
column9: Xq28 is incorrect because this region contains the EMD gene linked to Emery-Dreifuss muscular dystrophy.
column10: 19q13.3 is incorrect because this locus is associated with the DMPK gene in myotonic dystrophy type 1.

column2: Which gene is most commonly implicated in central core myopathy?
column7: RYR1 is correct because central core disease is most commonly caused by autosomal dominant mutations in the RYR1 gene located on chromosome 19q13.1, which encodes the ryanodine receptor; it is associated with a high risk of malignant hyperthermia with exposure to volatile anesthetics and shows selective involvement of type I muscle fibers.
column8: DMPK is incorrect because it is associated with myotonic dystrophy type 1, not central core disease.
column9: CAPN3 is incorrect because it is associated with autosomal recessive limb-girdle muscular dystrophy type 2A.
column10: MTM1 is incorrect because it is associated with X-linked centronuclear myopathy (myotubular myopathy), not central core disease.

column2: A muscle biopsy in a hypotonic infant shows thin, rod-shaped inclusions aligned with the Z-line. Which diagnosis is most likely?
column7: Nemaline myopathy is correct because this condition, also known as rod body myopathy, is characterized by nemaline (rod-like) inclusions aligned with the Z-line on muscle biopsy; it is most commonly inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern, and mutations are often found in the NEB gene located on chromosome 2q23.
column8: Becker muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it results from dystrophin mutations and does not produce rod-like inclusions.
column9: Multiminicore disease is incorrect because it is defined by multiple areas of sarcomeric disorganization rather than rod bodies.
column10: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it shows fiber degeneration and regeneration with absent dystrophin on staining, not rod-shaped inclusions.

column2: A muscle biopsy in a newborn with hypotonia and ophthalmoplegia shows muscle fibers with centrally located nuclei. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Centronuclear myopathy is correct because it is the most common type of myotubular myopathy, typically inherited in an X-linked recessive pattern due to mutations in the MTM1 gene on Xq28; it presents in neonates with hypotonia, ophthalmoplegia, respiratory failure, and biopsy showing centrally located nuclei in muscle fibers.
column8: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it presents later in life and does not show central nuclei on biopsy.
column9: Central core disease is incorrect because it shows core-like areas devoid of oxidative enzymes, not centralized nuclei.
column10: Nemaline myopathy is incorrect because it features rod-like inclusions rather than centrally placed nuclei.

column2: A child with scoliosis and restrictive lung disease undergoes muscle biopsy, which reveals multiple small zones of sarcomere disorganization. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Multiminicore disease is correct because it is an autosomal recessive congenital myopathy characterized by multiple small cores of sarcomeric disorganization seen on biopsy; it is often associated with scoliosis, joint contractures, and restrictive pulmonary disease.
column8: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it results from absence of dystrophin and shows necrosis and regeneration with fibrofatty replacement, not minicore lesions.
column9: Central core disease is incorrect because it typically shows a single large central core per fiber rather than multiple small cores.
column10: Spinal muscular atrophy is incorrect because it is a motor neuron disease with neurogenic atrophy, not a structural myopathy.

column2: A 4-year-old undergoing elective surgery under general anesthesia develops rapid-onset hypercapnia, muscle rigidity, and hyperthermia. Malignant hyperthermia is suspected, and dantrolene is administered. Review of the medical record reveals a diagnosis of a congenital myopathy. Which congenital myopathy is most likely?
column7: Central core disease is correct because it is an autosomal dominant congenital myopathy caused by mutations in the RYR1 gene, which encodes the skeletal muscle ryanodine receptor (a calcium release channel); abnormal calcium handling predisposes to malignant hyperthermia with anesthetic exposure. Biopsy reveals central cores—areas lacking mitochondria and oxidative enzyme activity—primarily in type I fibers.
column8: Nemaline myopathy is incorrect because it is characterized by rod-shaped inclusions and has no established link to malignant hyperthermia.
column9: Centronuclear myopathy is incorrect because it presents with centrally located nuclei and severe neonatal hypotonia but is not associated with malignant hyperthermia.
column10: Multiminicore disease is incorrect because its link to malignant hyperthermia is less well established, and it primarily presents with scoliosis and respiratory involvement.

column2: A newborn presents with severe hypotonia, external ophthalmoplegia, and respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. Genetic testing reveals a mutation in the MTM1 gene. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Centronuclear myopathy is correct because this X-linked congenital myopathy, caused by mutations in the MTM1 gene on chromosome Xq28, presents in the neonatal period with profound hypotonia, ophthalmoplegia, and respiratory failure; it is the most common form of myotubular myopathy.
column8: Nemaline myopathy is incorrect because although it can present in infancy with weakness, it typically does not include ophthalmoplegia or MTM1 mutation.
column9: Duchenne muscular dystrophy is incorrect because it presents after infancy with progressive proximal weakness and is caused by mutations in the dystrophin gene.
column10: Spinal muscular atrophy is incorrect because it is a motor neuron disease with tongue fasciculations and absent reflexes, not a primary muscle disorder.

column2: A 6-month-old infant with spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) type 1 is referred for early intervention therapy. The patient has received a one-time infusion of onasemnogene abeparvovec-xioi (Zolgensma). What best describes the mechanism of action of this treatment?
column3: Gene replacement therapy delivering a functional SMN1 gene via AAV9 vector
column4: Splicing correction of SMN2 pre-mRNA to increase functional protein
column5: Oral SMN2 mRNA stabilizer that enhances exon 7 skipping
column6: Protein replacement therapy with recombinant SMN protein
column7: Gene replacement therapy delivering a functional SMN1 gene via AAV9 vector is correct because onasemnogene abeparvovec-xioi introduces a functional SMN1 gene directly to motor neurons to compensate for the mutated gene in SMA.
column8: Splicing correction of SMN2 pre-mRNA to increase functional protein is incorrect because this is the mechanism of nusinersen (Spinraza), not onasemnogene abeparvovec-xioi.
column9: Oral SMN2 mRNA stabilizer that enhances exon 7 skipping is incorrect because risdiplam (Evrysdi) promotes exon 7 inclusion, not skipping.
column10: Protein replacement therapy with recombinant SMN protein is incorrect because no current SMA therapies use recombinant SMN protein administration.

column2: A 2-year-old child with spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) type 2 is being treated with intrathecal nusinersen (Spinraza). What is the primary mechanism of action of this drug?
column3: Antisense oligonucleotide that modifies SMN2 pre-mRNA splicing to include exon 7
column4: Gene therapy that inserts a wild-type SMN1 gene via viral vector
column5: Oral small molecule that stabilizes SMN protein post-translation
column6: RNA interference to silence mutant SMN1 transcription
column7: Antisense oligonucleotide that modifies SMN2 pre-mRNA splicing to include exon 7 is correct because nusinersen promotes production of full-length, functional SMN protein from the SMN2 gene.
column8: Gene therapy that inserts a wild-type SMN1 gene via viral vector is incorrect because this describes onasemnogene abeparvovec-xioi (Zolgensma).
column9: Oral small molecule that stabilizes SMN protein post-translation is incorrect because nusinersen does not act on protein stability or post-translational processes.
column10: RNA interference to silence mutant SMN1 transcription is incorrect because this approach would worsen SMA rather than treat it.

column2: A 10-year-old patient with spinal muscular atrophy (SMA) type 3 begins oral therapy with risdiplam (Evrysdi). What best describes how this drug exerts its therapeutic effect?
column3: Modifies SMN2 splicing to promote exon 7 inclusion and increase functional SMN protein
column4: Delivers functional SMN1 gene using an adeno-associated virus vector
column5: Inhibits translation of truncated SMN protein isoforms
column6: Binds SMN protein to prevent its degradation by the proteasome
column7: Modifies SMN2 splicing to promote exon 7 inclusion and increase functional SMN protein is correct because risdiplam works by altering SMN2 splicing patterns systemically via oral administration.
column8: Delivers functional SMN1 gene using an adeno-associated virus vector is incorrect because that is the mechanism of onasemnogene abeparvovec-xioi (Zolgensma).
column9: Inhibits translation of truncated SMN protein isoforms is incorrect because risdiplam enhances production of full-length protein, not inhibition.
column10: Binds SMN protein to prevent its degradation by the proteasome is incorrect because risdiplam does not act at the protein degradation level.

column2: What is the most likely inheritance pattern of Friedreich ataxia?
column7: Autosomal recessive is correct because Friedreich ataxia is caused by a GAA trinucleotide repeat expansion in the FXN gene on chromosome 9, inherited in an autosomal recessive pattern. This leads to frataxin deficiency, mitochondrial dysfunction, and adolescent-onset progressive gait ataxia, scoliosis, pes cavus, cardiomyopathy, diabetes, and areflexia.
column8: Autosomal dominant is incorrect because Friedreich ataxia is not dominantly inherited; this pattern is seen in some spinocerebellar ataxias.
column9: X-linked recessive is incorrect because Friedreich ataxia affects both sexes equally and is not linked to the X chromosome.
column10: Mitochondrial inheritance is incorrect because Friedreich ataxia follows Mendelian inheritance and does not demonstrate maternal transmission.

column2: Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology of Friedreich ataxia?
column4: Autoimmune demyelination of the dorsal columns
column5: Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra
column7: Frataxin deficiency is correct because Friedreich ataxia is an autosomal recessive disease caused by a GAA repeat in the FXN gene on chromosome 9. This leads to loss of frataxin,  which leads to mitochondrial iron overload and oxidative stress leading to adolescent-onset ataxia, scoliosis, pes cavus, cardiomyopathy, diabetes, and areflexia.
column8: Autoimmune demyelination of the dorsal columns is incorrect because this describes multiple sclerosis, not Friedreich ataxia.
column9: Degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra is incorrect because this describes the pathophysiology of Parkinson disease.
column10: Friedrich protein-3 dysfunction is incorrect because this protein is fictional and does not play a role in Friedreich ataxia.

column2: A 15-year-old girl presents with progressive difficulty walking and frequent falls over the past two years. Examination shows pes cavus, scoliosis, dysarthria, loss of deep tendon reflexes in the lower extremities, and a positive Romberg sign. She also has a history of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and impaired glucose tolerance. Brain MRI is normal, but spinal cord atrophy is noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Friedreich ataxia is correct because this autosomal recessive condition is caused by a GAA trinucleotide repeat in the FXN gene on chromosome 9, leading to frataxin deficiency and mitochondrial dysfunction. It typically presents in adolescence with progressive gait ataxia, scoliosis, pes cavus, areflexia, cardiomyopathy, diabetes, and spinal cord atrophy.
column8: Charcot-Marie-Tooth disease is incorrect because while it may present with pes cavus and distal weakness, it does not cause cardiomyopathy, diabetes, or spinal cord atrophy.
column9: Spinocerebellar ataxia is incorrect because it typically presents later in life and follows an autosomal dominant inheritance pattern.
column10: Multiple sclerosis is incorrect because it presents with CNS demyelinating lesions and typically follows a relapsing-remitting course without spinal cord atrophy or cardiomyopathy.

column2: A 45-year-old woman presents with persistent burning pain, swelling, and changes in skin temperature in her right hand three months after a distal radius fracture. She has no evidence of peripheral nerve injury. Physical exam reveals hyperalgesia, mild edema, increased sweating, and decreased active range of motion. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to confirm the diagnosis?
column3: Use of Budapest criteria for the diagnosis of complex regional pain syndrome type I
column7: Use of Budapest criteria for the diagnosis of complex regional pain syndrome type I is correct because the Budapest criteria are the gold standard for diagnosing complex regional pain syndrome type I and require continuing pain disproportionate to any inciting event along with signs and symptoms in at least three of four categories: sensory, vasomotor, sudomotor/edema, and motor/trophic.
column8: Thermography to assess temperature asymmetry is incorrect because while thermography may show autonomic dysfunction, it is not necessary for diagnosis and has limited specificity.
column9: Triple-phase bone scan is incorrect because although it may show increased uptake in early complex regional pain syndrome, its sensitivity and specificity are not sufficient to confirm the diagnosis.
column10: Diagnostic sympathetic nerve block is incorrect because this may be used to assess treatment response in sympathetically maintained pain, but it is not used to confirm the diagnosis of complex regional pain syndrome type I.

column2: A 38-year-old woman presents with chronic pain in her right lower extremity one year after a tibial plateau fracture. She describes burning, electric shock–like pain worsened by light touch. Examination reveals allodynia and hyperpathia. According to the gate control theory of pain proposed by Melzack and Wall, which of the following best describes the central neuronal mechanism that allows tactile input to modulate nociceptive transmission in the dorsal horn?
column7: Wide dynamic range neurons is correct because these neurons in the dorsal horn respond to both painful and non-painful stimuli — they have a "wide range" of input, including light touch (A-beta fibers), sharp pain (A-delta fibers), and burning pain (C fibers). The gate control theory proposes that stimulating non-painful input like touch can reduce pain by inhibiting wide dynamic range neuron output through spinal cord circuits. This is why rubbing or applying pressure can make pain feel better — it "closes the gate" on pain signals traveling to the brain.
column8: Spinoreticular tract neurons is incorrect because they carry pain signals to the brainstem for emotional processing but do not integrate touch and pain at the spinal cord level.
column9: A-delta nociceptive-specific neurons is incorrect because they respond only to sharp pain and do not process non-painful input like light touch.
column10: Dorsal column medial lemniscal neurons is incorrect because they carry fine touch and proprioception to the brain but do not participate in spinal modulation of pain.

column2: Which of the following opioids has the highest octanol-water partition coefficient, reflecting the greatest lipophilicity and fastest central nervous system penetration?
column7: Fentanyl is correct because fentanyl has an extremely high octanol-water partition coefficient (log P ≈ 4), indicating high lipophilicity. The partition coefficient measures how well a drug dissolves in fat (octanol) versus water, and fentanyl’s high value allows for rapid blood-brain barrier penetration and fast onset of action.
column8: Morphine is incorrect because it has a low octanol-water partition coefficient (log P ≈ 0.89), meaning it is more hydrophilic, crosses the blood-brain barrier slowly, and has a delayed onset of action.
column9: Hydromorphone is incorrect because it is more lipophilic than morphine (log P ≈ 1.4), but still significantly less lipophilic than fentanyl, resulting in a slower CNS penetration compared to fentanyl.
column10: Methylnaltrexone is incorrect because it is purposely modified to be highly hydrophilic (log P < 0), preventing blood-brain barrier penetration so it acts only on peripheral opioid receptors to reverse constipation without affecting central analgesia.
column12:
This list ranks opioids by lipophilicity based on their octanol-water partition coefficients (log P), with higher values like carfentanil and fentanyl indicating faster CNS penetration and lower values like morphine and oxycodone indicating slower onset due to reduced lipid solubility.
Knowing an opioid’s octanol-water partition coefficient (log P) is important because it predicts how quickly and extensively the drug enters the central nervous system, influencing its onset, duration, and risk of side effects.
• Fentanyl: ~4.0  
• Carfentanil: ~4.5  
• Methadone: ~3.9  
• Hydrocodone: ~2.1  
• Oxycodone: ~0.7  
• Hydromorphone: ~1.4  
• Morphine: ~0.89

column2: A 62-year-old woman with depression and hypertension managed with sertraline and amlodipine is prescribed tramadol for chronic low back pain. Two days later, she presents with confusion, agitation, tremor, diaphoresis, and tachycardia. Vitals show mild hypertension and a low-grade fever. Which of the following best explains the most likely cause of her symptoms?
column3: Serotonin syndrome from interaction between tramadol and sertraline
column4: Opioid withdrawal due to poor adherence to tramadol
column6: Hypertensive encephalopathy from uncontrolled blood pressure
column7: Serotonin syndrome from interaction between tramadol and sertraline is correct because tramadol has serotonergic activity, and when combined with an SSRI like sertraline, can precipitate serotonin syndrome. Though neuromuscular signs may be subtle early on, the combination of confusion, tremor, diaphoresis, and autonomic instability strongly suggests serotonin toxicity.
column8: Opioid withdrawal due to poor adherence to tramadol is incorrect because withdrawal more commonly presents with myalgias, rhinorrhea, yawning, GI upset, and restlessness—not tremor and autonomic hyperactivity in the setting of ongoing opioid use.
column9: Tramadol-induced seizure with postictal confusion is incorrect because a seizure would more likely result in a brief postictal phase with somnolence rather than sustained agitation, tremor, and autonomic symptoms.
column10: Hypertensive encephalopathy from uncontrolled blood pressure is incorrect because it usually presents with severe hypertension and symptoms like headache, visual disturbances, or focal neurologic deficits—not systemic autonomic symptoms and tremor.

column2: A 58-year-old man with chronic pain is transitioned from oxycodone to methadone due to escalating opioid tolerance. His other medications include sertraline, ondansetron, and omeprazole. One week later, he reports intermittent palpitations and near-syncope. Vital signs are stable. Physical exam is unremarkable. Which of the following best explains his symptoms?
column3: Methadone-induced QT prolongation due to hERG channel blockade
column4: Serotonin syndrome triggered by methadone and sertraline
column5: Drug-induced hypotension from methadone and ondansetron
column6: Worsening GERD symptoms misinterpreted as cardiac
column7: Methadone-induced QT prolongation due to hERG channel blockade is correct because methadone uniquely blocks the cardiac IKr potassium current via hERG channels, prolonging ventricular repolarization and predisposing to torsades de pointes, especially in patients on other QT-prolonging agents like ondansetron and sertraline.
column8: Serotonin syndrome triggered by methadone and sertraline is incorrect because serotonin syndrome presents with mental status changes, hyperreflexia, and autonomic instability—not isolated palpitations and near-syncope.
column9: Drug-induced hypotension from methadone and ondansetron is incorrect because neither drug is typically associated with significant hypotension, and the patient's vitals are stable.
column10: Worsening GERD symptoms misinterpreted as cardiac is incorrect because GERD does not explain near-syncope or palpitations in a patient on QT-prolonging medications.

column2: A 47-year-old woman with chronic cervical radiculopathy is started on amitriptyline for neuropathic pain. At her follow-up visit, she reports excessive daytime sleepiness despite taking the medication only at bedtime. Which of the following best explains the sedative effect of amitriptyline?
column3: Antagonism of central histamine H1 receptors
column4: Inhibition of norepinephrine reuptake in the locus coeruleus
column5: Blockade of muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the brainstem
column7: Antagonism of central histamine H1 receptors is correct because amitriptyline’s sedative effect is largely due to its potent antagonism of central H1 receptors, leading to significant somnolence, especially when initiated or dosed too high.
column8: Inhibition of norepinephrine reuptake in the locus coeruleus is incorrect because this mechanism enhances alertness and arousal rather than promoting sedation.
column9: Blockade of muscarinic acetylcholine receptors in the brainstem is incorrect because while anticholinergic effects contribute to cognitive dulling and dry mouth, they are not the primary cause of sedation.
column10: Downregulation of serotonin 5-HT2A receptors is incorrect because tricyclic antidepressants do not directly downregulate these receptors, and their involvement in sedation is minimal compared to histamine blockade.

column2: Which of the following best describes the primary mechanism of action of gabapentin in the treatment of neuropathic pain?
column3: Binds to the alpha-2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels
column4: Inhibits reuptake of serotonin and norepinephrine
column6: Acts as a direct GABA-A receptor agonist
column7: Binds to the alpha-2-delta subunit of voltage-gated calcium channels is correct because gabapentin reduces excitatory neurotransmitter release by binding to the alpha-2-delta subunit of presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, thereby modulating neuropathic pain signaling.
column8: Inhibits reuptake of serotonin and norepinephrine is incorrect because this is the mechanism of serotonin-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitors such as duloxetine, not gabapentin.
column9: Blocks voltage-gated sodium channels is incorrect because this mechanism is associated with drugs like carbamazepine and local anesthetics, not gabapentin.
column10: Acts as a direct GABA-A receptor agonist is incorrect because gabapentin does not bind to GABA receptors and has no direct GABAergic activity.

column2: A 45-year-old woman with spasticity from traumatic brain injury is being treated with baclofen, tizanidine, and valproic acid. She reports new-onset bruising on her legs but denies recent falls, nosebleeds, or gum bleeding. Her platelet count is 95,000/μL. Hemoglobin and WBC count are normal. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?
column7: Valproic acid–induced thrombocytopenia is correct because valproic acid is a well-known cause of dose-dependent thrombocytopenia, which may present with mild bruising in the absence of other cytopenias, especially when platelet counts drop below 100,000/μL.
column8: Baclofen-induced platelet suppression is incorrect because baclofen is not associated with thrombocytopenia or myelosuppression.
column9: Tizanidine-induced hepatic dysfunction causing coagulopathy is incorrect because tizanidine may cause elevated liver enzymes, but it does not typically impair clotting factor synthesis or cause thrombocytopenia.
column10: Undiagnosed immune thrombocytopenic purpura is incorrect because ITP typically presents with platelet counts <50,000/μL and more severe bleeding signs, without association with antiepileptic medications.

column2: Which of the following is a known side effect of tizanidine?
column7: Dry mouth is correct because tizanidine commonly causes dry mouth due to its central alpha-2 agonist activity, which reduces salivary gland secretion.
column8: Peripheral neuropathy is incorrect because tizanidine does not damage peripheral nerves and is not linked to neuropathic complications.
column9: Hyperkalemia is incorrect because tizanidine has no known effect on potassium balance or renal potassium handling.
column10: Gynecomastia is incorrect because tizanidine is not associated with hormonal effects or endocrine disruption.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: Which of the following is not a well-known theory of migraine headache etiology?
column7: Myofascial trigger point theory is correct because while muscle tension may contribute to headache symptoms, it is not considered a primary or widely accepted theory of migraine pathophysiology.
column8: Neurovascular theory is incorrect because this is a well-established theory suggesting that migraine involves both vascular and neural dysfunction contributing to headache generation.
column9: Cortical spreading depression theory is incorrect because this theory explains the aura phase of migraines as a wave of neuronal and glial depolarization across the cortex.
column10: Trigeminovascular theory is incorrect because it is a central model describing how activation of trigeminal afferents and associated vascular structures results in neurogenic inflammation and pain.

column2: A 38-year-old man presents with sudden-onset, severe left-sided periorbital pain that wakes him from sleep. The episodes last about 45 minutes and occur nightly for the past week. He also reports tearing and nasal congestion on the same side during the attacks. He is visibly agitated and paces during the pain. Which of the following is the most appropriate abortive treatment?
column3: High-flow oxygen therapy via non-rebreather mask
column7: High-flow oxygen therapy via non-rebreather mask is correct because the presentation is classic for cluster headache, and high-flow oxygen at 12–15 L/min is a first-line abortive treatment that often relieves symptoms within minutes.
column8: Oral sumatriptan is incorrect because oral formulations are too slow for cluster headache abortive use; subcutaneous sumatriptan is preferred when oxygen is unavailable.
column9: Amitriptyline is incorrect because it is used as a prophylactic for chronic migraines, not for aborting acute cluster attacks.
column10: Topiramate is incorrect because it is used for prevention, not abortive treatment of cluster headaches.

column2: A 38-year-old man reports severe, unilateral periorbital headaches that occur nightly for several weeks at a time, followed by headache-free intervals. During each episode, he experiences ipsilateral eye tearing and nasal congestion, and often paces due to the intensity of the pain. He is currently using high-flow oxygen for acute attacks. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to reduce the frequency of his headache episodes?
column7: Verapamil is correct because it is the first-line preventive treatment for cluster headaches, initiated during a bout to reduce the frequency and severity of attacks.
column8: Sumatriptan is incorrect because it is used for acute abortive treatment, not for preventing future cluster episodes.
column9: Rizatriptan is incorrect because it is a triptan used for migraine abortion, not for cluster headache prevention.
column10: Acetaminophen is incorrect because it is not effective for aborting or preventing cluster headaches due to their severity and rapid onset.

column2: A 40-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of recurrent unilateral, pulsating headaches accompanied by photophobia and nausea. Her symptoms typically last 12–24 hours and occur multiple times per month, causing her to miss work and avoid social activities. Which of the following drug classes directly targets a molecule implicated in the pathophysiology of migraine?
column7: CGRP monoclonal antibody is correct because calcitonin gene–related peptide plays a central role in migraine pathophysiology, and monoclonal antibodies targeting CGRP or its receptor are specifically designed for migraine prevention.
column8: Serotonin receptor agonist is incorrect because triptans (5-HT1B/1D agonists) are used for abortive migraine treatment, not preventive therapy targeting CGRP.
column9: GABA analog is incorrect because agents like gabapentin are sometimes used off-label for migraine prevention but do not directly act on CGRP or its receptor.
column10: Tricyclic antidepressant is incorrect because medications like amitriptyline are used for migraine prevention but do not directly affect CGRP-mediated pathways.
column12: Calcitonin gene–related peptide (CGRP) is a neuropeptide released during migraine attacks that causes vasodilation and promotes inflammation and pain transmission in the trigeminovascular system. CGRP plays a key role in the trigeminovascular theory of migraine, which proposes that activation of trigeminal afferents leads to the release of CGRP and other neuropeptides, resulting in vasodilation, neurogenic inflammation, and central sensitization that drive migraine pain.

column2: A 63-year-old man with a 40-pack-year smoking history presents with several months of joint pain in the wrists and ankles, as well as recent swelling in the lower legs. He denies trauma but reports dull, aching pain that worsens with activity. On exam, he has bilateral digital clubbing and tenderness over the distal tibiae. Radiographs reveal periosteal new bone formation along the long bones of the lower extremities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Hypertrophic pulmonary osteoarthropathy is correct because this condition presents with digital clubbing, periostitis of long bones, and joint pain, often in the setting of intrathoracic disease such as lung cancer, especially in heavy smokers.
column8: Rheumatoid arthritis is incorrect because it primarily affects the small joints of the hands in a symmetric fashion and is not associated with digital clubbing or periosteal bone changes.
column9: Osteomalacia is incorrect because it presents with diffuse bone pain and muscle weakness due to defective bone mineralization, but does not cause periostitis or clubbing.
column10: Polymyalgia rheumatica is incorrect because it typically causes proximal muscle stiffness and pain in older adults and is not associated with periosteal bone changes or digital clubbing.

column2: A 65-year-old man with advanced right-sided pleural mesothelioma reports severe, constant, burning pain over the right chest and upper abdomen. Despite high-dose opioids and adjuvant medications, his pain remains debilitating. He is alert, oriented, and expected to live several more months. Which of the following procedures is most appropriate to consider for targeted pain relief?
column3: Left anterolateral cordotomy at the C1–C2 level
column7: Left anterolateral cordotomy at the C1–C2 level is correct because cordotomy is a neurosurgical procedure that selectively lesions the contralateral spinothalamic tract—responsible for transmitting pain and temperature signals—to relieve intractable unilateral nociceptive pain, especially from cancer such as pleural or peritoneal mesothelioma. It is most effective in patients with unilateral pain, preserved cognitive function, and a prognosis of several months or more.
column8: Bilateral dorsal column stimulator placement is incorrect because spinal cord stimulation is primarily indicated for neuropathic pain syndromes like complex regional pain syndrome or failed back surgery syndrome, and it is not effective for deep visceral or oncologic pain.
column9: Right thoracic sympathetic block is incorrect because sympathetic blocks are used for sympathetically maintained pain, such as in CRPS, and not for constant nociceptive pain from malignancy.
column10: Intrathecal baclofen pump implantation is incorrect because this intervention is used for severe spasticity and not for pain management, especially not for somatic or visceral cancer pain.

column2: A 58-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic cancer has diffuse, bilateral abdominal and lower back pain that is poorly controlled despite escalating doses of oral morphine and adjuvant medications. She experiences significant sedation and constipation from her current regimen. Her prognosis is greater than 3 months, and she remains functional and cognitively intact. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pain management?
column5: Continue current regimen and reassess later
column7: Intrathecal morphine pump implantation is correct because intrathecal delivery allows for potent analgesia with reduced systemic side effects and is appropriate for medically refractory, diffuse cancer-related pain in patients with a life expectancy over a few months.
column8: Spinal cord stimulation is incorrect because SCS is best used for neuropathic pain syndromes and is not effective for bilateral visceral nociceptive cancer pain.
column9: Continue current regimen and reassess later is incorrect because the patient is already experiencing significant side effects and poor pain control, indicating a need for an interventional escalation rather than delay.
column10: Oral methadone rotation is incorrect because, while it may offer some pharmacologic benefit, it does not adequately address systemic side effects already limiting therapy, whereas intrathecal administration does.

column2: A 50-year-old woman presents with severe, focal, burning pain over the left medial ankle following an open reduction and internal fixation of an ankle fracture 9 months ago. She has failed trials of physical therapy, gabapentin, and duloxetine. On exam, she has allodynia and hyperalgesia confined to the L4 dermatome on the left. Which of the following is the most appropriate interventional treatment to target her symptoms?
column7: Left dorsal root ganglion stimulation is correct because DRG stimulation offers targeted, dermatomal-specific neuromodulation, making it particularly effective for focal neuropathic pain such as CRPS or post-surgical pain in a single dermatome.
column8: Spinal cord stimulation is incorrect because traditional SCS may not provide adequate coverage for highly focal pain and lacks the dermatomal specificity of DRG stimulation.
column9: Left lumbar sympathetic block is incorrect because while sympathetic blocks may be diagnostic or therapeutic for sympathetically maintained pain, they do not provide long-term relief for focal, neuropathic pain.
column10: Intrathecal morphine pump implantation is incorrect because it is used for diffuse, severe nociceptive pain and would not provide the focal coverage needed in this case.

column2: A 45-year-old man develops persistent focal pain over the lateral aspect of his right thigh following blunt trauma from a motorcycle accident 6 months ago. The pain is sharp, burning, and localized to the area innervated by the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve. He has tried physical therapy and multiple medications without improvement. There are no signs of allodynia, motor deficits, or dermatomal radiation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in pain management?
column7: Peripheral nerve stimulation is correct because PNS is ideal for treating focal, non-dermatomal, neuropathic pain localized to a named peripheral nerve such as the lateral femoral cutaneous nerve, especially in post-traumatic cases.
column8: Dorsal root ganglion stimulation is incorrect because DRG is best suited for dermatomal pain (e.g., CRPS or post-surgical pain in a specific dermatome), not for isolated peripheral nerve injury.
column9: Spinal cord stimulation is incorrect because it is designed for diffuse neuropathic pain or bilateral symptoms and is not effective for highly localized mononeuropathic pain.
column10: Intrathecal opioid pump placement is incorrect because this is reserved for diffuse or visceral pain syndromes and is excessive for a localized mononeuropathy.

column2: A 42-year-old woman presents with deep buttock pain radiating down the posterior thigh. The pain worsens with prolonged sitting and improves with walking. On exam, she has tenderness over the sciatic notch and pain reproduced with the FAIR (Flexion, Adduction, Internal Rotation) maneuver. Lumbar MRI is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Piriformis syndrome is correct because the patient presents with buttock pain radiating down the leg, worsened by sitting, normal lumbar imaging, and reproduction of pain with the FAIR test—all features characteristic of sciatic nerve compression by the piriformis muscle.
column8: Lumbar spinal stenosis is incorrect because stenosis typically causes bilateral leg symptoms and neurogenic claudication that improves with sitting, not worsens.
column9: Ischial bursitis is incorrect because it causes localized buttock pain over the ischial tuberosity without sciatic radiation or a positive FAIR test.
column10: Sacroiliac joint dysfunction is incorrect because it causes buttock or low back pain typically worsened by standing or transitional movements, not prolonged sitting.

column2: A 48-year-old man with chronic low back pain undergoes lumbar spine MRI, which reveals Modic type I changes at the L4-L5 vertebral endplates. These findings most likely represent which of the following?
column3: Vertebral endplate inflammation and bone marrow edema
column4: Fatty replacement of the bone marrow
column7: Vertebral endplate inflammation and bone marrow edema is correct because Modic type I changes reflect active inflammatory changes, and on MRI they appear hypointense on T1 and hyperintense on T2-weighted sequences due to bone marrow edema and vascular granulation tissue.
column8: Fatty replacement of the bone marrow is incorrect because Modic type II changes appear hyperintense on T1 and isointense to mildly hyperintense on T2, not the pattern described for type I.
column9: Subchondral bone sclerosis is incorrect because Modic type III changes show hypointensity on both T1- and T2-weighted images and reflect dense bone formation.
column10: Unclassified vertebral endplate degeneration is incorrect because there are only three validated Modic types (I–III) recognized in clinical imaging.

column2: A 52-year-old woman with chronic low back pain undergoes lumbar spine MRI, which reveals Modic type II changes at the L5-S1 vertebral endplates. These findings most likely represent which of the following?
column3: Fatty replacement of the bone marrow
column4: Active inflammation and bone marrow edema
column7: Fatty replacement of the bone marrow is correct because Modic type II changes are associated with fatty infiltration of bone marrow and appear hyperintense on T1 and isointense to mildly hyperintense on T2-weighted MRI, reflecting a chronic degenerative state.
column8: Active inflammation and bone marrow edema is incorrect because this corresponds to Modic type I changes, which show hypointensity on T1 and hyperintensity on T2.
column9: Subchondral bone sclerosis is incorrect because Modic type III changes are hypointense on both T1- and T2-weighted sequences and reflect dense bony remodeling.
column10: Unclassified vertebral endplate degeneration is incorrect because Modic changes are only classified as types I through III in validated imaging systems.

column2: A 58-year-old man is undergoing a lumbar medial branch block under fluoroscopic guidance. The attending physiatrist instructs a resident to make several adjustments to minimize both patient and operator radiation exposure. Which of the following adjustments would be least effective in reducing scatter radiation to the operator?
column3: Raising the patient away from the image intensifier
column4: Standing on the image intensifier side of the c-arm
column5: Using pulsed fluoroscopy at the lowest acceptable frame rate
column6: Positioning the image intensifier as close to the patient as possible
column7: Raising the patient away from the image intensifier is correct because increasing the distance between the patient and the image intensifier enlarges the irradiated field, requires a higher dose, and leads to more scatter radiation toward the operator.
column8: Standing on the image intensifier side of the c-arm is incorrect because this side produces significantly less scatter than the x-ray tube side, protecting the operator.
column9: Using pulsed fluoroscopy at the lowest acceptable frame rate is incorrect because it lowers total radiation output per unit time, decreasing exposure to both patient and staff.
column10: Positioning the image intensifier as close to the patient as possible is incorrect because it minimizes the dose required for imaging and reduces the amount of scatter radiation.

column2: A 65-year-old woman with lumbar spinal stenosis is scheduled for a fluoroscopy-guided lumbar interlaminar epidural steroid injection. Which of the following needle selections best balances precision, tactile feedback, and safety for this neuraxial procedure?
column7: 20-gauge Tuohy needle is correct because its curved tip facilitates safe advancement toward the epidural space, provides good tactile feedback for loss-of-resistance technique, and minimizes dural puncture risk in interlaminar neuraxial procedures.
column8: 25-gauge Quincke needle is incorrect because its cutting bevel increases the risk of dural puncture and post-dural headache, and its small gauge compromises tactile feedback.
column9: 22-gauge Whitacre needle is incorrect because it is a pencil-point needle designed for intrathecal access rather than epidural procedures and offers less directional control.
column10: 18-gauge Chiba needle is incorrect because it is a thin, flexible needle typically used for diagnostic injections or nerve blocks under CT guidance, not for neuraxial access.
column12: A Quincke needle has a sharp, cutting bevel tip designed to penetrate the dura cleanly and is most commonly used for diagnostic lumbar punctures and spinal anesthesia. A Tuohy needle has a blunt, curved tip with a lateral opening, allowing for safer navigation toward the epidural space and is typically used for epidural steroid injections and catheter placement.

column2: A 67-year-old man with chronic axial low back pain is scheduled for fluoroscopy-guided lumbar medial branch blocks at L4 and L5. Which of the following needle types is most appropriate for this procedure?
column7: 22-gauge Quincke needle is correct because its cutting bevel tip provides reliable directional control for bony contact under fluoroscopy, making it well-suited for precise medial branch targeting during diagnostic blocks.
column8: 22-gauge Whitacre needle is incorrect because its pencil-point design limits steering capability and is better suited for intrathecal procedures rather than targeted nerve blocks.
