column9: 20-gauge Tuohy needle is incorrect because its curved, blunt tip is designed for accessing the epidural space, not for perineural injections requiring bony contact.
column10: 18-gauge Huber needle is incorrect because it is typically used for accessing implanted ports or delivering medications through subcutaneous devices, not for spinal or perineural procedures.
column12: A Quincke needle has a sharp, cutting bevel tip designed to penetrate the dura cleanly and is most commonly used for diagnostic lumbar punctures and spinal anesthesia. A Tuohy needle has a blunt, curved tip with a lateral opening, allowing for safer navigation toward the epidural space and is typically used for epidural steroid injections and catheter placement.

column2: A 54-year-old woman undergoes a fluoroscopy-guided lumbar transforaminal injection for radicular pain. Within one minute of contrast and test injection, she becomes confused, reports a metallic taste, and develops perioral numbness and fine tremors. Her blood pressure and oxygen saturation remain normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity is correct because the rapid onset of neurologic symptoms including altered mental status, metallic taste, and perioral tingling shortly after spinal injection is classic for inadvertent intravascular injection of local anesthetic.
column8: Contrast reaction is incorrect because contrast media reactions typically present with flushing, itching, nausea, or anaphylactoid symptoms rather than isolated CNS changes.
column9: Anaphylactic reaction is incorrect because it usually presents with respiratory symptoms, urticaria, and hypotension, not isolated neurologic symptoms.
column10: Epidural hematoma is incorrect because it presents later with back pain and progressive neurologic deficits, not immediate CNS symptoms.

column2: A 64-year-old man receives a lumbar transforaminal epidural injection. Within 90 seconds of the procedure, he becomes lightheaded and develops bradycardia and wide-complex ventricular arrhythmia. He remains conscious but diaphoretic. What is the most likely cause of these symptoms?
column7: Local anesthetic systemic toxicity is correct because rapid onset of cardiovascular symptoms including bradycardia and ventricular arrhythmia shortly after spinal injection suggests systemic absorption or intravascular injection of local anesthetic, which can present with cardiac toxicity even without CNS prodrome.
column8: Contrast-induced bradyarrhythmia is incorrect because this is rare and typically transient without associated arrhythmias or hemodynamic instability.
column9: Vasovagal episode is incorrect because it usually involves bradycardia and hypotension but not wide-complex arrhythmias.
column10: Myocardial infarction is incorrect because MI typically presents with chest pain and ischemic EKG changes, not sudden bradyarrhythmia immediately after injection.

column2: A 63-year-old man becomes unresponsive and develops bradycardia and wide-complex ventricular tachycardia shortly after a fluoroscopy-guided injection. The procedure included a test injection followed by a therapeutic dose of local anesthetic. Local anesthetic systemic toxicity is suspected. What is the most appropriate next step in management?
column7: Administer 20% lipid emulsion intravenously is correct because lipid emulsion therapy is the first-line treatment for local anesthetic systemic toxicity, acting as a lipid sink to sequester circulating anesthetic and reverse cardiovascular and neurologic collapse.
column8: Administer intravenous calcium gluconate is incorrect because it is used to stabilize cardiac membranes in hyperkalemia, not to treat local anesthetic toxicity.
column9: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate is incorrect because it is used for sodium channel blockade (e.g., TCA overdose) and acidosis, but does not reverse the effects of systemic local anesthetics.
column10: Administer intravenous diphenhydramine is incorrect because it is used for allergic reactions and has no role in reversing LAST.

column2: A 62-year-old man is undergoing a fluoroscopy-guided caudal epidural steroid injection for lumbar radiculopathy. To minimize the risk of dural puncture, at or below which vertebral level should the advancing needle be kept?
column7: S3 is correct because the dural sac typically terminates at the S2 vertebral level, so advancing the needle no higher than S3 minimizes the risk of inadvertent dural puncture during caudal epidural injection. Since S2 is where the dural sac ends, and advancing the needle to or beyond S2 increases the risk of dural puncture, stopping at S3 is the safest practice.
column8: L5 is incorrect because it lies well above the end of the dural sac, and advancing the needle this high significantly increases the risk of dural puncture.
column9: S1 is incorrect because it is above the typical termination of the dural sac and poses increased risk of entering the thecal sac.
column10: S2 is incorrect because this is where the dural sac ends, so reaching this level may result in unintended dural puncture.

column2: During a fluoroscopy-guided lumbar transforaminal epidural steroid injection, needle placement is guided by the "safe triangle" to minimize neurovascular risk. Which of the following structures is not a border of this triangle?
column6: Lateral aspect of the vertebral body
column7: Intervertebral disc is correct because it lies anterior to the foramen and is not a structural border of the safe triangle used to guide needle placement in TF-ESIs.
column8: Inferior border of the pedicle is incorrect because it forms the inferior boundary of the triangle and helps guide safe needle advancement under fluoroscopy.
column9: Exiting spinal nerve root is incorrect because it defines the superior border of the safe triangle and must be avoided to prevent nerve injury.
column10: Lateral aspect of the vertebral body is incorrect because it forms the medial border of the safe triangle, marking the boundary between the foramen and the spinal canal.

column2: During a fluoroscopically guided stellate ganglion block for management of sympathetically mediated pain in the upper extremity, which structure is the primary target for local anesthetic deposition?
column3: Anterolateral aspect of the C6 vertebral body at the level of Chassaignac’s tubercle
column4: Posterior aspect of the transverse process of C7
column5: Midpoint between the carotid artery and internal jugular vein
column6: Anterior scalene muscle belly at C5
column7: Anterolateral aspect of the C6 vertebral body at the level of Chassaignac’s tubercle is correct because the stellate ganglion lies near the junction of the inferior cervical and first thoracic sympathetic ganglia, and fluoroscopic approach commonly targets the anterolateral surface of the C6 vertebral body to reduce risk to surrounding structures while achieving sympathetic blockade.
column8: Posterior aspect of the transverse process of C7 is incorrect because this location increases risk of vertebral artery puncture and pneumothorax and is not the standard fluoroscopic target for safety reasons.
column9: Midpoint between the carotid artery and internal jugular vein is incorrect because this corresponds to a vascular landmark rather than the sympathetic chain location.
column10: Anterior scalene muscle belly at C5 is incorrect because this muscle landmark is used for certain brachial plexus blocks, not stellate ganglion blocks.

column2: During rapid passive dorsiflexion of the ankle in a patient with incomplete spinal cord injury, you feel a catch early in the range followed by minimal resistance continuing for about one-third of the motion. What is the appropriate Modified Ashworth Scale grade?
column7: Grade 1+ is correct because there is a catch followed by minimal resistance through less than half the range of motion.
column8: Grade 1 is incorrect because it has a catch and release or minimal resistance only at the end of the range.
column9: Grade 2 is incorrect because resistance is felt through most of the range.
column10: Grade 3 is incorrect because passive movement is difficult due to considerable tone.

column2: While assessing post-stroke elbow spasticity, you feel a catch early in the range followed by resistance through slightly more than half of the range, but the limb can still be easily moved. What is the appropriate Modified Ashworth Scale grade?
column7: Grade 2 is correct because there is increased tone through most of the range of motion, but the limb is still easily moved.
column8: Grade 1+ is incorrect because it has resistance through less than half the range after the catch.
column9: Grade 3 is incorrect because movement is difficult due to considerable tone.
column10: Grade 4 is incorrect because there is no fixed contracture.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: During rapid passive knee extension in a patient with severe multiple sclerosis, you feel a marked increase in tone through most of the range, and moving the limb requires substantial effort but is still possible. What is the appropriate Modified Ashworth Scale grade?
column7: Grade 3 is correct because considerable increase in tone makes passive movement difficult.
column8: Grade 2 is incorrect because, although tone is increased through most of the range, movement is still easy in Grade 2.
column9: Grade 4 is incorrect because there is no fixed contracture.
column10: Grade 1+ is incorrect because resistance is minimal and through less than half the range after the catch.

column2: A patient with traumatic brain injury has an elbow fixed in a 5° flexion contracture. Within the remaining range of motion, the limb moves easily but has increased tone detected through most of that range. What is the appropriate Modified Ashworth Scale grade?
column7: Grade 4 is correct because the presence of any fixed contracture, even when movement is easy in the rest of the range, qualifies as Grade 4.
column8: Grade 3 is incorrect because although considerable tone makes movement difficult in Grade 3, there is no fixed contracture in that grade.
column9: Grade 2 is incorrect because the limb remains easily moved despite increased tone, but this does not account for the contracture.
column10: Grade 1+ is incorrect because resistance is minimal and through less than half the range after the catch.

column2: Which of the following best describes the pathophysiology of spasticity following an upper motor neuron lesion?
column3: Loss of descending inhibitory input increases gamma motor neuron activity, heightening muscle spindle sensitivity and Ia afferent firing, which enhances the monosynaptic stretch reflex and produces velocity-dependent increases in muscle tone
column4: Enhanced descending inhibition increases gamma motor neuron activity, reducing Ia afferent firing and muscle tone
column5: Loss of descending inhibitory input decreases gamma motor neuron activity, reducing muscle spindle sensitivity and stretch reflex excitability
column6: Loss of descending inhibitory input primarily affects Golgi tendon organs, reducing Ib afferent–mediated autogenic inhibition and causing tone to increase independent of velocity
column7: Loss of descending inhibitory input increases gamma motor neuron activity, heightening muscle spindle sensitivity and Ia afferent firing, which enhances the monosynaptic stretch reflex and produces velocity-dependent increases in muscle tone is correct because spasticity is characterized by an exaggerated stretch reflex due to heightened spindle sensitivity from increased gamma drive after UMN injury.
column8: Enhanced descending inhibition increases gamma motor neuron activity, reducing Ia afferent firing and muscle tone is incorrect because spasticity results from reduced, not enhanced, descending inhibition and increased, not reduced, Ia firing.
column9: Loss of descending inhibitory input decreases gamma motor neuron activity, reducing muscle spindle sensitivity and stretch reflex excitability is incorrect because gamma activity is increased, not decreased, in spasticity.
column10: Loss of descending inhibitory input primarily affects Golgi tendon organs, reducing Ib afferent–mediated autogenic inhibition and causing tone to increase independent of velocity is incorrect because the hallmark of spasticity is velocity dependence via the stretch reflex, not velocity-independent tone from Golgi tendon dysfunction.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A patient with chronic right hemiparesis after a middle cerebral artery stroke demonstrates velocity-dependent resistance to passive elbow extension. Which of the following best explains the underlying pathophysiology?
column3: Loss of descending inhibitory input increases gamma motor neuron activity, heightening muscle spindle sensitivity and Ia afferent firing, which enhances the monosynaptic stretch reflex
column4: Loss of descending inhibitory input reduces gamma motor neuron activity, decreasing muscle spindle sensitivity and Ia afferent firing, which lowers reflex excitability
column5: Increased alpha motor neuron inhibition decreases excitatory output to muscle, reducing tone and reflex responsiveness
column6: Enhanced Golgi tendon organ activation increases Ib afferent–mediated inhibition of alpha motor neurons, lowering muscle tone
column7: Loss of descending inhibitory input increases gamma motor neuron activity, heightening muscle spindle sensitivity and Ia afferent firing, which enhances the monosynaptic stretch reflex is correct because UMN lesions cause reduced inhibitory control, increasing gamma drive and stretch reflex gain, producing velocity-dependent tone.
column8: Loss of descending inhibitory input reduces gamma motor neuron activity, decreasing muscle spindle sensitivity and Ia afferent firing, which lowers reflex excitability is incorrect because gamma drive is increased, not decreased, after UMN lesions.
column9: Increased alpha motor neuron inhibition decreases excitatory output to muscle, reducing tone and reflex responsiveness is incorrect because spasticity involves reduced, not increased, alpha motor neuron inhibition.
column10: Enhanced Golgi tendon organ activation increases Ib afferent–mediated inhibition of alpha motor neurons, lowering muscle tone is incorrect because the Golgi tendon organ pathway is not the primary driver of spasticity.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: While assessing a patient with post-stroke ankle plantarflexor hypertonia, you measure resistance to passive dorsiflexion at both slow speed (V1) and fast speed (V3). At V1, the ankle dorsiflexes fully to neutral (0°), while at V3, you encounter a catch at 20° of plantarflexion followed by release. Which of the following best reflects how the Tardieu Scale interprets this finding?
column3: Large velocity-dependent component of spasticity with an R2–R1 difference of 20°, indicating dynamic contracture contribution
column4: Minimal velocity-dependent component with R2–R1 difference near zero, indicating fixed contracture
column5: Large fixed contracture component with minimal velocity-dependent contribution
column6: No evidence of spasticity based on equal R1 and R2 angles and absence of catch at high velocity
column7: Large velocity-dependent component of spasticity with an R2–R1 difference of 20°, indicating dynamic contracture contribution is correct because R2 is the full PROM at slow speed (0°) and R1 is the angle of catch at fast speed (20° PF), and the difference reflects spasticity severity.
column8: Minimal velocity-dependent component with R2–R1 difference near zero, indicating fixed contracture is incorrect because the measured difference is 20°, not near zero.
column9: Large fixed contracture component with minimal velocity-dependent contribution is incorrect because R2 is full PROM, showing no fixed contracture.
column10: No evidence of spasticity based on equal R1 and R2 angles and absence of catch at high velocity is incorrect because there is a catch at 20° PF at high velocity, confirming spasticity.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A patient with chronic incomplete spinal cord injury undergoes Tardieu Scale assessment of the knee flexors. At slow speed (V1), passive knee extension is limited to 30° of flexion. At fast speed (V3), a catch is felt at 28° of flexion. Which of the following best reflects how the Tardieu Scale interprets this finding?
column3: Minimal velocity-dependent component with R2–R1 difference of 2°, indicating fixed contracture as the primary limitation
column4: Large velocity-dependent component of spasticity with R2–R1 difference of 30°, indicating dynamic contribution
column5: No contracture present with spasticity as the sole limitation to range
column6: No evidence of spasticity or contracture based on full PROM and absence of catch at high velocity
column7: Minimal velocity-dependent component with R2–R1 difference of 2°, indicating fixed contracture as the primary limitation is correct because the small R2–R1 difference means most of the limitation is present at both speeds, reflecting structural shortening rather than spasticity.
column8: Large velocity-dependent component of spasticity with R2–R1 difference of 30°, indicating dynamic contribution is incorrect because the measured difference is only 2°.
column9: No contracture present with spasticity as the sole limitation to range is incorrect because PROM is limited to 30° flexion even at slow speed, confirming contracture.
column10: No evidence of spasticity or contracture based on full PROM and absence of catch at high velocity is incorrect because both PROM is limited and a catch occurs, confirming pathology.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: In the Modified Tardieu Scale, which statement correctly defines R1 and R2?
column3: R1 is the angle of muscle reaction (catch) during fast stretch, and R2 is the full passive range of motion during slow stretch
column4: R1 is the angle at which clonus is first observed during fast stretch, and R2 is the joint angle at rest before movement
column5: R1 is the angle of muscle reaction during slow stretch, and R2 is the maximal muscle length measured at fast stretch
column6: R1 is the angle at which resistance is first felt during either slow or fast stretch, and R2 is the point where the muscle reaches maximal tension
column7: R1 is the angle of muscle reaction (catch) during fast stretch, and R2 is the full passive range of motion during slow stretch is correct because the Tardieu Scale measures R1 at high velocity to detect spasticity and R2 at low velocity to measure total available range.
column8: R1 is the angle at which clonus is first observed during fast stretch, and R2 is the joint angle at rest before movement is incorrect because clonus onset and resting position are not how R1 and R2 are defined.
column9: R1 is the angle of muscle reaction during slow stretch, and R2 is the maximal muscle length measured at fast stretch is incorrect because this reverses the purpose of the two measurements.
column10: R1 is the angle at which resistance is first felt during either slow or fast stretch, and R2 is the point where the muscle reaches maximal tension is incorrect because the Tardieu Scale specifically differentiates between high- and low-velocity measurements, not just first resistance vs maximal tension.
column12: The Modified Tardieu Scale is used by passively moving a joint at both slow and fast velocities to measure the angles of muscle reaction (R1) and full passive range (R2), allowing differentiation between spasticity and fixed contracture based on the velocity-dependent difference between these values.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 52-year-old patient with post-stroke hemiparesis and severe lower limb spasticity starts a new oral antispasmodic in addition to physical therapy. Two weeks later, she reports mild fatigue but no dizziness or hallucinations. Labs reveal AST 95 U/L and ALT 102 U/L (both previously normal). Which adverse effect–monitoring pair is most consistent with this medication?
column3: Hepatotoxicity; monitor liver function tests at baseline and periodically during treatment
column4: Sedation; monitor for mental status changes and adjust dose gradually
column5: Hypotension; monitor blood pressure, especially when initiating or titrating
column6: Abrupt withdrawal may cause seizures or hallucinations; taper medication slowly
column7: Hepatotoxicity; monitor liver function tests at baseline and periodically during treatment is correct because the patient developed new AST and ALT elevations two weeks after starting dantrolene, a direct-acting skeletal muscle relaxant that inhibits calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum in skeletal muscle; this peripheral mechanism can cause dose-related hepatotoxicity, necessitating baseline and periodic LFT monitoring.
column8: Sedation; monitor for mental status changes and adjust dose gradually is incorrect because the patient has no CNS depression, which is more typical for baclofen or tizanidine.
column9: Hypotension; monitor blood pressure, especially when initiating or titrating is incorrect because there is no blood pressure drop, and hypotension is most associated with tizanidine’s alpha-2 agonist effects.
column10: Abrupt withdrawal may cause seizures or hallucinations; taper medication slowly is incorrect because this complication is linked to sudden baclofen discontinuation, not the presentation here.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 45-year-old man with incomplete spinal cord injury and lower limb spasticity is started on an oral antispasmodic. One month later, he reports drowsiness and difficulty concentrating at work. Vital signs and labs are normal. Which adverse effect–monitoring pair is most consistent with this medication?
column3: Sedation; monitor for mental status changes and adjust dose gradually
column4: Hepatotoxicity; monitor liver function tests at baseline and periodically during treatment
column5: Hypotension; monitor blood pressure, especially when initiating or titrating
column6: Abrupt withdrawal may cause seizures or hallucinations; taper medication slowly
column7: Sedation; monitor for mental status changes and adjust dose gradually is correct because the patient developed CNS depression after starting baclofen, a centrally acting GABA-B receptor agonist that inhibits excitatory neurotransmitter release in the spinal cord; this central mechanism reduces spasticity but often causes sedation, requiring close monitoring and slow dose titration.
column8: Hepatotoxicity; monitor liver function tests at baseline and periodically during treatment is incorrect because there is no lab evidence of liver injury, and hepatotoxicity is linked to dantrolene.
column9: Hypotension; monitor blood pressure, especially when initiating or titrating is incorrect because blood pressure is stable, and hypotension is more typical of tizanidine.
column10: Abrupt withdrawal may cause seizures or hallucinations; taper medication slowly is incorrect because although baclofen withdrawal can cause these effects, the patient has not discontinued the drug.

column2: A 60-year-old woman with multiple sclerosis is started on an oral antispasmodic to help with lower limb stiffness. At a follow-up visit one week later, she reports feeling lightheaded when standing. Blood pressure is 92/58 mmHg (baseline 128/76 mmHg). Labs are normal. Which adverse effect–monitoring pair is most consistent with this medication?
column3: Hypotension; monitor blood pressure, especially when initiating or titrating
column4: Hepatotoxicity; monitor liver function tests at baseline and periodically during treatment
column5: Sedation; monitor for mental status changes and adjust dose gradually
column6: Abrupt withdrawal may cause seizures or hallucinations; taper medication slowly
column7: Hypotension; monitor blood pressure, especially when initiating or titrating is correct because the patient’s symptomatic BP drop after starting tizanidine, a centrally acting alpha-2 adrenergic agonist that reduces excitatory neurotransmitter release and muscle tone, reflects its known mechanism-related risk of hypotension, making BP monitoring essential during initiation and titration.
column8: Hepatotoxicity; monitor liver function tests at baseline and periodically during treatment is incorrect because her LFTs are normal and hypotension, not hepatotoxicity, is the defining concern here.
column9: Sedation; monitor for mental status changes and adjust dose gradually is incorrect because the primary problem is hypotension, not CNS depression.
column10: Abrupt withdrawal may cause seizures or hallucinations; taper medication slowly is incorrect because this complication is associated with baclofen, not tizanidine.

column2: A 33-year-old man is undergoing inpatient rehabilitation after a severe traumatic brain injury. Due to marked lower limb spasticity, he is started on an oral antispasticity medication. Three days later, he experiences a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. He has no prior seizure history, his electrolytes are normal, and he is afebrile. Which antispasticity medication is most likely responsible for this effect?
column7: Baclofen is correct because it is a GABA-B receptor agonist that, while reducing spasticity, can rarely lower the seizure threshold, particularly in patients with brain injury.
column8: Dantrolene is incorrect because its primary concern is hepatotoxicity, not seizure risk.
column9: Tizanidine is incorrect because it is an alpha-2 adrenergic agonist whose main adverse effects are hypotension and sedation.
column10: Diazepam is incorrect because, as a benzodiazepine, it typically raises rather than lowers seizure threshold.

column2: You inherit a post-stroke patient from another provider who has a classic upper limb flexion synergy pattern and has been receiving botulinum toxin injections to biceps brachii, flexor digitorum superficialis, and pronator teres. On your exam, the patient’s elbow flexion spasticity and finger flexor tone are well controlled, but the forearm remains in excessive pronation at rest, limiting functional tasks. The pronator teres dose is already near the recommended maximum. Which change in the injection plan would most likely help restore a more neutral forearm position?
column3: Discontinue chemodenervation of the biceps brachii
column4: Discontinue chemodenervation of the flexor digitorum superficialis
column6: Increase the dose to the pronator teres
column7: Discontinue chemodenervation of the biceps brachii is correct because in addition to elbow flexion, the biceps brachii is a strong supinator; reducing its activity with botulinum toxin removes a counterforce to pronator tone, so stopping its injection can promote more active supination and improve balance when pronator teres is already maximally treated.
column8: Discontinue chemodenervation of the flexor digitorum superficialis is incorrect because it primarily flexes the fingers and does not meaningfully affect forearm rotation.
column9: Add chemodenervation to the brachialis is incorrect because brachialis is a pure elbow flexor without supination or pronation function, so weakening it will not address pronation.
column10: Increase the dose to the pronator teres is incorrect because the patient is already at the maximum safe dose, and further weakening of pronation requires a different strategy.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: You inherit a post-stroke patient from another provider with a classic upper limb flexor synergy pattern. They have been receiving botulinum toxin injections to flexor carpi radialis (FCR) and flexor carpi ulnaris (FCU) for wrist flexion spasticity. On exam, wrist flexion tone is well controlled, but the patient now has difficulty stabilizing the wrist in slight extension during grasp-release tasks. Wrist extensors are weak, and excessive extension occurs when trying to grip objects. Which change in the injection plan would most likely improve functional wrist stability?
column3: Reduce or discontinue chemodenervation of the wrist flexors
column6: Increase chemodenervation to the pronator teres
column7: Reduce or discontinue chemodenervation of the wrist flexors is correct because maintaining some wrist flexor tone can help counteract weak extensors, improving stability during grasp; over-weakening wrist flexors can worsen excessive extension and reduce functional control.
column8: Increase chemodenervation to wrist extensors is incorrect because this would further weaken a muscle group already too weak, worsening the imbalance.
column9: Add chemodenervation to finger extensors is incorrect because finger extensors are not contributing to the wrist positioning problem and weakening them would impair hand opening.
column10: Increase chemodenervation to the pronator teres is incorrect because this muscle controls forearm rotation, not wrist stability in flexion/extension.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: Which of the following best describes the primary mechanism of action of botulinum toxins at the neuromuscular junction?
column3: Inhibit presynaptic acetylcholine release by cleaving SNARE proteins
column4: Block postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors at the motor endplate
column5: Increase presynaptic gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) release in the spinal cord
column6: Inhibit presynaptic norepinephrine release at alpha-2 adrenergic receptors
column7: Inhibit presynaptic acetylcholine release by cleaving SNARE proteins is correct because botulinum toxins enter the presynaptic terminal, cleave specific SNARE proteins (e.g., SNAP-25, synaptobrevin), and prevent vesicular fusion, thereby blocking acetylcholine release and producing temporary chemodenervation.
column8: Block postsynaptic acetylcholine receptors at the motor endplate is incorrect because this is the mechanism of neuromuscular blocking agents like curare, not botulinum toxin.
column9: Increase presynaptic gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) release in the spinal cord is incorrect because botulinum toxins act peripherally at the neuromuscular junction, not centrally in the spinal cord.
column10: Inhibit presynaptic norepinephrine release at alpha-2 adrenergic receptors is incorrect because this describes the action of alpha-2 agonists like tizanidine, not botulinum toxin.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: Which of the following correctly matches a botulinum toxin serotype with its SNARE protein target?
column7: Type B – synaptobrevin (VAMP) is correct because botulinum toxin type B specifically cleaves synaptobrevin (also known as VAMP), a vesicle-associated protein critical for acetylcholine vesicle docking and release.
column8: Type A – syntaxin is incorrect because type A cleaves SNAP-25, not syntaxin.
column9: Type C – synaptobrevin (VAMP) only is incorrect because type C cleaves both syntaxin and SNAP-25, not synaptobrevin alone.
column10: Type D – SNAP-25 is incorrect because type D cleaves synaptobrevin (VAMP), not SNAP-25.
column12:
Botulinum Toxin serotype paired with its corresponding SNARE protein target
A—SNAP-25
B—synaptobrevin (VAMP)
C—syntaxin & SNAP-25; D—synaptobrevin (VAMP)
E—SNAP-25
F—synaptobrevin (VAMP)
G—synaptobrevin (VAMP)
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 52-year-old man with severe post-stroke lower limb spasticity has been receiving botulinum toxin type A injections every 3 months for 5 years. For the first 4 years, his muscle tone improved significantly after each session, but over the past 9 months, two consecutive treatments performed under ultrasound guidance have had no noticeable effect. His spasticity pattern has not changed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
column3: Switch to botulinum toxin type B
column4: Increase the dose of botulinum toxin type A
column5: Shorten the injection interval for botulinum toxin type A
column6: Discontinue chemodenervation and use only oral antispasticity medications
column7: Switch to botulinum toxin type B is correct because repeated high-dose or frequent botulinum toxin type A injections can lead to neutralizing antibody formation, preventing the toxin from binding to SNAP-25 and blocking acetylcholine release; type B cleaves a different SNARE protein (synaptobrevin/VAMP) and is not affected by antibodies to type A.
column8: Increase the dose of botulinum toxin type A is incorrect because antibodies block its action regardless of dose.
column9: Shorten the injection interval for botulinum toxin type A is incorrect because it increases the risk of antibody formation and will not overcome existing resistance.
column10: Discontinue chemodenervation and use only oral antispasticity medications is incorrect because switching to another serotype can preserve functional benefits before abandoning injections entirely.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A patient with post-stroke spasticity loses response to botulinum toxin type A after years of effective treatment. You decide to switch to botulinum toxin type B. Which of the following best explains why type B may remain effective in this patient?
column3: Type B cleaves synaptobrevin (VAMP), a different SNARE protein than type A
column4: Type B binds to the same SNAP-25 protein but with higher affinity
column5: Type B increases gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) release in the spinal cord
column6: Type B prevents acetylcholine synthesis at the presynaptic terminal
column7: Type B cleaves synaptobrevin (VAMP), a different SNARE protein than type A is correct because antibodies to type A prevent it from cleaving SNAP-25, but type B acts on a different SNARE protein (synaptobrevin/VAMP), avoiding cross-resistance and allowing continued chemodenervation.
column8: Type B binds to the same SNAP-25 protein but with higher affinity is incorrect because type B does not target SNAP-25 at all.
column9: Type B increases gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) release in the spinal cord is incorrect because botulinum toxins act peripherally at the neuromuscular junction, not centrally on inhibitory neurotransmission.
column10: Type B prevents acetylcholine synthesis at the presynaptic terminal is incorrect because it blocks vesicle fusion, not neurotransmitter synthesis.
column12:
Botulinum Toxin serotype paired with its corresponding SNARE protein target
A—SNAP-25
B—synaptobrevin (VAMP)
C—syntaxin & SNAP-25; D—synaptobrevin (VAMP)
E—SNAP-25
F—synaptobrevin (VAMP)
G—synaptobrevin (VAMP)
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A patient with an implanted intrathecal baclofen pump reports hearing a single intermittent tone once per hour. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this alarm?
column7: Low reservoir volume alarm is correct because a single intermittent tone once per hour is the standard low-volume alert, indicating the pump’s medication reservoir is nearing empty and requires refill soon.
column8: Motor stall alarm is incorrect because it is typically a continuous or repeating alarm that occurs immediately after pump malfunction, not a once-per-hour tone.
column9: End-of-service life alarm is incorrect because it produces a more frequent, repeating alarm pattern to indicate battery depletion requiring pump replacement.
column10: Critical error alarm is incorrect because it is generally an urgent, repetitive alarm that signals a serious malfunction requiring immediate attention, not the intermittent hourly tone described.

column2: A patient with an intrathecal drug delivery pump reports that the pump has begun producing a single tone every 10 minutes continuously. This began suddenly and is accompanied by a message on the programmer indicating "service life exceeded." Which alarm does this pattern most likely represent?
column7: End-of-service life alarm is correct because a 10-minute repeating tone pattern signifies the pump’s battery has reached the end of service life, requiring replacement to prevent therapy interruption.
column8: Low reservoir volume alarm is incorrect because it produces a once-per-hour tone, not a frequent 10-minute alert.
column9: Motor stall alarm is incorrect because it typically triggers a continuous or short-interval repeating tone immediately after pump failure, without a “service life exceeded” message.
column10: Critical error alarm is incorrect because it usually involves multiple urgent tones in succession indicating a severe malfunction unrelated to routine battery depletion.

column2: A patient with an intrathecal pump suddenly develops worsening spasticity and reports that the pump began emitting a rapid repeating alarm immediately after a fall onto the pump site. Which of the following alarms is most likely responsible?
column7: Motor stall alarm is correct because a rapid, repeating alarm immediately after trauma suggests pump rotor malfunction or blockage, interrupting drug delivery.
column8: Low reservoir volume alarm is incorrect because it produces a once-per-hour tone and is unrelated to acute mechanical injury.
column9: End-of-service life alarm is incorrect because it develops gradually with battery depletion, not acutely after trauma.
column10: Critical error alarm is incorrect because it typically indicates a major system failure unrelated to rotor movement, and would not be specifically triggered by pump impact.

column2: A 42-year-old man with severe lower limb spasticity after a spinal cord injury undergoes a successful intrathecal baclofen (ITB) trial with a 75 mcg bolus, producing significant tone reduction for 6 hours. Which of the following is the most appropriate starting continuous daily infusion dose within most manufacturer guidelines?
column7: 200 mcg/day is correct because dividing the 75 mcg bolus by 6 hours gives 12.5 mcg/hour; multiplying by 24 yields 300 mcg/day projected, and reducing to about two-thirds (≈200 mcg/day) is a safe, conservative starting point within most manufacturer guidelines.
column8: 300 mcg/day is incorrect because it uses 100% of the projected dose without any safety reduction, increasing the risk of excessive weakness or overdose.
column9: 350 mcg/day is incorrect because it exceeds the upper limit of the typical 50–100% starting range in most manufacturer recommendations.
column10: 400 mcg/day is incorrect because it is well above the calculated and recommended starting range, significantly increasing risk of dangerous side effects.

column2: You recently referred a 12-year-old boy with spastic diplegic cerebral palsy and a fixed equinus contracture unresponsive to casting and bracing to orthopedic surgery for Achilles tendon lengthening. The surgeon explains they will perform a Z-plasty. Which description best matches the basic surgical technique for this procedure?
column3: Creating two opposing diagonal incisions in the tendon and transposing the flaps to increase length
column4: Making a single longitudinal incision through the tendon and closing it with overlap to lengthen
column5: Completely transecting the tendon and inserting a tendon graft to restore continuity
column6: Making multiple small transverse cuts in the tendon to gradually lengthen with postoperative stretching
column7: Creating two opposing diagonal incisions in the tendon and transposing the flaps to increase length is correct because a Z-plasty involves angled cuts creating tendon flaps that are shifted to lengthen the overall tendon without complete transection.
column8: Making a single longitudinal incision through the tendon and closing it with overlap to lengthen is incorrect because this describes a sliding lengthening, not a Z-plasty.
column9: Completely transecting the tendon and inserting a tendon graft to restore continuity is incorrect because this is tendon reconstruction, not Z-plasty lengthening.
column10: Making multiple small transverse cuts in the tendon to gradually lengthen with postoperative stretching is incorrect because this describes a step-cut or fractional lengthening, not Z-plasty.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: You recently referred a 9-year-old girl with spastic hemiplegic cerebral palsy and persistent foot deformity despite orthotic management to orthopedic surgery. The orthopedic surgeon plans a split anterior tibial tendon transfer (SPLATT). Which description best matches the basic surgical technique for this procedure?
column3: Dividing the anterior tibial tendon longitudinally and transferring half to the lateral foot to balance inversion and eversion forces
column4: Dividing the anterior tibial tendon longitudinally and transferring half to the dorsal midfoot to improve dorsiflexion without altering inversion-eversion balance
column5: Dividing the anterior tibial tendon longitudinally and transferring half to the medial foot to increase inversion strength
column6: Dividing the peroneus longus tendon longitudinally and transferring half to the medial foot to reduce eversion
column7: Dividing the anterior tibial tendon longitudinally and transferring half to the lateral foot to balance inversion and eversion forces is correct because in dynamic equinovarus from spastic hemiplegia, medial overpull of the anterior tibial tendon causes excessive inversion; SPLATT lengthwise splits the tendon and reroutes one slip laterally to counteract this force, improving foot position during gait.
column8: Dividing the anterior tibial tendon longitudinally and transferring half to the dorsal midfoot to improve dorsiflexion without altering inversion-eversion balance is incorrect because SPLATT specifically repositions the slip laterally to address inversion deformity, not solely dorsiflexion.
column9: Dividing the anterior tibial tendon longitudinally and transferring half to the medial foot to increase inversion strength is incorrect because SPLATT transfers the split tendon laterally to reduce inversion, not medially to enhance it.
column10: Dividing the peroneus longus tendon longitudinally and transferring half to the medial foot to reduce eversion is incorrect because this involves a peroneus tendon transfer, not SPLATT, and targets excessive eversion rather than inversion.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 58-year-old retired professional boxer presents with progressive memory loss, personality changes, and difficulty with balance. Neurologic examination reveals mild dysarthria, parkinsonian features, and impaired coordination. MRI shows cerebral atrophy with prominent changes in the septum pellucidum. Which of the following best describes the underlying condition?
column3: Dementia pugilistica, a form of chronic traumatic encephalopathy caused by repeated head trauma in boxing
column4: Rapidly progressive dementia with myoclonus due to prion disease
column5: Neurodegenerative disease with resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia due to dopaminergic neuron loss
column6: Autoimmune demyelinating disorder of the central nervous system with relapsing-remitting course
column7: Dementia pugilistica, a form of chronic traumatic encephalopathy caused by repeated head trauma in boxing is correct because the patient’s history of professional boxing, cognitive decline, behavioral changes, parkinsonism, ataxia, and septum pellucidum abnormalities are classic for this trauma-induced neurodegenerative condition.
column8: Rapidly progressive dementia with myoclonus due to prion disease is incorrect because this describes Creutzfeldt–Jakob disease, which is unrelated to repetitive head injury.
column9: Neurodegenerative disease with resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia due to dopaminergic neuron loss is incorrect because this describes Parkinson’s disease, not boxing-related cumulative brain trauma.
column10: Autoimmune demyelinating disorder of the central nervous system with relapsing-remitting course is incorrect because this describes multiple sclerosis, which is immune-mediated rather than trauma-induced.

column2: Which histologic finding is most characteristic of chronic traumatic encephalopathy?
column3: Perivascular accumulation of hyperphosphorylated tau in neurons and astrocytes at the depths of cortical sulci
column4: Beta-amyloid plaques with diffuse cortical distribution and neurofibrillary tangles in the hippocampus
column5: Alpha-synuclein inclusions in neuronal cytoplasm with associated neuronal loss in the substantia nigra
column6: Patchy perivenular demyelination with lymphocytic infiltration of the white matter
column7: Perivascular accumulation of hyperphosphorylated tau in neurons and astrocytes at the depths of cortical sulci is correct because this distinctive perivascular sulcal-depth tau pattern is the histopathologic hallmark of CTE, reflecting repetitive head trauma.
column8: Beta-amyloid plaques with diffuse cortical distribution and neurofibrillary tangles in the hippocampus is incorrect because this describes Alzheimer’s disease pathology.
column9: Alpha-synuclein inclusions in neuronal cytoplasm with associated neuronal loss in the substantia nigra is incorrect because this describes Parkinson’s disease or Lewy body dementia pathology.
column10: Patchy perivenular demyelination with lymphocytic infiltration of the white matter is incorrect because this describes multiple sclerosis histopathology.

column2: A 68-year-old man with a diagnosis of Parkinson’s disease is undergoing gait training in physical therapy. The physical therapist reports the patient is having frequent backward falls and difficulty looking down to navigate steps, which is limiting progress. On exam, vertical gaze palsy and axial rigidity are present, but there is minimal limb rigidity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column3: Progressive supranuclear palsy characterized by early postural instability and vertical gaze palsy
column4: Multiple system atrophy with autonomic failure, cerebellar ataxia, and parkinsonism
column5: Corticobasal degeneration with asymmetric limb rigidity, apraxia, and cortical sensory loss
column6: Parkinson’s disease with asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity
column7: Progressive supranuclear palsy characterized by early postural instability and vertical gaze palsy is correct because the patient’s early frequent backward falls, vertical gaze palsy, and axial rigidity are classic for PSP and explain the rehab difficulties noted by the therapist.
column8: Multiple system atrophy with autonomic failure, cerebellar ataxia, and parkinsonism is incorrect because MSA presents with early autonomic dysfunction and cerebellar signs, not vertical gaze palsy.
column9: Corticobasal degeneration with asymmetric limb rigidity, apraxia, and cortical sensory loss is incorrect because CBD presents with asymmetric cortical and extrapyramidal features, not early postural instability with gaze palsy.
column10: Parkinson’s disease with asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity is incorrect because the early postural instability and vertical gaze palsy are atypical for PD and suggest a Parkinson-plus syndrome.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 62-year-old man with a diagnosis of Parkinson’s disease is in physical therapy for gait and balance training. The physical therapist notes the patient becomes lightheaded and nearly faints when transitioning from sitting to standing, making it difficult to progress in therapy. He also reports urinary incontinence and erectile dysfunction. On examination, there is parkinsonism without a significant response to levodopa and marked orthostatic hypotension. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column3: Multiple system atrophy (Shy–Drager syndrome) with severe autonomic failure and parkinsonism
column4: Progressive supranuclear palsy with early postural instability and vertical gaze palsy
column5: Corticobasal degeneration with asymmetric limb rigidity, apraxia, and cortical sensory loss
column6: Parkinson’s disease with asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity
column7: Multiple system atrophy (Shy–Drager syndrome) with severe autonomic failure and parkinsonism is correct because profound orthostatic hypotension, urinary incontinence, erectile dysfunction, and poor levodopa responsiveness indicate MSA with predominant autonomic failure, explaining the PT limitations.
column8: Progressive supranuclear palsy with early postural instability and vertical gaze palsy is incorrect because PSP presents with gaze palsy and early falls, not predominant autonomic dysfunction.
column9: Corticobasal degeneration with asymmetric limb rigidity, apraxia, and cortical sensory loss is incorrect because CBD features cortical signs and asymmetric motor symptoms, not severe autonomic failure.
column10: Parkinson’s disease with asymmetric resting tremor, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity is incorrect because PD does not present with early severe autonomic symptoms like those in MSA.
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: A 60-year-old man with T12 paraplegia is being fitted for a new manual wheelchair. During measurement, his hip width is found to be 18 inches. To optimize comfort and reduce shear forces, how much wider should the seat width typically be compared to his measured hip width?
column7: 1–2 inches is correct because standard wheelchair fitting guidelines recommend adding approximately 1 inch on each side to the measured hip width to allow clearance for clothing and arm movement without compromising postural support.
column8: 0 inches is incorrect because matching the exact hip width would cause friction and pressure on the hips during propulsion.
column9: 3–4 inches is incorrect because excessive seat width increases lateral trunk lean during propulsion and decreases efficiency.
column10: 5–6 inches is incorrect because overly wide seats can lead to poor posture, shoulder strain, and difficulty accessing the pushrims.

column2: A 55-year-old woman with incomplete T4 spinal cord injury is being measured for a new ultralight manual wheelchair. Her popliteal-to-back measurement while seated is 20 inches. To optimize pelvic support and reduce risk of skin breakdown at the popliteal fossa, what should the seat depth be set to?
column7: 18 inches is correct because standard fitting guidelines recommend setting seat depth approximately 2 inches less than the popliteal-to-back measurement, allowing clearance behind the knees to prevent pressure while maintaining thigh support.
column8: 20 inches is incorrect because matching popliteal-to-back distance exactly would create pressure at the popliteal fossa and impair circulation.
column9: 16 inches is incorrect because an excessively short seat depth reduces thigh support and increases pressure on the ischial tuberosities.
column10: 22 inches is incorrect because a seat depth longer than the leg length causes significant pressure at the popliteal fossa and can push the pelvis forward.

column2: A 48-year-old man with complete T8 paraplegia is being fitted for a rigid manual wheelchair. He has good trunk control and no history of skin breakdown. To maximize upper limb mobility for propulsion while still providing adequate trunk support, where should the back height be set relative to his scapula?
column3: Just below the inferior angle of the scapula
column4: At the superior border of the scapula
column6: Above the superior border of the scapula
column7: Just below the inferior angle of the scapula is correct because in users with good trunk control, setting the back height here allows full shoulder movement for propulsion while maintaining lower thoracic support.
column8: At the superior border of the scapula is incorrect because it restricts scapular movement, making propulsion less efficient.
column9: At the mid-scapular level is incorrect because it still partially impedes scapular motion compared to a lower back height.
column10: Above the superior border of the scapula is incorrect because it limits upper limb range of motion and is reserved for users with poor trunk control who require maximum support.

column2: A 42-year-old man with complete C5 spinal cord injury uses a power wheelchair with a tilt-in-space seating system. He has a history of recurrent stage II sacral pressure injuries despite using a high-quality pressure-relieving cushion. Which primary benefit of tilt-in-space is most relevant for preventing further skin breakdown in this patient?
column3: Redistributing pressure away from the ischial tuberosities without changing hip and knee angles
column4: Increasing hip flexion to improve propulsion mechanics
column5: Reducing spasticity by altering lower extremity positioning
column6: Improving environmental access by raising the seat height
column7: Redistributing pressure away from the ischial tuberosities without changing hip and knee angles is correct because tilt-in-space maintains joint angles while rotating the seating system posteriorly, shifting weight onto the trunk and backrest to offload the pelvis and protect skin integrity in patients with SCI who are at high risk for pressure injuries.
column8: Increasing hip flexion to improve propulsion mechanics is incorrect because tilt-in-space does not change hip flexion and is not intended for propulsion efficiency.
column9: Reducing spasticity by altering lower extremity positioning is incorrect because spasticity control requires targeted positioning or tone-modulating components, not tilt alone.
column10: Improving environmental access by raising the seat height is incorrect because this is a function of a seat elevator, not tilt-in-space.

column2: A 38-year-old woman with complete C4 spinal cord injury uses a power wheelchair with a recline seating system. She has limited tolerance for upright sitting due to orthostatic hypotension. Which primary benefit of a recline system is most relevant for addressing her symptoms?
column3: Changing hip and knee angles to allow supine positioning for blood pressure stabilization
column4: Redistributing pressure away from the ischial tuberosities without changing joint angles
column5: Improving propulsion efficiency by increasing elbow extension range
column6: Enhancing community mobility by elevating the seat height
column7: Changing hip and knee angles to allow supine positioning for blood pressure stabilization is correct because recline systems alter seat-to-back angle, allowing the user to assume a more horizontal position, which can help manage orthostatic hypotension in individuals with high-level SCI.
column8: Redistributing pressure away from the ischial tuberosities without changing joint angles is incorrect because this describes tilt-in-space, not recline.
column9: Improving propulsion efficiency by increasing elbow extension range is incorrect because recline systems do not improve propulsion mechanics and are not designed for manual wheelchair performance.
column10: Enhancing community mobility by elevating the seat height is incorrect because this is a function of a seat elevator, not a recline system.

column2: A 45-year-old man with complete C6 spinal cord injury presents for wheelchair seating evaluation. He has a history of recurrent pressure injuries over the sacrum and also experiences symptomatic orthostatic hypotension when upright for prolonged periods. Which seating system feature combination would best address both issues?
column7: Tilt-in-space combined with recline is correct because tilt provides pressure relief without changing joint angles, while recline allows supine positioning to manage orthostatic hypotension, making the combination ideal for patients with both skin integrity and blood pressure concerns.
column8: Tilt-in-space alone is incorrect because it does not address orthostatic hypotension.
column9: Recline alone is incorrect because it does not effectively redistribute pressure away from the ischial tuberosities without altering hip angle.
column10: Seat elevator combined with tilt-in-space is incorrect because seat elevation does not manage orthostatic hypotension and is not necessary for skin protection.

column2: A 28-year-old man with complete T10 spinal cord injury is training for a marathon using his ultralight manual wheelchair. He wants to maximize propulsion efficiency on smooth paved roads while minimizing rolling resistance. Which wheel type is most appropriate for his needs?
column3: Spoked wheels with high-pressure pneumatic tires
column4: Mag wheels with high-pressure pneumatic tires
column7: Spoked wheels with high-pressure pneumatic tires is correct because spoked wheels are lighter and have less rotational inertia than mag wheels, while high-pressure pneumatic tires reduce rolling resistance and improve efficiency on smooth surfaces.
column8: Mag wheels with high-pressure pneumatic tires is incorrect because although high-pressure pneumatic tires are efficient, mag wheels are heavier and increase propulsion effort over long distances.
column9: Spoked wheels with solid tires is incorrect because solid tires increase vibration and rolling resistance compared to high-pressure pneumatic tires.
column10: Mag wheels with solid tires is incorrect because they combine the heavier weight of mag wheels with the greater rolling resistance and vibration of solid tires.

column2: A 33-year-old man with T12 spinal cord injury is adjusting his ultralight manual wheelchair for improved maneuverability in tight indoor spaces. Which axle position change will most effectively decrease his wheelchair’s turning radius?
column7: Move the rear axle forward is correct because shifting the axle forward shortens the wheelchair’s wheelbase, reducing turning radius and improving maneuverability, though it may decrease rearward stability.
column8: Move the rear axle backward is incorrect because increasing the wheelbase lengthens the turning radius, making the chair less maneuverable.
column9: Lower the rear axle height is incorrect because this primarily changes seat-to-floor height and propulsion biomechanics, not turning radius.
column10: Raise the rear axle height is incorrect because this primarily affects seat height and camber relative to the user’s arm position, not turning radius.

column2: A 40-year-old man with T9 spinal cord injury recently experienced a backward fall while navigating an incline in his ultralight manual wheelchair. He wants to improve rearward stability, even if it means sacrificing some propulsion efficiency. Which axle position change is most appropriate?
column7: Move the rear axle backward is correct because shifting the axle rearward increases the wheelchair’s wheelbase, moving the user’s center of mass forward relative to the axle, which improves rearward stability but increases turning radius and decreases propulsion efficiency.
column8: Move the rear axle forward is incorrect because it reduces rearward stability and increases the risk of tipping backward.
column9: Lower the rear axle height is incorrect because this primarily changes seat height and biomechanics rather than directly increasing rearward stability.
column10: Increase wheel camber is incorrect because camber improves lateral stability, not rearward stability on inclines.

column2: A 27-year-old man with complete T4 spinal cord injury wants to adjust his ultralight manual wheelchair to reduce the energy cost of propulsion during daily community mobility. He accepts that this change may make the wheelchair less stable when going up ramps. Which axle position adjustment is most appropriate?
column7: Move the rear axle forward is correct because this positions the user’s shoulder closer to the axle, improving pushrim access, decreasing stroke length, and reducing rolling resistance, all of which enhance propulsion efficiency at the expense of rearward stability.
column8: Move the rear axle backward is incorrect because it increases rearward stability but lengthens the stroke and increases propulsion effort.
column9: Lower the rear axle height is incorrect because it primarily changes seat-to-floor height and propulsion biomechanics rather than fore–aft efficiency.
column10: Increase wheel camber is incorrect because camber improves lateral stability and turning, not propulsion efficiency in the fore–aft plane.

column2: A 25-year-old woman with complete T6 spinal cord injury is competing in wheelchair basketball. She wants to improve lateral stability and turning speed without significantly increasing rolling resistance on indoor courts. Which wheelchair modification is most appropriate?
column6: Use solid tires instead of pneumatic tires
column7: Increase wheel camber to 15° is correct because greater camber widens the wheelbase, improving lateral stability and enabling quicker turns, which benefits court sports without greatly increasing rolling resistance at moderate camber angles.
column8: Increase wheel diameter from 24″ to 26″ is incorrect because larger wheels may increase top speed but do not specifically enhance lateral stability or maneuverability.
column9: Move the rear axle backward is incorrect because this improves rearward stability but increases turning radius and reduces maneuverability.
column10: Use solid tires instead of pneumatic tires is incorrect because solid tires increase rolling resistance and vibration without affecting lateral stability.
column12: The camber angle is the degree to which the tops of wheelchair wheels are tilted inward toward the frame, measured relative to vertical.

column2: A 32-year-old man with complete T9 spinal cord injury uses an ultralight manual wheelchair for both indoor and outdoor mobility. He is considering increasing his wheel camber from 2° to 12° after trying a friend’s basketball chair. Which is the most likely drawback of this change for his daily mobility?
column3: Increased difficulty fitting through narrow doorways
column4: Increased risk of backward tipping on inclines
column5: Reduced lateral stability during sharp turns
column6: Increased vibration and impact transmission over uneven surfaces
column7: Increased difficulty fitting through narrow doorways is correct because greater camber widens the wheelchair’s overall footprint, which can make navigating narrow indoor spaces more challenging despite gains in lateral stability.
column8: Increased risk of backward tipping on inclines is incorrect because camber does not significantly affect fore–aft stability.
column9: Reduced lateral stability during sharp turns is incorrect because increasing camber actually improves lateral stability.
column10: Increased vibration and impact transmission over uneven surfaces is incorrect because this is more related to tire type and pressure, not camber angle.
column12: The camber angle is the degree to which the tops of wheelchair wheels are tilted inward toward the frame, measured relative to vertical.

column2: A 36-year-old man with complete T7 spinal cord injury uses an ultralight manual wheelchair for daily community mobility. He frequently encounters cracked sidewalks and uneven pavement. Which caster selection would best improve performance and safety in this environment?
column7: Large, pneumatic casters is correct because they roll over uneven terrain more easily than small casters, and the pneumatic tires absorb shock and vibration, improving comfort and stability outdoors.
column8: Small, solid casters is incorrect because they are prone to catching in cracks and transmit more vibration.
column9: Small, pneumatic casters is incorrect because although pneumatic tires help with shock absorption, the small diameter still performs poorly on uneven terrain.
column10: Large, solid casters is incorrect because the solid material increases vibration and decreases comfort, even though the larger diameter improves obstacle negotiation.

column2: A 42-year-old woman with complete T9 spinal cord injury uses an ultralight manual wheelchair primarily in her small apartment. She wants to improve maneuverability in tight spaces and reduce turning radius. Which caster type would best meet her needs?
column7: Small, solid casters is correct because their smaller diameter improves turning radius and maneuverability in confined spaces, and solid construction rolls more easily on smooth indoor floors with minimal maintenance.
column8: Large, pneumatic casters is incorrect because they increase turning radius and are better suited for outdoor terrain.
column9: Small, pneumatic casters is incorrect because while they retain maneuverability, they are less durable indoors and offer no advantage on smooth flooring.
column10: Large, solid casters is incorrect because the increased diameter reduces maneuverability indoors.

column2: A 46-year-old man with T8 spinal cord injury is being fitted for a new ultralight manual wheelchair. How should the seat-to-footrest distance be measured to ensure proper lower extremity support?
column3: From the popliteal fossa to the heel with the user’s shoes on
column4: From the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the heel without shoes
column5: From the popliteal fossa to the heel without shoes
column6: From the posterior buttock to the heel with the knees fully extended
column7: From the popliteal fossa to the heel with the user’s shoes on is correct because this accounts for shoe sole thickness, ensuring the correct seat-to-footrest distance to support the thighs without creating pressure under the thighs or at the heels.
column8: From the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the heel without shoes is incorrect because ASIS is not a reference point used in wheelchair footrest measurement.
column9: From the popliteal fossa to the heel without shoes is incorrect because it fails to account for shoe height, which may result in an incorrect footrest height.
column10: From the posterior buttock to the heel with the knees fully extended is incorrect because this measures seat depth and lower extremity length in a position not used for footrest fitting.

column2: Which wheelchair cushion type maximally distributes pressure?
column7: Air-villous cushion is correct because its interconnected air cells allow immersion and envelopment of the pelvis, providing the greatest pressure redistribution and shear reduction.
column8: Foam cushion is incorrect because it provides limited long-term pressure relief and compresses over time, increasing localized pressure.
column9: Gel cushion is incorrect because it offers less effective pressure redistribution than air-villous designs and may bottom out with prolonged use.
column10: Viscoelastic foam cushion is incorrect because, although it conforms to body contours for comfort, it does not match the pressure redistribution capacity of an air-villous cushion.

column2: Which wheelchair cushion type provides the most stability for a user with poor sitting balance?
column7: Viscoelastic foam cushion is correct because it conforms to body contours, offers a stable base of support, and minimizes unwanted movement, making it ideal for users with poor sitting balance.
column8: Air-villous cushion is incorrect because its high immersion and envelopment can make the surface less stable for users with limited trunk control.
column9: Gel cushion is incorrect because while it can improve comfort, it does not provide as much consistent stability as viscoelastic foam.
column10: Standard foam cushion is incorrect because it lacks the contouring and stability benefits of viscoelastic foam and compresses over time.

column2: A 58-year-old man with complete T10 spinal cord injury has difficulty engaging his standard push-to-lock brakes due to limited grip strength from peripheral neuropathy. Which wheel lock type would most improve his ability to secure the wheelchair independently?
column7: Pull-to-lock brake is correct because it requires a pulling motion rather than pushing, which may be easier for individuals with reduced grip strength or limited hand function.
column8: Push-to-lock brake is incorrect because it requires a pushing motion against resistance, which can be difficult with weak grip.
column9: Scissor lock is incorrect because although it’s low-profile and positioned under the seat, it requires fine motor control and reach, which may not suit this patient.
column10: Hill-holder brake is incorrect because it prevents rolling backward on inclines but does not replace the need for easy, secure manual locking on level ground.

column2: A 29-year-old man with complete T6 spinal cord injury is an active manual wheelchair user who frequently performs lateral transfers. He complains that his wheel locks interfere with his hands and clothing during transfers. Which wheel lock type would be most appropriate?
column7: Scissor lock is correct because it is mounted under the seat and away from the handrim area, providing more clearance for transfers and reducing interference, while still allowing secure wheel locking.
column8: Push-to-lock brake is incorrect because it protrudes alongside the wheel and can snag clothing or obstruct hand movement during transfers.
column9: Pull-to-lock brake is incorrect because, although it may be easier for some users to operate, it still protrudes into the transfer path.
column10: Hill-holder brake is incorrect because it is designed to prevent rollback on inclines, not to minimize interference during transfers.

column2: A 67-year-old woman with a history of ischemic stroke is undergoing inpatient rehabilitation. She has limited mobility on her left side and spends most of the day in a wheelchair. Which intervention is most effective for preventing osteoporosis in this setting?
column3: Weight-bearing activities as tolerated during therapy sessions
column7: Weight-bearing activities as tolerated during therapy sessions is correct because mechanical loading through ambulation, standing frames, or supported weight-bearing stimulates osteoblast activity, countering bone loss in patients with reduced mobility.
column8: High-dose vitamin D supplementation is incorrect because while vitamin D supports calcium absorption, it does not replace the osteogenic stimulus provided by mechanical loading.
column9: Bisphosphonate therapy is incorrect because it slows bone resorption but does not stimulate new bone formation in the absence of weight-bearing activity.
column10: Calcium supplementation is incorrect because adequate calcium is necessary for bone health but insufficient to prevent osteoporosis without mechanical stress.

column2: A 62-year-old woman with incomplete T12 spinal cord injury undergoes dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) testing. Her T-score at the femoral neck is −2.0, and she has no history of fragility fractures. According to WHO criteria, how should her bone density be classified?
column7: Osteopenia is correct because a T-score between −1.0 and −2.5 meets WHO criteria for osteopenia in the absence of fragility fracture.
column8: Osteoporosis is incorrect because it requires a T-score ≤ −2.5 or a fragility fracture.
column9: Severe osteoporosis is incorrect because it requires both a T-score ≤ −2.5 and a fragility fracture.
column10: Normal bone density is incorrect because it is defined as a T-score ≥ −1.0.

column2: A 68-year-old woman with chronic rheumatoid arthritis undergoes DXA testing. Her T-score at the lumbar spine is −2.8, and she has a history of vertebral compression fracture. According to WHO criteria, how should her bone density be classified?
column7: Severe osteoporosis is correct because a T-score ≤ −2.5 with a fragility fracture meets WHO criteria for severe osteoporosis.
column8: Osteopenia is incorrect because it requires a T-score between −1.0 and −2.5 without fracture.
column9: Osteoporosis is incorrect because, while the T-score meets this threshold, the presence of fracture elevates the classification to severe.
column10: Normal bone density is incorrect because it is defined as a T-score ≥ −1.0.

column2: A 55-year-old man with no major medical history undergoes DXA testing for screening purposes. His T-score at the total hip is −0.5, and he has no history of fractures. According to WHO criteria, how should his bone density be classified?
column7: Normal bone density is correct because a T-score ≥ −1.0 meets WHO criteria for normal bone density.
column8: Osteopenia is incorrect because it requires a T-score between −1.0 and −2.5.
column9: Osteoporosis is incorrect because it requires a T-score ≤ −2.5 or a fragility fracture.
column10: Severe osteoporosis is incorrect because it requires both a T-score ≤ −2.5 and a fragility fracture.

column2: A 62-year-old woman with incomplete T12 spinal cord injury undergoes dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) testing. Her T-score at the femoral neck is −2.6, and she has no history of fragility fractures. According to WHO criteria, how should her bone density be classified?
column7: Osteoporosis is correct because a T-score of ≤ −2.5 at the femoral neck, total hip, or lumbar spine meets WHO criteria for osteoporosis, regardless of fracture history.
column8: Osteopenia is incorrect because it is defined as a T-score between −1.0 and −2.5.
column9: Severe osteoporosis is incorrect because this requires a T-score ≤ −2.5 and a history of fragility fracture.
column10: Normal bone density is incorrect because it is defined as a T-score ≥ −1.0.

column2: Which statement best describes the difference between a T-score and a Z-score in bone density testing?
column3: A T-score compares bone mineral density to a young healthy adult reference, while a Z-score compares it to an age- and sex-matched population.
column4: A T-score compares bone mineral density to an age- and sex-matched population, while a Z-score compares it to a young healthy adult reference.
column5: A T-score measures trabecular bone density, while a Z-score measures cortical bone density.
column6: A T-score is used only for premenopausal women, while a Z-score is used only for postmenopausal women.
column7: A T-score compares bone mineral density to a young healthy adult reference, while a Z-score compares it to an age- and sex-matched population is correct because the T-score expresses standard deviations from the mean BMD of a healthy 20–30-year-old of the same sex, while the Z-score expresses standard deviations from the mean BMD of an age- and sex-matched reference group.
column8: A T-score compares bone mineral density to an age- and sex-matched population, while a Z-score compares it to a young healthy adult reference is incorrect because it reverses the definitions of T-score and Z-score.
column9: A T-score measures trabecular bone density, while a Z-score measures cortical bone density is incorrect because both scores can be calculated for any skeletal site and do not distinguish trabecular from cortical bone density.
column10: A T-score is used only for premenopausal women, while a Z-score is used only for postmenopausal women is incorrect because both scores can be calculated for any adult; the choice of which score to emphasize depends on age and menopausal status, not exclusivity.
column12:
The T-score and Z-score are both reported in standard deviations (SD) from a reference mean bone mineral density (BMD), meaning they express how far an individual’s measured BMD is above or below a defined average.
Because standard deviation is a statistical measure of variability, each unit change in a T- or Z-score represents one SD from the reference group’s mean — for example, a T-score of −2.5 means the patient’s BMD is 2.5 SDs below the young healthy adult mean.
This SD-based scaling allows clinicians to quantify fracture risk and compare results across different populations, skeletal sites, and machines, provided proper calibration and reference databases are used.

column2: A 21-year-old competitive cyclist reports fatigue, decreased performance, and recurrent tibial stress fractures. His BMI is 18.0 kg/m². Which diagnosis best explains his condition?
column7: Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S) is correct because it applies to both male and female athletes with low energy availability affecting bone health, metabolism, and performance.
column8: Hypogonadism is incorrect because although RED-S can cause low testosterone, the diagnosis encompasses broader metabolic, skeletal, and performance consequences.
column9: Overtraining Syndrome is incorrect because it is due to training overload without adequate recovery, not necessarily low energy availability.
column10: Osteoporosis is incorrect because it refers to low bone density but does not account for the underlying cause or other systemic effects.

column2: A 55-year-old woman with a history of rheumatoid arthritis is wheelchair-dependent following an incomplete T10 spinal cord injury. She has not had any prior bone mineral density (BMD) testing. When should she undergo BMD screening?
column3: Now, due to secondary osteoporosis risk factors
column4: At age 65, regardless of symptoms or risk factors
column5: Only if she sustains a fragility fracture
column6: Only if her T-score is expected to be below −2.5
column7: Now, due to secondary osteoporosis risk factors is correct because current guidelines recommend BMD screening in postmenopausal women under 65 with major risk factors such as chronic inflammatory disease, immobility, and spinal cord injury.
column8: At age 65, regardless of symptoms or risk factors is incorrect because high-risk patients should be screened earlier than the general population threshold.
column9: Only if she sustains a fragility fracture is incorrect because screening aims to prevent fractures, not just respond after one occurs.
column10: Only if her T-score is expected to be below −2.5 is incorrect because T-scores are obtained from the BMD test itself, and testing should be based on risk assessment, not predicted results.

column2: According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommendations, at what age should routine bone mineral density (BMD) screening begin for men with no major osteoporosis risk factors?
column7: 70 years is correct because USPSTF-aligned specialty guidelines recommend initiating routine screening for men at age 70 when no major risk factors are present.
column8: 60 years is incorrect because earlier screening is only for men with significant risk factors.
column9: 65 years is incorrect because this is the female threshold, not the male threshold.
column10: 75 years is incorrect because screening should begin earlier to detect and treat osteoporosis before fracture risk rises sharply.

column2: According to the U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommendations, at what age should routine bone mineral density (BMD) screening begin for women with no major osteoporosis risk factors?
column7: 65 years is correct because USPSTF recommends routine screening in all women beginning at age 65 when no additional major risk factors are present.
column8: 55 years is incorrect because earlier screening is only for younger postmenopausal women with risk factors.
column9: 60 years is incorrect because the threshold for universal screening is higher.
