column10: 70 years is incorrect because screening should already have been initiated by this age in women without risk factors.

column2: A medication used for osteoporosis treatment is administered intravenously once yearly. It binds to hydroxyapatite in bone, is ingested by osteoclasts during bone resorption, and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway, impairing osteoclast function and inducing apoptosis. Which of the following medications has this mechanism of action?
column7: Zoledronic acid is correct because it is an intravenous nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that localizes to bone mineral, is taken up by osteoclasts, and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase, leading to decreased bone resorption.
column8: Alendronate is incorrect because it is an oral nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate with a similar mechanism but a different route and dosing schedule.
column9: Teriparatide is incorrect because it is a recombinant parathyroid hormone analog that stimulates osteoblast activity and increases bone formation.
column10: Raloxifene is incorrect because it is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that reduces bone resorption through estrogen receptor binding in bone.

column2: A medication used in the treatment of osteoporosis is taken orally once weekly. It binds to hydroxyapatite in bone, where it is ingested by osteoclasts during bone resorption. Inside the osteoclast, it inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway, impairing osteoclast function and inducing apoptosis. Which of the following medications has this mechanism of action?
column7: Alendronate is correct because it is an oral nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that binds to bone mineral, is taken up by active osteoclasts, and inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase, thereby reducing bone resorption.
column8: Teriparatide is incorrect because it is a recombinant parathyroid hormone analog that stimulates osteoblast activity and increases bone formation.
column9: Denosumab is incorrect because it is a monoclonal antibody against RANKL, preventing osteoclast differentiation and survival.
column10: Raloxifene is incorrect because it is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that decreases bone turnover through estrogen receptor binding in bone.

column2: A medication used for osteoporosis treatment is administered by daily subcutaneous injection. It is a recombinant form of human parathyroid hormone (PTH 1-34) that stimulates osteoblast activity more than osteoclast activity when given intermittently, leading to increased bone formation and density. Which of the following medications has this mechanism of action?
column7: Teriparatide is correct because it is a recombinant parathyroid hormone analog that increases bone formation by stimulating osteoblast activity when administered intermittently.
column8: Alendronate is incorrect because it is an oral nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that inhibits osteoclast function via farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase inhibition.
column9: Zoledronic acid is incorrect because it is an intravenous nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that inhibits the same enzyme as alendronate but via annual IV infusion.
column10: Raloxifene is incorrect because it is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that reduces bone turnover through estrogen receptor binding in bone.

column2: A medication used for osteoporosis treatment in postmenopausal women selectively binds to estrogen receptors, acting as an estrogen agonist in bone and an estrogen antagonist in breast and uterine tissue. It reduces bone resorption and turnover while lowering the risk of estrogen receptor–positive breast cancer. Which of the following medications has this mechanism of action?
column7: Raloxifene is correct because it is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that acts as an agonist on bone and antagonist on breast and uterine tissue, reducing vertebral fracture risk and providing breast cancer risk reduction.
column8: Alendronate is incorrect because it is an oral nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate that inhibits osteoclast function via farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase inhibition.
column9: Zoledronic acid is incorrect because it is an intravenous nitrogen-containing bisphosphonate with similar intracellular mechanism to alendronate but different route and dosing.
column10: Teriparatide is incorrect because it is a recombinant parathyroid hormone analog that stimulates osteoblast activity and increases bone formation.

column2: Which of the following fracture types is most common in patients with osteoporosis?
column7: Vertebral body fracture is correct because vertebral compression fractures are the most common type of osteoporotic fracture, often occurring with minimal trauma and frequently affecting the thoracic spine.
column8: Hip fracture is incorrect because although common and morbid in osteoporosis, it occurs less frequently than vertebral fractures.
column9: Distal radius fracture is incorrect because it is a common fragility fracture but ranks below vertebral fractures in incidence.
column10: Proximal humerus fracture is incorrect because it is less common than vertebral, hip, and distal radius fractures in osteoporosis.

column2: In osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures, which pattern of vertebral body deformity is most commonly observed?
column7: Anterior wedging is correct because osteoporosis weakens the anterior column of the vertebra, leading to collapse of the anterior portion and wedge-shaped deformity, most often in the thoracic spine.
column8: Biconcave deformity is incorrect because it is more characteristic of certain metabolic bone diseases like osteomalacia.
column9: Posterior wedging is incorrect because posterior vertebral body collapse is rare and usually occurs with trauma or malignancy, not osteoporosis.
column10: Burst fracture pattern is incorrect because it involves high-energy trauma with comminution and retropulsion, not typical low-energy osteoporotic fractures.

column2: An 82-year-old woman with severe osteoporosis and kyphosis reports focal left-sided flank pain that worsens with side bending toward the painful side. Examination reveals the lower ribs approximating the iliac crest with mild skin indentation. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
column7: Costal iliac impingement syndrome is correct because the patient has severe kyphosis and loss of height from osteoporosis, causing the lower ribs to approximate the iliac crest and irritate soft tissues, producing focal pain exacerbated by side bending.
column8: Intercostal neuralgia is incorrect because it typically presents with burning or shooting pain along the rib interspaces due to nerve irritation, not rib-to-pelvis contact.
column9: Lumbar radiculopathy is incorrect because it causes dermatomal pain with possible neurologic deficits rather than localized rib–pelvis impingement pain.
column10: Quadratus lumborum strain is incorrect because it presents with muscle tenderness after overuse or strain, without the bony approximation seen in costal iliac impingement.

column2: A 45-year-old man sustains deep partial-thickness burns to the anterior neck. Which positioning strategy best prevents contracture in this region?
column3: A. Neck in extension with no pillow
column4: B. Neck flexed with head of bed elevated
column5: C. Neck in neutral with soft cervical collar
column6: D. Neck extended with a small pillow under the occiput
column7: Neck in extension with no pillow is correct because anterior neck burns tend to contract into flexion, and extension without a pillow maintains tissue length and prevents flexion contracture.
column8: Neck flexed with head of bed elevated is incorrect because it encourages flexion contracture formation.
column9: Neck in neutral with soft cervical collar is incorrect because neutral positioning does not adequately counteract the strong tendency toward flexion.
column10: Neck extended with a small pillow under the occiput is incorrect because even a small pillow allows partial flexion, which promotes contracture.

column2: A 28-year-old woman sustains burns to the axilla. Which anti-contracture position is most appropriate?
column4: B. Shoulder adduction with elbow flexed
column5: C. Shoulder neutral with elbow extended
column7: Shoulder abduction 100°, external rotation is correct because axillary burns tend to contract into adduction and internal rotation; this position maintains joint range and reduces the risk of axillary webbing.
column8: Shoulder adduction with elbow flexed is incorrect because it positions the shoulder in the direction of contracture.
column9: Shoulder neutral with elbow extended is incorrect because neutral positioning does not adequately stretch the axilla.
column10: Shoulder abduction 45°, internal rotation is incorrect because internal rotation promotes anterior axillary fold shortening despite some abduction.

column2: A 33-year-old man sustains circumferential burns to the dorsal aspect of the hand and fingers. What is the optimal positioning to prevent contracture?
column3: A. Wrist extension 20°, MCP flexion 80°, IP extension, thumb abduction
column4: B. Wrist flexion 20°, MCP extension, IP flexion, thumb adduction
column5: C. Wrist neutral, MCP extension, IP extension, thumb neutral
column6: D. Wrist extension 20°, MCP flexion 30°, IP flexion, thumb adduction
column7: Wrist extension 20°, MCP flexion 80°, IP extension, and thumb abduction is correct because dorsal hand burns tend to contract into wrist flexion, MCP extension, and thumb adduction; this position maintains functional length and prevents clawing.
column8: Wrist flexion 20°, MCP extension, IP flexion, thumb adduction is incorrect because it replicates the contracture posture.
column9: Wrist neutral, MCP extension, IP extension, thumb neutral is incorrect because it allows MCP hyperextension and thumb adduction, promoting functional loss.
column10: Wrist extension 20°, MCP flexion 30°, IP flexion, thumb adduction is incorrect because MCP flexion is inadequate to prevent extensor tendon shortening and thumb position promotes adduction contracture.

column2: A 30-year-old man is 6 weeks post deep partial-thickness burns to both forearms and hands. He is being fitted for custom compression garments to minimize hypertrophic scarring. At what level of sustained pressure will blood flow begin to be reduced in the underlying tissue?
column7: 25 mmHg is correct because sustained external pressure at or above capillary closing pressure (~25 mmHg) can begin to reduce local blood flow, which is why compression garments are designed to apply slightly less than this to avoid ischemia while still controlling scar hypertrophy.
column8: 15 mmHg is incorrect because it is below capillary closing pressure and generally insufficient to reduce local blood flow.
column9: 35 mmHg is incorrect because it exceeds the level needed to reduce blood flow and increases risk of tissue ischemia.
column10: 10 mmHg is incorrect because it is well below capillary closing pressure and will not significantly alter perfusion.

column2: In a randomized controlled trial (RCT) comparing two rehabilitation interventions for post-stroke patients, the p-value for improvement in Functional Independence Measure (FIM) scores is 0.04. Which of the following is the most accurate interpretation of this finding?
column3: There is a 4% probability of observing this difference, or a more extreme one, if there is truly no difference between the interventions.
column4: There is a 96% probability that the experimental intervention is effective.
column5: The probability that the null hypothesis is true is 4%.
column6: The experimental intervention is clinically significant.
column7: There is a 4% probability of observing this difference, or a more extreme one, if there is truly no difference between the interventions is correct because a p-value represents the probability of obtaining results at least as extreme as those observed, assuming the null hypothesis is true, and does not indicate the probability the null is true or the magnitude of clinical effect.
column8: There is a 96% probability that the experimental intervention is effective is incorrect because a p-value does not directly provide the probability that a treatment works.
column9: The probability that the null hypothesis is true is 4% is incorrect because the p-value does not measure the probability that the null hypothesis is true.
column10: The experimental intervention is clinically significant is incorrect because statistical significance does not imply clinical significance without consideration of effect size and clinical context.

column2: A cohort study examines the relationship between chronic opioid use and risk of developing constipation. The relative risk (RR) for constipation in the opioid group compared with non-opioid users is 3.0. Which of the following is the best interpretation?
column3: Patients taking opioids have three times the risk of developing constipation compared with those not taking opioids.
column4: The relative risk means patients taking opioids have a 3% higher risk of developing constipation.
column5: The relative risk means there is a threefold higher probability that the null hypothesis is false.
column6: The relative risk means opioid use causes constipation in three times as many patients.
column7: Patients taking opioids have three times the risk of developing constipation compared with those not taking opioids is correct because a RR of 3.0 indicates that the exposed group has triple the risk of the outcome compared to the unexposed group.
column8: The relative risk means patients taking opioids have a 3% higher risk of developing constipation is incorrect because RR is a ratio, not a small absolute percentage change.
column9: The relative risk means there is a threefold higher probability that the null hypothesis is false is incorrect because RR does not provide probabilities about the null hypothesis.
column10: The relative risk means opioid use causes constipation in three times as many patients is incorrect because RR from an observational study cannot establish causation.

column2: A case-control study investigates the association between prior knee injury and the development of knee osteoarthritis. The odds ratio (OR) for knee injury as a risk factor is 4.5. Which is the most accurate interpretation?
column3: Patients with a prior knee injury have 4.5 times the odds of developing knee osteoarthritis compared with those without prior injury.
column4: The odds ratio means patients with a prior knee injury have a 4.5% higher likelihood of developing knee osteoarthritis.
column5: The odds ratio means there is a 4.5-fold greater probability that the null hypothesis is false.
column6: The odds ratio means prior knee injury causes knee osteoarthritis in 4.5 times as many patients.
column7: Patients with a prior knee injury have 4.5 times the odds of developing knee osteoarthritis compared with those without prior injury is correct because an OR > 1 indicates increased odds of the outcome in the exposed group.
column8: The odds ratio means patients with a prior knee injury have a 4.5% higher likelihood of developing knee osteoarthritis is incorrect because OR is a ratio of odds, not a small percentage difference.
column9: The odds ratio means there is a 4.5-fold greater probability that the null hypothesis is false is incorrect because OR does not provide probabilities about the null hypothesis.
column10: The odds ratio means prior knee injury causes knee osteoarthritis in 4.5 times as many patients is incorrect because OR from a case-control study cannot establish causation.

column2: A study compares two physical therapy programs for improving 6-minute walk distance in patients with multiple sclerosis. The mean difference between groups is 30 meters with a 95% confidence interval of 10 to 50 meters. Which of the following is the best interpretation?
column3: We can be 95% confident that the true mean difference in improvement lies between 10 and 50 meters, and the result is statistically significant because the interval does not cross 0.
column4: There is a 95% probability that the true mean difference is exactly 30 meters.
column5: Ninety-five percent of patients improved between 10 and 50 meters.
column6: The confidence interval proves that the physical therapy program is clinically significant.
column7: We can be 95% confident that the true mean difference in improvement lies between 10 and 50 meters, and the result is statistically significant because the interval does not cross 0 is correct because a 95% confidence interval that excludes 0 indicates a statistically significant difference in means.
column8: There is a 95% probability that the true mean difference is exactly 30 meters is incorrect because the confidence interval represents a range, not a single point probability.
column9: Ninety-five percent of patients improved between 10 and 50 meters is incorrect because the CI applies to the estimate of the population mean difference, not individual patient outcomes.
column10: The confidence interval proves that the physical therapy program is clinically significant is incorrect because statistical significance does not guarantee clinical significance.

column2: A randomized trial evaluates a new fall-prevention program in elderly patients. In the control group, 24% experienced at least one fall in 12 months. In the intervention group, 16% experienced at least one fall. Which of the following is the best interpretation of the absolute risk reduction (ARR)?
column3: The program reduced the absolute risk of falling by 8 percentage points, meaning 8 fewer falls per 100 patients compared with the control group.
column4: The program reduced the relative risk of falling by 33%, meaning the proportion of patients falling was one-third lower in the intervention group.
column5: The program reduced the odds of falling by 33%, meaning the ratio of patients falling to those not falling decreased by one-third.
column6: The program prevented falls in 8% of patients, meaning those individuals did not fall because of the intervention.
column7: The program reduced the absolute risk of falling by 8 percentage points, meaning 8 fewer falls per 100 patients compared with the control group is correct because ARR is calculated by subtracting the event rate in the intervention group (16%) from the event rate in the control group (24%), giving 8%. This means that for every 100 patients treated with the program instead of usual care, 8 fewer patients will experience at least one fall in a year.
column8: The program reduced the relative risk of falling by 33%, meaning the proportion of patients falling was one-third lower in the intervention group is incorrect because that is relative risk reduction, not ARR.
column9: The program reduced the odds of falling by 33%, meaning the ratio of patients falling to those not falling decreased by one-third is incorrect because ARR is based on risk (probability), not odds.
column10: The program prevented falls in 8% of patients, meaning those individuals did not fall because of the intervention is incorrect because ARR is a group-level statistic and cannot be attributed to specific individuals.

column2: Researchers compare two gait-retraining methods after incomplete spinal cord injury. Their analysis finds a statistically significant improvement with the new method, but in truth, there is no real difference between methods. Which of the following best describes this error?
column3: Type I error — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true, meaning the study concluded there was a difference when none existed.
column4: Type I error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, meaning the study missed a real effect.
column5: Type II error — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, meaning the study claimed a difference when there was none.
column6: Type II error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, meaning the study missed a true difference.
column7: Type I error — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is actually true, meaning the study concluded there was a difference when none existed is correct because a Type I error is a false positive finding.
column8: Type I error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, meaning the study missed a real effect is incorrect because that describes a Type II error, not Type I.
column9: Type II error — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, meaning the study claimed a difference when there was none is incorrect because that is a false positive, which is Type I.
column10: Type II error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, meaning the study missed a true difference is incorrect because that is the correct definition of Type II error but the scenario describes Type I error.

column2: A small pilot study tests whether adding aquatic therapy to standard rehabilitation improves balance after stroke. The results show no statistically significant difference, but in reality, aquatic therapy truly does improve balance. Which of the following best describes this error?
column3: Type II error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, meaning the study missed a true difference due to factors like small sample size or low power.
column4: Type II error — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, meaning the study claimed a difference when there was none.
column5: Type I error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, meaning the study missed a real effect.
column6: Type I error — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, meaning the study claimed a difference when there was none.
column7: Type II error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, meaning the study missed a true difference due to factors like small sample size or low power is correct because this is a false negative result.
column8: Type II error — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, meaning the study claimed a difference when there was none is incorrect because that is a false positive (Type I).
column9: Type I error — failing to reject the null hypothesis when it is false, meaning the study missed a real effect is incorrect because that is a false negative (Type II).
column10: Type I error — rejecting the null hypothesis when it is true, meaning the study claimed a difference when there was none is incorrect because that describes Type I error, but the scenario is Type II.

column2: A clinical trial compares standard physical therapy to robot-assisted therapy for improving gait speed in patients with multiple sclerosis. Which of the following best states the null hypothesis for this trial?
column3: There is no true difference in mean gait speed improvement between standard therapy and robot-assisted therapy.
column4: Robot-assisted therapy improves gait speed more than standard therapy.
column5: Robot-assisted therapy improves gait speed less than standard therapy.
column6: Both therapies result in exactly identical gait speed improvements in every patient.
column7: There is no true difference in mean gait speed improvement between standard therapy and robot-assisted therapy is correct because the null hypothesis always states there is no true difference between groups.
column8: Robot-assisted therapy improves gait speed more than standard therapy is incorrect because this is the alternative hypothesis.
column9: Robot-assisted therapy improves gait speed less than standard therapy is incorrect because this is also an alternative hypothesis.
column10: Both therapies result in exactly identical gait speed improvements in every patient is incorrect because the null hypothesis applies to population means, not individual patient outcomes.

column2: A large multicenter study of a new robotic exoskeleton is designed with 85% power to detect a clinically meaningful improvement in walking speed. Which is the best interpretation of this 85% power?
column3: If a true effect exists, the study has an 85% probability of detecting it as statistically significant.
column4: The study has an 85% probability that the null hypothesis is false.
column5: The study has an 85% probability of finding a clinically meaningful effect.
column6: The study will find a statistically significant result in 85% of patients.
column7: If a true effect exists, the study has an 85% probability of detecting it as statistically significant is correct because statistical power is the probability of avoiding a Type II error when a true effect is present.
column8: The study has an 85% probability that the null hypothesis is false is incorrect because power does not relate to the probability of the null hypothesis being true or false.
column9: The study has an 85% probability of finding a clinically meaningful effect is incorrect because power is about detecting statistical significance, not clinical significance.
column10: The study will find a statistically significant result in 85% of patients is incorrect because power applies to the study as a whole, not to individual patient outcomes.

column2: A randomized trial tests a new stretching protocol to prevent shoulder contractures in post-stroke patients. In the control group, 20 of 100 patients developed a contracture after 12 months. In the intervention group, 10 of 100 patients developed a contracture. What is the absolute risk reduction (ARR)?
column7: 10% is correct because ARR = (20/100) – (10/100) = 0.20 – 0.10 = 0.10, or 10%, meaning 10 fewer contractures per 100 patients occurred with the intervention compared to control.
column8: 50% is incorrect because that represents the relative risk reduction (10/20 = 0.50), not ARR.
column9: 0.50 is incorrect because ARR is not expressed as odds; this could be misinterpreted as an odds ratio.
column10: 5% is incorrect because it underestimates the actual ARR calculated from the given numbers.

column2: A cohort study examines the effect of a new fall prevention program in elderly patients. In the control group, 30 of 200 patients experienced a fall over one year. In the intervention group, 15 of 200 patients experienced a fall. What is the relative risk (RR) of falling in the intervention group compared to the control group?
column7: 0.50 is correct because RR = (15/200) ÷ (30/200) = 0.075 ÷ 0.15 = 0.50, meaning the intervention group had half the risk of falling compared to the control group.
column8: 2.00 is incorrect because this is the reciprocal of the correct RR, which would represent the risk in the control group relative to the intervention group.
column9: 0.15 is incorrect because this is the absolute risk in the control group, not the relative risk.
column10: 1.50 is incorrect because this would indicate the intervention group had a higher risk, which is inconsistent with the data provided.

column2: A case-control study investigates the association between prior ankle fracture and the development of post-traumatic ankle arthritis. Among 80 patients with ankle arthritis (cases), 40 had a prior ankle fracture. Among 100 patients without ankle arthritis (controls), 20 had a prior ankle fracture. What is the odds ratio (OR) for developing ankle arthritis in patients with a prior ankle fracture?
column7: 4.00 is correct because OR = (40/40) ÷ (20/140) = 1.0 ÷ 0.1429 ≈ 4.0, meaning the odds of arthritis are 4 times higher in those with a prior fracture compared to those without.
column8: 0.25 is incorrect because it is the reciprocal of the correct OR (controls vs cases).
column9: 2.00 is incorrect because it underestimates the odds ratio given the data provided.
column10: 0.50 is incorrect because it implies lower odds in the exposed group, which contradicts the data.
column12: To set up a 2×2 table for an odds ratio question, start by labeling the rows according to exposure status—the top row for people who were exposed to the risk factor and the bottom row for those not exposed. Then, label the columns according to outcome status—the left column for those with the outcome present and the right column for those with the outcome absent. This creates four cells: a (exposed with outcome), b (exposed without outcome), c (unexposed with outcome), and d (unexposed without outcome). Once the counts are placed in these cells, calculate the odds ratio using either OR=(a/b)÷(c/d)OR=(a/b)÷(c/d) or the equivalent shortcut OR=(a×d)/(b×c)OR=(a×d)/(b×c). This approach works for case–control studies and other situations where the outcome and exposure are compared in a categorical manner.

column2: A rehabilitation outcomes researcher finds that the average Functional Independence Measure (FIM) score at discharge for stroke patients in their dataset is 85 with a standard deviation of 10. A patient’s score is 105. Assuming a normal distribution, which of the following best describes what the patient’s z score represents?
column3: The patient’s score is 2 standard deviations above the mean, corresponding to approximately the 97.5th percentile
column4: The patient’s score is 20% higher than the mean, corresponding to approximately the 80th percentile
column5: The patient’s score is 2 standard deviations above the mean, corresponding to approximately the 84th percentile
column6: The patient’s score is 2 standard deviations below the mean, corresponding to approximately the 2.5th percentile
column7: The patient’s score is 2 standard deviations above the mean, corresponding to approximately the 97.5th percentile is correct because (105 − 85) ÷ 10 = 2, and a z score of +2 in a normal distribution falls above 97.5% of scores
column8: The patient’s score is 20% higher than the mean, corresponding to approximately the 80th percentile is incorrect because percent differences from the mean do not correspond directly to normal distribution percentiles
column9: The patient’s score is 2 standard deviations above the mean, corresponding to approximately the 84th percentile is incorrect because a z score of +1 corresponds to about the 84th percentile, not +2
column10: The patient’s score is 2 standard deviations below the mean, corresponding to approximately the 2.5th percentile is incorrect because the calculated z score is positive, not negative
column12: A z score tells you how far a value is from the average, measured in standard deviations, and lets you find the percentage of values below it in a normal (bell-shaped) curve. One standard deviation above the mean (z = +1) covers about 84% of all values below it, and about 68% of all values fall within one standard deviation above or below the mean. Two standard deviations above the mean (z = +2) covers about 97.5% of all values below it, and about 95% of all values fall within two standard deviations of the mean. This makes z scores a quick way to translate “how far from average” into a percentile or probability.

column2: A PM&R researcher investigates whether a history of traumatic brain injury (TBI) is associated with the later development of multiple sclerosis (MS). They identify patients with MS and a control group without MS, then review past records to determine prior TBI exposure. What type of study design is this?
column7: Case-control study is correct because the investigation starts with identifying cases and controls based on outcome status, then looks backward in time to assess prior exposure
column8: Prospective cohort study is incorrect because this design starts with exposure status and follows participants forward in time for outcome development
column9: Case series is incorrect because it describes outcomes in a group with the same condition without a comparison group
column10: Cross-sectional study is incorrect because it assesses exposure and outcome at a single point in time

column2: A group of 500 patients with spinal cord injury (SCI) and 500 without SCI are followed for 15 years to determine the incidence of chronic kidney disease. The exposure (SCI) is known at baseline, and the outcome is tracked over time. What type of study design is this?
column7: Cohort study is correct because the study begins with exposure status and follows participants over time to determine the occurrence of the outcome
column8: Case-control study is incorrect because it starts with outcome status and looks backward for exposures
column9: Case series is incorrect because it lacks a comparison group and does not follow exposed and unexposed groups prospectively
column10: Cross-sectional study is incorrect because it measures exposure and outcome at the same time without follow-up

column2: A physiatrist publishes a report describing 12 patients with post-polio syndrome who underwent a novel aquatic therapy program, summarizing their functional outcomes without including a control group. What type of study design is this?
column7: Case series is correct because it reports detailed information on a group of patients with the same condition, without a comparison group
column8: Case-control study is incorrect because it requires identifying cases and controls and comparing prior exposures
column9: Cohort study is incorrect because it requires comparison between exposed and unexposed groups over time
column10: Cross-sectional study is incorrect because it assesses exposure and outcome at a single point in time in a population-based sample

column2: A PM&R research team uses hospital records from 2005–2015 to investigate whether early inpatient rehabilitation after spinal cord injury (SCI) is associated with lower 10-year mortality. Patients are grouped based on whether they received early inpatient rehabilitation (exposed) or not (unexposed), and mortality outcomes are determined from follow-up data already in the records. What type of study design is this?
column7: Retrospective cohort study is correct because the study starts with exposure groups and determines outcomes using data collected entirely in the past
column8: Retrospective case-control study is incorrect because this design starts with outcome status and then looks back for exposures
column9: Prospective cohort study is incorrect because this design follows exposure groups forward in real time from study initiation
column10: Prospective case-control study is incorrect because this design recruits cases and controls moving forward and assesses exposures as they occur

column2: A PM&R team evaluates a new bedside test for detecting deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in patients with spinal cord injury. Compared with venography (gold standard), the test yields the following: of 80 patients who truly had a DVT, 68 tested positive; of 120 patients without DVT, 30 tested positive. What is the sensitivity of this test?
column7: 85% is correct because sensitivity = true positives ÷ (true positives + false negatives) = 68 ÷ 80 = 0.85 = 85%
column8: 75% is incorrect because it results from dividing true positives by all positive test results, which is the positive predictive value
column9: 70% is incorrect because it miscalculates using true positives ÷ total sample size, which is not sensitivity
column10: 65% is incorrect because it confuses the proportion of true positives among all negative test results

column2: A PM&R researcher validates a new screening questionnaire for sleep apnea in stroke rehabilitation patients against overnight polysomnography (gold standard). Results show: of 100 patients without sleep apnea, 90 tested negative; of 50 patients with sleep apnea, 10 tested negative. What is the specificity of this test?
column7: 90% is correct because specificity = true negatives ÷ (true negatives + false positives) = 90 ÷ 100 = 0.90 = 90%
column8: 80% is incorrect because it results from using true negatives ÷ all negative test results, which is the negative predictive value
column9: 75% is incorrect because it miscalculates using true negatives ÷ total sample size, which is not specificity
column10: 70% is incorrect because it confuses the proportion of true negatives among all positive test results

column2: A PM&R clinic tests a new handheld nerve conduction device for diagnosing carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS), using full EMG/NCS as the gold standard. Results show: of 60 patients who tested positive, 50 truly had CTS; of 40 patients who tested negative, 5 actually had CTS. What is the positive predictive value of this test?
column7: 83% is correct because PPV = true positives ÷ all positive test results = 50 ÷ 60 = 0.833 = 83%
column8: 88% is incorrect because it results from dividing true positives by all patients with the disease, which is sensitivity
column9: 80% is incorrect because it miscalculates using true positives ÷ total sample size, which is not PPV
column10: 75% is incorrect because it confuses the proportion of true positives among all negative results

column2: A PM&R team evaluates a bedside ultrasound screen for deep vein thrombosis (DVT) in stroke rehab patients, using venography as the gold standard. Results show: of 70 patients who tested negative, 63 truly did not have DVT; of 30 patients who tested positive, 5 actually did not have DVT. What is the negative predictive value of this test?
column7: 90% is correct because NPV = true negatives ÷ all negative test results = 63 ÷ 70 = 0.90 = 90%
column8: 85% is incorrect because it results from dividing true negatives by all patients without the disease, which is specificity
column9: 80% is incorrect because it miscalculates using true negatives ÷ total sample size, which is not NPV
column10: 75% is incorrect because it confuses the proportion of true negatives among all positive results

column2: A patient with ALS is in late-stage disease and refuses initiation of noninvasive ventilation, stating they prefer comfort-focused care at home. The PM&R physician knows ventilation could prolong life with minimal burden and the patient’s family is pleading for its use. The patient demonstrates full decision-making capacity and understands the consequences. The physician honors the patient’s decision. Which ethical principle is primary?
column7: Autonomy is correct because the overriding factor is respecting a competent patient’s informed refusal, even when the intervention is likely to extend life and is supported by family wishes
column8: Beneficence is incorrect because promoting benefit is secondary to honoring the patient’s personal values here
column9: Nonmaleficence is incorrect because avoiding harm is not the main driver; the focus is on respecting choice
column10: Justice is incorrect because equitable distribution of care is not at issue

column2: A patient recovering from a severe TBI requests early use of a new, intensive virtual reality–based rehab program. Preliminary studies suggest potential benefit, but it also carries a high risk of provoking severe headaches and seizures, which the patient has experienced before. The PM&R physician recommends against it. Which ethical principle is primary?
column7: Nonmaleficence is correct because the decision centers on avoiding a predictable and significant harm that outweighs the uncertain potential benefit
column8: Beneficence is incorrect because although potential benefit exists, the decision is driven by harm prevention
column9: Autonomy is incorrect because the patient’s preference is not prioritized over safety concerns
column10: Justice is incorrect because this is not about fair allocation of limited resources

column2: During a national shortage of intrathecal baclofen, a rehab hospital can only refill pumps for half its patients this month. The medical director creates a policy prioritizing those at highest risk for withdrawal, even though some lower-priority patients may experience increased spasticity and discomfort. Which ethical principle is primary?
column7: Justice is correct because the central focus is fair and rational allocation of a scarce resource across the patient population, despite potential harm to some
column8: Nonmaleficence is incorrect because while harm avoidance is considered, fairness in distribution drives the decision
column9: Beneficence is incorrect because the decision is not aimed at maximizing benefit for one patient, but balancing fairness for all
column10: Autonomy is incorrect because this is not about patient choice

column2: A high-functioning stroke survivor insists on continuing three hours of daily inpatient therapy, despite meeting all functional goals and no longer qualifying under insurance guidelines. The rehab team explains that continuing therapy would use resources needed for patients with greater need. The patient disagrees, but the therapy schedule is reduced per policy. Which ethical principle is primary?
column7: Justice is correct because the decision prioritizes equitable and appropriate resource allocation based on patient need, even over an individual’s request
column8: Autonomy is incorrect because patient preference does not override fair distribution principles
column9: Beneficence is incorrect because the change is not aimed at maximizing one patient’s benefit
column10: Nonmaleficence is incorrect because avoiding harm is not the driving factor

column2: During an inpatient rehab stay, a patient with TBI demonstrates clear understanding of their medical situation, can explain the risks and benefits of proposed treatment, and communicates a consistent choice to decline a medication. The attending physician determines the patient can make this decision. What is this determination called?
column7: Capacity is correct because it is a clinical determination made by a healthcare provider that a patient can understand, appreciate, and reason about medical decisions at a specific time
column8: Competence is incorrect because it is a legal determination made by a court, not a clinical one
column9: Informed consent is incorrect because it is the process of agreeing to treatment after receiving adequate information, not the ability to make the decision
column10: Decision-making authority is incorrect because it refers to the legal right to make decisions, which may be assigned to someone else if capacity is lacking

column2: A patient with advanced dementia is brought to court for a hearing to determine if they can manage their own finances and healthcare decisions. The judge rules they are unable to do so and appoints a legal guardian. What is the term for the ability being assessed and determined here?
column7: Competence is correct because it is a legal determination by a court regarding a person’s overall ability to make decisions, often across multiple domains
column8: Capacity is incorrect because it is assessed by a clinician for a specific medical decision at a specific time, not by a court
column9: Informed refusal is incorrect because it describes a patient declining treatment after understanding the implications, not the legal status of decision-making ability
column10: Medical decision-making skill is incorrect because it is not a formal ethical or legal term in this context

column2: A 30-year-old woman with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis is 28 weeks pregnant. Which of the following best describes the expected pattern of relapse rate during pregnancy and postpartum?
column3: Relapse rate decreases during pregnancy, especially in the third trimester, and increases in the first 3–6 months postpartum
column4: Relapse rate decreases during pregnancy, especially in the third trimester, and remains low postpartum
column5: Relapse rate is unchanged during pregnancy and increases in the first 3–6 months postpartum
column6: Relapse rate increases during pregnancy and increases further in the postpartum period
column7: Relapse rate decreases during pregnancy, especially in the third trimester, and increases in the first 3–6 months postpartum because pregnancy’s immunomodulatory effects are lost after delivery, leading to a temporary rebound in disease activity
column8: Relapse rate decreases during pregnancy, especially in the third trimester, and remains low postpartum is incorrect because relapse risk rises again soon after delivery
column9: Relapse rate is unchanged during pregnancy and increases in the first 3–6 months postpartum is incorrect because pregnancy itself typically lowers relapse risk
column10: Relapse rate increases during pregnancy and increases further in the postpartum period is incorrect because pregnancy is generally protective until after delivery

column2: A 65-year-old woman in outpatient neurorehabilitation performs a program in which she slowly slides her heel up and down the opposite shin while watching her leg, repeatedly moves her legs from side to side in a seated position, and practices stepping between floor markers in a precise sequence. She has normal muscle strength but loses balance easily when visual input is removed. Which specific rehabilitation program is she performing?
column4: PNF (proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation) lower limb patterns
column7: Frenkel’s exercises is correct because the described slow, repetitive, visually guided lower limb movements are designed to improve coordination in sensory ataxia from proprioceptive loss
column8: PNF (proprioceptive neuromuscular facilitation) lower limb patterns is incorrect because PNF emphasizes functional diagonal movement patterns often with resistance, not isolated slow coordination drills
column9: Cerebellar coordination training is incorrect because these typically use rapid alternating or target-based movements and do not rely primarily on visual compensation for proprioceptive loss
column10: Bobath approach gait training is incorrect because it focuses on normalizing tone and postural control in neurologic conditions such as stroke, not proprioceptive-based coordination loss
column13: Stroke, Speech, Swallowing, and Other Neurologic Disorders

column2: At initial contact, a patient’s forefoot rapidly slaps the ground immediately after the heel strikes. This gait deviation is most likely due to which underlying problem?
column4: Weak concentric contraction of the plantarflexors
column6: Weak eccentric control of knee extension
column7: Impaired eccentric control of plantarflexion is correct because foot slap occurs when the dorsiflexors (primarily tibialis anterior) fail to control the rate of plantarflexion after heel strike, causing the forefoot to drop rapidly
column8: Weak concentric contraction of the plantarflexors is incorrect because this affects push-off in terminal stance, not initial contact
column9: Impaired concentric hip flexor function is incorrect because this affects limb advancement in swing, not controlled foot placement at initial contact
column10: Weak eccentric control of knee extension is incorrect because this is controlled by the quadriceps during loading response, not by the ankle dorsiflexors

column2: While observing a patient walk, you notice that during right stance phase the pelvis drops noticeably on the left side. The trunk leans slightly to the right with each step, and there is no evidence of limb length discrepancy. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this gait deviation?
column3: Weakness of the right gluteus medius
column4: Weakness of the left gluteus medius
column5: Tightness of the right hip adductors
column7: Weakness of the right gluteus medius is correct because this causes a Trendelenburg gait, where hip abductor weakness on the stance side allows the contralateral pelvis to drop
column8: Weakness of the left gluteus medius is incorrect because this would not cause Trendelenburg gait during right stance; swing-side abductor weakness does not produce pelvic drop in stance
column9: Tightness of the right hip adductors is incorrect because this does not produce Trendelenburg gait; it limits abduction but does not destabilize the pelvis in stance
column10: Weakness of the right iliopsoas is incorrect because this affects swing-phase hip flexion, not stance-phase Trendelenburg gait

column2: A 52-year-old patient presents after a flare of right hip osteoarthritis. On observation, the patient spends markedly less time on the right leg during stance and quickly shifts weight to the left leg, resulting in a visibly shortened stance phase on the right. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this gait deviation?
column3: Pain in the right lower extremity
column5: Weakness of the left hip abductors
column7: Pain in the right lower extremity is correct because this produces an antalgic gait, in which stance time on the painful limb is shortened to reduce discomfort
column8: Weakness of the right quadriceps is incorrect because this produces a knee instability pattern, not an antalgic gait
column9: Weakness of the left hip abductors is incorrect because this causes a left-sided Trendelenburg gait, not an antalgic gait from right-sided pain
column10: Spasticity of the right gastrocnemius is incorrect because this may produce an equinus gait, not an antalgic gait

column2: During gait assessment, you observe that the patient’s right leg swings outward in a lateral arc during swing phase before returning to midline for initial contact. The knee remains extended throughout swing, and the patient denies hip pain. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this gait deviation?
column3: Inability to shorten the right limb during swing
column4: Weakness of the right hip abductors
column5: Pain in the right hip joint
column7: Inability to shorten the right limb during swing is correct because this produces a circumduction gait, often due to limited knee flexion, dorsiflexion, or hip flexion preventing normal foot clearance
column8: Weakness of the right hip abductors is incorrect because this produces a Trendelenburg gait, not circumduction gait
column9: Pain in the right hip joint is incorrect because this causes an antalgic gait, not circumduction gait
column10: Weakness of the right plantarflexors is incorrect because this reduces push-off power, not causing circumduction gait

column2: While walking, the patient lifts the right knee and hip higher than normal during swing phase, and the forefoot slaps down at initial contact. This pattern is consistent across multiple steps, and the patient reports difficulty clearing the toes when walking quickly. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this gait deviation?
column3: Weakness of the right ankle dorsiflexors
column6: Weakness of the right hip abductors
column7: Weakness of the right ankle dorsiflexors is correct because this produces a steppage gait, where exaggerated hip and knee flexion is needed to clear a foot with dorsiflexor weakness, often followed by foot slap
column8: Weakness of the right plantarflexors is incorrect because this impairs push-off, not producing steppage gait
column9: Pain in the right forefoot is incorrect because this causes an antalgic gait, not steppage gait
column10: Weakness of the right hip abductors is incorrect because this produces a Trendelenburg gait, not steppage gait

column2: While walking, a patient demonstrates marked right trunk lean during right stance phase. On video review, you note the pelvis remains relatively level despite hip abductor weakness on the right. Which of the following best explains this finding?
column3: Lateral trunk lean toward the stance limb to reduce demand on the weak hip abductors
column4: Pelvic drop on the contralateral side due to stance-side hip abductor weakness
column5: Lateral trunk lean away from the stance limb to assist with limb clearance
column6: Excessive hip and knee flexion during swing to clear the foot
column7: Lateral trunk lean toward the stance limb to reduce demand on the weak hip abductors is correct because this describes a compensated Trendelenburg gait, where the trunk shifts over the stance leg to minimize hip abductor force demand and keep the pelvis level
column8: Pelvic drop on the contralateral side due to stance-side hip abductor weakness is incorrect because this describes an uncompensated Trendelenburg gait, where the pelvis tilts without significant trunk lean
column9: Lateral trunk lean away from the stance limb to assist with limb clearance is incorrect because this is not characteristic of Trendelenburg gait; it may occur in other gait deviations such as contralateral vaulting or clearance strategies
column10: Excessive hip and knee flexion during swing to clear the foot is incorrect because this describes a steppage gait, not a compensated or uncompensated Trendelenburg gait

column2: During gait observation, a patient demonstrates a noticeable pelvic drop on the left side during right stance phase. The trunk remains upright without significant lateral lean. Hip strength testing reveals weakness of the right hip abductors. Which of the following best explains this finding?
column3: Pelvic drop on the contralateral side due to stance-side hip abductor weakness
column4: Lateral trunk lean toward the stance limb to reduce demand on the weak hip abductors
column5: Lateral trunk lean away from the stance limb to assist with limb clearance
column6: Excessive hip and knee flexion during swing to clear the foot
column7: Pelvic drop on the contralateral side due to stance-side hip abductor weakness is correct because this describes an uncompensated Trendelenburg gait, in which the pelvis tilts toward the swing side without trunk lean to counterbalance
column8: Lateral trunk lean toward the stance limb to reduce demand on the weak hip abductors is incorrect because this describes a compensated Trendelenburg gait, not the uncompensated form
column9: Lateral trunk lean away from the stance limb to assist with limb clearance is incorrect because this is not characteristic of Trendelenburg gait; it is seen in clearance strategies like vaulting or opposite-side lean
column10: Excessive hip and knee flexion during swing to clear the foot is incorrect because this describes a steppage gait, not Trendelenburg gait

column2: A 35-year-old man with T10 complete spinal cord injury uses a manual wheelchair for mobility. Compared to an able-bodied individual ambulating at a similar steady pace, which statement best describes his energy expenditure?
column3: Energy cost of wheelchair propulsion is higher due to reduced mechanical efficiency and reliance on smaller upper limb muscle groups
column4: Energy cost of wheelchair propulsion is more than double due to complete loss of lower limb muscle contribution
column5: Energy cost of wheelchair propulsion is equal when matched for external work performed
column6: Energy cost is lower because the rolling motion eliminates lower limb workload
column7: Energy cost of wheelchair propulsion is higher due to reduced mechanical efficiency and reliance on smaller upper limb muscle groups is correct because wheelchair propulsion uses smaller, less fatigue-resistant upper limb muscles with lower mechanical efficiency, producing greater oxygen consumption and metabolic cost compared to walking at the same speed
column8: Energy cost of wheelchair propulsion is more than double due to complete loss of lower limb muscle contribution is incorrect because while energy cost is higher, it is not typically more than double; studies show a moderate but significant increase
column9: Energy cost of wheelchair propulsion is equal when matched for external work performed is incorrect because even at equivalent external workloads, metabolic cost is higher in wheelchair propulsion due to decreased efficiency
column10: Energy cost is lower because the rolling motion eliminates lower limb workload is incorrect because upper limb inefficiency outweighs the absence of lower limb activity, resulting in higher—not lower—energy expenditure

column2: A 28-year-old military veteran with a history of traumatic bilateral transfemoral amputations is undergoing gait training with bilateral prostheses. Compared to able-bodied walking at a similar speed, what is the approximate increase in metabolic cost for this patient?
column7: ~280% above normal is correct because bilateral transfemoral amputees require nearly three times the energy of able-bodied walking due to loss of both knee and ankle joints and reliance on less efficient hip-based propulsion
column8: ~150% above normal is incorrect because this is more consistent with bilateral transtibial or unilateral transfemoral amputation
column9: ~90% above normal is incorrect because this reflects the energy demand for a unilateral transfemoral amputation
column10: ~40% above normal is incorrect because this matches the energy increase for a unilateral transtibial amputation

column2: A 64-year-old man with peripheral vascular disease has undergone a left transtibial amputation. Compared to able-bodied walking at a similar speed, what is the approximate increase in metabolic cost for this patient?
column7: ~40% above normal is correct because a unilateral transtibial amputation retains the knee joint, leading to a moderate increase in metabolic cost while still being more efficient than transfemoral levels
column8: ~280% above normal is incorrect because this is seen in bilateral transfemoral amputations
column9: ~25% above normal is incorrect because this is closer to the value for a traumatic unilateral transtibial amputation
column10: ~90% above normal is incorrect because this is typical of a unilateral transfemoral amputation

column2: A 71-year-old man with peripheral arterial disease has undergone a right transfemoral amputation. Compared to able-bodied walking at a similar speed, what is the approximate increase in metabolic cost for this patient?
column7: ~90% above normal is correct because a unilateral transfemoral amputation requires substantially more energy due to the loss of both knee and ankle function on one side, necessitating hip-driven gait mechanics
column8: ~280% above normal is incorrect because this is associated with bilateral transfemoral amputations
column9: ~40% above normal is incorrect because this corresponds to a unilateral transtibial amputation
column10: ~25% above normal is incorrect because this is closer to the value for a traumatic unilateral transtibial amputation

column2: A 32-year-old man presents to a rehabilitation clinic after a transradial amputation. Epidemiologic data are reviewed during his counseling session. In the United States, what is the leading cause of acquired upper limb amputation?
column7: Trauma is correct because most acquired upper limb amputations result from traumatic events such as motor vehicle accidents, occupational injuries, or combat-related trauma, making it the leading cause in the United States
column8: Malignancy is incorrect because while cancer can necessitate upper limb amputation, it is a much less common cause than trauma
column9: Congenital limb deficiency is incorrect because this represents a cause of congenital limb loss, not acquired amputation
column10: Peripheral vascular disease is incorrect because this is the most common cause of lower limb amputation, not upper limb

column2: A 25-year-old man with a unilateral transradial amputation is being evaluated for prosthetic fitting. Which terminal device is most commonly prescribed for individuals with upper limb deficiency?
column7: Voluntary-opening hook is correct because it is the most commonly used terminal device due to its durability, light weight, low maintenance, and excellent grip precision, making it widely preferred for functional use
column8: Voluntary-closing hook is incorrect because while it offers strong pinch force, it is less common due to the greater physical effort required for operation
column9: Myoelectric hand is incorrect because it provides a more natural appearance and multiple grip patterns but is heavier, more expensive, and requires maintenance, making it less commonly prescribed overall
column10: Cosmetic hand is incorrect because it offers good appearance but minimal functional capability, so it is not the most common choice for active daily use

column2: A 52-year-old man with a unilateral transradial amputation is using a body-powered prosthesis with a voluntary-opening hook. He reports difficulty performing self-feeding and grooming activities, requiring exaggerated shoulder flexion and abduction to bring objects to his mouth. You observe that he must elevate his prosthetic elbow high and abduct his shoulder to position utensils appropriately. Which prosthetic component would most directly address this problem?
column7: Wrist flexion unit is correct because it allows the terminal device to flex toward the user, enabling tasks like feeding and grooming without excessive compensatory shoulder or trunk motion, thereby improving functional efficiency
column8: Wrist rotation unit is incorrect because it provides pronation and supination but does not bring the terminal device closer to the mouth
column9: Step-up elbow is incorrect because it increases elbow range for short residual limbs, not to alter terminal device angle for feeding tasks
column10: Adjustable friction wrist is incorrect because it controls resistance to rotation but does not change terminal device positioning in the sagittal plane

column2: A 41-year-old woman with a right transradial amputation uses a body-powered prosthesis with a voluntary-opening hook and fixed wrist attachment. She reports difficulty turning a key, using a screwdriver, and orienting eating utensils without repositioning her entire upper body. On exam, she compensates for lack of prosthetic rotation by rotating her trunk and abducting her shoulder. Which prosthetic component would most directly improve her ability to perform these tasks?
column7: Wrist rotation unit is correct because it allows controlled pronation and supination of the terminal device, enabling efficient tool use and object manipulation without excessive trunk or shoulder compensation
column8: Wrist flexion unit is incorrect because it changes the terminal device angle in the sagittal plane for tasks like feeding but does not improve rotation
column9: Step-up elbow is incorrect because it increases elbow range of motion for short residual limbs, not rotational control
column10: Adjustable friction elbow is incorrect because it changes the resistance of elbow motion, not terminal device orientation in the transverse plane

column2: A 35-year-old man with a unilateral below-elbow amputation is being fitted for a body-powered prosthesis. He needs to perform light assembly work requiring repeated repositioning of the terminal device angle without using the contralateral hand. Which prosthetic wrist option best meets this need?
column7: Friction wrist is correct because it allows easy manual rotation of the terminal device into the desired position with minimal resistance, enabling frequent angle adjustments during tasks without requiring contralateral hand use or locking/unlocking mechanisms.
column8: Quick-change wrist unit is incorrect because it is designed for rapid swapping of terminal devices, not for frequent rotation of a single device during tasks.
column9: Flexion wrist unit is incorrect because it primarily allows positioning of the terminal device in flexion for activities like midline feeding, not repeated rotational adjustments.
column10: Constant-friction wrist with locking mechanism is incorrect because although it can hold positions firmly, it requires more effort or an unlocking step to reposition, reducing efficiency for frequent adjustments.

column2: A 29-year-old woman sustained a traumatic amputation at the proximal third of her forearm. Examination shows limited forearm length, good shoulder motion, and no skin breakdown. She plans to return to her job in a warehouse, which requires frequent lifting and carrying of heavy boxes. She wants minimal harnessing and the ability to control terminal device rotation through the socket. Which socket design is most appropriate?
column7: Muenster socket is correct because for very short transradial residual limbs it provides high trim lines for maximum suspension, excellent rotational control, and minimizes the need for heavy harnessing—ideal for physically demanding work.
column8: Self-suspending transcarpal socket is incorrect because it is designed for distal partial hand amputations and cannot provide adequate suspension for a short forearm.
column9: Figure-of-eight harness with conventional socket is incorrect because although it provides cable control, it does not maximize suspension or rotational stability in a short residual limb.
column10: Split socket is incorrect because its primary purpose is to allow pronation/supination of longer residual limbs, not to optimize suspension and rotational stability in a short limb.

column2: A 42-year-old man with a mid-length transradial amputation is being evaluated for a new body-powered prosthesis. He needs to perform tasks that require frequent pronation and supination of the residual limb while minimizing harness movement. Which socket design is most appropriate?
column7: Split socket is correct because it is designed for mid-length transradial limbs to allow independent pronation and supination of the residual limb while still maintaining adequate suspension for functional use.
column8: Muenster socket is incorrect because it limits pronation and supination in exchange for greater suspension, making it more suitable for short transradial limbs.
column9: Self-suspending transcarpal socket is incorrect because it is intended for very distal partial hand amputations and does not accommodate a transradial amputation.
column10: Figure-of-eight harness with conventional socket is incorrect because although it can provide suspension and cable control, it does not enhance rotational freedom of the residual limb like a split socket does.

column2: A 34-year-old man with a very short transradial amputation is being fitted for a body-powered prosthesis. He has full elbow range of motion but minimal residual limb length for suspension and rotational control. To improve stability, maintain alignment, and increase terminal device motion, which prosthetic component is most appropriate?
column7: Step-up hinges is correct because they mechanically amplify elbow motion to increase terminal device range, making them ideal for very short transradial amputations with limited forearm motion, and they are frequently used in combination with a split socket to enhance suspension and alignment.
column8: Polycentric hinges is incorrect because they are designed to maintain anatomical rotation during flexion, not to amplify motion for short residual limbs.
column9: Single-axis hinges is incorrect because they provide simple hinge motion without mechanical advantage, making them less effective for short transradial limbs requiring amplified range.
column10: Flexible hinges is incorrect because they allow limited pronation/supination but do not enhance motion range in very short limbs.

column2: A 45-year-old man with a mid-length transradial amputation is being fitted for a body-powered prosthesis. He has preserved pronation and supination at the residual limb and needs to maintain natural forearm rotation during elbow flexion. Which prosthetic component is most appropriate?
column7: Polycentric hinges is correct because they maintain anatomical forearm rotation during elbow flexion, which is important in mid-length transradial amputations where pronation and supination are preserved, and they can be used with a split socket to optimize rotational function.
column8: Step-up hinges is incorrect because they are intended to mechanically amplify elbow motion for very short transradial limbs with limited motion, not to preserve anatomical rotation.
column9: Single-axis hinges is incorrect because they provide basic hinge motion without preserving natural rotation.
column10: Flexible hinges is incorrect because while they allow some pronation/supination, they do not maintain anatomical rotation during elbow flexion as effectively as polycentric hinges.

column2: A 38-year-old man with a mid-length transradial amputation is being fitted for a body-powered prosthesis. He needs reliable suspension for heavy lifting, with a harness system that uses shoulder movement to control the terminal device via cable transmission. Which harness system is most appropriate?
column7: Figure-of-eight harness is correct because it provides secure suspension for transradial prostheses and uses scapular abduction and humeral flexion to efficiently transmit force to the control cable, making it ideal for heavy-duty functional use.
column8: Figure-of-nine harness is incorrect because it eliminates the axilla loop, reducing suspension stability and making it better suited for lighter work.
column9: Shoulder saddle harness is incorrect because it is primarily used to distribute weight in heavy above-elbow prostheses, not as the main suspension system for transradial limbs.
column10: Chest strap harness is incorrect because it is typically used for very short transhumeral amputations or shoulder disarticulations, not mid-length transradial prostheses.

column2: A 46-year-old man with a transradial amputation is being fitted for a body-powered prosthesis. He primarily performs desk work and light household tasks, and he wants a comfortable harness that avoids axillary pressure while still allowing control of the terminal device. Which harness system is most appropriate?
column7: Figure-of-nine harness is correct because it eliminates the axilla loop for increased comfort, making it suitable for lighter functional demands while still allowing cable control of a transradial prosthesis.
column8: Figure-of-eight harness is incorrect because although it provides better suspension, it places pressure in the axilla and is typically chosen for heavier-duty tasks.
column9: Shoulder saddle harness is incorrect because it is mainly used to distribute load for heavy above-elbow prostheses, not for light-duty transradial use.
column10: Chest strap harness is incorrect because it is generally reserved for very short transhumeral amputations or shoulder disarticulations rather than transradial prostheses.

column2: A 40-year-old man with a mid-length transradial amputation is being fitted for a body-powered prosthesis. He needs a cable system that maximizes mechanical efficiency for operating a voluntary-opening terminal device, using gross body motions such as scapular abduction and humeral flexion for control. Which cable system is most appropriate?
column7: Dual-control cable system is correct because it is the standard for transradial prostheses, allowing separate control of elbow flexion and terminal device operation via gross body motions, providing high efficiency for voluntary-opening devices.
column8: Single-control cable system is incorrect because it operates only one function, making it more suited for simple partial hand devices than full transradial prostheses.
column9: Triple-control cable system is incorrect because it is designed for higher-level amputations requiring elbow lock control in addition to elbow and terminal device operation.
column10: Cross-back cable system is incorrect because it refers to a harness routing style rather than a distinct multi-function control system for transradial prostheses.

column2: A 37-year-old man with a mid-length transradial amputation is being trained to use his new dual-control cable system. Which combination of functions can this system operate?
column3: Elbow flexion and terminal device operation
column4: Elbow flexion and terminal device locking
column5: Terminal device operation and elbow lock control
column6: Terminal device pre-positioning and elbow flexion
column7: Elbow flexion and terminal device operation is correct because a dual-control cable system in a transradial prosthesis allows the user to separately control elbow flexion and operate the terminal device using gross body movements such as scapular abduction and humeral flexion.
column8: Elbow flexion and terminal device locking is incorrect because voluntary-opening and voluntary-closing devices are controlled, not locked, in a dual-control system.
column9: Terminal device operation and elbow lock control is incorrect because elbow locking is not part of a dual-control system; it is an additional feature in triple-control setups for higher-level amputations.
column10: Terminal device pre-positioning and elbow flexion is incorrect because pre-positioning refers to manual alignment, not a cable-controlled function in standard dual-control systems.

column2: During a transfemoral amputation, the surgeon sutures the adductor magnus muscle directly to the distal femur through drilled holes in the bone. What is the primary purpose of this surgical technique?
column3: Improve residual limb stability and muscle function
column7: Improve residual limb stability and muscle function is correct because myodesis, described in the question stem, involves anchoring muscle to bone, preserving muscle tension, enhancing stability, and improving voluntary control of the residual limb for optimal prosthetic function.
column8: Decrease phantom limb pain is incorrect because while proper amputation technique may affect pain outcomes, myodesis is not primarily performed for this purpose.
column9: Prevent neuroma formation is incorrect because neuroma prevention focuses on nerve handling, not muscle fixation to bone.
column10: Reduce postoperative edema is incorrect because edema control is managed through limb wrapping, elevation, and compression, not by muscle-to-bone attachment.

column2: During a transtibial amputation, the surgeon sutures the anterior and posterior muscle groups to each other over the end of the tibia without drilling into the bone. What is the primary purpose of this surgical technique?
column3: Provide soft tissue coverage and shape to the residual limb
column7: Provide soft tissue coverage and shape to the residual limb is correct because myoplasty, described in the question stem, involves suturing opposing muscle groups together over the bone, cushioning the residual limb and creating a balanced, functional contour for prosthetic fitting.
column8: Directly enhance muscle-to-bone stability is incorrect because this is the role of myodesis, not myoplasty.
column9: Prevent phantom limb pain is incorrect because phantom pain is not the primary target of myoplasty.
column10: Reduce risk of neuroma formation is incorrect because neuroma prevention depends on surgical nerve handling techniques, not muscle-to-muscle attachment.

column2: A 10-year-old sustains a severe forefoot injury. The surgeon removes the forefoot but preserves the calcaneus and talus, then fuses the calcaneus to the distal tibia to maintain a stable, weight-bearing heel. Which amputation was performed?
column7: Boyd’s amputation is correct because it removes the forefoot while preserving both the calcaneus and talus, fusing the calcaneus to the tibia for weight-bearing stability.
column8: Pirogoff amputation is incorrect because it retains part of the calcaneus rotated upward and fused to the tibia.
column9: Syme’s amputation is incorrect because it removes the foot at the ankle joint, preserving only the heel pad.
column10: Chopart amputation is incorrect because it is performed through the midtarsal joint, not involving tibial fusion.

column2: A patient undergoes partial foot amputation in which the calcaneus is transected, rotated upward, and fused to the distal tibia, allowing for a shortened but weight-bearing limb. Which amputation was performed?
column7: Pirogoff amputation is correct because it involves partial preservation of the calcaneus, which is rotated and fused to the tibia, providing a weight-bearing end.
column8: Boyd’s amputation is incorrect because it preserves the entire calcaneus and talus and fuses the calcaneus to the tibia.
column9: Syme’s amputation is incorrect because it removes the foot at the ankle and retains only the heel pad.
column10: Lisfranc amputation is incorrect because it is performed through the tarsometatarsal joints and preserves the tarsal bones.

column2: During an amputation for severe hindfoot trauma, the foot is removed at the ankle joint, the talus is excised, and the heel pad is preserved and secured for weight-bearing. Which amputation has been performed?
column7: Syme’s amputation is correct because it removes the foot at the ankle joint, excises the talus, and retains the heel pad for direct end-bearing.
column8: Boyd’s amputation is incorrect because it preserves the calcaneus and fuses it to the tibia.
column9: Pirogoff amputation is incorrect because it preserves part of the calcaneus and fuses it to the tibia.
column10: Chopart amputation is incorrect because it is through the midtarsal joint and retains the hindfoot bones.

column2: A patient undergoes a midfoot-level amputation that transects through the talonavicular and calcaneocuboid joints, preserving the talus and calcaneus but removing the forefoot and midfoot. Which amputation has been performed?
column7: Chopart amputation is correct because it is performed through the midtarsal joint, removing the forefoot and midfoot while preserving the talus and calcaneus.
column8: Lisfranc amputation is incorrect because it is through the tarsometatarsal joints and preserves the entire midfoot.
column9: Syme’s amputation is incorrect because it removes the foot at the ankle joint and preserves only the heel pad.
column10: Boyd’s amputation is incorrect because it preserves the calcaneus and talus and fuses the calcaneus to the tibia.

column2: A patient undergoes amputation through the tarsometatarsal joints, removing all metatarsals but preserving the entire midfoot and hindfoot, including the cuneiforms and cuboid. Which amputation has been performed?
column7: Lisfranc amputation is correct because it is performed through the tarsometatarsal joints, removing the metatarsals but preserving all tarsal bones and the hindfoot.
column8: Chopart amputation is incorrect because it is through the midtarsal joint, removing the midfoot as well as the forefoot.
column9: Pirogoff amputation is incorrect because it involves partial calcaneal preservation fused to the tibia.
column10: Syme’s amputation is incorrect because it removes the foot at the ankle joint and preserves only the heel pad.

column2: During prosthetic training for a new transtibial amputee, the rehabilitation team evaluates the residual limb shape. Which residual limb contour is considered ideal for optimal prosthetic fit and load distribution?
column6: Distal taper with sharp bony prominence
column7: Cylindrical shape is correct because a uniformly contoured limb with equal circumference from proximal to distal allows even pressure distribution within the socket and minimizes risk of skin breakdown.
column8: Conical shape is incorrect because it results in uneven pressure distribution and potential for distal end pressure points.
column9: Bulbous distal end is incorrect because it complicates socket donning and may require excessive socket flaring.
column10: Distal taper with sharp bony prominence is incorrect because it increases risk of focal pressure injury and poor load tolerance.

column2: A patient is being fitted for a transfemoral prosthesis. The rehabilitation team assesses the residual limb to ensure optimal prosthetic fit. Which residual limb shape is considered ideal for comfort, suspension, and load distribution?
column6: Distal narrowing with sharp bony prominence
